And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . ‚ÄčSubscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Iran war exposes 60-40 portfolio frailty
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 3 (Reuters) - For investors, the conflagration in the Middle East and flaring geopolitical tensions worldwide raise an uncomfortable question: how can you hedge these risks if the traditional equity-bond portfolio no longer works?
The answer isn't immediately obvious, but the volatility that has rocked world markets since the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran ‚Äčover the weekend suggests the pressure on investors to find one is only going to intensify.
Implied volatility in U.S. Treasuries on Monday rose the most since April, ‚ÄĆas bonds sank across the curve. Implied U.S. equity market volatility on Tuesday had its biggest rise since October, as stocks around the world tumbled.
Historically, the first port of call for investors scrambling to hedge against geopolitical, economic, or financial market risk has ‚Äď along with gold ‚Äď been Treasuries, long viewed as the safest and most liquid financial asset on the planet.
Bonds were traditionally expected to appreciate during risk-off periods, muting the volatility in equities. This led to the widespread ‚Äčadoption of the traditional portfolio allocation: 60% to stocks, and 40% to bonds.
But since the COVID-19 pandemic, America's mounting fiscal deficit and public debt, along with elevated inflation, have gradually but steadily ‚Äčeroded Treasuries' status as the natural hedge against equity risk.
As a result, the 60/40 portfolio's in-built safety mechanisms have eroded.
This was among the main findings ‚Ā†in an International Monetary Fund blog , opens new tab last month - a timely report, as it turns out - in which the authors note that bonds and stocks now often move in tandem, especially during sharp market ‚Äčsell-offs.
Investors of all stripes, even conservative institutions like pension funds and insurers, are thus exposed to greater volatility and deeper losses. That, of course, also increases financial stability risks for policymakers.
"If diversification fails, volatility ‚Äčcan cascade into broader financial instability. Investors and policymakers must rethink risk management for a new era where traditional hedges fail," they said.
The IMF note argues that investors must explore alternative hedging and diversification strategies, but what exactly should these be?
The blog post highlighted private assets as a potential portfolio buffer against market panic given that they often have lower levels of volatility than publicly traded assets. But recent events in private credit show that ‚Äčthe opaque private markets universe comes with its own risks.
The post also suggested that commodities should be considered. But those arguing that 'hard assets' offer the best hedge against political risk will have been ‚Äčdisappointed by the performance of gold and precious metals this week. Bullion , the most established "safe-haven" asset and traditional hedge against inflation, rose only 1% on Monday and is down 2% on Tuesday.
Meanwhile, platinum and silver have tumbled 10% since ‚ÄĆtrading opened on ‚Ā†Monday. This suggests speculative, short-term flows are driving precious metals prices as much as traditional economic "fundamentals."
Many other commodities ‚Äď with the notable exception of oil and gas ‚Äď are also under selling pressure, including corn, wheat and especially copper. Of course, these are short-term moves, but if they don't reverse, one wonders where the hedging and diversification outlets for investors are.
It's worth noting, however, that before the Iran strikes, Treasuries appeared to be regaining their diversification mojo. The daily correlation between stocks and bonds turned negative during the first two months of the year and was approaching average levels recorded in ‚Äčthe pre-pandemic decade, according to Truist Advisory Services.
Was ‚Äčthat just a short-term anomaly? Potentially not, argues ‚Ā†Keith Lerner, chief investment officer at Truist.
While the current jump in oil prices may make Treasuries less attractive, given the risk of higher inflation and tighter monetary policy in response, a sustained period of high energy prices will soon become a recession risk, Lerner argues. If so, bonds look attractive ‚Äčagain.
"Our view that high-quality bonds should generally still act as good portfolio diversifier has not changed," Lerner said.
Ultimately though, when political machinations are ‚Äčdriving market prices and sentiment ‚Ā†rather than economic or policy fundamentals, risk-management playbooks and diversification models often go out of the window. Investors, whose expertise is understanding how economies and markets work, are not usually great at predicting how long wars will last or how they will end.
"Whether this conflict is long or short, at least let it be a lesson for you to increase your diversification to a wider range of outcomes," said Bob Elliott, ‚Ā†CEO and ‚ÄčCIO at Unlimited.
That is the challenge investors face now ‚Äď and it's not an easy one.
(The opinions expressed here are those ‚Äčof Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ‚Äčbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: UK halts demolition of parts of Grenfell Tower after handprints found on walls
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - Britain's government said on Tuesday it has paused the demolition of parts of Grenfell Tower after bereaved families ‚Äčwarned they would take legal action if walls bearing handprints were ‚ÄĆnot preserved.
A fire tore through the 24-storey social housing block in one of London's wealthiest areas in June 2017, killing 72 people. Demolition began in September last ‚Äčyear.
During pre-demolition visits , families discovered sections of stairwell walls with handprints ‚Äčthey believe belong to victims or survivors, as well as ‚Ā†an Arabic inscription reading "Allahu Akbar" ("God is greatest") on another floor.
Following a ‚Äčrequest to preserve the walls, a government official said they would ‚Äčnot keep any sections above the ninth floor, citing sensitivity and loss of life. Both the handprints and the inscription were located above this level.
Families filed a pre-action ‚Äčletter last week seeking a judicial review of the decision.
Grenfell Next ‚Äčof Kin (GNK), which advocates for families of the deceased, argued that former deputy prime ‚ÄĆminister ‚Ā†Angela Rayner had promised in 2025 that parts of the tower could be preserved for a memorial if the community wished.
They urged the government to protect the areas while discussions with families and memorial designers ‚Äčtake place.
"Due to ‚Äčongoing legal ‚Ā†action, we have paused deconstruction works in the relevant areas," a spokesperson for the government ministry responsible for ‚ÄčGrenfell said.
GNK said the protection was secured only ‚Äčbecause bereaved ‚Ā†families were forced to take legal action.
The government's legal department said sections between the 12th and 14th floors would be cordoned off while demolition ‚Ā†continues ‚Äčelsewhere. Demolition work in those areas has ‚Äčbeen halted pending response to the pre-action letter.
The "Allahu Akbar" inscription between the 17th and 18th ‚Äčfloors has already been destroyed.

item.title: Trump's Iran attack rattles Russian hardliners who call for Putin to double down on war in Ukraine
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 3 (Reuters) - When President Donald Trump returned to the White House last year, some Russian hardliners were cautiously optimistic, hoping his unpredictability and transactional nature might benefit Moscow on Ukraine.
But his attack on Iran means many now see him as a growing threat ‚Äčto Russia itself and are questioning if Trump is the pragmatic, potentially pro-Moscow strongman ready to deal in realpolitik that they thought he was.
Some hawks are publicly demanding that Moscow ‚ÄĆabandon U.S.-brokered peace talks with Ukraine and double down on fighting there instead, arguing that the U.S.-Iran nuclear talks which preceded the U.S.-Israeli air war were a cynical ploy which showed Washington cannot be trusted.
"The unprincipled United States is a threat to the entire world," said nationalist tycoon Konstantin Malofeyev, who is married to a top Kremlin official. "This is the United States we are trying to negotiate with regarding Ukraine. Yes, it wants a weak Europe. But it also wants a weak Russia."
Boris Rozhin, an influential ‚Äčwar blogger who goes by the moniker, "Colonel Cassad" and has nearly 800,000 followers on the Telegram app, said Trump was a monster, driven mad by impunity.
"To seriously count on any agreements or deals with ‚Äčit (the monster) is either foolishness or treason," opined Rozhin.
And Andrei Sidorov, a prominent academic, went further, telling state TV that Trump was "a dangerous man" and that he ‚Ā†regretted that the U.S. president had survived an assassination attempt in July 2024 before he was re-elected later that year.
"Now we understand who is in charge of the world," said Sidorov. "If you look at what Trump ‚Äčis doing now, step-by-step, practically nobody is able to stop him. Let's be honest - Russia is bogged down in Ukraine. Practically all we do now is deal with the Ukrainian question. (And) our main adversary (the U.S.) is acting as ‚Äčan intermediary in those negotiations."
The Kremlin, which still hopes that Trump may be able to help end its war in Ukraine on its own terms and oversee a wider lucrative U.S.-Russia rapprochement, has condemned U.S. actions as "unprovoked aggression." But it has avoided criticising Trump personally and has not offered any tangible material help to Iran beyond diplomatic support.
It has also said it believes that it is in its own interests to continue peace talks on Ukraine - even if events in Iran mean there is uncertainty ‚Äčabout the timing and venue for the next round of talks.
The Kremlin's statement on Ukraine was a signal that it will, for now at least, keep trying to pull off a delicate balancing act - staying on ‚Äčgood enough terms with Trump to keep him engaged on Ukraine while also calling out his policies with which it disagrees.
Russian and Western analysts do not believe there is much Moscow, which has imported, refined and then started to manufacture its ‚ÄĆown Iranian-designed ‚Ā†drones, can do to help Tehran at this stage anyway.
Some also see a potential silver lining for Russia due to the Iran events. Kirill Dmitriev, Putin's special envoy, has raised the possibility that increasing oil prices - which have not yet shot up as much as Moscow needs to balance its budget - could help the state budget which is under strain, while discounts on Russian oil sold to countries like China and India could fall.
Ukraine might also get fewer supplies of U.S. arms and munitions, with air defence missiles sent to Gulf states instead, and less overall U.S. attention and support if the Middle East conflict goes on for some time, some Russian analysts have ‚Äčsuggested.
The harsh rhetoric emanating from hawks does however reflect ‚Äčgenuine unease among Russia's security and political establishment. ‚Ā†They see an increasingly aggressive U.S. president weakening Moscow's influence on the world stage at a time when Russia is tied down in Ukraine and unable to protect its own interests in the same way the Soviet Union once did.
Trump, say the hawks, is systematically taking out Russia's allies. They point to the fate of ‚ÄčSyria's Bashar al-Assad, who was toppled in December 2024 by opposition forces whose leader was later feted by Trump in the White House; to Venezuela's Nicolas Maduro, ‚Äčwho was seized at gunpoint ‚Ā†by U.S. forces in January; and to Iran's Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was assassinated at the weekend in joint U.S.-Israeli strikes.
The fate of long-time ally Cuba, also in Washington's sights, is something that worries them too.
Trump's critics accuse him of being too soft on Moscow and of mistakenly bringing Putin in from the cold with a summit in Alaska last year, but some Russian hardliners are so spooked by Trump's removal of important Moscow allies that they fear he ‚Ā†might even one ‚Äčday turn his attention to Russia, something he has never indicated is on his agenda.
"If Iran holds out, everything could go the ‚Äčother way. If it collapses, we're next," ultra-nationalist philosopher and hardline ideologue Alexander Dugin - who once viewed Trump as a great hope for Russia - told his followers.
"With Trump, when he was faithful to the original MAGA ideology, we had common ground. As Trump rapidly distanced ‚Äčhimself from MAGA and moved closer to the neocons, those points of contact rapidly disappeared. It is better to have nothing to do with Trump as he is today," said Dugin.

item.title: Syria sends thousands of troops to Lebanon border, sources say
item.description:
DAMASCUS/BEIRUT, March 3 (Reuters) - Syria has reinforced its border with Lebanon with rocket units and thousands ‚ÄĆof troops, eight Syrian and Lebanese sources said on Tuesday, as conflict spread in the region including between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon.
The sources included five Syrian military officers, a Syrian security official and two Lebanese security officials who spoke ‚Äčon condition of anonymity.
The Syrian officers said the Syrian reinforcement operation began in February but ‚Äčsped up in recent days. The Syrian and Lebanese armed forces did not immediately ‚Ā†respond to requests for comment.
The Syrian officers, including a senior member of the military, said the move ‚Äčwas aimed at preventing arms and drugs smuggling as well blocking Iran-backed Lebanese Hezbollah or other militants from ‚Äčinfiltrating Syria.
A Syrian officer told Reuters that military formations from several Syrian army divisions, including the 52nd and 84th Divisions, have expanded their presence along the border in western Homs countryside and south of Tartus.
The reinforcements include infantry units, armored ‚Äčvehicles and short-range Grad and Katyusha rocket launchers, the official said.
The Syrian security official said Damascus had ‚Äčno plans for military action against any neighboring country. ‚ÄúBut Syria is prepared to deal with any security threat to itself ‚ÄĆor ‚Ā†its partners,‚ÄĚ he said.
Still, the move has fueled concern among some European and Lebanese officials over a possible incursion.
The Syrian military officers vehemently denied any such plans, saying Syria wants balanced relations with its neighbor after decades of strained ties linked to Syria's outsized influence in Lebanon and Hezbollah's support for the former ‚Äčgovernment of Syrian President ‚ÄčBashar al-Assad during a ‚Ā†14-year civil war.
Syria had troops stationed in Lebanon from 1976 until 2005 including during Lebanon's civil war that ended in 1990.
Hezbollah resumed firing at Israel on ‚ÄčMonday more than a year after reaching a ceasefire to a months-long war ‚Äčin 2024. Since ‚Ā†that ceasefire, Israel continued near-daily strikes.
Israel this week ordered much of Lebanon's south evacuated, with tens of thousands of people displaced. Israeli airstrikes across Lebanon's South and southern Beirut have killed dozens and prompted thousands of ‚Ā†people to ‚Äčflee towards Syria.
A senior Lebanese security official said Syrian authorities ‚Äčtold Beirut that Syria's deployment of rocket launchers along the mountains that form Lebanon's eastern border with Syria was a ‚Äúdefensive measure against ‚Äčany action or attack that Hezbollah might launch against Syria."

item.title: Trump says US will cut all trade with Spain over military bases, defence spending
item.description:
WASHINGTON/MADRID, March 3 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday the United States would cut off all trade with Spain ‚Äčafter the European country refused to let the U.S. military use its bases for missions linked to strikes on Iran.
"Spain ‚ÄĆhas been terrible," Trump told reporters during a meeting with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, adding that he had told Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to "cut off all dealings" with Spain.
"We're going to cut off all trade with Spain. We don't want anything to do with Spain," he added.
The U.S. relocated 15 aircraft, including refuelling tankers, from the Rota and ‚ÄčMoron military bases in southern Spain after the country's Socialist leadership said it would not allow them to be used to attack Iran .
Trump ‚Äčagain referenced Spain's refusal to heed U.S. calls for all NATO members to spend 5% of their GDP on defence, and ‚Ā†added: "Spain has absolutely nothing that we need.
"All business having to do with Spain, I have the right to stop it. Embargoes - do anything I ‚Äčwant with it - and we may do that with Spain," he said.
Bessent, speaking alongside Trump, said he would instruct the United States Trade Representative ‚Äčand the Department of Commerce to begin investigations into how to penalise Spain.
Although the Supreme Court last month struck down Trump's ability to use the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose broad global tariffs, the president said the decision "totally reaffirmed" his ability to impose a full trade embargo under the law.
IEEPA, passed in 1977, has been heavily ‚Äčused for sanctions to cut Iranian, Russian and North Korean entities out of the U.S. dollar-based financial system and to impose licensing requirements to ‚Äčcontrol or halt exports of sensitive technologies to certain countries.
"The Supreme Court reaffirmed your ability to implement an embargo," Bessent told Trump.
The Spanish government responded in ‚ÄĆa statement ‚Ā†that the U.S. must be mindful of the autonomy of private businesses, international law and bilateral trade agreements between the U.S. and the European Union.
Madrid said it had the necessary resources to contain the potential impact of a trade embargo and support affected sectors, but said it would continue to push for free trade and economic cooperation with its partners.
Spain is the world's top exporter of olive oil and also sells auto parts, steel and ‚Äčchemicals to the United States, but ‚Äčis less vulnerable to Trump's threats ‚Ā†of economic punishment than other European nations.
The U.S. had a trade surplus with Spain for the fourth year in a row in 2025, at $4.8 billion, according to U.S. Census Bureau Data, with U.S. exports of $26.1 billion ‚Äčand imports of $21.3 billion.
Germany's Merz said pressure was being brought to bear on Spain from within Europe on defence ‚Äčspending.
"We are trying to ‚Ā†convince Spain to catch up with the 3% or 3.5% which we agreed on in NATO," he said.
"And as the president said, it's correct: Spain is the only one who is not willing to accept that and we are trying to convince them that this is part of our common security that ‚Ā†we all ‚Äčhave to comply with these numbers."
Spain's Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez, one of a dwindling ‚Äčnumber of left-leaning voices in Europe, has risked Trump's ire with a series of other policy moves, including refusing to let vessels transporting weapons to Israel dock in Spain.

item.title: Lebanon to delay May elections due to conflict with Israel, officials say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 3 (Reuters) - Lebanon's leaders have agreed on ‚ÄĆa plan to postpone parliamentary elections scheduled for May and to extend parliament's term by two years after the resumption of conflict ‚Äčbetween Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah this week and growing ‚Äčwar in the Middle East, two senior Lebanese ‚Ā†officials said.
The sources said Lebanon's president, prime minister and parliament ‚Äčspeaker had agreed on the move on Tuesday. It would ‚Äčstill require the approval of a majority of Lebanon's 128-member legislature.
Lebanon, which last held parliamentary polls in 2022, has been pulled into the with Hezbollah on Tuesday launching ‚Äčmissiles at Israel for a second consecutive day and Israel sending troops ‚ÄĆinto ‚Ā†the south and carrying out waves of airstrikes.
The theatre for numerous conflicts between Israel and Hezbollah, Lebanon was drawn into the spillover from the war between the United States, Israel ‚Äčand Iran on Monday, ‚Äčwhen Hezbollah ‚Ā†opened fire with drones and missiles.
With dozens of people killed in retaliatory Israeli strikes, Hezbollah's ‚Äčmove to enter the conflict has sharpened long-standing ‚Äčdivisions ‚Ā†in Lebanon over its status as an armed group - the only Lebanese faction to keep its weapons after the 1975-90 civil ‚Ā†war.
"It ‚Äčdoes not appear that conditions will ‚Äčbe conducive to holding elections for some time," one of the officials said.

item.title: Russian-flagged sanctioned LNG tanker on fire in the Mediterranean, crew fate unknown ‚Äď sources
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - The Russian flagged liquefied natural gas tanker Arctic Metagaz ‚Äčis on fire in the ‚ÄĆMediterranean with the fate of its crew unknown, shipping and maritime security ‚Äčsources said on Tuesday.
The ‚Äčvessel, which is under U.S. and ‚Ā†UK sanctions, last reported its ‚Äčposition as sailing off the ‚Äčcoast of Malta on Monday, according to ship tracking data on the MarineTraffic ‚Äčplatform.
The vessel may have been ‚Äčattacked by a naval drone with Ukraine ‚ÄĆsuspected ‚Ā†of carrying out the operation, one of the sources said without providing evidence.
The vessel‚Äôs Russia-based manager ‚ÄčLLC SMP ‚ÄčTechmanagement, Russian ‚Ā†LNG producer Novatek and Russia‚Äôs transport ministry didn‚Äôt ‚Äčimmediately respond to requests ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†comment.
The Security Service of Ukraine separately did not immediately respond to ‚Ā†a ‚Äčrequest for comment.

item.title: Private credit pioneers see downside of redemption
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Redemption has become a dirty word for private credit's most sophisticated pioneers. Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab on Monday said it had reached into its own pocket to help its $82 billion loan fund meet $3.7 billion of cash ‚Äčcalls from investors. It‚Äôs a sign of how the practice of packaging hard-to-trade assets into more accessible investment vehicles has turned from ‚ÄĆa driver of future growth to a drag on sentiment.
Blackstone‚Äôs BCRED is a non-traded business development company, part of a group of funds which have mushroomed to over $300 billion of assets by loading up on bilateral private credit loans. The sector appealed to wealthy individuals by offering them access to higher-yielding assets, paired with the ability to withdraw their money ‚Äčevery quarter.
BCRED's latest announcement suggests those individuals are having second thoughts. Rival manager Blue Owl recently suspended withdrawals from a much smaller fund. Blackstone's ‚Äčvehicle on Monday revealed , opens new tab that it had received redemptions equivalent to 7.9% of its shares for the first quarter, up ‚Ā†from 4.5% in the final three months of 2025.
The fund honored those requests, but only because Blackstone employees and the asset manager together ponied up $400 million. ‚ÄčThis enabled the fund to stay within the rules set by its share tender process, which currently caps redemptions at 7%. Even though it drew in some $2 ‚Äčbillion of new money in the period, net outflows were still roughly $1.7 billion, before counting Blackstone‚Äôs contributions.
The question for BCRED and its peers is what happens if investors keep pulling out at the same pace. Even though BCRED has delivered a near-10% annual return since its launch in 2021, further redemptions are all too plausible. Private lenders' exposure to the ‚Äčsoftware industry, which is under threat from artificial intelligence, is a major concern. UBS analysts estimate that private credit portfolios have up to a quarter ‚Äčof their assets in software loans.
On paper, BCRED still has some wiggle room. It had $8 billion , opens new tab of liquidity at the end of 2025, suggesting it could absorb another three quarters of ‚ÄĆsimilar net ‚Ā†outflows before having to sell assets. It may also be able to increase borrowing. Meanwhile, maturing loans will bring in more cash. If BCRED's credits repay about the same in 2026 as last year, and the fund uses half of the proceeds to redeem its debt, it will have nearly $6 billion more in reserve.
Still, volatile markets could see loan repayments slow, dragging down the value of BCRED‚Äôs assets. And the closer BDC funds get to exhausting their cash buffers, ‚Äčthe more nervous investors will become.
If necessary, ‚ÄčBCRED could limit quarterly redemptions to ‚Ā†5% of net assets, or less. That would buy it time to raise cash by selling assets. But such a move would spook the private individuals who have piled into BDCs in recent years, and scare away retail investors ‚Äčthat alternative asset managers hope will be a source of future funds. The debt markets' most vibrant growth market ‚Äčcould turn into ‚Ā†a drag.
Follow @Unmack1 , opens new tab on X.

item.title: France sending aircraft carrier to Mediterranean, Macron says
item.description:
PARIS, March 3 (Reuters) - President Emmanuel Macron on Tuesday said France was sending its ‚Äčaircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle to the Mediterranean and ‚ÄĆworking to build a coalition that would help secure maritime traffic imperiled by the escalating crisis in the Middle East.
In a televised ‚Äčaddress to the nation, Macron said that action needed ‚Äčto be taken with the Straits of Hormuz closed ‚Ā†and the Suez Canal and Red Sea shipping routes threatened ‚Äčby the widening conflict.
"We have economic interests to protect, because ‚Äčoil prices, gas prices and the international trade situation are being profoundly disrupted by this war.," Macron said.
He said France had also sent a ‚Äčfrigate to Cyprus and had shot down drones in the skies ‚Äčabove its Gulf allies.
Rafale jets were among the assets being used .
"We have defense ‚ÄĆagreements ‚Ā†that bind us to Qatar, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The latter are particularly targeted, and we owe them solidarity," Macron said.
He said France was helping its citizens who wanted ‚Äčto leave the ‚Äčregion get ‚Ā†out and that two flights were due to arrive in Paris on Tuesday evening. Security had ‚Äčalso been reinforced at some sites in France, ‚Äčhe ‚Ā†added.
Macron said Iran itself "bears primary responsibility" for the U.S.-Israeli action, but added: "the United States of America and Israel have decided to ‚Ā†launch military ‚Äčoperations; they were conducted outside international ‚Äčlaw, which we cannot approve."

item.title: Authorities put down limited fire in vicinity of US consulate in Dubai after drone strike, media office says
item.description: March 3 (Reuters) - Authorities have ‚Äčput down ‚ÄĆa limited fire ‚Äčin ‚Äčthe vicinity of ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčU.S. consulate ‚Äčin Dubai due to ‚Äča ‚Äčdrone strike and ‚ÄĆno ‚Ā†injuries were reported, Dubai's ‚Äčmedia ‚Äčoffice ‚Ā†said on ‚ÄčTuesday.

item.title: Exclusive: US turns up heat on Venezuela with threat to indict new leader Delcy Rodriguez
item.description:
CARACAS/WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - The Trump administration is quietly building a legal case against Venezuelan interim president Delcy Rodriguez including readying a draft criminal indictment, one of several tools it is using to strengthen its leverage with Caracas, according to four people familiar with the matter.
Federal prosecutors have put together possible corruption and money laundering charges, and ‚Äčhave communicated to Rodriguez that she is at risk of prosecution unless she continues to comply with Trump‚Äôs demands following the U.S. ouster of former Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro in January, the sources said.
Reuters has not seen ‚ÄĆthe charges in written form being prepared against Rodriguez but spoke with four people briefed on the matter. The news agency is the first to report the effort to craft the draft indictment of Rodriguez for alleged money laundering and corruption, which the sources said Rodriguez had been made aware of verbally.
The U.S. Attorney's Office in Miami is preparing the draft charges, the people said, adding that the document has been evolving over the past two months. The probe focuses on Rodriguez‚Äôs alleged involvement in laundering of funds from Venezuela‚Äôs state oil company PDVSA, three of the sources said, and covers activities between 2021 and 2025, two ‚Äčof the sources said.
The Department of Justice declined to comment on the story. After a summary of the report was published on the Reuters World News morning podcast, Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche wrote on X, ‚Äúcompletely FALSE from @reuters. Not sure ‚Äčhow such fake news makes its way to publication.‚ÄĚ
In a statement, Reuters said: ‚ÄúWe stand by our reporting that the Department of Justice is preparing an indictment against Delcy Rodriguez, the new president of Venezuela.‚ÄĚ
The ‚Ā†White House and the U.S. State Department did not respond to Reuters‚Äô questions for this story.
Separate from the draft indictment, U.S. officials have presented Rodriguez with a list of at least seven former high-level party officials, associates and their family members that it wants her ‚Äčto arrest or to keep in Venezuela‚Äôs custody for potential extradition, four sources said. This was first reported by Spain‚Äôs ABC newspaper.
Rodriguez is facing this threat just two months after taking power following a lightning raid by U.S. special forces who captured Maduro and whisked the ‚Äčlongtime authoritarian leader away to New York for trial on charges of narcoterrorism and cocaine trafficking. Maduro pleaded not guilty and is being held in New York pending trial.
In public, Trump has heaped praise on Rodriguez for cooperating with the U.S. and hailed Venezuela as ‚Äúour new friend and partner‚ÄĚ in his annual State of the Union address.
But the draft indictment is yet another bargaining chip the United States has added as it attempts to compel members of the Venezuelan government, once loyal to Maduro, to carry out its wishes.
The Venezuelan communications ministry, which handles all press queries for the government, did not respond to a ‚Äčdetailed list of questions about the potential charges being crafted against Rodriguez.
The drafting of an indictment does not necessarily indicate a case will be presented by a prosecutor to a grand jury, which would then have to find there is probable cause to believe Rodriguez ‚Äčcommitted a crime. Grand juries meet in secret, and Reuters was unable to determine whether prosecutors had begun presenting any evidence against Rodriguez to a grand jury.
Since the capture of Maduro and his wife, Cilia Flores, on Jan. 3, Trump has relied on Rodriguez, Maduro‚Äôs 56-year-old former vice president, to ‚ÄĆguarantee stability in ‚Ā†Venezuela while he prioritizes U.S. companies‚Äô access to the OPEC nation‚Äôs oil reserves.
Flores has also pleaded not guilty to federal charges including drug trafficking.
Other members of Rodriguez‚Äôs administration who already have been indicted in the United States include her hard-line Interior Minister Diosdado Cabello, and Defense Minister Vladimir Padrino, who are, along with Rodriguez, longtime members of the United Socialist Party of Venezuela, or PSUV, founded by the late Hugo Ch√°vez. Cabello and Padrino, both still in power in post-Maduro Venezuela, have consistently denied wrongdoing.
Many of the individuals whom the U.S. wants Rodriguez to arrest or keep in detention already have indictments in the United States for money laundering, drug trafficking and other crimes, according to four sources familiar with the matter.
Laura Dogu, the newly appointed U.S. envoy to Venezuela, put the request to Rodriguez, the sources told Reuters.
Among the ‚Äčhigh-profile names given to Rodriguez is Alex Saab, 54, who rose ‚Äčto prominence as a close Maduro ally to become one ‚Ā†of the most influential financial operators within the Chavista movement, according to four sources.
Following an Interpol notice requested by the United States, Saab was previously arrested by local authorities in Cabo Verde in 2020 while traveling to Iran on an official mission for the Maduro government. Extradited to the United States, he faced bribery and money laundering charges for allegedly routing $350 million from a corrupt Venezuelan housing construction scheme ‚Äčthrough the U.S. financial system, before he was released in 2023 by the Biden administration in a prisoner swap.
Reuters reported Saab was arrested in early February and two sources said he remains ‚Äčdetained by Venezuelan intelligence service SEBIN. ‚Ā†The U.S. has a new sealed money-laundering indictment against Saab, two of the sources said, but its status is not immediately known.
If extradited, Saab could provide information to U.S. authorities that could bolster their criminal case against Maduro, according to a source briefed on the case.
It is unclear whether Saab, a Colombian who was granted Venezuelan citizenship by Maduro, will be extradited to the United States. Venezuelan law prohibits the extradition of Venezuelan nationals, a barrier for several of the people Washington is seeking.
Saab‚Äôs U.S.-based lawyer Neil M. Schuster did not respond to detailed questions about whether Saab ‚Ā†has been detained, ‚Äčany charges he may be facing or whether he had offered any testimony about Maduro during his previous U.S. detention.
Other names on the list include the media ‚Äčmogul Raul Gorrin, who has also been detained by SEBIN in Venezuela within the past month, according to three sources familiar with the matter.
Gorrin faces multiple federal charges in the U.S., primarily tied to bribery, money laundering, and corruption involving Venezuela‚Äôs state oil company, PDVSA.
Gorrin did not respond to emails and a text message seeking comment, nor ‚Äčdid his media business Globovisi√≥n.
Howard Srebnick, a Miami attorney who has previously represented Gorrin, did not immediately return a request for comment.

item.title: Trump's national security team comes to convince Congress to back Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump's top national security advisers were to spend much of the day on Tuesday making the case to members of Congress ‚Äčfor the U.S.-Israel war on Iran, as Democrats and some of his fellow Republicans clamored for more information.
Secretary of ‚ÄĆState Marco Rubio, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, CIA Director John Ratcliffe, and General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joints Chiefs of Staff, were to hold briefings first for the entire 100-member Senate and later the 432-member House of Representatives.
Trump's Republicans control slim majorities in both the Senate and House and have been ‚Äčstrongly supportive of his policy initiatives, as is typical when the White House and Congress are controlled by the same ‚Äčparty.
But the Middle East war has prompted a few members of his party to join Democrats in saying ‚Ā†the president should not send troops to fight abroad without first obtaining Congress' approval.
Others said they were looking forward to getting more ‚Äčinformation, especially if the White House comes to Congress to request additional funding for the war.
"America First was supposed to be a rejection ‚Äčof the globalist war machine," Republican Representative Warren Davidson of Ohio said in a post on X.com. "I look forward to seeing the intelligence the administration found so persuasive, then voting."
And there was concern when Rubio told reporters on Monday that the U.S. had attacked Iran because Israel planned to do so. "Have we now delegated ‚Äčthe most solemn decision that can be made in our society, the decision to go to war, to another country," Senator Angus King ‚Äčof Maine, an independent who caucuses with Democrats, asked at an Armed Services committee hearing on Tuesday.
The Senate's Republican majority leader, John ‚ÄĆThune of ‚Ā†South Dakota, and Representative Mike Johnson of Louisiana, the Republican Speaker of the House, have both said it is too early to know whether Congress will need to consider a supplemental funding bill.
Johnson told reporters on Monday evening, after a separate briefing for congressional leaders, that the issue had come up during that meeting. "There are more details to be determined, how long the operation goes and what the need is," ‚Äčhe said.
Such a resolution would face ‚Äčstiff opposition from Democrats. New ‚Ā†York Representative Hakeem Jeffries, who leads Democrats in the House, told a news conference hours before the administration's briefing that Trump should get Congress' consent for the war before asking lawmakers to fund it.
The ‚ÄčSenate is expected to vote on Wednesday and the House on Thursday on war powers resolutions seeking ‚Äčto block Trump from ‚Ā†continuing to attack Iran without congressional authorization.
Jeffries said he expected strong Democratic support for the measure.
"There is a requirement under the Constitution that it is members of Congress that make the decision as to whether to get us entangled in this kind of armed conflict and that's what ‚Ā†the resolution ‚Äčthis week will be all about," Jeffries said.
Congressional Republicans have blocked previous attempts to ‚Äčpass resolutions forcing Trump to obtain lawmakers' approval for military action. Even if a resolution were to pass this time, it is not expected to get the two-thirds majorities ‚Äčin both the House and Senate needed to override Trump's veto.

item.title: How many people have been killed in the US-Israel war on Iran?
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Scores of people have been killed across the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, with ‚ÄčGulf states that host U.S. military bases and personnel and ‚ÄĆLebanon quickly drawn into the conflict.
Here are the death tolls from the war so far as reported by involved countries as of March 3, the ‚Äčfourth day of the war. Reuters has not independently verified ‚Äčthese deaths.

item.title: Merz says he urged Trump to increase pressure on Putin
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich ‚ÄčMerz, speaking after a meeting ‚ÄĆwith Donald Trump on Tuesday, said he had urged the president ‚Äčto put pressure on ‚ÄčRussian President Vladimir Putin over the ‚Ā†war in Ukraine.
"Russia is ‚Äčplaying for time here, and in ‚Äčdoing so is also acting against the will of the American president. ‚ÄčIn today's talks, I called ‚Äčfor increasing the pressure on Moscow," he ‚ÄĆtold ‚Ā†reporters.
The United States, Russia and Ukraine are taking part in trilateral talks aimed at securing a ‚Äčpeace ‚Äčdeal. Merz ‚Ā†though said only a pact supported by Europe ‚Äčcould be lasting.
"We are ‚Äčnot ‚Ā†prepared to accept an agreement that is negotiated over our ‚Ā†heads," ‚Äčhe said.

item.title: North Korea to hold election for Supreme People's Assembly following party Congress
item.description: SEOUL, March 4 (Reuters) - North Korea ‚Äčwill hold ‚ÄĆan election on March ‚Äč15 ‚Äčto pick delegates for ‚Ā†its ‚ÄčSupreme People's ‚ÄčAssembly, KCNA state news agency ‚Äčsaid on ‚ÄčWednesday, likely in ‚ÄĆpreparation ‚Ā†for a new session of ‚Äčits ‚Äčparliament ‚Ā†to legislate policies ‚Äčadopted at ‚Äčlast ‚Ā†month's ruling party ‚Ā†congress.

item.title: TRADING DAY Selling snowballs, turmoil spreads
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 3 (Reuters) - The equity rout sparked by the deepening Middle East crisis spread on Tuesday as Wall Street finally buckled, with soaring energy prices and widening cracks in most other asset markets forcing investors and policymakers to rethink their outlook .
More on that below. In my column today I look at how the current turmoil has ‚Äčexposed the frailties of the traditional "60-40" investment portfolio . With stocks and bonds both selling off, where is the protection from diversifying?
If you have more ‚ÄĆtime to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* What goes up, must come down
Few corners of world markets are escaping the selloff now gathering force. Pockets that might have been insulated by solid underlying fundamentals or seen as reasonable diversification options are being hammered just as hard as anything else.
South Korean ‚Äčequities, gold and silver were among the biggest losers on Tuesday. It's no coincidence that they were among the biggest gainers recently - gold and silver at the ‚Äčend of last year, and the KOSPI up 50% in the first two months of this year. In the scramble for liquidity, assets that rose most on waves of frenzied speculation ‚ÄĆhave most ‚Ā†room to fall.
* Kevin help us now
Two quarter-point rate cuts from the Fed this year are no longer fully priced into the 2026 U.S. rate futures strip. Right now, investors are viewing the negative energy supply shock and surging prices as an inflation threat rather than a growth risk, and are pricing the Fed accordingly.
With inflation already comfortably above the Fed's target, it could not be a more challenging environment for Kevin Warsh, President Donald Trump's Fed chair nominee, who is expected to succeed Jerome ‚ÄčPowell in May. Might the Fed's first ‚Äčmove under Warsh's leadership be a ‚Ā†rate hike?
* Private credit still a problem
While events in the Middle East have a chokehold on global asset prices, the problems brewing under the hood of the private credit market haven't gone away. If anything, the surge in redemptions from Blackstone's flagship private ‚Äčcredit fund shows they are intensifying.
Shares in Blackstone tumbled 5% on Tuesday, and shares in rivals KKR and Apollo ‚Äčalso fell. They've all ‚Ā†lost around 30% this year, and are down 45-50% from their all-time highs. The geopolitical turmoil has accelerated the scramble for cash and liquidity, snowballing the selling.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect ‚Äčthe views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Defense executives plan to meet at White House as strikes on Iran diminish stockpiles
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - The Trump administration plans ‚Äčto meet with executives from the biggest U.S. defense contractors at the White House on Friday to discuss ‚ÄĆaccelerating weapons production, as the Pentagon works to replenish supplies after strikes on Iran and several other recent military efforts, five people familiar with the plan told Reuters.
Companies including Lockheed Martin (LMT.N) , opens new tab and Raytheon parent RTX (RTX.N) , opens new tab , along with other key suppliers, have been invited to ‚Äčattend the meeting, the people said, speaking on condition of anonymity because the discussions are private.
The meeting underscores ‚Äčthe urgency felt in Washington to shore up weapons stocks after the Iran operation ‚Ā†drew heavily on munitions. Since Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022 and Israel began military operations in Gaza, the ‚ÄčU.S. has drawn down billions of dollars' worth of weapons stockpiles, including artillery systems, ammunition and anti-tank missiles. The conflict in ‚ÄčIran has consumed longer-range missiles than those furnished to Kyiv.
At least one of the people said the gathering was expected to center on pressing weapons makers to move faster to boost output.
Lockheed, the Pentagon and White House did not immediately respond to requests for ‚Äčcomment. RTX declined to comment. In a social media post Monday, Trump said there was a "virtually unlimited ‚Äčsupply" of U.S. munitions and that "wars can be fought "forever," and very successfully, using just these supplies."
The White House meeting comes as Deputy ‚ÄĆDefense ‚Ā†Secretary Steve Feinberg has been leading Pentagon work in recent days on a supplemental budget request of around $50 billion that could be released as soon as Friday, one of the people said. The new money would pay for replacing the weapons used in recent conflicts including those in the Middle East. The figure is preliminary and could change.
The push ‚Äčto boost production has intensified ‚Äčfollowing U.S. military strikes ‚Ā†on Iran, where the U.S. deployed Tomahawk cruise missiles , F-35 stealth fighters and low-cost one-way attack drones on Saturday.
Tomahawk missile maker Raytheon has a new agreement with the Pentagon to ‚Äčeventually ramp production to 1,000 units annually. The Pentagon currently plans to buy 57 of ‚Äčthe missiles in ‚Ā†2026 at an average cost of $1.3 million each.
The administration has been steadily ratcheting up pressure on defense contractors to prioritize production over shareholder payouts. President Donald Trump signed an executive order in January to identify contractors deemed to be underperforming on ‚Ā†contracts while ‚Äčdistributing profits to shareholders.
The Pentagon is expected to release a list ‚Äčof underperforming contractors. Companies named will have 15 days to submit board-approved plans to correct the situation. If those plans are judged insufficient, the Pentagon ‚Äčcan pursue enforcement actions, including contract terminations.

item.title: Pentagon identifies first US soldiers killed in Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. military on Tuesday identified four of the first American soldiers ‚ÄĆkilled in the war against Iran as the Trump administration warned the intensifying conflict would lead to more American casualties.
Among six U.S. military deaths so far, the four soldiers were members of an Iowa unit of the U.S. Army Reserve. ‚ÄčThey died on Sunday when a drone slammed into a U.S. military facility in Port ‚ÄčShuaiba, Kuwait, the U.S. military said on Tuesday.
The Pentagon said four ranged in ‚Ā†age from 20 to 42 and served in the 103rd Sustainment Command from Des Moines, Iowa, part ‚Äčof the army's global logistics and supply operation.
The military identified the four U.S. Army Reserve soldiers, as:
* ‚ÄčCaptain Cody A. Khork, 35, of Winter Haven, Florida.
* Sargeant 1st Class Noah L. Tietjens, 42, of Bellevue, Nebraska
* Sargeant 1st Class Nicole M. Amor, 39, of White Bear Lake, Minnesota
* Sargeant Declan J. Coady, 20, of West Des Moines, Iowa
Major General Todd ‚ÄčErskine, who leads the 79th Theater Sustainment Command, in a statement extended "my deepest sympathy and my ‚Äčrespect" to relatives and unit members of the four.
President Donald Trump and other senior officials have warned the Iran conflict ‚ÄĆwill result ‚Ā†in more U.S. military deaths as Tehran retaliates against U.S. and Israeli strikes.
The U.S. military's Central Command said on Tuesday that Iran has launched over 500 ballistic missiles and over 2,000 drones in its retaliatory attacks throughout the Middle East so far.
The risks to U.S. forces in the Middle East came ‚Äčup during a closed-door briefing ‚Äčto lawmakers on Tuesday ‚Ā†by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, CIA Director John Ratcliffe and Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
"They ‚Äčtold us in that room that there are going to be more Americans ‚Äčthat are ‚Ā†gonna die -- that they are not ... going to be able to stop these drones," Senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat, said.
The facility in Kuwait where the four deaths occurred was protected by concrete blast walls but did ‚Ā†not ‚Äčhave a fortified roof, two officials told Reuters.
It was unclear ‚Äčif there were air defenses in place but no alarm apparently sounded as the drone approached, one of the officials added, speaking ‚Äčon condition of anonymity.

item.title: Nepal to vote in first election since Gen Z-led protests toppled government
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 4 (Reuters) - Nepal is heading to a general ‚Äčelection on Thursday, the first after youth-led protests last September demanding an end to corruption, ‚ÄĆmore jobs and cleaner politics led to the deaths of 77 people and forced the government to resign.
For decades, the small Himalayan nation ensconced between China and India has been riven by political instability , with 32 changes in government since 1990, leaving its largely ‚Äčagrarian economy hamstrung and forcing millions to seek work abroad.
Nearly 19 million of Nepal's 30 million ‚Äčpeople are eligible to vote to pick a 275-member legislature, of which 165 candidates ‚Ā†are directly elected and 110 selected via proportional representation.
About one million of these voters - most of them youth - ‚Äčwere added after last year's protests , which has amplified calls for overhauling Nepal's political system and reforming the economy ‚Äčto create formal jobs with better wages.
Bibas Pariyar, a 22-year-old painter employed in Kathmandu, said he plans to return to his home district of Gorkha - famed for soldiers who have served in the British and Indian militaries - on Thursday to vote.
"We need new ‚Äčpeople who can give work to people, reform agriculture and pay adequate remuneration for workers," Pariyar said.
"The old ‚Äčpoliticians only amassed money for themselves through corruption and did nothing for the people."
In the race are ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†old guard, including the centrist Nepali Congress (NC) and the Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist or UML), which have dominated national politics for decades.
But most analysts say the centrist Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) is at the forefront. Rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah , 35, joined the three-year-old party in January as its prime ministerial candidate.
A former mayor of the capital city ‚ÄčKathmandu who emerged as the ‚Äčface of September protests, ‚Ā†Shah is going head-to-head against the UML's K.P. Sharma Oli, 74, a four-time premier who quit following the September killings of the demonstrators.
Nepal's election will be the second in ‚Äčthe region - following Bangladesh - to be triggered by Gen Z-led protests, but the ‚Äčdynamics are markedly ‚Ā†different, said Jay Nishaant, founder of the Nepal Democracy Foundation think tank.
"For any election, three things decide the outcome: agenda, leadership and organisation," Nishaant said.
"That's where Nepal may diverge from Bangladesh. Bangladesh's July 2024 student leaders had a ‚Ā†clear agenda ‚Äčand recognisable faces, but not a time-tested grassroots machine."
In Bangladesh's ‚ÄčFebruary general election, the main youth-driven party won only six seats in the 300-member parliament, underlining the challenge of turning street momentum into ‚Äčvotes.

item.title: Cuba charges six exiles with terrorism in speedboat attack
item.description:
HAVANA, March 3 (Reuters) - Cuban prosecutors formally charged six people with "crimes of terrorism" and ordered them held in pretrial detention on Tuesday in ‚Äčconnection with an incident last week in which Cuban forces killed four ‚ÄĆCuban nationals and wounded six others aboard a speedboat that entered Cuban waters.
Cuba's Interior Ministry announced the gunbattle at sea last Wednesday, accusing the Cuban exiles of opening fire on a border ‚Äčguard vessel, saying they came from the United States with the intent ‚Äčto sow chaos and attack military units on the Communist-ruled island.
Cuban ‚Ā†forces returned fire and took the six survivors into custody, Cuba said.
There was ‚Äčno further update as to the condition of the wounded detainees, nor their whereabouts ‚Äčat a time of heightened tensions between the U.S. and Cuba. Washington has effectively halted all oil shipments to the Communist-ruled island while demanding political and economic changes.
Cuban officials have said ‚Äčthe purported infiltrators came armed with nearly 13,000 rounds of ammunition, 13 rifles and ‚Äč11 pistols, displaying the captured armaments from the studio on a special television program on Friday.
They ‚ÄĆalso ‚Ā†showed pictures of the vessels, each peppered with bullet holes from the firefight they said took place at a range of 20 meters (66 feet).
Cuba has said at least two of those taken into custody had been previously placed on a list of ‚Äčaccused terrorists.
Prosecutor Edward Robert ‚ÄčCampbell told the ‚Ā†state television program on Friday the accused faced a host of possible charges including crimes associated with terrorist acts.
If convicted they could ‚Äčface prison terms of up to 10 to 15 years ‚Äčfor the ‚Ā†lesser offenses and 20 to 30 years - or even the death penalty - for the more serious charges, Campbell said.
U.S. politicians have expressed skepticism over Cuba's version of events, ‚Ā†calling ‚Äčfor independent investigations. Secretary of State Marco Rubio it ‚Äčwas not a U.S. operation and that no U.S. government personnel were involved.

item.title: Greg Abbott wins Texas Republican gubernatorial primary, US media projects
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Governor Greg Abbott won the Texas Republican gubernatorial primary ‚Äčon Tuesday as he seeks ‚ÄĆhis fourth consecutive term in office, U.S. media projected.
Abbott, ‚Äč68, a close ally ‚Äčof President Donald Trump, ‚Ā†has dominated Texas politics ‚Äčsince he was first elected ‚Äčgovernor in 2014. He has pushed an increasingly conservative agenda that ‚Äčhas seen Texas, ‚Äčthe most populous GOP-led state, on ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†vanguard of anti-abortion laws before the Supreme Court knocked down Roe v. Wade, ‚Äčban ‚Äčimmigration "sanctuary cities" ‚Ā†in 2017, create the country's biggest ‚Äčschool voucher program and ‚Äčloosen ‚Ā†gun laws.
Recent polls show Abbott as a strong ‚Ā†favorite ‚Äčto win reelection ‚Äčin November.

item.title: US, Ecuadorian forces took action in Ecuador to combat drug trafficking, US military says
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. military's Southern Command ‚Äčsaid on Tuesday ‚ÄĆthat Ecuadorian and U.S. forces launched ‚Äčoperations against what ‚Äčit said were designated ‚Ā†terrorist organizations ‚Äčin Ecuador.
The Southern Command, ‚Äčwhich is the U.S. military's combatant command ‚Äčthat encompasses 31 ‚Äčcountries through South and ‚ÄĆCentral ‚Ā†America and the Caribbean, said that the action was ‚Äčmeant ‚Äčto ‚Ā†combat illicit drug trafficking, but ‚Äčdid not ‚Äčprovide ‚Ā†more details in a statement on ‚Ā†social ‚Äčmedia website ‚ÄčX.

item.title: Britain to bar study visas for four nations, halt Afghan work visas
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Britain said on Tuesday it would block study visas ‚ÄĆfor nationals from four countries and halt work visas for Afghans, using what it called an "emergency brake" to curb rising asylum claims from people entering through legal routes.
Immigration remains one of Britain's most politically sensitive issues, and Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄčStarmer's government has sought to show it is tightening the system as the populist Reform UK ‚Äčparty gains ground in opinion polls.
The interior ministry, which is set to block ‚Ā†study visas for nationals from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar and Sudan, said asylum applications by students from these ‚Äčcountries had jumped more than fivefold between 2021 and 2025.
It also said claims by Afghans on work ‚Äčvisas were now outstripping the number of visas issued.
"Britain will always provide refuge to people fleeing war and persecution, but our visa system must not be abused," interior minister Shabana Mahmood said in a statement.
"That is why I am taking ‚Äčthe unprecedented decision to refuse visas for those nationals seeking to exploit our generosity."
According to the government, asylum claims made after entering on legal visas have more than trebled since 2021 and ‚ÄĆaccounted for ‚Ā†39% of the 100,000 people who applied last year.
It said that nearly 16,000 nationals from the four listed countries were currently being supported at public expense, including more than 6,000 in hotels, adding to pressure over the cost of asylum accommodation, which it put at 4 billion pounds ($5.34 billion) a year.
The changes ‚Äčwould take effect on ‚ÄčMarch 26, the government ‚Ā†said, adding that it intended to create new capped "safe and legal routes" once the asylum system stabilises.
Britain has granted sanctuary to more than 37,000 Afghans through ‚Äčresettlement schemes since 2021 and issued about 190,000 humanitarian visas last year.
It ‚Äčsaid it had ‚Ā†secured cooperation from Angola, Namibia and the Democratic Republic of Congo on returns, after warning in November that their nationals risked losing access to UK visas.
Starmer has previously said that Britain's asylum rules were more permissive compared ‚Ā†with parts ‚Äčof Europe and acted as a "pull factor" for people seeking ‚Äčto reach the country.
His government announced plans in November to make refugee status temporary and speed up removals of people who arrive ‚Äčillegally.
($1 = 0.7487 pounds)

item.title: A Kuwaiti F/A-18 fighter jet caused the accidental downing of three US F-15s, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - A Kuwaiti F/A-18 ‚Äčfighter jet ‚ÄĆwas the cause ‚Äčof the ‚Äčaccidental downing of ‚Ā†three ‚ÄčU.S. F-15s on ‚ÄčSunday, the Wall Street Journal ‚Äčreported ‚Äčon Tuesday, citing ‚ÄĆthree ‚Ā†people familiar with initial reports ‚Äčof ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†incident.
Reuters could not ‚Äčimmediately ‚Äčverify ‚Ā†the report.

item.title: UK Maritime Trade Operations receives a report of an incident east of UAE's Fujairah
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The United Kingdom Maritime ‚ÄčTrade Operations said ‚ÄĆit received a report of an ‚Äčincident 7 ‚Äčnautical miles east of ‚Ā†Fujairah in ‚Äčthe United Arab ‚ÄčEmirates.
The master of a vessel reported that ‚Äčit was ‚Äčstruck by an unknown ‚ÄĆprojectile ‚Ā†causing damage to the steel plating, UKMTO said. ‚ÄčThe ‚Äčcrew ‚Ā†was reported safe and ‚Äčwith no ‚Äčfire ‚Ā†on the vessel or water ‚Ā†intake.

item.title: CIA station in Saudi Arabia struck by suspected Iranian drone, source says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - The Central Intelligence Agency's station at the U.S. Embassy in ‚ÄčSaudi Arabia was hit on Monday by ‚ÄĆa suspected Iranian drone, a source familiar with the matter told Reuters on Tuesday.
There is no ‚Äčindication the station was the target, ‚Äčthe source said.
The CIA declined to comment.
The ‚Ā†attack came amid ongoing strikes in the Middle ‚ÄčEast following Saturday's U.S. and Israeli attacks on ‚ÄčIran.
The embassy, which is located in the Saudi capital of Riyadh, is among the U.S. sites that ‚Äčhave been hit so far in the ‚Äčconflict. Saudi Arabia's defense ministry said the embassy was ‚ÄĆhit ‚Ā†by two drones, which resulted in a limited fire and some material damage.
Washington's mission in the kingdom subsequently warned Americans to avoid ‚Äčthe embassy ‚Äčuntil further ‚Ā†notice "due to an attack" on the facility.
On Tuesday, it canceled routine ‚Äčand emergency U.S. citizen services appointments.
"There ‚Äčis ‚Ā†a threat of imminent missile and UAV attacks over Dhahran. Do not come to the ‚Ā†U.S. ‚ÄčConsulate," the mission said ‚Äčin a security alert.

item.title: Trump administration debates allowing Tencent to keep its gaming stakes, FT reports
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The White House is debating ‚ÄĆwhether to allow Tencent (0700.HK) , opens new tab to keep its stakes in major video game groups as U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump prepares to meet ‚ÄčChinese President Xi Jinping in China in ‚Ā†April, the Financial Times reported on ‚ÄčTuesday.
Top officials have held internal meetings to assess ‚Äčwhether Tencent's investments in U.S. and Finnish gaming firms pose a national security risk, the newspaper said, citing several ‚Äčpeople familiar with the deliberations.
A meeting ‚Äčamong several cabinet officials scheduled for Tuesday to review ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†issue was postponed due to scheduling issues, FT said.
Reuters could not immediately verify the report. The White House and Tencent also did not immediately ‚Äčrespond to ‚ÄčReuters' request ‚Ā†for comment.
Hong Kong-listed Tencent holds a stake in U.S. video game developer ‚ÄčEpic Games, the creator of "Fortnite", and ‚Äčowns ‚Ā†Los Angeles-based Riot Games, the developer of "League of Legends."
In 2016, it bought a majority ‚Ā†stake ‚Äčin Supercell, the Finnish mobile-game ‚Äčmaker behind "Clash of Clans", for about $8.6 billion.

item.title: U.S., Kurds discuss potential Iran military operation, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United States in recent days about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the ‚Äčwestern part of the country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based on the ‚ÄĆIran-Iraq border in the semiautonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, as the United States and Israel pound Iranian targets with bombs and missiles.
The goal would be to create room for Iranians opposed to the Islamic regime to rise up now that Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and other top officials ‚Äčhave been killed since the U.S.-Israeli attack began on Saturday, two of the sources said.
A final decision has not yet been made on the ‚Äčoperation and its possible timing, added the sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity to talk freely about sensitive military ‚Ā†planning.
The groups have requested U.S. military support and Iraqi leaders in Erbil and Baghdad have also been in touch with the Trump administration in recent days, ‚Äčthey said.
The forces are in talks with the United States about CIA help to provide weapons, two of the sources said.
CNN was the first to report on ‚Äčthe CIA's involvement with the groups and the potential ground operation. Axios said this week that President Trump held a telephone call with two of Iraqi Kurdistan‚Äôs top leaders.
Reuters could not independently confirm the extent of the CIA‚Äôs involvement in the planning of the operation, if it had facilitated the weapons or if any U.S. forces planned to go into Iran ‚Äčwith the Kurdish groups.
The CIA declined to comment. The White House and the Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Kurdish Regional Government ‚Äčdid not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Any operation from Iraq would probably require significant U.S. military and intelligence support.
The Pentagon says the two ‚ÄĆU.S. bases ‚Ā†in Erbil have supported the international coalition fighting Islamic State militants.
Kurdish groups in Iraqi Kurdistan have a long history of working with the U.S. but their shifting allegiances and ideologies have at times strained ties with Washington.
The United States worked with some of the Kurdish groups in Iraq both during the Iraq War and the battle against ISIS.
But it is unclear how successful the Iranian Kurdish groups would be in their fight inside Iran. The groups‚Äô fighters have varying degrees of ‚Äčexperience on the battlefield.
A source cited by ‚ÄčCNN said the plan would be ‚Ā†for Kurdish armed forces to take on Iranian security forces to make it easier for an uprising of unarmed Iranians in the country‚Äôs cities.
It is not clear exactly how such an operation in Iran by the Kurds will be received ‚Äčby other countries in the region.
An armed uprising by the Iranian Kurds could have serious consequences for Iran's stability.
It ‚Äčcould add fuel to ‚Ā†an armed separatist movement among the country's ethnic Baluch minority that keeps close ties with separatists in Pakistan's neighboring restive province of Baluchistan.
It is unlikely that Islamabad would tolerate any move toward Baluch independence.
Turkey, a strong supporter of Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa, has seen the integration deal between Damascus and Kurdish forces as critical to restoring state ‚Ā†authority across ‚ÄčSyria.
It had threatened its own military operation against the Syrian Kurdish Democratic Forces (SDF) in the north, ‚Äčif the group did not agree to come under central government control.
Ankara has been working to advance its long-running efforts towards securing peace with the outlawed Kurdistan Workers Party (PKK) and is unlikely to ‚Äčbe sympathetic towards the arming of Kurdish groups close to its borders.

item.title: At least four killed in Israeli strike on building in Lebanon's Baalbek
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - An Israeli strike on a four-storey residential building has killed at ‚Äčleast four people and wounded six others in the ‚ÄĆeastern Lebanese city of Baalbek with rescue teams working to pull families from beneath the rubble, state news agency NNA said on Wednesday.
The strike was ‚Äčpart of a sharp escalation in fighting along the Lebanese-Israeli border ‚Äčsince Monday after Hezbollah fired drones and missiles at ‚Ā†Israel. Since then, the Iran-aligned group has launched further rockets and ‚ÄčIsrael has carried out waves of air strikes across Lebanon and ‚Äčsent troops into the south.
Shi'ite Muslim Hezbollah, established by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, opened fire on Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme ‚Äčleader in joint strikes by Israel and the United States.
Hezbollah said ‚Äčon Wednesday its fighters fired a salvo of rockets at a gathering of ‚ÄĆIsraeli ‚Ā†forces near the Metula area along the frontier in response to Israeli strikes on dozens of Lebanese towns and cities, including Beirut's southern suburbs.
The Israeli military on Wednesday warned residents of 16 villages ‚Äčin Lebanon to ‚Äčevacuate, saying ‚Ā†Hezbollah activity was forcing it to act against the group and cautioning that anyone near Hezbollah fighters, ‚Äčfacilities or weapons would be putting their lives ‚Äčat risk.
NNA ‚Ā†also reported several Israeli strikes early on Wednesday, including one on a hotel and another on an apartment in eastern Lebanon.
Lebanon's health ministry ‚Ā†said ‚Äčon Tuesday that Israeli strikes had ‚Äčkilled at least 50 people and wounded 335 since the latest escalation began.

item.title: Taiwan indicts 62 over suspected scam centre operator Prince Group
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 4 (Reuters) - Taipei prosecutors on Wednesday indicted 62 people linked to the Prince Group, a multinational network accused of running a vast network of scam centres, ‚Äčincluding the company's chairman who was arrested and deported to China from Cambodia earlier this year.
Chen ‚ÄĆZhi, who founded the conglomerate the U.S. alleges is a front for a multibillion-dollar online fraud and money laundering operation, was pictured by Chinese state media hooded and handcuffed as he was led off ‚Äča plane at a Beijing airport in January.
Taipei prosecutors said in a ‚Äčstatement that Taiwan was one of the locations where Chen had ‚Ā†funnelled illicit funds via shell companies, buying luxury goods, sports cars and real ‚Äčestate.
"This was done to conceal and disguise the source and flow of the criminal ‚Äčproceeds," it said.
The funds remitted into Taiwan from overseas by members of the group for alleged money-laundering purposes totalled around T$10.8 billion ($339.12 million), where they bought 24 properties, 35 vehicles, and held T$55.53 ‚Äčmillion in other assets such as cash, designer bags, and shoes, the statement ‚Äčsaid.
More than T$5.5 billion in assets have been seized in Taiwan, the prosecutors' office added.
Chen's whereabouts ‚ÄĆare ‚Ā†currently unknown and it was not possible to contact him for comment.
In November, the Prince Group denied any wrongdoing in a statement issued via a U.S. law firm.
On Monday, Taiwan put 33 luxury cars, including Ferraris, up for auction after they ‚Äčwere seized as part ‚Äčof the investigation.
"To ‚Ā†conceal and disguise criminal proceeds, they exploited Taiwanese nationals to carry out money-laundering activities in Taiwan through online gambling and ‚Äčunderground remittances," the prosecutors' office said.
"This not only seriously disrupted ‚ÄčTaiwan's financial ‚Ā†order and social stability, but also damaged Taiwan's international image."
Authorities across Asia, including Singapore and Hong Kong , have also seized assets or detained individuals linked to the Prince Group.
The transnational ‚Ā†scam ‚Äčindustry emerged in Southeast Asia during the pandemic. It ‚Äčis believed to generate billions of dollars a year for organised crime using trafficked workers to defraud victims ‚Äčfrom around the world.
($1 = 31.8470 Taiwan dollars)

item.title: Saudi Arabia to take all necessary measures to defend its security, cabinet says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia will take all ‚Äčnecessary measures to ‚ÄĆdefend its security and protect ‚Äčits territory, citizens ‚Äčand residents, the ‚Ā†state news agency ‚Äčreported early on ‚ÄčWednesday, citing a cabinet statement.
An Iranian attack ‚Äčtargeted the ‚ÄčU.S. embassy in Riyadh on ‚ÄĆTuesday ‚Ā†amid ongoing Iranian missile and drone strikes on ‚ÄčGulf ‚Äčstates ‚Ā†that host U.S. bases, ‚Äčfollowing U.S. ‚Äčand ‚Ā†Israeli strikes on Iran on Saturday.

item.title: Hyundai Motor's robots herald hardware reboot
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Robots are hard at work for Hyundai Motor Co (005380.KS) , opens new tab . Although cars generate the profit, its Boston Dynamics unit is more responsible for the sudden rise ‚Äčin market capitalisation. As investors increasingly value technological hardware as much as software, more companies across Asia will ‚ÄĆbe rewarded.
The Hyundai conglomerate's automotive shares have roughly doubled this year, compared to a 40% rise in the domestic benchmark index. Vehicles, including its Tucson SUV, power the bottom line. Hyundai Motor Co is on track for $9 billion of net profit next year, according to Visible Alpha. A basket of international peers ‚Äčtrades at about 7.5 times earnings on average, which would impute roughly $70 billion of equity value.
There's also a stake in ‚Äčsister carmaker Kia (000270.KS) , opens new tab , worth $16 billion, and $17 billion of book value from investments in other related companies, as ‚Ā†of September. Korea Investment analysts appraise the financing division at $15 billion. Add it all up, slap on a 40% conglomerate discount, ‚Äčand the company is worth about $70 billion.
Hyundai Motor Co's market value is now closer to $100 billion, however. Its rally began in January after ‚ÄčBoston Dynamics showcased , opens new tab the Atlas humanoid cyborg at the annual consumer tech expo in Las Vegas and unveiled , opens new tab plans for mass production. Every scrap of related news since has stoked further excitement: the stock leapt 11% on Friday after Seoul said the Hyundai group would invest some $6 billion in an artificial intelligence data centre ‚Äčand robot factory.
It‚Äôs more than just hype. Hyundai is well-positioned to sell autonomous machines like its four-legged Spot , opens new tab , wheeled Mobed , opens new tab and biped Atlas. The ‚Äčgroup's decision to buy an 80% stake in Boston Dynamics from SoftBank in 2021 accelerated a multi-decade push into the technology; Hyundai Motor Co owns a ‚ÄĆroughly 28% ‚Ā†indirect holding. The parent‚Äôs broad portfolio also manufactures everything from ships to space exploration vehicles, enabling it to experiment and gather data for industrial applications. Essential components are also available internally.
China‚Äôs homegrown champions, such as Unitree, present formidable competition, but Hyundai is better positioned to tap the lucrative U.S. market, where higher labour costs make a compelling case for automating production. It's already introducing Atlas to production lines ‚Äčin Georgia.
This dynamic will ripple ‚Äčacross South Korea and Japan, ‚Ā†where building a range of precision machines and components is a speciality largely overlooked while investors favoured coding experts selling subscriptions. Rainbow Robotics and gear-maker Nabtesco are among those picking up steam. It augurs ‚Äčyet more promise for rebooted hardware.
Follow Katrina Hamlin on Bluesky , opens new tab and Linkedin , opens new tab .

item.title: Tanker heads to UAE port via Strait of Hormuz to load oil, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 4 (Reuters) - An oil tanker sailed through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčon Tuesday, heading to a United Arab Emirates ‚ÄĆport to load crude in a rare voyage since the Iran war disrupted shipping in the Middle East, according to ‚Äčindustry sources and shiptracking data.
Suezmax tanker Pola ‚Äčswitched off its AIS tracker late on ‚Ā†March 2, when it approached the Strait, and ‚Äčre-appeared on March 3 off Abu Dhabi, LSEG data ‚Äčshowed.
The vessel is heading to the port of Jebel Dhanna to load Abu Dhabi Murban crude for Thailand, said two ‚Äčtrade sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity ‚Äčas the matter is a sensitive one.
Dynacom Tankers which manages ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†vessel could not be immediately reached for comment outside office hours.
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran halted energy exports from the Middle East, with Tehran attacking ships ‚Äčand energy facilities, ‚Äčclosing navigation ‚Ā†in the Gulf and forcing production stoppages in countries from Qatar to Iraq.
Crude ‚Äčtanker transits through the strait fell ‚Äčto ‚Ā†four vessels on March 1, the day after hostilities broke out, versus an average of 24 a day ‚Ā†since ‚ÄčJanuary, Vortexa vessel-tracking data showed.

item.title: US assault on Iran ahead of schedule, says US Middle East Commander
item.description:
DUBAI/TEL AVIV, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. assault on Iran was ahead of schedule, said the top American commander for the Middle East, as Israel ‚Äčand the U.S. targeted sites deep inside the country , and Iran carried out strikes around the Gulf.
The five-day-old war continued to rattle global markets , as airline and tourism industries ‚ÄĆscrambled to deal with more than 20,000 flight cancellations and governments rushed to bring stranded travellers home from the Middle East.
The Israeli military said early on Wednesday it had begun a wave of strikes targeting Iranian launch sites, aerial defence systems and infrastructure.
In Israel, air raid sirens sounded early on Wednesday warning of Iranian missiles, and loud blasts as the missiles were intercepted shook buildings, said witnesses.
U.S. Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. ‚Äčforces in the Middle East as the head of Central Command, said the first 24 hours of the 'Operation Epic Fury' bombardment of Iran were "nearly double the scale" of ‚Äčthe first 24 hours of the 'Shock and Awe' campaign that opened the Iraq War in 2003.
"We are seeing that Iran's ability to hit ‚Ā†us, and our partners, is declining, while our combat power, on the other hand, is building," Cooper said in a video briefing released on Tuesday evening. "My overall operational assessment is that ‚Äčwe are ahead of our game plan."
Cooper said Iran's air defences had been badly degraded, its navy had no operational vessels on key waterways after 17 were sunk, and that more ‚Äčthan 2,000 Iranian targets had been hit.
Some 50,000 U.S. troops were taking part in operations, and that "more capabilities" were on the way, he said.
The U.S. military on Tuesday identified four of the first American soldiers killed in the war, as the Trump administration warned the intensifying conflict would lead to more U.S. casualties. Trump has not ruled out using ground forces.
A source familiar with Israel's war plan told Reuters that the campaign had been planned ‚Äčto last two weeks and was going through its target list faster than expected, with early success in killing Iran's leaders - including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in the opening ‚Äčsalvos on Saturday.
Trump said on Monday initial U.S. projections were for the operation to last four to five weeks.
Iran said deaths from the attacks had reached 787 on Tuesday. That included 165 girls killed on ‚ÄĆthe war's ‚Ā†first day when their school was bombed, the highest toll among several civilian sites reported to have been hit.
As Iranians have fled cities, the capital Tehran has become a ghost town.
‚ÄúHow long will this continue? Where are the shelters? Where is the government?‚ÄĚ Bijan, 32, a bank employee, told Reuters by telephone from Tehran. ‚ÄúEvery night my wife and I hide in the basement. The whole city is empty. There is smoke and blood everywhere.‚ÄĚ
Israel continued to target the pro-Iran Hezbollah group in neighbouring Lebanon on Wednesday, after the militants fired on Israel in retaliation for the ‚Äčdeath of Khamenei , 86, who had ruled Iran for ‚Äč37 years.
The Israeli military issued an ‚Ā†evacuation warning on Wednesday to 16 villages in southern Lebanon, urging them to leave their homes, saying anyone near Hezbollah fighters, facilities or weapons would be putting their lives at risk.
Several people were killed in an Israeli air strike on a four-storey residential building in the eastern Lebanese ‚Äčcity of Baalbek, the state news agency NNA reported early on Wednesday.
Trump has sought to justify the assault on Iran, saying he ‚Äčhad ordered the campaign ‚Ā†because he had "a feeling" Iran would attack after negotiations over its nuclear program stalled.
Iran has called the war an unprovoked attack.
"We have told the enemy that if you try to harm our main centres, we will hit all economic centres in the region," Revolutionary Guards adviser Ebrahim Jabari said in Iranian media.
Iran has fired missiles and drones at neighbouring Arab states that host U.S. bases, and strangled shipping ‚Ā†through the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz , where a fifth of the world's supplies of oil and liquefied natural gas travel past its coast.

item.title: Gauff worried about civilian loss of life in Middle East
item.description:
INDIAN WELLS, Calif., March 3 (Reuters) - World number four Coco Gauff expressed concern about the situation in the Middle East on Tuesday and said innocent lives were being ‚Äčtaken in the wake of the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran.
U.S. President ‚ÄĆDonald Trump's administration has given conflicting reasons for the attack, while Iran has said the U.S. assault was unprovoked.
Iranian state media has said that among the civilian casualties were more than 160 killed in a ‚Äčstrike on a girls' school on Saturday. Administration officials have said they are ‚Äčlooking into the incident and that the U.S. would not deliberately target ‚Ā†schools.
"First off, what's happening is unfortunate, and my thoughts and prayers are out there ‚Äčfor everyone affected and for the innocent lives being taken," American Gauff told reporters at ‚Äčthe Indian Wells tournament.
"I think it's a lot of unnecessary violence going on."
The war in Iran has spilled over into the tennis world.
An ATP Challenger event in the United Arab Emirates was abruptly halted and ‚Äčthen cancelled on Tuesday due to a security alert, which led to players and ‚Äčpersonnel fleeing the courts to designated safe areas.
Meanwhile some players, ATP staff, and coaches, including Gauff's coach ‚ÄĆGavin ‚Ā†MacMillan, have been stranded in Dubai following a tournament there as the conflict has led to widespread travel disruptions in the region.
