The WTA has strong ties to the Middle East, with the season-ending WTA Finals being held in Riyadh last year and Saudi Arabia's Public Investment ‚ÄčFund (PIF) being the naming-rights ‚Äčsponsor the WTA ‚Ā†women's rankings.
Gauff, 21, added that she has always felt safe competing in the region, and expressed gratitude for having left the area ‚Äčbefore the escalation of violence.
"I think it's just a lot of ‚Äčunfortunate circumstances ‚Ā†that are happening right now," she said.
"I am thankful that I was able to get out of that before things turned to where it is now."
Reflecting on the broader implications of ‚Ā†global ‚Äčstrife, Italian player Jannik Sinner said: "There are certain scenarios ‚Äčwe cannot control, so I tried to be focused.
"But you also realize that there are much more important things ‚Äčin life than playing tennis."

item.title: Kevin Warsh has a point on AI and inflation
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Kevin Warsh claims artificial intelligence will justify lower interest rates. That is a mightily convenient view for the person nominated by President Donald Trump to be the next chair of the Federal Reserve. The former Morgan Stanley banker is still onto something, though. Letting economic orthodoxy constrain genuine growth would be a mistake.
The debate among economists over the likely impact of AI has been raging for years. ‚ÄčIn the United States, the technology may already be helping workers be more efficient. Revised data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics imply hiring slowed far more than initially thought in 2025 even as GDP ‚ÄĆstayed strong, meaning workers produced more per hour. Sectors that have embraced AI, notably telecom operators, broadcasters and publishers, have raised productivity more than hotels and restaurants, where the technology has fewer obvious applications. The Kansas City Fed cautions , opens new tab that direct AI use remains patchy, but industries that expect a big role for large-language models seem more willing to limit headcount .
Rapid adoption of AI would be different from the personal computer revolution, which took until the 1990s to deliver efficiency benefits. Back then, Warsh recently recalled in a YouTube interview , opens new tab with Aven CEO Sadi Khan, Fed Chair Alan Greenspan ‚Äúsat on his hands‚ÄĚ in the ‚Äčexpectation that a growth boost would not push up prices. He was right. Output per hour grew at an average rate of 2.7% between 1994 and 2004, even as inflation fell to 1.9%, echoing a similar innovative period in ‚Äčthe 1960s.
Warsh thinks the U.S. is due for another technological windfall. AI will "be a significant disinflationary force,‚ÄĚ he wrote in November , opens new tab in The Wall Street Journal. Investors are warming to that view. ‚Ā†The recent software selloff reflects anticipation that their clients will use tools like Anthropic‚Äôs Claude Code to develop those functionalities themselves.
The problem with Warsh's belief is that it runs counter to mainstream economic theory. This holds that faster trend growth raises the ‚Äúnatural rate of ‚Äčinterest‚ÄĚ as firms and households bring forward spending. If the central bank does not raise borrowing costs in lockstep, demand can overheat. In this scenario any disinflation caused by technological progress is temporary.
Indeed, Fed estimates depict the natural rate rising when productivity accelerates. Some ‚Äčof the central bank's top officials, such as Lisa Cook , opens new tab , Michael Barr , opens new tab and Philip Jefferson , opens new tab , seem to be leaning the same way. Their views could limit Warsh's wiggle room, especially as his politically convenient thesis may make it harder to prove his commitment to defending the central bank's independence . Even Greenspan's dedication to tolerating technology-led growth had limits , opens new tab , since the Fed kept policy quite tight as the telecom‚ÄĎled investment boom got going in the mid-1990s and only cut interest rates after hedge fund Long Term Capital Management imploded in 1998.
Still, obsessing about an unobservable natural rate is a mistake. Surveys show companies rarely cite ‚Äčborrowing costs as decisive for investment, and households base spending mainly on their income and age. Meanwhile, figures from the Bureau of Economic Analysis stretching back to 1977 show a clear historical correlation between sectors that experience faster productivity gains and those that ‚Äčraise prices less. In the specific case of AI, fresh cross-sector research , opens new tab from the euro zone finds that, when the share of firms using it rises by 10 percentage points, producer inflation falls by up to 0.6 percentage points.
The catch is that, while clothes and smartphones have become ‚ÄĆcheaper, healthcare, education ‚Ā†and housing have become more expensive. This is a pattern known as ‚ÄúBaumol disease.‚ÄĚ The American economist William Baumol showed in the 1960s that as productivity rises in tradable industries ‚ÄĒ those producing goods and services that can be consumed far from the point of production ‚ÄĒ income flows to activities that are hard to automate or transport, so their prices and share of employment increase. In a paper , opens new tab he co-authored last year, Nobel laureate Michael Spence estimates that nontradable sectors now make up 77% of U.S. jobs and 68% of value added.
Using his approach to classify output and employment reveals the limits of the 1990s boom. Productivity in tradable industries surged, averaging 4.4% growth, but the impact on non-tradable sectors was small. The number of jobs in tradable industries grew only 6% from 1994 to 2004, ‚Äčas tech firms shrank after the dotcom bubble burst, while ‚Äčemployment in nontradable areas expanded 19%. In the former, workers‚Äô ‚Ā†share of value added fell from 62% to 57%, showing how investment-led growth skews gains toward owners of capital. It appears to support the view of the information technology wave as a one-off bump , opens new tab that mostly just benefitted the makers of hardware and software. Adopters of new digital systems like giant retailer Walmart gained in the U.S., but not in Europe.
A better takeaway, however, is that ‚Äčthe macroeconomic environment matters. In the 1990s, the American labor market was far tighter than the European one, having quickly recovered from a recession, so U.S. firms had incentives to ‚Äčsqueeze more output from existing workers. That ‚Ā†tradable productivity growth plummeted to 1% in the 2010s suggests that it was the global financial crisis that killed the boom, not digital saturation.
Fast-forward to today, and hiring has also slowed after a growth period. Spending could weaken further if AI widens income inequality, since the wealthy consume a smaller proportion of their earnings. In that case, monetary policy should be looser. If stock market doomsters are right and industries like law, education and health care are able to automate key processes, the deflationary effect will be even more pronounced.
Of course there ‚Ā†are big differences. ‚ÄčThe 1990s were a time when globalization weighed on prices. Now, tariffs are pushing up prices of tradable goods in the U.S. and the data center ‚Äčinvestment boom is straining power, land and supply chains. All these factors place a floor on official borrowing costs.
Still, it would be a mistake for the Fed to try to restrain the AI boom to fit a textbook model, or to assert its independence from an interventionist president. In that sense, Warsh has ‚Äča point.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Netanyahu's war alliance with Trump faces test as Iran crisis widens
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 4, (Reuters) - Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu delivered on a career-long ambition to topple Iran's leadership, but his lockstep alignment with U.S. President Donald Trump faces a test as their joint military campaign threatens to drag on, with its goals potentially shifting in the coming weeks.
At the outset of the bombing campaign on Saturday, both Trump and Netanyahu said regime change was the goal. But in ‚Äčremarks at the White House on Monday, two days after Israeli air strikes killed Iran‚Äôs Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and much of his leadership, Trump did not mention overthrowing Iran's government as his top priority.
The U.S. goal, he said, was ‚ÄĆto destroy Iran‚Äôs missiles and navy, and to stop it from obtaining a nuclear weapon. His Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth said at a press conference that same day that the operation was not a ‚Äúso-called regime-change war.‚ÄĚ
Netanyahu, by contrast, has called on Iran's citizens to take to the streets and overthrow their rulers as recently as Monday night. "We're going to create the conditions, first, for the Iranian people to get control of their destiny," he told Fox News.
Asked about the U.S. and Israeli goals, a U.S. official familiar with the White House's objectives told Reuters that the two countries' military campaigns have different objectives. "Regime change is one of theirs," said the official, who spoke on the condition of ‚Äčanonymity.
In the build-up to war, Netanyahu successfully convinced Trump that it was a now-or-never moment to prevent Tehran from acquiring nuclear weapons and destroy its ballistic missile capabilities. Trump has said the operation could take ‚Äúfour or five weeks‚ÄĚ or ‚Äúwhatever it takes.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúI don‚Äôt get bored, I ‚Äčnever get bored,‚ÄĚ he said at the White House on Monday in response to questions about his capacity for sustained focus.
But Israeli officials privately acknowledge that ultimately it will be Trump who decides when the war ‚Ā†ends. Dan Shapiro, a former U.S. ambassador to Israel under the Obama administration, said that Trump may decide to seek an "early off-ramp" from the war.
"If President Trump decides that he's reached the end of this operation before Netanyahu wants it to end, he's still going to end it," said ‚ÄčShapiro, of the Washington-based Atlantic Council think-tank.
President Trump faces domestic pressures that could affect his thinking as the war drags on and expands.
The operation is unpopular in the United States, with only one in four Americans saying they back U.S. strikes on Iran, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling. Primary votes began Tuesday ‚Äčin the battleground states of Texas and North Carolina that may decide who controls Congress after the fall midterm elections.
With the crisis disrupting shipping and energy production, rising gas prices could become a daily reminder of the affordability crisis facing many Americans. Gas is up 11 cents per gallon in the U.S. this week, with much higher spikes in global markets suggesting more increases for American consumers.
Inside the U.S., support for Israel has become a partisan issue, with some 59% of Americans holding an unfavourable view of Israel's government, up from 51% a year ago, according to a Pew Research Center poll from October.
The White House and Netanyahu's office did not respond to requests for ‚Äčcomment.
In power for most of the last three decades, Netanyahu has often clashed with American leaders, notably publicly criticizing former Democratic President Barack Obama for negotiating a nuclear deal with Iran. Democratic President Joe Biden‚Äôs administration often clashed with Netanyahu and withheld some weapons from Israel during ‚Äčits military assault in Gaza.
After Trump's return to office in 2025, Netanyahu met with the president seven times and repeatedly pushed in phone calls to focus his attention away from Israel's war in Gaza and toward Iran's ballistic missiles and nuclear ambitions, painting the clerical rulers in Tehran as a common enemy, a U.S. official ‚ÄĆwith direct knowledge ‚Ā†of their conversations said.
The officials and others who shared details about U.S.-Israeli planning and objectives spoke on the condition of anonymity to describe sensitive military discussions.
Even as Trump dispatched envoys to nuclear talks with Iran in Geneva and Oman, the U.S. and Israel had been at work for months planning their military operation, and timing for the attack was decided weeks ago, an Israeli official said.
Netanyahu‚Äôs last meeting with Trump was a hastily-arranged visit on February 11, 2026 which included a three-hour meeting at the White House, uncharacteristically closed to the press.
The day after that meeting, the USS Gerald Ford aircraft carrier, the world‚Äôs largest warship, departed the Caribbean where it was supporting U.S. military action in Venezuela, for the Mediterranean.
‚ÄúI have tried to persuade successive American administrations to take firm action, and President Trump did,‚ÄĚ Netanyahu told Fox News on Monday.
Trump rejected the notion that Israel might have forced his country into war, ‚Äčtelling reporters at the White House on Tuesday: "Based on the way the ‚Äčnegotiation was going, I think they were going to attack ‚Ā†first, and I didn't want that to happen. So if anything I might have forced Israel's hand."
For the 76-year-old Netanyahu, his prosecution of a war that is supported by most Israelis represents an opportunity to seal his legacy ahead of elections, due by October, in which he faces formidable challenges.
His far-right coalition faces fissures, he's on trial for corruption he denies and Israelis are still reeling from a multi-front war that began in 2023 ‚Äčand which Netanyahu has promised will transform the Middle East.
Israel's longest-serving leader has shown remarkable political skill in the past. Despite successive polls showing that he will lose the ballot in October, Netanyahu still has ‚Äča fair chance of victory if Israeli ‚Ā†fatalities and the economic costs to Israel of the war remain low, said Udi Sommer, a political scientist at Tel Aviv University.
"If it succeeds, relatively quickly (like) in June 2025, it will work very much in his favour as Israel's protector and the one who had woven a particularly successful relationship with the administration in Washington," Sommer said.
Netanyahu's security credentials were shattered on October 7, 2023, when Iran-backed Hamas militants launched a surprise attack on Israel, killing more than 1,200 people and taking 251 hostages.
It was followed by a two-year military campaign against Hamas in Gaza, where Israel's longest war has killed at least 72,000 people, according to ‚Ā†Palestinian health officials, left ‚Äčmuch of the enclave in ruins, and exacted the highest Israeli military fatalities in decades.
Netanyahu has rejected responsibility for the security failures of October 7 and has pointed to Israel's ‚Äčsubsequent gains in weakening Iran's proxies, Hamas and Hezbollah in Lebanon. Their ally Bashar al-Assad in Syria has also been ousted.
Even if Israel achieves its military objectives in Iran, that will not wash away the outrage of many Israeli voters, including among Netanyahu's own right-wing base, said political analyst Amotz Asa-el of the Jerusalem-based Shalom Hartman research institute.
"The past three years' ‚Äčevents have been so traumatic and so dramatic and so revolting to that swing vote that I don't think any kind of salvation in Iran will offset this," he said.

item.title: OpenAI looking at contract with NATO, source says
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - OpenAI is considering a contract to deploy its AI technology on North Atlantic Treaty ‚ÄčOrganization's (NATO) "unclassified" networks, a person familiar with the matter said on Tuesday, days after the ‚ÄĆChatGPT-owner struck a deal with the Pentagon.
The Wall Street Journal first reported that OpenAI was considering an agreement with NATO.
The newspaper said the OpenAI CEO, Sam Altman, had initially said in a ‚Äčcompany meeting that it was looking to deploy on all NATO classified ‚Äčnetworks, but a company spokeswoman later clarified to the Journal that Altman misspoke and ‚Ā†the contract opportunity was for NATO's "unclassified networks."
NATO, a 32-member military alliance, did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to a request for comment outside regular business hours.
OpenAI, which is backed by ‚ÄčMicrosoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab , Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab and others, announced a deal late last week to deploy its technology in the Pentagon's classified network, after U.S. President Donald Trump directed the government to stop working with rival Anthropic.
Anthropic's removal followed ‚Äča standoff in contract talks with the Pentagon over the use of the firm's ‚Äčtechnology. Anthropic CEO, Dario Amodei, had stressed the company's opposition to the Pentagon using its AI models for ‚ÄĆmass ‚Ā†domestic surveillance or to power fully autonomous weapons.
The Pentagon has said previously it had no interest in using AI to conduct mass surveillance of Americans or using AI to develop weapons that operate without human involvement, but wanted any lawful use of AI to ‚Äčbe allowed.
In an ‚Äčupdated statement on Monday after ‚Ā†striking a deal on Friday, OpenAI said its AI systems "shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and ‚Äčnationals," adding that the Pentagon also affirmed that AI services ‚Äčwould not ‚Ā†be used by intelligence agencies such as the National Security Agency (NSA).
"I think this was an example of a complex, but right decision with extremely difficult brand consequences and very negative ‚Ā†PR ‚Äčfor us in the short term,‚ÄĚ Altman said in ‚Äča company meeting on Tuesday, referring to the Pentagon deal, according to the Wall Street Journal.

item.title: Philippine lawmakers advance impeachment of Vice President Duterte
item.description:
MANILA, March 4 (Reuters) - Philippine lawmakers ordered Vice President Sara Duterte on Wednesday to respond to impeachment complaints against her after finding substance to allegations ‚Äčinvolving unexplained wealth, the misuse of public funds and making public threats against ‚ÄĆPresident Ferdinand Marcos Jr.
The daughter of former President Rodrigo Duterte is seen as a frontrunner for the 2028 presidential elections, and has said she will run for the top ‚Äčpost . She survived a similar impeachment bid last year, which the Supreme Court ‚Äčstruck down for violating constitutional safeguards.
If convicted by the Senate ‚Ā†at trial, Duterte would be removed from office and disqualified from ‚Äčholding any future government position.
Duterte's defence team during the impeachment proceedings did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to a request for comment.
Wednesday's vote in the House of Representatives' justice committee comes amid a bitter falling out between the two leaders, both scions of ‚Äčpowerful political families, who swept to power in 2022 before becoming rivals.
Before any ‚Äčvote on whether an impeachment trial should go ahead, the House will consider Duterte's response to the charges, ‚ÄĆif ‚Ā†she makes one, as well as any reply from the complainants.
The complaints against Duterte include the misuse of confidential funds, bribery and unexplained wealth, and publicly threatening to have Marcos killed, along with his wife and a cousin ‚Äčwho was House ‚Äčspeaker at the ‚Ā†time.
"This is a very good development that the process of accountability is now moving," Representative Leila de Lima ‚Äčtold Reuters. De Lima endorsed one of the complaints ‚Äčagainst Duterte.
Four ‚Ā†impeachment complaints were initially lodged against Duterte, but one complaint was dismissed over a rule that prohibits such complaints from being filed within a year after previously being ‚Ā†submitted. Another ‚Äčcomplaint was withdrawn to fast-track the proceedings ‚Äčand because it was similar to another complaint.
Marcos survived a separate impeachment bid against him last month after his allies ‚Äčin Congress voted to dismiss it.

item.title: South Africa's Lekota, who led breakaway from ANC, dies at 77
item.description: JOHANNESBURG, March 4 (Reuters) - South African anti-apartheid activist Mosiuoa Lekota, who broke away from the African ‚ÄčNational Congress to found a new ‚ÄĆpolitical party, died in the early hours of Wednesday morning, his party said in a statement. ‚ÄčHe was 77 years old.

item.title: Myanmar junta to ration fuel for private vehicles, blaming Middle East shipping disruptions
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Myanmar's junta announced the launch of a sweeping fuel rationing system for private vehicles on Wednesday, blaming disruptions to the global energy supply chain caused by escalating hostilities in the ‚ÄčMiddle East.
The country's National Defence and Security Council (NDSC) said the new regulations, effective ‚ÄĆMarch 7, 2026, were a response to "global political situations" and armed conflicts in the Middle East, which have obstructed oil shipments.
Under a new "even-odd" licensing scheme, even-numbered plates will only be allowed to drive on even dates, and ‚Äčodd-numbered plates on odd dates, the announcement said. Electric vehicles and electric motorcycles are ‚Äčexempt.
The NDSC warned businesses and individuals not to hoard fuel for resale at ‚Ā†inflated prices, saying that violators would be prosecuted.
Global shipping costs have surged amid an ongoing U.S. and ‚ÄčIsraeli war against Iran and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting tankers bound ‚Äčfor Asian ports.
Myanmar is heavily reliant on refined fuel imports from Singapore and Malaysia, which serve as regional processing hubs for Middle Eastern crude.
The disruptions have already caused shortages, though secondary supplies are available via Russia and ‚Äčits neighbour Thailand.
Residents in Yangon, Myanmar's commercial hub, expressed concern that the rationing plan would drive ‚Äčup the cost of living and further complicate daily life in a city already plagued by power outages.
"Operating ‚ÄĆvehicles ‚Ā†on alternating even and odd days based on license plate numbers is an incredibly frustrating system for people in a city like Yangon, where we depend so heavily on cars," said one Yangon resident.
Skyrocketing prices have already made it difficult to meet demand, raising questions about the extent of ‚Äčthe country's strategic reserves, ‚Äčhe added.
Fuel supplies in ‚Ā†the border town of Myawaddy ran out as early as the evening of March 3, forcing local stations to temporarily shut down and residents ‚Äčto queue up at gas stations in Thailand's Mae Sot, according to ‚Äča resident.
"Since ‚Ā†yesterday, many people have been crossing over to the Mae Sot side to fill up their tanks. I went to stand in line myself and saw a massive number of vehicles queuing up at ‚Ā†Thai gas ‚Äčstations," the resident said.
Myanmar has been in turmoil since ‚Äč2021, when the military ousted the elected government led by Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi, sparking a wave of ‚Äčanti-junta protests that have morphed into a nationwide civil war.

item.title: China says it seeks communications¬†with US but vows to hold its 'red lines'
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - China is willing to work with the United States to promote communication on all ‚Äčlevels, while upholding its "red lines" and principles, Lou ‚ÄĆQinjian, a spokesperson for its parliament, said on Wednesday.
The National People's Congress opens its annual session on Thursday, where it will unveil ‚Äčthis year's economic targets and policy priorities.
The meeting comes ‚Äčat a sensitive moment for China-US relations, as ‚Ā†both sides look to stabilise ties ahead of an expected ‚Äčsummit of leaders Donald Trump and Xi Jinping in Beijing ‚Äčat the end of March.
China and the United States should respect each other and coexist peacefully, Lou said.
"China has its own principles and ‚Äčred lines, and as always, will resolutely defend its ‚Äčsovereignty, security and development interests," he told a press conference.
Diplomacy between heads ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†state has an "irreplaceable strategic role" in guiding the two countries' ties, Lou added, urging them to "expand the list of cooperation (areas) while reducing the list of problems".
He called on the ‚ÄčU.S. Congress to ‚Äčview China "objectively", ‚Ā†and do more to benefit ties.
A White House official has confirmed Trump will travel to ‚ÄčChina from March 31 to April 2, although ‚ÄčBeijing ‚Ā†has made no official announcement.
Top trade negotiators of both sides are expected to meet in Paris next week to discuss potential ‚Ā†business ‚Äčdeals linked to the highly anticipated ‚Äčmeet, Bloomberg News said on Tuesday.

item.title: Iranians to bid farewell to late Supreme Leader Khamenei at Tehran ceremony
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Iranians will bid farewell to late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ‚Äčat a ceremony in Tehran late on Wednesday, ‚ÄĆa senior Iranian official told state media.
Hojjatoleslam Mahmoudi, head of Iran‚Äôs Islamic Propagation Council, ‚Äčsaid the farewell ceremony would continue ‚Äčfor three days and the funeral ‚Ā†procession will be announced later.
The official ‚Äčsaid the public will be able ‚Äčto pay their respects to the body of late Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei at Tehran‚Äôs ‚ÄčImam Khomeini Prayer Hall starting at ‚Äč10 p.m. (1830 GMT).
"The Mosalla (prayer hall) will be receiving ‚ÄĆvisitors ‚Ā†and the dear people can attend and take part in the farewell ceremony and mark a strong presence once ‚Äčagain," he ‚Äčsaid in ‚Ā†comments carried by Iranian media.
Iran's Ali Khamenei, who based his ‚Äčiron rule of Iran on ‚Äčhostility ‚Ā†to the U.S. and Israel, was killed on Saturday, aged 86, in air strikes ‚Ā†by ‚ÄčIsrael and the U.S., ‚Äčaccording to Iranian state media.

item.title: Sri Lanka rescues 30 people on board distressed Iranian ship, foreign minister says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The Sri Lankan military ‚Äčhas rescued at least ‚ÄĆ30 people on board a sinking Iranian ship near Sri ‚ÄčLankan waters on ‚ÄčWednesday, the country's foreign minister ‚Ā†told parliament.
The Sri Lankan ‚Äčnavy dispatched a rescue mission ‚Äčafter a distress call from the Iranian ship, a defence ministry spokesperson said earlier ‚Äčon Wednesday.
Foreign minister ‚ÄčVijitha Herath did not give further ‚ÄĆdetails ‚Ā†but said Sri Lanka would take appropriate action.
Local media reported the ship reported ‚Äčdistress ‚Äčoff ‚Ā†the coast of Galle in the southern ‚Äčpart of the ‚Äčcountry, ‚Ā†and that the injured had been admitted to a ‚Ā†hospital ‚Äčin Galle.

item.title: Two senior Communist Party officials absent at top Chinese political event
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - Two senior Chinese officials were absent on ‚ÄčWednesday from the ranks of ‚ÄĆChina's highest tier of power at the opening of the country's ‚Äčtwo largest political events ‚Äčof the year, with top ‚Ā†Communist Party cadres ensnared ‚Äčin a wave of purges.
Zhang Youxia, ‚ÄčChina's highest-ranking general, was not among the party's 23-member politburo at ‚Äčthe opening of the Chinese ‚ÄčPeople's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), according to ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†Reuters witness. Neither was Ma Xingrui, formerly the party's top official in the western Chinese ‚Äčregion ‚Äčof Xinjiang.
Zhang, ‚Ā†also a vice chairman on the Central ‚ÄčMilitary Commission (CMC), was put ‚Äčunder ‚Ā†investigation in January. Ma has not been in the public ‚Ā†eye ‚Äčsince late October.

item.title: Gold flubs its lines amid Middle East mayhem
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - The most peculiar market move in an alarming week of Middle East conflict was how the most obvious "safe haven" - gold - flubbed its lines. Instead of piling into gold, investors seemed to sprint for dollar cash, offloading ‚Äčanything that caught a speculative head of steam before last weekend's attacks.
Three days after Saturday's attacks on Iran, the initial bid for ‚ÄĆprecious metals faded quickly. On Tuesday, there was a major reversal , with gold suddenly falling 4% and silver dropping as much as 10%.
A return of the dollar's long-lost "safety bid", which saw the greenback (.DXY) , opens new tab climb this week despite heavy losses in U.S. stocks and bonds, was cited as one basic reason for gold's reversal.
Both public and private funds in the Middle East region now ‚Äčfacing Iranian retaliatory strikes may be opting for dollar liquidity. A spike in dollar-denominated oil and gas prices may also have spurred demand for cash in the ‚Äčworld's reserve currency.
The main reason for the dollar rise, however, is most likely the hit to major European and Asian ‚Ā†economies from a protracted energy supply disruption and price spike compared with the relatively insulated U.S.
Either way, a rebounding dollar takes the shine off gold.
But there are other possible reasons ‚Äčfor gold's "no-show" so far this week.
One is gold's correlation with the Swiss franc . Both are traditionally the safest of safe havens in stressful times and get bid ‚Äčup in tandem - especially now that others such as Japan's yen and U.S. Treasuries have been neutralised as such in recent years.
But an extraordinary warning of franc-selling intervention from the Swiss National Bank on Monday quickly reversed the currency's gains against both the dollar and the euro. The resulting unwind of haven trades may have added pressure on gold.
A ‚Äčmore prosaic explanation is that investors who loaded up on gold in a speculative frenzy - one that almost doubled its price to fresh records over the past year - ‚Äčare simply cashing out of their best-performing trades as risk and volatility rise.
That would tally, for example, with the sudden reversal of the year's best-performing stock market to date. South ‚ÄĆKorea's Kospi ‚Ā†benchmark (.KS11) , opens new tab fell more than 7% on Tuesday as Seoul returned from a holiday to reverse some of a near 50% year-to-date surge.
Gold and silver were the second- and third-best-performing major markets of 2026 before the strikes, behind the blockbuster Kospi, while Japan's Nikkei (.N225) , opens new tab ‚Äď up about 15% before the weekend - has since dropped more than 4%.
With volatility rising and another potential energy shock brewing for the global economy, many portfolios may simply be looking to increase cash and liquidity.
The fact that gold has not ‚Äčyet performed as a haven in that ‚Äčenvironment says a lot about ‚Ā†the nature of the buying and the rally over the past year - and not least how it hinged heavily on the ongoing reversal of more than a decade of one-way dollar strength.
As IMF First Deputy Managing Director Dan Katz said on Tuesday, ‚Äčthe dollar's behaviour this week shows its role as a haven persists and the U.S. currency remains the "heart of ‚Äčthe international monetary ‚Ā†system."
Gold may yet rise for other reasons. But if its recent parabolic climb was fuelled by doomsday narratives about the dollar's demise, then this week's moves may prompt a rethink.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab ‚Ā†and X. , opens new tab
Plus, sign ‚Äčup for my weekday newsletter, Morning Bid U.S. and listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or ‚Äčthe Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Investors can still outwit AI, but only if they‚Äôre unpredictable
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Investors know artificial intelligence is coming for their jobs. Machines can already run complex analyses of investments and portfolios, threatening fund managers' livelihoods.
But new studies show where AI falls short ‚Äď and it's in those blind spots where human managers still have the ability to generate "alpha" or excess returns.
For weeks now, stocks have been sorted ‚Äčinto AI winners and AI losers . Share prices of companies with business models that may be disrupted by AI have sold off, while the makers of AI hardware and software ‚ÄĆhave kept rising. It is no secret that many academics and companies are testing the ability of AI to pick stocks and manage portfolios. So should fund managers fear for their jobs?
In recent weeks, I have come across several academic studies that examine both the capabilities and limitations of AI. The most comprehensive and interesting is a new NBER working paper , opens new tab by Boston University Assistant Professor Pietro Bini and his colleagues.
They ask four leading generative AI (genAI) models (GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama) to answer a set ‚Äčof questions used to measure behavioural biases in finance and economics. They then evaluate whether these models give a rational answer or an answer that is biased in the same manner as most ‚Äčhumans.
What emerges from the data is an interesting split. When dealing with common cognitive biases, like the gambler‚Äôs fallacy , opens new tab or base-rate neglect, , opens new tab where genAI can ‚Ā†fall back on well-established mathematical formulas, the answers are largely free from bias. One can therefore expect that in forecasting situations in which humans may be subject to such biases, genAI will likely outperform investors ‚Äčmade of flesh and bone.
But when dealing with problems that have a large degree of qualitative uncertainty or where the answer requires a judgment call, genAI is just as biased as most humans. When the model can‚Äôt ‚Äčrely on a mathematical answer, it has to deduce a solution from its training data. And the training data is mostly man-made and thus codifies the same biases as humans. It‚Äôs a case of garbage-in, garbage-out.
Importantly, these results indicate where human investors might be able to outperform machines moving forward.
In another study, Lauren Cohen from Harvard Business School , opens new tab and her collaborators trained AI to learn the decision processes of thousands of U.S. equity fund managers. The goal was to predict which ‚Äčstocks a fund manager would buy, sell, or hold next quarter or the following year.
If AI can do this consistently, fund managers really should be worried about their jobs.
The bad news is that the AI-equipped researchers ‚Äčmanaged to correctly predict 71% of all future trades. The good news is that fund managers' alpha was rooted mostly in the other 29%.
Naturally, fund managers have different processes, so there is variability in how well AI can anticipate ‚ÄĆtheir actions.
Funds that ‚Ā†are run based on a prescriptive, inflexible process (or by an inflexible fund manager) are, unsurprisingly, more predictable. These funds tend to invest more in stocks that are clear-cut cases for a specific investment style.
For example, value funds with a rigid, prescriptive process tend to invest only in the most obvious value stocks and ignore the ones that are ambiguous cases.
But if everyone knows that company A is a value company, then the shares would already be in the portfolios of most fund managers following that investment style. And that leaves less room for new investors ‚Äď including AI models ‚Äď to gain any advantage by buying the stock.
Company B, meanwhile, may be a ‚Äčvalue stock, but may struggle with a difficult competitive ‚Äčenvironment, ineffective management or other factors that cannot ‚Ā†easily be quantified. A fund manager would need to use judgment to determine whether this was truly a value stock or something that was "cheap" for a reason. This is where fund managers become unpredictable to AI.
If a fund manager "sees something" in company B that isn‚Äôt in the data, an AI model will struggle to ‚Äčpredict the purchase. Yet, if that judgment turns out to be correct, the shares of company B will likely rally much more as new investors flock ‚Äčto buy the stock.
The result ‚Ā†is that fund managers whose actions are harder for AI to predict, who appear more random and are better at handling qualitative factors, tend to outperform both their peers and the broader market.
Of course, these human advantages may prove fleeting. As AI models learn from ever richer data sets and from the very managers they now struggle to predict, today's blind spots could narrow, shifting the frontier of truly human alpha yet again.
The lesson for investors is ‚Ā†thus that in ‚Äčthe age of AI, a fund‚Äôs alpha will increasingly come from being able to predict the unpredictable.
(The views expressed here are ‚Äčthose of Joachim Klement , an investment strategist for Panmure Liberum.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest ‚Äčnews in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Canada PM Carney says Iran conflict a failure of the international order
item.description:
SYDNEY/TORONTO, March 4 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime ‚ÄčMinister Mark Carney said on Wednesday the ongoing conflict in the Middle East was a "failure" of the international order, and ‚ÄĆthe United States had not consulted its allies before striking Iran.
U.S. and Israeli forces launched strikes against Iran on Saturday after negotiations over Tehran's nuclear programme stalled.
The war has since spread beyond Iran's borders, with Iranian attacks hitting Gulf states including Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar, as well as ‚ÄčU.S. embassies in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, prompting Washington to close diplomatic missions across the region.
"The current conflict is ‚Äčanother example of the failure of the international order, despite decades of UN Security Council resolutions, ‚Ā†the work of the International Atomic Energy Agency in a succession of sanctions and diplomatic frameworks," Carney said during a visit ‚Äčto Australia in a speech at the Lowy Institute think tank in Sydney.
"Iran's nuclear threat remains, and now the United States and ‚ÄčIsrael have acted without engaging the UN or consulting with allies, including Canada."
Earlier, Carney called for calm in the Middle East and said all countries engaged in hostilities, including the U.S. and Israel, must respect international rules of engagement.
"Canada calls for a rapid de-escalation of hostilities and is prepared ‚Äčto assist in achieving this goal," Carney told reporters.
When asked about previous comments he made supporting the UN Charter's prohibition of ‚Äčthe use of force, Carney said "it appears that these actions are inconsistent with international law," but said it was up to the U.S. and ‚ÄĆIsrael ‚Ā†to make the case whether they were consistent with international law.
"It's for others more expert than me ... to make that determination," he said.
Carney said Canada was not informed in advance of the strikes and was not asked to participate.
"We would not have been in a position ... to take a judgement that met our standards if we had been asked to participate," Carney said.
After the strikes began ‚Äčat the weekend, Carney said ‚ÄčCanada supported the U.S. "acting to ‚Ā†prevent Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon and to prevent its regime from further threatening international peace and security."
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific ‚Äčregion also taking in Japan and India, with his stop in Australia aimed at bolstering relations ‚Äčbetween the two ‚Ā†so-called "middle powers".
"Canada is focused on building a dense web of connections, ad hoc coalitions that work issue by issue, with partners that share enough common ground on those issues to act together," Carney said on Wednesday.
"In contrast, great powers can afford to go it alone. ‚Ā†They have ‚Äčthe market size, the military capacity to leverage, to dictate terms. Middle powers ‚Äčlike Canada do not."
Australia and Canada are expected to deepen cooperation in areas such as defence and maritime security, critical minerals, trade and artificial intelligence, Carney's office said ‚Äčahead of the visit.

item.title: Spain's Sanchez to Trump: 'You cannot play Russian roulette with the destiny of millions'
item.description:
MADRID, March 4 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez on Wednesday doubled down on his opposition to the attack ‚Äčon Iran by the U.S. and Israel, warning ‚ÄĆthat the conflict risked playing "Russian roulette" with the lives of millions.
Sanchez was responding after U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to cut trade with Madrid over its ‚Äčposition on the conflict.
"This is how humanity's great disasters ‚Äčstart ... You cannot play Russian roulette with destiny ‚Ā†of millions," Sanchez said in a televised address to the ‚Äčnation.
Tensions between the two NATO allies increased after Sanchez denounced the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israeli bombings of Iran as reckless and illegal, and later banned U.S. aircraft from using naval and air bases in southern Spain for ‚Äčthe offensive against Tehran.
Sanchez said the world could not ‚Äčsolve its problems with conflicts and bombs.
"The position of the Spanish government ‚ÄĆcan ‚Ā†be summarised in four words: 'No to the war,'" he said, adding the stance was not disingenuous but coherent.
"We're not going to be complicit in something that's bad for the ‚Äčworld nor contrary ‚Äčto our ‚Ā†values and interests simply to avoid reprisals from someone," Sanchez said, appearing to reference Trump's ‚Äčtrade threats.
Sanchez highlighted the negative knock-on effects ‚Äčof the ‚Ā†Iraq war, from a rise in jihadist terrorism to soaring energy prices, to argue that the consequences of this attack ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčIran were just as nebulous and ‚Äčthat it would not lead to a more just international order.

item.title: Hungary's opposition leads in polls, far-right party gains support
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 4 (Reuters) - Hungary's centre-right Tisza party leads Prime Minister Viktor Orban's governing Fidesz before an April 12 election, while the far-right Our Homeland is gaining support, two ‚Äčopinion polls showed on Wednesday.
Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to ‚ÄĆhis rule in 16 years although the outcome of the vote remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing that many voters are still undecided.
The poll by Zavecz Research, conducted between February 22 ‚Äčand 28, shows that Tisza widened its lead to 12 points among decided ‚Äčvoters from 10 points in a January poll.
The poll, published by ‚Ā†news site 24.hu late on Tuesday, showed 50% of decided voters supported Tisza, ‚Äčup from 48% in January, while 38% backed Fidesz, down from 39% a month ‚Äčearlier.
Zavecz showed 38% support for Tisza among all voters, while Fidesz was backed by 32%.
According to their data, some 20% of respondents said that they still did not know who to back, down ‚Äčfrom 23% in February.
Tisza is led by former government insider Peter Magyar who ‚Äčhas said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros in frozen European Union funds ‚ÄĆto boost ‚Ā†the economy, and firmly anchor Hungary in the EU and NATO.
A second poll, published by newspaper Nepszava on Wednesday and conducted by Publicus Institute, showed 47% of decided voters supporting Tisza and 39% backing Fidesz, as support for both parties was down ‚Äč1 percentage point ‚Äčsince a January poll.
While ‚Ā†most polls have shown a Tisza lead, Fidesz points to other surveys that still show it on course to victory, though ‚Äčits opponents say these have mainly been conducted by institutes ‚Äčwith financial ‚Ā†or personal ties to the ruling party.
Both polls showed that the far-right Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) would be the only other party to pass the 5% threshold required for entry ‚Ā†into parliament.
Zavecz ‚ÄčResearch put support for Our Homeland at 7% ‚Äčamong decided voters, up from 5% a month ago. Publicus said that 6% of decided voters back the ‚Äčfar-right party, up from 5% in January.

item.title: Russian drone hits empty passenger train in Ukraine's south, deputy PM says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A Russian drone hit an empty passenger train in Ukraine's southern Mykolaiv ‚Äčregion early on Wednesday, injuring a ‚ÄĆrailway worker, Deputy Prime Minister Oleksiy Kuleba said.
He added that there was also an ‚Äčattempted Russian drone attack on a ‚Äčtrain operating between the eastern-central city ‚Ā†of Dnipro and Kovel in the ‚Äčnorthwest late on Tuesday, but it was ‚Äčstopped by railway workers and the drone hit a few metres away from the locomotive.
Ukrzaliznytsia, Ukrainian ‚Äčnational railways, said that Russia had ‚Äčintensified drone attacks on railway infrastructure, and that rolling ‚ÄĆstock ‚Ā†was among the main targets.
It reported that 18 strikes had been recorded since the start of March, damaging 41 ‚Äčfacilities.
Locomotives, freight ‚Äčcars and ‚Ā†specialised equipment used to repair infrastructure have also been targeted, ‚Äčthe operator said, and railway depots ‚Äčand ‚Ā†bridges had also come under attack this month.
A Russian drone attack on a commuter ‚Ā†train ‚Äčin Dnipropetrovsk region killed ‚Äčone person and wounded seven more people on Monday.

item.title: South Africa offers to mediate in Middle East conflict if asked
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 4 (Reuters) - South Africa is willing ‚Äčto play a mediating role ‚ÄĆin the Middle East conflict if asked, President Cyril Ramaphosa told local media on the ‚Äčsidelines of an energy conference ‚Äčin Cape Town.
"South Africa is always ‚Ā†ready to play a contributing role, ‚Äčeither in mediation or whatever. And ‚Äčif a gap opens or if we are asked, we always live up to our ‚Äčobligations," Ramaphosa said, according to ‚Äčcomments broadcast on local broadcaster Newzroom Afrika.
"If the ‚ÄĆopportunity ‚Ā†were to open, we would talk and say: there must be a ceasefire. Dialogue is always the best ‚Äčway of ‚Äčending conflict ‚Ā†and then ending the war. And we want this ‚Äčwar to come to an ‚Äčend ‚Ā†immediately," he said.
Ramaphosa added that the South African government was doing everything ‚Ā†it ‚Äčcould to bring home ‚Äčits citizens stranded in the region.

item.title: Mojtaba Khamenei, seen as possible next Supreme Leader, has survived assault on Iran, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Mojtaba Khamanei, the ‚Äčson of ‚ÄĆIran's late Supreme Leader, has ‚Äčsurvived the ‚Äčassault on the country ‚Ā†by U.S. ‚Äčand Israeli ‚Äčair strikes that killed Ayatollah Ali ‚ÄčKhamenei and ‚Äčseveral military and other ‚ÄĆinfluential ‚Ā†figures, two Iranian sources told Reuters on ‚ÄčWednesday.
He ‚Äčis ‚Ā†seen by the establishment ‚Äčas ‚Äča ‚Ā†possible successor to his father, ‚Ā†the ‚Äčsources ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: Appeal hearings begin in ex-South Korea President Yoon's obstruction case
item.description: SEOUL, March 4 (Reuters) - A South Korean court held an appeals hearing on Wednesday in a case involving ‚Äčformer South Korean President Yoon Suk Yeol, who ‚ÄĆwas sentenced to five years in jail by a lower court on charges including obstructing arrest after his attempt to impose ‚Äčmartial law.

item.title: Russia says France's plan to expand its nuclear arsenal is destabilising
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 4 (Reuters) - Russia said on Wednesday that France's plan to expand its nuclear arsenal was a highly ‚Äčdestabilising move that posed a potential threat to ‚ÄĆMoscow.
French President Emmanuel Macron announced the plan on Monday, saying other European countries would also be able to take part ‚Äčin French nuclear exercises. France and Germany said ‚Äčthey had set up a nuclear steering group ‚Ā†to discuss deterrence issues.
Macron first said in March ‚Äč2025 that he would launch a strategic dialogue on extending ‚Äčthe protection of France's nuclear umbrella to European allies that have until now relied on the United States.
Russian Foreign Ministry spokeswoman ‚ÄčMaria Zakharova told reporters that Macron's announcement this ‚Äčweek was "an extremely destabilising development".
It represented "a significant strengthening and expansion of ‚ÄĆNATO's ‚Ā†nuclear potential, which, in the event of a direct military conflict with Russia, could be used in a coordinated manner against our country," she said.
Kremlin spokesman ‚ÄčDmitry Peskov, speaking ‚Äčseparately to ‚Ā†reporters, said the French move vindicated Moscow's position that French and British nuclear ‚Äčweapons should be part of any future ‚Äčnegotiation on ‚Ā†the global nuclear balance.
Russia says it is open to such talks following the expiry last month of New START , ‚Ā†the ‚Äčlast bilateral treaty that limited ‚Äčthe numbers of Russian and U.S. strategic nuclear warheads and missiles.

item.title: Drone attacks Iranian Kurdish opposition group's HQ in Iraq's Kurdistan, security sources say
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A drone struck an arms ‚Äčdepot in an attack ‚ÄĆon the headquarters of an Iranian Kurdish opposition group in the ‚Äčtown of Dekala in ‚ÄčIraq's Kurdistan region on Wednesday, wounding ‚Ā†two fighters, security sources ‚Äčsaid.
Iran has long accused Iraq's ‚Äčautonomous northern Kurdish region of sheltering militant groups involved in attacks against ‚Äčthe Islamic Republic, with Iran's ‚ÄčRevolutionary Guards in turn repeatedly targeting ‚ÄĆtheir ‚Ā†bases.
Reuters reported this week, as the United States and Israel pounded Iranian targets, that Iranian Kurdish ‚Äčmilitias have ‚Äčconsulted ‚Ā†with the U.S. in recent days about whether, ‚Äčand how, to attack ‚ÄčIran's ‚Ā†security forces in the western part of the country.

item.title: At least 101 people missing after submarine attack on Iranian ship off Sri Lanka, sources say
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A submarine attack on an Iranian ship off the coast of Sri Lanka has left at least ‚Äč101 people missing, one dead and 78 injured, sources ‚ÄĆin Sri Lanka's navy and defence ministry told Reuters on Wednesday.
It was unclear who attacked the ship, which the sources said has now sunk.
A ‚ÄčSri Lankan navy spokesman said the report of 101 people ‚Äčbeing missing was not true, and that 32 people ‚Ā†injured in the incident had been rescued by Sri Lankan ‚Äčnavy and were under treatment in hospital.
Sri Lanka's foreign minister told ‚Äčparliament earlier in the day that the navy had responded to a distress call from the ship and launched rescue operations at 6:00 a.m. (1230 GMT).
"What ‚Äčwe know at this point is that 79 people were ‚Äčrescued and brought to the hospital and one of them was seriously injured. ‚ÄĆAnother ‚Ā†101 are believed to be missing and the vessel has sunk," a Sri Lankan navy source, who declined to be named as he was not authorised to speak to the media, said.
The ‚Äčsources said one ‚Äčof the people ‚Ā†brought to the hospital had died.
Foreign minister Vijitha Herath did not give further details in parliament ‚Äčbut said the military had rescued at least ‚Äč30 people ‚Ā†who were on board and said Sri Lanka would take appropriate action.
Local media reported the ship had signalled it was in distress ‚Ā†off ‚Äčthe coast of Galle in southern Sri ‚ÄčLanka, and that the injured had been admitted to a hospital in the city.

item.title: Uncertainty faces China Inc's projects in Iran after US‚ÄďIsraeli strikes
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - Weeks before U.S.‚ÄďIsraeli strikes in Iran, wide swathes of state-backed Chinese companies were eyeing projects in areas from energy and heavy industry to trade promotion, data showed, spotlighting ‚Äčthe risks the war presents for business in China.
One of Iran's closest allies and the biggest buyer of its oil, China has so far kept ‚ÄĆsilent about the business and trade impact of the attacks, though it condemned the U.S.-led operation as "unacceptable" and called for restraint.
Chinese government procurement and tender records on Iran show contracts issued in recent months, pointing to the vibrant commercial engagement of the two nations, though the total size of China's investment in the projects was not immediately clear.
But the inclusion of many state-backed firms suggests efforts by the world's second largest economy ‚Äčto boost ties with Iran could transmit the business impact of the deepening Middle East crisis beyond just the private sector .
Beijing has long backed U.S.-sanctioned Tehran in ‚Äčefforts to deepen its strategic and economic heft in the Middle East, signing a 25-year cooperation deal in 2021, though full details were never ‚Ā†disclosed.
China and Iran have drawn closer politically through participation in regional blocs, but economic cooperation has stagnated in recent years in the absence of big benefits to China in ‚Äčnon-oil trade, an adviser to the foreign ministry said.
"China viewed the Iran protests as a cautionary tale, due to Tehran's domestic economic mismanagement, governance failures and corruption," added the expert, who ‚Äčspoke on condition of anonymity.
None of the entities identified in the documents responded to requests for comment on the status of projects and whether recent events had affected operations.
The Chinese records show contracts, some issued or active in January and February, in areas such as steel fabrication, transmission-level grid equipment, hydropower commissioning, overland freight corridors and Tehran trade exhibitions.
Shanghai Baoye, a major engineering and ‚Äčconstruction contractor within state-owned China Metallurgical Group Corp, issued a tender last month for structural steel supplies for a project based in Iran, they showed.
It also awarded a subcontract worth ‚Äč7.7 million yuan ($1.1 million) to a local firm for project-related equipment.
Pinggao Electric, widely associated with China's State Grid network, published a procurement result last month that referred to an Iran project involving a ‚ÄĆmobile substation, ‚Ā†according to documents reviewed by Reuters.
Iran is also embedded in China's Belt and Road Initiative, a multi-trillion-dollar infrastructure scheme intended to stretch from East Asia to Europe.
In December, China Railway Container Transport, a state-owned subsidiary of China State Railway Group, published a shortlist of overseas service providers for westbound Central Asia routes, including Iran.
Filings reviewed by Reuters showed a two-way flow of Chinese equipment and engineering services into Iran, while Iranian raw materials and petrochemicals run back to Chinese industry.
Steelmaker Henan Fengbao Special Steel published notices in August 2025 for the purchase ‚Äčof iron ore pellets labelled as being ‚Äčof Iranian origin.
In petrochemicals, Jiangsu Sopo Group ‚Ā†issued procurement notices last year for polyethylene specified as "Iran Petrochemical LFI 2119," with delivery to its plant in Zhenjiang.
The same resin appeared in multiple inquiries, suggesting repeat sourcing.
Chinese provincial authorities were also actively organising market access to Iran.
Procurement records from the ‚Äčcommerce department of the eastern province of Zhejiang show contracts for organisational services tied to Tehran trade exhibitions across pharmaceuticals, electronic ‚Äčcomponents and automotive parts.
Zhejiang ‚Ā†is one of China's most export-oriented provinces, home to thousands of private manufacturers in machinery, electronics and automotive supply chains.
A February open tender sought contractors for an international exhibition of auto parts to be held in Iran later this year, with a bid deadline of March 2.
In northwestern Shaanxi and northeastern Heilongjiang, provincial commerce departments and state-linked oilfield equipment firms similarly announced participation in ‚Ā†Iran's international oil ‚Äčand gas exhibitions.
Still there may yet be a silver lining for China.
"The crisis will certainly damage all ‚Äčforeign direct investment into Iran, not just China's," said Michael Feller, chief strategist at consultancy Geopolitical Strategy.
"The advantage for China, however, is that should the war end, its firms will have the pick of reconstruction contracts or ‚Äča better risk appetite than Western firms if there is a US-backed regime put in charge."

item.title: Cyprus calls off alert after¬† suspect object spotted close to Lebanon
item.description:
NICOSIA, March 4 (Reuters) - Cyprus said a suspect object had been detected ‚Äčclose to Lebanese airspace on Wednesday, ‚ÄĆand a government source said two Greek F-16 fighter jets had been scrambled to intercept it.
The alert was ‚Äčcalled off more than an hour ‚Äčlater.
"The incident for which there was relevant ‚Ā†information has been investigated and is considered ‚Äčclosed. The competent authorities are monitoring the situation ‚Äčon an ongoing basis and all appropriate measures are being taken by the available means within the framework ‚Äčof established procedures," government spokesperson Konstantinos Letymbiotis ‚Äčsaid in a post on X.
No further details were immediately ‚ÄĆavailable.
Two ‚Ā†aircraft that were preparing to land at Cyprus' Larnaca airport were diverted while the jets were scrambled, the government source said.
The incident followed ‚Äča strike by ‚Äčan Iranian-made ‚Ā†drone on a British Royal Air Force base in Akrotiri in ‚ÄčCyprus on Monday as the U.S. ‚Äčand ‚Ā†Israeli air war against Iran continued. Two other drones were intercepted by Cyprus later that day.
Cypriot officials ‚Ā†said ‚Äčthey believed the drones on ‚ÄčMonday were fired by Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon.

item.title: Discussions with Board of Peace 'on hold' due to Iran war, Indonesia says
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 4 (Reuters) - Indonesia's foreign minister said talks on U.S. President Donald Trump's Gaza " Board of Peace ," of which the Southeast Asian nation is a key troop-contributing ‚Äčmember, were on hold due to the Middle East war.
The U.S. ‚ÄĆand Israeli air war against Iran has killed scores of civilians, thrown global air transport into chaos and sent oil prices surging after the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
"All BoP discussions are ‚Äčon hold as all attention has shifted to the situation in Iran," Minister ‚ÄčSugiono, who goes by one name, said late on Tuesday in ‚Ā†response to a question on calls for Indonesia to exit the peace board ‚Äčin the aftermath of the fresh conflict in the Middle East.
"We will also consult ‚Äčwith our friends and colleagues in the Gulf because they are also under attack," Sugiono told reporters after attending an event alongside President Prabowo Subianto.
Indonesia's participation on the board has drawn criticism from ‚Äčexperts and Muslim groups at home, who say it compromises the world‚Äôs largest ‚ÄčMuslim-majority nation‚Äôs longstanding support for the Palestinian cause.
Indonesia backs a two-state solution.
The Indonesian Ulema Council, a ‚ÄĆleading clerical ‚Ā†body, said on March 1 that Indonesia should leave the board, citing Trump's attack on Iran as rendering the initiative ineffective.
Meanwhile, Nahdlatul Ulama, Indonesia's largest Muslim organisation, said Jakarta should use its position to press Israel and the United States to halt ‚Äčthe violence.
Trump first ‚Äčproposed the board in ‚Ā†September when he unveiled a plan to end Israel‚Äôs war in Gaza, later expanding its remit to address other global conflicts ‚Äčtypically handled by the United Nations.
Sugiono also said Prabowo is ‚Äčwilling to ‚Ā†be a mediator in the Iran war in a bid "to cool down and de-escalate the situation in the region."
Indonesia is readying 1,000 troops for potential deployment in Gaza by early ‚Ā†April as ‚Äčpart of a proposed multinational peacekeeping force, its ‚Äčarmy said, as part of the UN-mandated International Stabilization Force. It has also been given the deputy commander ‚Äčrole of the force.

item.title: Saudi Aramco's Ras Tanura struck again by unknown projectile, four sources say
item.description:
RIYADH, March 4 (Reuters) - Saudi Aramco's Ras Tanura, which houses its largest domestic ‚Äčrefinery and a key crude export terminal, was struck again on ‚ÄĆWednesday by an unknown projectile, four sources said, two days after a source said a drone attack on the complex shut the refinery.
Initial indications show an attack ‚Äčon the Ras Tanura refinery was carried out by a drone ‚Äčand resulted in no damage, Saudi Arabia's defence ministry spokesman ‚Ā†said, according to the state news agency.
The agency also cited an energy ‚Äčministry source as saying that there was no disruption to supplies.
Saudi Arabia ‚Äčand other regional Gulf oil producers, such as the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Iraq, have been unable to move oil through the Strait of Hormuz since the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israel launched attacks on Iran on Friday.
Hundreds of ships have anchored ‚Äčon either side of the Strait as a precaution, and Iran has said it ‚ÄĆwould ‚Ā†fire on any vessel that attempted to transit the shipping choke point.
Ras Tanura sits on Saudi Arabia's eastern coast on the Gulf. Aramco is attempting to reroute some of its crude exports to the Red Sea to ‚Äčavoid the Strait, ‚Äčsources have said.
Aramco ‚Ā†did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment.
Saudi Arabia's heavily fortified energy facilities have been targeted ‚Äčpreviously, most notably in September 2019 when unprecedented drone ‚Äčand missile ‚Ā†attacks on the Abqaiq and Khurais plants temporarily knocked out more than half of the kingdom's crude production and roiled global markets.
Ras Tanura was attacked ‚Ā†by Yemen's ‚ÄčIran-aligned Houthis in 2021, in what Riyadh ‚Äčcalled a failed assault on global energy security.

item.title: Russia says US attacked Iran on false pretext, condemns call for Iranians to seize power
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 4 (Reuters) - Russia accused the United States on Wednesday of using an imaginary threat from ‚ÄčIran as a pretext to overthrow its constitutional order, and ‚ÄĆsaid Washington's calls for Iranians to seize power from their leaders were cynical and inhumane.
Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova said the ‚ÄčU.S. had used negotiations with Iran, which were ‚Äčtaking place as recently as last week, as ‚Ā†a cover to disguise a plan for regime change ‚Äčthere.
Russia has a strategic partnership treaty with Iran and ‚ÄčPresident Vladimir Putin has condemned what he called the cynical murder of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed on ‚Äčthe first day of the war launched by the U.S. ‚Äčand Israel on Saturday.
"There is no doubt that the imaginary, invented ‚ÄĆIranian ‚Ā†threat, repeatedly stated over many years, was merely a pretext for the implementation of a long-cherished plan to violently overthrow the constitutional order of a sovereign state... ‚Äčthat Washington and ‚ÄčTel Aviv dislike," ‚Ā†Zakharova told reporters.
Without mentioning U.S. President Donald Trump by name, Zakharova alluded to ‚Äčhis call for Iranians to take power ‚Äčfrom the ‚Ā†clerical leadership.
"It is even more cynical and inhumane to hear calls for the Iranians to seize power, as ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčWest says, when the West is ‚Äčliterally tearing ripping these hands from the Iranians," she said.

item.title: Are the US attacks on Iran legal?
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. military has joined Israel and attacked more than 1,000 targets in Iran and killed many of its top officials, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Below is a look at the legality of the U.S. attacks, which critics say exceed the president's authority and fails to comply with international law.
President Donald Trump has provided varying objectives and justifications. He has said he felt Iran was going to strike first and the attack was meant ‚ÄĆto eliminate imminent threats to the United States, its military bases overseas and allies, although he did not provide details and some claims were not backed by U.S. intelligence reports. Trump also said Iran could obtain a nuclear weapon within one month but he did not provide evidence and this contradicted his claims in June that the U.S. military had "obliterated" Iran's nuclear program.
The attacks on Iran ‚Äčare pushing the boundaries of Trump's constitutional authority, according to legal experts.
Under the U.S. Constitution, the president commands the armed forces and directs foreign relations but only ‚ÄčCongress has the power to declare war.
Presidents of both parties have conducted military strikes without congressional approval when it was in the ‚Ā†national interest but less intense in duration and scope than what would be considered a war -- a limit that Trump may be testing.
Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth ‚Äčhave both described the action as a war and Hegseth called it "the most lethal, most complex and most-precision aerial operation in history." Trump said it could last five weeks or more ‚Äčand cautioned that there will be more U.S. casualties.
Congress has provided authorization for large military operations, such as President George W. Bush's invasions of Afghanistan in 2001 and Iraq in 2003.
The War Powers Resolution (WPR) of 1973 acts as a check on presidential power.
Under the WPR, the president can only involve the military in an armed conflict when Congress has declared war or provided specific authority or ‚Äčin response to an attack on U.S. territory or its military. It requires the president to report regularly to Congress, which the administration started to do on Monday.
The ‚ÄčWPR also requires unauthorized military actions to be terminated within 60 days unless the deadline is extended.
It provides a procedure for Congress to withdraw the military from a conflict and members of both ‚ÄĆparties have ‚Ā†said they plan to put such legislation to a vote this week.
It's highly unlikely such a vote would garner a two-thirds majority needed to override a Trump veto but some lawmakers said it would put members on the record in an election year. Legal experts said popular opposition might be the main check on Trump's ability to continue the attacks.
Legal experts said many countries will consider the attacks unjustified under the United Nations Charter, which states that member states must refrain from using force or ‚Äčthe threat of force against other states. There ‚Äčare exceptions when force is authorized ‚Ā†by the U.N. Security Council or used in self-defense in response to armed attack, neither of which applies.
There is also the concept of pre-emptive self-defense, which would arguably allow the United States to attack Iran if it had proof of an imminent, overwhelming attack.
The ‚ÄčUnited States has a veto at the U.N. Security Council, shielding Washington.
Legal experts said violating international law still carries a cost and both ‚Äčthe United Kingdom and Spain have limited ‚Ā†the use of their bases in the attacks, citing the lack of justification for the conflict.
Legal experts said it is not clear cut.
Israel is reported to have carried out the actual strike that killed Khamenei, while the U.S. offered intelligence and operational support.
Republican President Ronald Reagan in 19intens81 signed Executive Order 12333 which prohibited anyone working for ‚Ā†the U.S. ‚Äčgovernment or acting on its behalf from engaging in assassination. It also barred participation in assassination by the ‚ÄčU.S. intelligence community.
However, the killing of a leader which might qualify as assassination in peacetime could be a legitimate act of war during an armed conflict, legal experts said.
In Khamenei's case, legality would partly depend on ‚Äčwhether the U.S. was at war when he was killed and whether he was considered a military leader.

item.title: Philippines apprehends Filipino nationals suspected of spying for China
item.description:
MANILA, March 4 (Reuters) - The Philippine government said on Wednesday it had apprehended some of its citizens on suspicion of spying for China in ‚ÄĆa "serious national security matter" that underscores the need for stricter laws against espionage and foreign interference.
Authorities did not provide details about the case, including how many people had been apprehended or whether charges had been filed.
"The operations of these individuals acting on the behest of Chinese intelligence have been addressed and terminated," the National Security Council ‚Äčsaid in a statement.
"Necessary actions have been taken against the individuals concerned, all Filipino nationals, who have all confessed their complicity in ‚Äčespionage activities and are cooperating with authorities," the statement added.
Relations between the Philippines and China have grown increasingly ‚Ā†fraught amid repeated confrontations in the South China Sea, with both sides also engaging in an intensifying war of words that has played out on social ‚Äčmedia.
China's foreign ministry and its embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
At least three ‚ÄčFilipino nationals were involved, according to two security sources, who declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the matter.
Reuters spoke with the three accused last month under an agreement with security sources on condition that their identities were not revealed.
One of the accused said he was initially approached by a Filipina acquaintance when he ‚Äčwas a junior staffer at the Department of National Defence with an offer to write opinion articles in return for pay.
That later expanded to ‚Äčproviding information related to the South China Sea and the defence ministry's bilateral engagement with Philippine allies including the United States, he said.
He did not immediately realise ‚ÄĆhe was ‚Ā†working for Chinese interests and only became suspicious later, but it was hard to stop as he needed the money, he said, adding that he did this work between 2023 and 2025.
Last year, Philippine authorities arrested at least a dozen Chinese nationals on suspicion of espionage , accusing them of illegally obtaining sensitive information on military camps and critical infrastructure that could undermine Manila's national security and defence.
China has dismissed the espionage allegations ‚Äčas fabricated and politically motivated, accusing ‚ÄčManila of "stigmatisation".
China also arrested three Filipinos ‚Ā†last year, accusing them of spying.
Philippine lawmakers from both the ruling party and the opposition are seeking to overhaul decades-old espionage laws that would expand their traditionally wartime focus to cover peacetime and cyber-enabled threats.
That would widen ‚Äčthe definition of spying to include data breaches and other tech-driven intrusions. Lawmakers are also pushing for ‚Äča new foreign interference ‚Ā†law to address covert influence by external actors.
"When passed into law, these measures would expand the investigative powers of government, allow the prevention and disruption of hostile networks, and protect our sensitive information and critical infrastructure," the council said.
"We assure the public that safeguards are in place and working, and our ‚Ā†security agencies ‚Äčremain proactive and vigilant, leading to the success of Philippine counter-intelligence operations," it added.
China has ‚Äčexpansive territorial claims in the South China Sea that overlap with the exclusive economic zones of Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam. In 2016, an international arbitral tribunal ruled ‚Äčthat China's claims have no basis under international law. Beijing does not recognise that ruling.

item.title: Thai poll body certifies election results, clearing path for new parliament
item.description: BANGKOK, March 4 (Reuters) - Thailand's election commission ‚Äčsaid on Wednesday it has certified 499 of ‚ÄĆ500 parliamentary seats won in last month's general election , clearing the way for a new house to ‚Äčconvene and a new government to be ‚Äčformed.

item.title: Iran crisis tests Trump standing with young men who helped power 2024 win
item.description:
MANCHESTER, New Hampshire, March 4 (Reuters) - When Michael Leary learned the United States had struck Iran , he questioned whether the move honored the "America First" pledge that earned his vote for President Donald Trump and feared it could pull ‚Äčthe country into another Middle East quagmire.
Yet the 19-year-old student said he welcomed news of the death of Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and was not ready to condemn Trump's decision, ‚ÄĆexpressing hope the joint operation with Israel would be swift and spare American lives.
"One of my things with Trump was it was going to be ‚ÄėAmerica First.‚Äô That was the rhetoric he was running on,‚ÄĚ said Leary, who cast his first presidential ballot for Trump in 2024.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not that I disagree with the war or the strikes ... We need to learn more and see what‚Äôs going to happen. But it felt like a step back from what he was saying.‚ÄĚ
That mix of ‚Äčapproval and unease ‚ÄĒ support for killing Khamenei while worrying that Trump's push for "regime change" could pull the U.S. into a prolonged conflict ‚ÄĒ was echoed by five other Trump voters on a ‚Äčstudent panel that Reuters interviewed this week at Saint Anselm College in New Hampshire.
Young male voters were one of the biggest surprises of the 2024 election, swinging toward ‚Ā†Trump after years of Democrats dominating the youth vote. But recent public opinion polls show that support slipping amid frustration over persistent inflation and hard-line immigration enforcement, tactics that some view as overly harsh.
Only one in ‚Äčfour Americans support the U.S. strikes, Reuters/Ipsos polling at the weekend found.
The student panel, while a small sample, offers an early snapshot of how some young men are processing the Iran strikes, suggesting Trump may have ‚Äča limited window to deliver clear gains for the United States and stabilize a conflict that has spread to Lebanon , rattled global markets and sent oil prices sharply higher.
A swift end to the Iran war could help Trump project an image as a decisive commander in chief, but a drawn-out conflict risks alienating the young men who helped power his 2024 resurgence.
John Fitzpatrick, a 20-year-old politics major, said he supported "decapitating" an Iranian regime he viewed as a longstanding threat to Americans and ‚Äčdismissed Iran‚Äôs retaliatory strikes as "scrambling for one last gasp of air."
"It would be nice to see regime change ‚ÄĒ not that we should have boots on the ground or be as deeply entrenched as we were ‚Äčin Iraq," said Fitzpatrick, who chairs the Saint Anselm College Republicans. "I think it‚Äôs overall positive."
Artemius Gehring, 20, agreed, saying Trump's objective was to bring closure to a longstanding conflict stretching back to the 1979 hostage crisis, when Iranian ‚ÄĆmilitants seized the ‚Ā†U.S. embassy in Tehran and held dozens of Americans for 444 days.
"I think what he‚Äôs trying to do is just end it,‚ÄĚ Gehring said. "It‚Äôs the right move.‚ÄĚ
LACK OF AN END-GAME A WORRY
Tyler Witzgall, a 20-year-old sophomore, said that while he supported the killing of Khamenei he was worried about the apparent lack of a concrete plan by the Trump administration to replace him, a vacuum he feared could fuel instability or even civil war.
"He's telling the people of Iran to rise up and take over the government, and that's easier said than done," Witzgall said. "Why are we taking these actions when there's no specific plan right now ‚Äčor none that we know of?"
Witzgall said the ‚ÄčIran strikes, along with the capture of Venezuela‚Äôs ‚Ā†Nicol√°s Maduro in January, reflected what he saw as an overemphasis on foreign policy. He said he voted for Trump to boost the economy and deliver on domestic priorities and would like to see him focus more of his attention there.
Trump‚Äôs promises to rein in inflation, boost growth and toughen immigration enforcement ‚Äčhelped attract young men to his campaign. Exit polling analyzed by the Pew Research Center shows he won 46% of men ages 18 to 29 ‚Äčin the 2024 election, compared ‚Ā†with 51% for the Democratic nominee, former Vice President Kamala Harris. That marks a big shift from 2020, when Trump lost young men to President Joe Biden by 14 points, 53% to 39%.
Yet recent polling shows those gains have evaporated. In February, some 33% of men aged 18-29 approved of Trump's performance in the White House, down from 43% in the same month of 2025, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling during those periods.
How the Iran crisis ‚Ā†is resolved could ‚Äčdetermine whether Trump's approval rating rises or falls, with potential consequences for Republicans in November‚Äôs midterms. A CNN poll of ‚Äč1,004 Americans found that voters ages 18 to 34 registered the strongest opposition to the strikes, with 71% saying they disapproved.
Leary said it was too soon to say whether the Iran attacks were the correct course of action.
"It could absolutely turn into the right ‚Äčmove, or we could stay in Iran for 30-plus years, spend a ton of money - money that could have been spent at home."

item.title: US lawmakers set to vote on war powers as Iran conflict widens
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Lawmakers in the U.S. Senate were set to begin voting on Wednesday on ‚ÄĆa bipartisan war powers resolution aiming to stop the military campaign against Iran and require that any hostilities against it be authorized by Congress.
The latest effort by Democrats and a few Republicans to rein in President Donald Trump's repeated troop ‚Äčdeployments, sponsors describe it as a bid to take back Congress' responsibility to declare war, ‚Äčas spelled out in the U.S. Constitution.
"I do think it's really important to put ‚Ā†every member of Congress on the record about this," Democratic Senator Tim Kaine of Virginia, a lead ‚Äčsponsor of the resolution, told a telephone press conference ahead of the afternoon's vote.
"If you don't have ‚Äčthe guts to vote 'Yes' or 'No' on a war vote, how dare you send our sons and daughters into war where they risk their lives?"
Trump's fellow Republicans hold slim majorities in both the Senate and House of Representatives, and have blocked previous ‚Äčefforts for resolutions seeking to curb his war powers.
Republicans accused Democrats of playing politics with national security ‚Äčand said Trump had ordered only limited operations, such as the capture of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro in January, not ‚ÄĆfull-scale wars.
The U.S.-Israel ‚Ā†war on Iran, which began five days earlier, is already more extensive, leading to damage in Iran, Israel and throughout the Middle East, and claiming its first U.S. casualties.
The House vote on the measure is expected on Thursday.
On Tuesday, Republican House Speaker Mike Johnson of Louisiana said he thought there were enough votes to ‚Äčdefeat the resolution, describing ‚Äčit as an attempt ‚Ā†to push something that could put U.S. troops in harm's way and inspire Iranian forces.
"Imagine a scenario where Congress would vote to tell the commander-in-chief that ‚Äčhe was no longer allowed to complete this mission. That would be a ‚Äčvery dangerous ‚Ā†thing," he told reporters.
His remarks followed a classified briefing on the Iran conflict from top administration officials.
Even if the resolution passes the Senate, it must also pass the House and garner two-thirds majorities in both chambers to survive ‚Ā†an ‚Äčexpected Trump veto.
However, Kaine said that if the Iran conflict continued, ‚Äčhe and the measure's other backers could try again.
"Sometimes people will see things that concern them, and they'll vote 'No,' but then ‚Äčlater as events continue to develop they may vote 'Yes,'" he said.

item.title: Britain needs to keep a cool head over Middle East, UK PM Starmer says
item.description:
