LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Britain ‚Äčneeds to keep a cool head ‚ÄĆover the conflict in the Middle East to address the public's concerns about escalation, ‚ÄčPrime Minister Keir Starmer said ‚Äčon Wednesday.
Starmer was speaking after U.S. President ‚Ā†Donald Trump heaped fresh criticism on his leadership ‚Äčover Britain's limited support for U.S. strikes ‚Äčon Iran.
"I know the whole country is worried about the potential for escalation," Starmer told ‚Äčparliament.
"We need to act, therefore, with ‚Äčclarity, with purpose and with a cool head."
He ‚ÄĆalso ‚Ā†said Britain had been liaising closely with the United States on pre-deploying military assets to the region.
"For a number ‚Äčof weeks ‚Äčnow, ‚Ā†we've been pre-deploying our capabilities to the region. In doing so, ‚Äčwe've been liaising very closely ‚Äčwith ‚Ā†the United States," he said.
Starmer said those assets included air defence and counter-drone ‚Ā†systems ‚Äčas well as F-35 ‚Äčjets.

item.title: Hezbollah's entry into Iran crisis deepens its isolation at home
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 4 (Reuters) - Hezbollah‚Äôs decision to enter the Middle East war in support of its patron Iran has opened a rift with its main political ally at home in Lebanon, leaving the group deeply ‚Äčisolated as the country lurches into another devastating conflict with Israel .
The move , which caught even some top Hezbollah officials off guard, has sharpened splits in Lebanon over Hezbollah's status as an armed group, ‚ÄĆa reality that for decades made it Lebanon's most powerful political player and a regional military force.
Hezbollah, founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, launched rockets and drones into neighbouring Israel on Monday to avenge the killing of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei .
The attack drew swift Israeli retaliation in which dozens of Lebanese were killed and tens of thousands displaced, just 15 months after a previous war in which Israel wrought devastation on Lebanon that deepened its already severe economic crisis.
The turmoil revived a perennial criticism of Hezbollah among ‚Äčsome Lebanese that the group, for decades better armed than Lebanon's army, acts as a "state-within-a-state" that repeatedly plunges the country into conflict by fighting with Israel.
The group's attack even strained ties with Parliament Speaker Nabih ‚ÄčBerri , a Shi'ite politician who has moved in political lockstep with the group for decades, four Lebanese sources familiar with the rift said.
Berri's view reflects wider frustration in ‚Ā†Hezbollah's core Shi'ite constituency over the attack, according to interviews with more than a dozen Shi'ite Lebanese, including Hezbollah loyalists.
Facing criticism in Lebanon that it had put Iran's interests first, senior Hezbollah politician Mahmoud Qmati ‚Äčon Tuesday told Lebanese broadcaster Al-Jadeed that the group's "political and military decisions are Lebanese."
But top Hezbollah political officials had not been briefed on the intent to attack, according to two Lebanese political figures close to the group and ‚Äča senior Lebanese security official, all of whom spoke on condition of anonymity.
They said the group's top decision-making bodies, the Shura Council and Jihad Council, had agreed on the move but had not told most of the party's political leaders, leaving them "in a state of confusion" on Monday.
Asked about internal divisions that left officials in the dark, Qmati said Hezbollah was operating with "the highest degree of unity."
The attack also cracked open a faultline between Hezbollah and Berri, who heads the Shi'ite Muslim Amal Movement and whose alliance ‚Äčwith Hezbollah had created a powerful duopoly over Shi'ite representation in Lebanon's multi-sectarian system.
Berri has long been a key interlocutor with Hezbollah, negotiating with other actors on its behalf, including to secure the 2024 ceasefire that ended ‚Äčthe last war with Israel.
In the week before Israeli and U.S. strikes on Iran, Berri had received "assurances" from Hezbollah that it did not aim to launch a war with Israel or retaliate against ‚ÄĆIsrael for its ‚Ā†attacks on Iran, said the two Lebanese political figures and two other Lebanese political sources familiar with Berri's position.
Reassured, he passed on the messages to top Lebanese officials including President Joseph Aoun.
After the group's attack, Berri was upset with Hezbollah and felt he had been "fooled," the four political figures said.
In a Lebanese cabinet session on Monday, Amal-affiliated ministers did not vote against a decree that outlawed Hezbollah's military activities. A fifth Lebanese political figure close to Hezbollah said Berri felt he could not confront widespread pressure to isolate the group.
"It's understandable because Berri was promised Hezbollah would not intervene. This certainly sets things up for a political confrontation between the government and Hezbollah," said Michael ‚ÄčYoung, at the Carnegie Middle East Center.
"Hezbollah is in ‚Äča very bad position and has been put ‚Ā†in a corner," he said.
Two Hezbollah-affiliated ministers in cabinet told Reuters they opposed the cabinet's decision.
"We objected to the cabinet's approach to the discussion and its demand to hold the resistance (Hezbollah) accountable. The resistance is not to be held accountable," said Lebanon's health minister Rakan Nassereddine.
Qmati, the Hezbollah official, said Hezbollah "did not let down our great ally, ‚ÄčSpeaker Berri... this will become clear in the coming days."
One of the politicians, who is close to both Berri and Hezbollah, said the rift between the ‚Äčtwo Shi'ite parties couldn't last.
"We have to repair this, but we can't even talk to top Hezbollah figures right now. They're unreachable," the politician said.
Even Hezbollah's constituents were stunned by the group's attack, a dozen of them told Reuters on condition of anonymity, as public criticism of the group remains a taboo for most of the Shi'ite community.
One person who has actively supported Hezbollah told Reuters that they had attended a rally organized by the group on Sunday evening to protest against ‚Ā†Khamenei's killing. They ‚Äčreturned home, convinced that was the most Hezbollah would do.
"We were frankly surprised when the Israelis said rockets had been launched from ‚ÄčLebanon and we kept waiting for Hezbollah's denial. But the real surprise came when Hezbollah claimed responsibility," the person said.
"Frankly, we are not in favour of starting a war with unforeseen consequences. Absolutely not. And whoever made this decision is wrong. Look at how scattered the people ‚Äčare in the streets, not knowing where to go. Does this community, which has made so much sacrifice, deserve this?"

item.title: Turkey says NATO defences destroyed missile from Iran headed toward its airspace
item.description:
ANKARA, March 4 (Reuters) - NATO air and missile defence systems stationed in the eastern Mediterranean ‚Äčdestroyed a ballistic missile fired from Iran as ‚ÄĆit headed into Turkish airspace after passing Syria and Iraq, the Turkish Defence Ministry said on Wednesday.
The incident marks the first time ‚Äčthat NATO member Turkey, Iran's neighbour to the ‚Äčnorthwest, has been drawn into the expanding conflict ‚Ā†between Iran and the U.S. and Israel that now ‚Äčinvolves several countries in the region.
"We warn all parties to ‚Äčrefrain from actions that would lead to further escalation of conflict in the region. In this context, we will continue to consult ‚Äčwith NATO and our other allies," the ministry said, ‚Äčadding there were no casualties or injuries.
"All necessary steps to defend our ‚ÄĆterritory ‚Ā†and airspace will be taken resolutely and without hesitation. We remind all parties that we reserve the right to respond to any hostile actions against our country," it ‚Äčadded.
Two other initial ‚Äčstatements by ‚Ā†senior Turkish officials about the incident did not mention NATO's Article 4, which states ‚Äčthat allies will "consult together whenever, in the opinion ‚Äčof ‚Ā†any of them, the territorial integrity, political independence or security" of a member is threatened.
Ankara said debris from the air ‚Ā†defence ‚Äčmissile that intercepted the ballistic missile ‚Äčfell in the Dortyol district of Hatay province in southeastern Turkey.

item.title: Italy likely to send military aid to Gulf states, sources say
item.description:
ROME, March 4 (Reuters) - Italy's government is likely to provide military aid, including an air defence ‚ÄĆsystem, to Gulf nations that have requested help with equipment in the face of Iranian air strikes, two sources said on Wednesday.
The sources, who asked not to be named, said that while a final decision had ‚Äčnot yet been made, Italy would probably send one of its SAMP/T batteries ‚Äčthat can intercept ballistic missiles. They would not say which country would ‚Ā†receive the system, or which battery Rome was considering redeploying.
Attacks by Israeli and U.S. ‚Äčforces against Iran have prompted Tehran to launch retaliatory strikes on ports, cities and oil facilities throughout ‚Äčthe Gulf, a vital energy-producing region. Italy is not particularly reliant on energy supplies from the Gulf, but its energy minister said on Wednesday the country could reactivate some coal-fired power plants if the conflict leads ‚Äčto an energy crisis.
On Monday, Defence Minister Guido Crosetto said Rome had received requests ‚Äčfor air defence and anti-drone systems from the Gulf, including the SAMP/T, a Franco-Italian battery.
A third source said ‚ÄĆItaly ‚Ā†could also provide anti-drone systems, which could be transferred quickly, although a technical assessment was still under way.
Italy is already supplying the SAMP/T to Ukraine to help its war effort against the Russian invasion, and the third source ruled out taking away from Kyiv's air defences to ‚Äčsupport the Gulf states.
When ‚Äčasked about the ‚Ā†possible supply of defence tools in response to the latest Middle East conflict, Cabinet Undersecretary Alfredo Mantovano told an event in Rome that ‚Äčthe government was "evaluating requests".
As the conflict widens to the Mediterranean, Britain, France ‚Äčand Greece said ‚Ā†on Tuesday that they would send air defence forces to Cyprus after drones struck the Royal Air Force Akrotiri base on the island.
Italy also hosts U.S. military bases on its territory.
Foreign Minister ‚Ā†Antonio Tajani ‚Äčsaid late on Tuesday that Rome had not yet ‚Äčreceived any requests to use them for operations against Iran, and would evaluate any requests if they were to ‚Äčarrive.

item.title: Iran intelligence operatives signalled openness to talks with CIA to end war, NYT reports
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Operatives from Iran's Ministry of Intelligence signalled openness to the U.S. Central ‚ÄčIntelligence Agency (CIA) to talks on ending ‚ÄĆthe war , the New York Times reported on Wednesday, citing officials briefed on the matter.
The ‚Äčoffer was made through an unnamed ‚Äčcountry's spy agency, the NYT said, ‚Ā†citing Middle Eastern officials and officials ‚Äčfrom a Western nation who spoke on ‚Äčcondition of anonymity.
The White House and the CIA did not immediately respond to a request for ‚Äčcomment.
Officials in Washington are sceptical as ‚Äčto whether Iran or the Trump administration is ‚ÄĆreally ‚Ā†ready for an "off-ramp", at least in the short term, the report added.
Iran's ambassador to the United Nations in Geneva on ‚ÄčTuesday ruled out ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†now any negotiations with the United States, days after the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israel launched joint strikes ‚Äčon ‚Ā†his country.
President Donald Trump said on Tuesday that Tehran wanted to talk but it ‚Ā†was ‚Äčtoo late, as the ‚ÄčUnited States continued its military operation against Iran.

item.title: Two men go on trial in London accused of spying for Hong Kong and China
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Two men went on trial in London on Wednesday accused by British ‚Äčprosecutors of carrying out hostile surveillance on well-known pro-democracy dissidents for Hong Kong and, ultimately, China.
Hong ‚ÄĆKong was under British rule for 156 years before reverting to Chinese sovereignty in 1997, and there have been growing tensions between the two nations over a sweeping national security crackdown since sometimes violent pro-democracy protests swept the territory in 2019.
Chung Biu Yuen, 65, ‚Äčand Chi Leung Wai, 38, both dual Chinese and British nationals, deny charges of assisting a foreign intelligence ‚Äčservice between December 2023 and May 2024 and conducting "foreign interference" by forcing entry into a ‚Ā†residential address on May 1.
Prosecutor Duncan Atkinson told London's Old Bailey court the two men had been tasked to ‚Äčcarry out "shadow policing operations on behalf of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region and thereby the People's Republic of China".
Atkinson said this included targeting dissidents now resident in Britain for whom the Hong Kong government had issued bounties of about 100,000 pounds ($133,710) for information leading to their whereabouts or capture.
"Messaging on Mr Yuen's phone between him and Wai indicated that surveillance ‚Äčon pro-democracy protester Nathan Law had been ongoing since 2021," Atkinson said. "The evidence includes messages regarding surveillance against Nathan ‚ÄčLaw and discussion around his associates badmouthing Hong Kong."
Yuen, a retired Hong Kong police officer, worked at Hong Kong's Economic and Trade ‚ÄĆOffice (HKETO) ‚Ā†in London. Wai, known as Peter Wai, worked as a UK Border Force officer and as a volunteer part-time officer for the City of London Police, Atkinson said.
The two men are also accused of gathering intelligence on behalf of HKETO about Monica Kwong who left Hong Kong in December 2023 after being accused of fraud by her employer Tina ‚ÄčZou, Atkinson said.
The defendants carried ‚Äčout surveillance on Kwong's ‚Ā†residence in Pontefract, northern England, and used deception and then force to attempt to get into her home, Atkinson said.
Wai is also accused of misusing his job with the ‚ÄčBorder Force to gain access to the interior ministry's computer system.
The Chinese Embassy in ‚ÄčLondon has accused ‚Ā†Britain of fabricating the charges against the men and said it had no right to interfere in Hong Kong's affairs.
In January, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer visited China and held talks with President Xi Jinping as part of an effort to ‚Ā†improve bilateral ‚Äčrelations, which have been strained in recent years amid mutual accusations ‚Äčof spying.
Starmer is seeking to strike a difficult balance between warning of the security threat Beijing poses while trying to boost economic ties. His government ‚Äčrecently approved China's plans to build in London its largest embassy in Europe.
($1 = 0.7479 pounds)

item.title: Heavy shelling, explosions spark fear along Pakistan-Afghanistan border
item.description:
LAL PUR, Afghanistan/PESHAWAR, Pakistan, March 4 (Reuters) - People living along Pakistan's border with Afghanistan ‚Äčsaid they were considering fleeing their homes because of heavy shelling and explosions as fighting between troops from ‚ÄĆboth sides entered a seventh day on Wednesday.
The South Asian allies-turned-foes have engaged in their worst fighting in years following Pakistani airstrikes on major Afghan cities last week, increasing volatility in a region also on edge over U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran .
Islamabad has said its airstrikes, which have at times directly ‚Äčtargeted the Taliban government, are aimed at ending Afghan support for militants carrying out attacks on Pakistan. The Taliban has ‚Äčdenied aiding militant groups.
Residents of towns and villages in Pakistan's ‚Ā†northwest said fighting between border forces starts in the evenings, placing their homes in the line of fire, often at sunset ‚Äčwhen families are breaking their fast in the holy month of Ramadan.
"There is complete silence in the day, but the moment we ‚Äčsit for iftar dinner, the two sides start shelling," Farid Khan Shinwari from Landi Kotal, a town near the Torkham border crossing, told Reuters.
"We open our fast in extremely difficult situations, as you never know when a shell can hit your house."
Residents in the town and nearby villages said there ‚Äčhad been heavy shelling and some explosions heard in the past few days, prompting many to flee their homes.
On the other side ‚Äčof the border, Afghans shared similar stories of skirmishes and families fleeing their homes.
Hundreds had been displaced to an open dirt field under makeshift ‚ÄĆtents, while ‚Ā†others had no shelter at all. Officials say around 1,500 families have fled their homes.
Fighting along the 2,600-km (1,615-mile) border has ebbed and flowed over the week-long conflict, with both sides saying they have inflicted heavy losses on the other country and gained ground in the fighting.
Reuters has been unable to verify these accounts.
Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan told Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz ‚ÄčSharif that Ankara would help reinstate ‚Äča ceasefire, the Turkish Presidency ‚Ā†said on Tuesday, as other countries that had offered to mediate have since been hit by the conflict in the Gulf .
On Wednesday, both countries reported exchanges of heavy fire, with Afghanistan's defence ministry ‚Äčsaying Taliban forces shot down a Pakistani drone and captured seven border posts.
A spokesperson for the ‚Äčministry said 110 ‚Ā†civilians, including 65 women and children, had been killed since the fighting began and another 123 were wounded. The United Nations mission for Afghanistan has listed 42 deaths so far.
Pakistan's Information Minister Attaullah Tarar disputed both figures, saying: "Pakistan exercises great care in only targeting terrorists and ‚Ā†support infrastructure. ‚ÄčNo civilian structures have been targeted."
On Saturday, Pakistan struck "ammunition and critical equipment" at ‚Äčthe Bagram air base north of Kabul, Tarar said, a key American command centre through the 20-year Afghan war.

item.title: Trump's Hormuz shipping plan is too little, too late in race to avert energy shock
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump‚Äôs current plan to revive shipping through the Strait of Hormuz via financial guarantees and security assistance will require a Herculean international effort.
Even if successful, it will likely offer only limited relief as time quickly runs out to avert the worst global economic fallout from the closure of this vital energy artery.
Trump said ‚Äčon Tuesday he had ordered the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation to provide political risk insurance and financial guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf. He also said the U.S. Navy ‚ÄĆcould begin escorting vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow shipping lane between Iran and Oman through which around a fifth of global oil and gas supplies normally passes.
The measures are Washington‚Äôs attempt to ease pressure on global energy markets after traffic through the Strait effectively ground to a halt on Saturday following the start of the joint U.S.-Israeli aerial bombardment of Iran.
Tehran retaliated by striking neighbouring countries, including energy infrastructure, forcing the shutdown of Qatar‚Äôs liquefied natural gas production and Saudi Arabia‚Äôs largest oil refinery .
At least four tankers were ‚Äčalso targeted in or near Hormuz, prompting many ship insurers and charterers to suspend transit in and out of the Gulf. The closure triggered a surge in oil and gas prices ‚Äď with Brent ‚Äčrising above $84 per barrel at one point, the highest level since July 2024 ‚Äď and sent stock markets tumbling, particularly in Asia, as investors braced for a severe economic ‚Ā†shock.
Yet the U.S. response is unlikely to reassure shippers under current conditions.
Tanker freight rates have soared in recent days, with costs on many routes hitting record highs. Chartering a crude carrier capable of carrying 2 million ‚Äčbarrels of oil from the Gulf to Asia now costs around $30 million - roughly 5% of the cargo‚Äôs value at current prices and nearly five times its level at the start of the year.
But reducing costs would ‚Äčdo little to address fears that vessels could still be attacked. U.S. naval escorts would certainly lower the risk, but they are unlikely to offer full protection against Iran's extensive use of drones, missiles and fast-attack boats.
This would not be the first time Washington has intervened to secure shipping lanes in the region.
During the "Tanker War" phase of the Iran‚ÄďIraq conflict in the late 1980s, the U.S. escorted and protected Kuwaiti oil tankers under Operation Earnest Will to deter Iranian attacks.
The scale of the challenge today, however, ‚Äčis much bigger.
Oil and gas exports from the region have nearly doubled since then to roughly 20 million bpd last year. Qatar, now the world‚Äôs second‚ÄĎlargest LNG producer, exported around 80 million metric tons last year - about ‚Äča fifth of global demand. In the 1980s, it wasn‚Äôt even a player in the global energy markets.
Securing such enormous volumes of oil, gas and tankers would be a formidable task and would almost certainly require assistance from other countries' ‚ÄĆnavies.
And, even more ‚Ā†importantly, organising such an effort would take days, if not weeks.
Time, however, is running out for both producers and consumers.
The Hormuz blockade is already forcing Gulf producers to curb output. Iraq cut production on Tuesday by more than 1.1 million bpd, roughly a quarter of its total output, due to a lack of storage capacity. Officials said output could fall by more than 3 million bpd within days if the disruption persists.
Other producers face similar constraints.
Saudi Arabia, the world‚Äôs largest crude exporter, shipped around 7 million bpd in February and is now diverting some output to the Red Sea port of Yanbu via a pipeline with a capacity of 5 million ‚Äčbpd. But Yanbu‚Äôs export capacity is limited to no ‚Äčmore than 2 million bpd, so the kingdom has ‚Ā†been forced to place large volumes into onshore storage.
And Saudi Arabia already holds about 82 million barrels of crude in onshore storage, around 56% of capacity, according to Kayrros data.
The United Arab Emirates, which exported about 3.3 million bpd last month, can divert up to 1.5 million bpd through a pipeline that bypasses Hormuz. But, again, ‚Äčthat means tapping storage, which is around 40% full with about 34 million barrels already being held, Kayrros said.
As a result, Saudi Arabia, the UAE and ‚ÄčKuwait could see storage fill ‚Ā†within days, forcing deeper production cuts.
Pressure is also intensifying on consumers.
Asian refiners heavily dependent on Middle Eastern oil are struggling to replace supplies and are likely to cut operating rates. Two Chinese refineries have already reduced runs, while India has curtailed gas supplies to its industrial base due to shortages.
The shock is rippling throughout Asian financial markets. South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI index fell 18% so far this week, partly on fears that the country‚Äôs vast petrochemical and manufacturing sectors - both highly ‚Ā†dependent on Middle ‚ÄčEastern energy - could be disrupted.
The key question remains how long the war will last. Trump has indicated it could drag on for ‚Äčseveral weeks, but the global energy system may not be able to wait that long ‚Äď even if his plans to reopen Hormuz prove successful.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ‚Äčdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Morning Bid: Seoul-sapping selloff
item.description:
March 4 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
The ongoing Middle East eruption shocked Asia's stock markets on Wednesday, with South Korea's Kospi plummeting 12% for its worst day ever and Japan's ‚ÄčNikkei and Taiwan's benchmark each losing about 4%.
The Korean won hit its weakest in 17 years, while Seoul and several other Asian ‚ÄĆcenters were forced to suspend trading periodically thanks to the scale of the selloff.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why gold lost some of its safe‚ÄĎhaven shine this week.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days ‚Äča week.
Asia's big manufacturing economies are heavily exposed to imported energy products from the Middle East, so both the price rise and ‚Äčsupply uncertainty caused by the conflict are alarming.
However, there were tentative signs that markets elsewhere are taking a breather, as ‚Ā†investors await further developments. Both U.S. and global Brent crude oil prices were up another 3% but held below Tuesday's 8-month and 19-month highs, respectively.
Europe's stocks ‚Äčpopped up about 0.5% in what looked like a pause after two days of heavy selling. U.S. stock futures were marginally higher too. And the dollar's rise flattened ‚Äčout for the most part, even though government bond yields continued to push higher.
Gold and precious metals, unexpected losers this week during the geopolitical shock, regained some poise on Wednesday as the dash for cash abated somewhat.
President Trump announced plans to provide shipping insurance and possible naval support for energy supplies to exit an effectively closed Gulf - and that may be helping ‚Äčat the margins, although these moves could take some time to have an impact. World markets asking when this energy blockage will abate are having to ‚Äčthink in weeks rather than days.
The conflict in Iran and across the region is impossible to predict. Many are now focusing on who's set to take over as Supreme Leader ‚ÄĆfollowing the ‚Ā†killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei last weekend. Some in markets took heart from a New York Times story that said Tehran officials had made a secret outreach , opens new tab to Washington over the weekend on how to end the conflict.
But for investors who think the coast will be clear once Gulf tensions ease, there are plenty of other things to worry about. For one, there‚Äôs rising concern about private credit funds run by the likes of Blackstone and BlackRock.
Today's calendar sees more routine issues ‚Äčback on the radar, with the ‚ÄčADP's private sector jobs report and ‚Ā†ISM's service sector survey both coming out. The former may be looked at more closely in light of the big U.S. payrolls report due on Friday.
Chart of the day
European gas prices have soared this week on the energy supply disruption ‚Äčin the Middle East and Qatar in particular, hitting their highest levels in three years - almost 20% higher ‚Äčthan they were this ‚Ā†time last year.
As the Iran conflict unfolds, Europe is emerging from the winter with gas storage levels running well below five-year averages. The EU on Wednesday told member states it does not see any immediate impact on the security of natural gas supply.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. February ADP employment figures (8:15 AM EST), February S&P Global/ISM ‚Ā†services PMIs (9:45-10:00 ‚ÄčAM EST)
* U.S. Federal Reserve issues latest Beige Book
* U.S. corporate earnings: Broadcom
Want to receive the ‚ÄčMorning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the ‚Äčauthor. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hormuz shutdown worsens after US hits Iranian warship; tankers stranded for fifth day
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S.‚ÄďIran war widened on Wednesday after a U.S. strike hit an Iranian warship off ‚ÄčSri Lanka, deepening a crisis that has paralysed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz for a fifth day and choked off ‚ÄĆvital Middle East oil and gas flows.
The U.S. submarine strike on the Iranian vessel came as U.S. President Donald Trump pledged to provide insurance and navy escorts to ships exporting oil and gas from the Middle East in a bid to contain soaring energy prices.
At least 200 ships, including oil and liquefied natural gas tankers as well as cargo ships, remained at anchor in ‚Äčopen waters off the coast of major Gulf producers including Iraq, Saudi Arabia and Qatar, according to Reuters estimates based on ship-tracking data from ‚Äčthe MarineTraffic platform.
Hundreds of other vessels remained outside Hormuz unable to reach ports, shipping data showed. The waterway is a ‚Ā†key artery for around a fifth of the world's oil and LNG supply.
The Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen Prestige was also damaged by a projectile as it ‚Äčsailed toward the northern end of the Strait of Hormuz earlier, prompting the crew to abandon ship, shipping sources said.
Qatar suspended its gas output and Iraq cut ‚Äčits oil production as both ran out of storage for gas and oil, unable to load it in tankers. Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Kuwait were also struggling to load oil but it was not yet clear if they cut output.
Despite the gridlock, a rare voyage took place on Tuesday when the Suezmax tanker Pola sailed through ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz to the UAE to load crude, according to industry sources and LSEG ship-tracking data.
The Pola had switched off its AIS transponder ‚Äčlate on March 2 as it approached the Strait and reappeared the next day off Abu Dhabi.
Trump on Tuesday said he had instructed the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation to ‚ÄĆprovide political-risk ‚Ā†insurance and financial guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf.
"No matter what, the United States will ensure the free flow of energy to the world," he wrote in a social media post.
Oil prices dipped on Wednesday, having risen for the fourth day and gaining 12% since the war started on Saturday, as U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran disrupted Middle East supplies. The pace of gains slowed from past sessions.
On Wednesday, Goldman Sachs raised its forecast for Brent crude oil in the second quarter by $10 ‚Äčto $76 per barrel. It also raised its ‚Äčprediction for WTI by $9 to $71 ‚Ā†per barrel.
The bank said it sees longer-than-expected disruption to exports of oil and gas through the Strait of Hormuz and damage to oil production facilities as risks. It also assumes that low oil flows through Hormuz will lead to large ‚Äčdeclines in OECD inventories and Middle East production in March.
"Providing protection for all tankers operating in areas currently threatened ‚Äčby Iran is unrealistic ‚Ā†as this would require a very high number of warships and other military assets," said Jakob Larsen, chief safety and security officer with shipping association BIMCO.
Some Asian refiners face output cuts because they are unable to get prompt replacement cargoes from their suppliers in the Gulf due to the shipping standstill, four traders ‚Ā†and three ‚Äčanalysts said.
Asia sources 60% of its oil from the Middle East, leaving the region vulnerable ‚Äčto the disruptions. Refiners in Indonesia and Japan are sourcing more oil from the U.S. to replace the shortfall. India will consider buying more from Russia, sources at two companies have said.
Saudi Aramco's ‚Äčlargest domestic refinery and key crude export terminal Ras Tanura was struck on Wednesday , four sources said.

item.title: Iran war breaks UN Charter, strike on school shocking, UN probe says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 4 (Reuters) - An independent United Nations probe investigating rights violations in Iran condemned ‚Äčon Wednesday attacks by Israel and the United States on Iran as well ‚ÄĆas Tehran's retaliatory strikes across the region, saying they violated the UN Charter.
The United Nations Charter bans the use of force against the territorial integrity or political independence ‚Äčof any state.
"These attacks, which were followed by Iran's retaliatory strikes ‚Äčacross the region, run counter to the UN Charter," the U.N. Independent ‚Ā†International Fact-Finding Mission on Iran said in a statement.
It also expressed deep ‚Äčshock over a strike that hit the Shajareh Tayyebeh girls school in Minab ‚Äčin southern Iran on Saturday, the first day of the U.S. and Israeli attacks.
Most of the victims appear to have been schoolgirls aged seven to 12, it said.
Earlier on ‚ÄčWednesday a separate U.N. panel of experts said more than 160 children were ‚Äčkilled, citing reports.
The U.N. fact-finding probe said the Iranian population was now caught between ‚ÄĆa large-scale ‚Ā†military campaign that may go on for weeks and a government in Tehran which has a long record of human rights abuses.
Tens of thousands of people were detained and face torture and the death penalty, the U.N. ‚Äčprobe said, following ‚Äča brutal crackdown on ‚Ā†protests that began on December 28, 2025, in response to the country's economic crisis.
It said protesters currently detained in ‚Äčprisons could be put at risk from any U.S.-Israeli strikes. ‚ÄčA ‚Ā†British couple jailed in Iran described on Tuesday explosions shaking Evin prison where they are being held and damage to their wing as the conflict intensifies.
The statement ‚Ā†said the ‚Äčkilling of dozens of Iranian officials - who ‚Äčhave included Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei - in the U.S.-Israeli airstrikes was not an acceptable means to ‚Äčdeliver justice under international law.

item.title: Swedish fighter jets patrol Iceland in show of force over Arctic
item.description:
KEFLAVIK, Iceland, March 4 (Reuters) - Swedish fighter jets are patrolling the skies around Iceland for the first time, as NATO allies ‚Äčtry to show both U.S. President Donald Trump and his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin that they are serious about protecting ‚ÄĆthe Arctic.
Sweden has sent six of its Saab Gripen fighter jets, the crown jewel of its defence industry, as part of NATO mission Arctic Sentry.
The mission is seeking to strengthen NATO's presence in the strategically important Arctic region as part of an effort to defuse severe tensions within the alliance prompted by Trump's push for the ‚ÄčU.S. to acquire Greenland.
Trump has forced NATO allies to sharply increase military spending, including in the Arctic. He has been ‚Äčparticularly unimpressed by Denmark's military presence on Greenland, which he mockingly described as "two dog sleds".
The ‚Ā†Swedish fighters are mainly on the lookout for Russian long-range bombers that can take off from air bases on the Kola Peninsula, ‚Äčwhich is home to Russia's Northern Fleet and also has nuclear missile silos.
"Iceland as well as all of the Arctic would be important (in case ‚Äčof a conflict) because that's the warning chain for attacks against the U.S. or Canada across the Arctic," said Major General Frode Arnfinn Kristoffersen, deputy chief of staff, operations, at NATO Joint Force Command Norfolk.
"Russia sees the Arctic as vital to becoming a great power and has also built up new Arctic bases ‚Äčand opened up former Soviet military sites, including deep-water ports and airfields," he said.
Russia has repeatedly dismissed claims it is seeking to take over ‚ÄčGreenland, or is a threat in the Arctic, saying they are false "horror stories" made up by Western leaders to justify their own behaviour.
Keflavik ‚ÄĆAir Base, ‚Ā†built during World War Two and up until 2006 an American base, is home to the NATO air policing missions that have been done periodically since 2008.
The cold and windy conditions are the same as those on Greenland and the rest of the Arctic.
"We have experiences from operating in this kind of harsh environment...so for us, it's quite natural to also be able to operate here in Iceland," said Major ‚ÄčGeneral Jonas Wikman, Sweden's Air Force ‚Äčchief.
The JAS 39 Gripen is ‚Ā†a fourth-generation light single-engine supersonic multi-role aircraft, meaning it can do air-to-ground, air-to-surface, and air-to-air operations.
"It's perfectly suitable for this weather. It's built in Sweden for the Swedish climate, and this is basically what we ‚Äčhave back in Sweden," said Lieutenant Colonel Robin Arvidsson, fighter pilot and squadron commander in the Swedish ‚ÄčAir Force.
It has ‚Ā†a low logistical footprint and can be refuelled and rearmed by one officer and four conscripts in as little as 10 minutes.
While NATO allies have rushed to increase the alliance's presence in the Arctic, some analysts and politicians have questioned the rationale behind the build-up, saying Russia does ‚Ā†not currently ‚Äčpose a threat to Greenland.
"This is very dangerous because it leads to an action-reaction ‚Äčcycle," said Pavel Devyatkin, senior associate at Washington-based think tank, The Arctic Institute.
"There's a very high chance of an accidental incident, of an escalation," he said. "These moves from ‚ÄčSweden and from NATO are basically appeasing President Trump," he said.

item.title: US says Iran firing fewer missiles, US strikes to expand inland
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran is firing fewer missiles than it did at ‚ÄĆthe start of the war on Saturday, the top U.S. general said on Wednesday, as he argued Iran's ‚Äčmilitary capabilities were greatly diminished as the ‚ÄčUnited States expands its strikes inside Iran.
"We will ‚Ā†now begin to expand inland striking progressively ‚Äčdeeper into Iranian territory, and creating additional freedom ‚Äčof maneuver for US forces," General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, told a news briefing at ‚Äčthe Pentagon.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äčthe United States was winning in its war against Iran, ‚ÄĆeven ‚Ā†as he lamented six U.S. troops killed so far in the conflict. He added the United States would outlast Iran.
Hegseth confirmed that the U.S. military ‚Äčsank an ‚ÄčIranian warship ‚Ā†off the coast of Sri Lanka, calling it the first sinking of ‚Äčan enemy ship by torpedo since World ‚ÄčWar ‚Ā†Two.
The U.S. sinking was first reported by Reuters.
"An American submarine sunk an Iranian warship that thought it was ‚Ā†safe ‚Äčin international waters. Instead, it ‚Äčwas sunk by a torpedo. Quiet death," Hegseth said.

item.title: Iran's Revolutionary Guards take wartime lead, ensuring harder line, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran‚Äôs Revolutionary Guards have tightened their grip on wartime decision‚ÄĎmaking despite the loss of top commanders, senior sources say, driving a hardline strategy that is propelling Tehran's drone‚ÄĎand‚ÄĎmissile campaign across the region.
Anticipating the decapitation of their leadership, the Guards had already delegated far down the ranks before Saturday's U.S.-Israeli attack , a resilience-building ‚Äčstrategy that could also risk miscalculation or a wider war with mid-ranking officers empowered to attack neighbouring states . On Wednesday, Iran fired on Turkey , a NATO nation.
Inside Iran, the Guards' central role at all levels of the ‚ÄĆsystem and draconian approach to security may also make it harder for protests to erupt , undermining any U.S. or Israeli hopes their attack will spur an uprising and regime change.
The choice of the next supreme leader, after Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's death on Saturday, could further cement their role, said Kasra Aarabi, head of research on the Guards at United Against Nuclear Iran, a U.S.-based policy organisation.
Khamenei's son Mojtaba , widely seen as a likely candidate, has very close ties with the Guards , exercising significant control over them and enjoying extensive support, including from more radical junior ranks.
"If the conflict suddenly stops and ‚Äčthe regime survives, we can be certain the Guards will have an even more important role," said Aarabi.
Reuters spoke to six Iranian and regional sources with close knowledge of the Guards for this ‚Äčarticle, with all confirming they had taken a far greater role in the hierarchy since the war began on Saturday and were now involved in every big decision.
A security official close to ‚Ā†the Guards said the Guards' new head, Ahmad Vahidi, was present in every high-ranking meeting and that its overriding objective was always the survival of Iran's Islamic revolutionary system and its goals.
Deputy defence minister and Guardsman Reza Talaeinik spelled out the ‚Äčelite force's efforts to build resilience in a television interview on Tuesday, saying each figure in the command structure had named successors stretching three ranks down ready to replace them.
"The role of each unit and section has been organised in such a way ‚Äčthat if any commander is killed, a successor immediately takes their place," he said.
Israeli strikes last year killed the Guards' overall head and the heads of their intelligence, aerospace and economic units. On Saturday an airstrike killed the latest Guards head, Mohammad Pakpour.
Decentralisation has been part of the Guards' doctrine in case of attack for nearly 20 years and was developed after watching the collapse of Iraqi forces during the U.S.-led invasion in 2003, Aarabi said.
"The whole idea was to decentralise so that if one particular province came under attack, it could defend itself and sustain the ‚Äčregime's authority and rule," he said.
Crucially, the plan was designed to ensure that the Guards could continue to act as both the main spearhead of Iran's military response to external attacks, and as the ‚Äčenforcers of internal security inside the Islamic Republic, he added.
The approach appears to be holding for now, though sustained attacks that continue to pick off both senior and more junior Guards commanders could eventually test the Guards' ability to maintain strategic coherence.
To be sure, the Guards ‚ÄĆare not an ‚Ā†entirely homogeneous unit, with their own factional rivalries, personal disputes and differences over the group's role. But one of the sources said they are more "united than ever when Iran is under attack".
There may also be signs, five days into the Israeli and U.S. strikes, of the command structure starting to degrade, said Aarabi, pointing to what he called increasingly wild attacks on civilian targets in Gulf monarchies.
How far that may also reflect a deliberate strategy to show the attack on Iran was a mistake with global implications is not certain.
Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said Iran's response to the attack had already been planned.
"These units are operating based on general instructions given to them in advance, rather than direct, real-time command from the current political leadership," he told Al Jazeera.
While ‚Äčthe Guards are now involved in almost every strategic decision ‚Äčtaken in Iran - even beyond the central role they ‚Ā†held before the war - they can also count on a surviving political leadership in which the three top men are former Guards members.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps was founded soon after Iran's 1979 revolution to defend the new republic against both internal and external foes, and as a counterweight to the regular armed forces.
Answering directly to the supreme leader, it has ‚Äčemerged as a state-within-a-state, combining military power, an intelligence network and economic might all focused on maintaining the survival of Iran's Islamic system of power.
That role was put to ‚Äčthe test when Iraq invaded months ‚Ā†after the revolution, unleashing an eight-year war of attrition that was a formative experience for many of the current generation of Iranian leaders.
Senior Iranian figures who served with the Guards in the war include the three non-clerics occupying the most critical positions in Iran since Khamenei's death.
President Masoud Pezeshkian was a battlefield surgeon, Parliament Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf fought on frontlines before heading the Guards' air force unit, while Ali Larijani, Khamenei's top adviser, was a staff officer behind the lines.
From the early 2000s, as the wartime generation began moving into ‚Ā†more leadership positions ‚Äčand as Iran's long confrontation with the West accelerated, the role of the Guards in the Iranian state also began to increase.
The Guards were put ‚Äčin charge of Iran's nuclear programme, a project Tehran has always maintained is for purely peaceful purposes, but which Western countries believe is cover for building an atomic bomb.
As sanctions imposed over the nuclear project bit, the Guards took a role in the economy, their construction arm Khatam al-Anbia winning major contracts ‚Äčincluding in the all-important energy sector.
The Guards increasingly also served as the conduit to Shi'ite proxies across the Middle East, while their volunteer paramilitary, the Basij, was used to crush internal unrest.

item.title: At least 80 killed in US strike on Iranian warship, Sri Lankan minister says
item.description: March 4 (Reuters) - At least ‚Äč80 were ‚ÄĆpeople killed ‚Äčin a ‚ÄčU.S. submarine strike ‚Ā†on ‚Äčan Iranian ‚Äčwarship in the ‚ÄčIndian ‚ÄčOcean, Sri Lanka's ‚ÄĆdeputy ‚Ā†foreign minister told local ‚Äčtelevision ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Wednesday.

item.title: US kills Iranian leader of Trump assassination plot, Pentagon says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄčU.S. military said on Wednesday that it ‚ÄĆkilled an Iranian official who headed a unit behind an alleged assassination plot against President Donald Trump but that ‚Äčthe target was not the initial focus ‚Äčof the war.
"The leader of the unit ‚Ā†who attempted to assassinate President Trump has been ‚Äčhunted down and killed. Iran tried to kill ‚ÄčPresident Trump and President Trump got the last laugh," Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told a news briefing.
"While that ‚Äčwas not the focus of the effort by ‚Äčany stretch of the imagination - in fact, never raised by ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†President or anybody else - I ensured, and others ensured, that those who were responsible for that were eventually part of the target list," ‚ÄčHegseth told reporters.
Hegseth ‚Äčdid not ‚Ā†name the individual but said the operation took place on Tuesday.
In ‚Äč2024, the U.S. Justice Department charged an Iranian ‚Äčman ‚Ā†in connection with an alleged plot ordered by the IRGC to assassinate Trump, then U.S. president-elect.
Tehran has ‚Ā†denied ‚Äčaccusations that it had targeted ‚ÄčTrump and other U.S. officials.

item.title: Who is Mojtaba Khamenei, frontrunner to be Iran's supreme leader?
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba has emerged as frontrunner to succeed his late father as Iran's supreme leader after years spent forging close ties with the elite Revolutionary Guards and building influence in the ‚Äčclerical establishment.
Mojtaba Khamenei, 56, has survived the U.S.-Israeli air war on Iran and is seen by Iran's establishment as a potential successor to his father, who was killed in ‚ÄĆan airstrike on Saturday, two Iranian sources said on Wednesday.
A powerful mid-ranking cleric, Mojtaba has opposed reformers seeking to engage with the West as it tries to curb Iran's nuclear programme, and has long greater freedoms.
His close ties with the Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) give him added leverage across Iran's political and security apparatus and he has built up influence behind the scenes as his father's "gatekeeper", sources familiar with the matter said.
"He has strong constituency and support within the IRGC, in ‚Äčparticular amongst the younger radical generations," said Kasra Aarabi, head of researching the IRGC at United Against Nuclear Iran, a U.S.-based policy organisation.
"So if Mojtaba is alive, there is a ‚Äčhigh chance that he will succeed (his father)," he said, describing Mojtaba as already operating as a "mini supreme leader".
The Assembly of ‚Ā†Experts that will select the new leader is "close to a conclusion" and will announce its decision soon, Assembly member Ayatollah Ahmad Khatami told state TV on Wednesday, without naming the candidates.
The supreme leader ‚Äčhas the final say on matters of state, including foreign policy and Iran's nuclear programme. Western powers want to prevent Tehran developing nuclear arms. Iran says its nuclear programme is for civilian purposes only.
If elected, Mojtaba ‚Äčwill face pressure from U.S. sanctions that have hammered the economy and could face opposition from Iranians who have shown they are ready to stage mass protests to press their demands for greater freedoms despite bloody crackdowns by the authorities.
Mojtaba was born in 1969 in the city of Mashhad and grew up as his father was helping lead the opposition to the Shah. As a young man, he served in the Iran-Iraq war.
Mojtaba studied under religious conservatives ‚Äčin the seminaries of Qom, Iran's center of Shi'ite theological learning, and has the clerical rank of Hojjatoleslam.
He has never held a formal position in the Islamic Republic's government, despite being widely seen ‚Äčas the gatekeeper to his father. He has appeared at loyalist rallies, but has rarely spoken in public.
His role has long been a source of controversy in Iran, with critics rejecting any hint of dynastic politics in ‚ÄĆa country that ‚Ā†overthrew a U.S.-backed monarch in 1979.
The U.S. Treasury Department imposed sanctions on Mojtaba in 2019, saying he represented the supreme leader in "an official capacity despite never being elected or appointed to a government position" aside from working in his father's office.
Its website said Khamenei had delegated some of his responsibilities to Mojtaba, whom it said had worked closely with the commander of the IRGC's Quds Force and the Basij, a religious militia affiliated with the Guards, "to advance his father‚Äôs destabilising regional ambitions and oppressive domestic objectives".
Mojtaba was a particular target for criticism by protesters during unrest over the death of a young ‚Äčwoman in police custody in 2022, after she ‚Äčwas arrested for allegedly breaching the Islamic Republic's ‚Ā†strict dress codes.
In 2024, a video was widely shared in which he announced the suspension of Islamic jurisprudence classes he was teaching at Qom, fuelling speculation about the reasons.
Mojtaba bears a strong resemblance to his father, and wears the black turban of a sayyed, indicating his family traces its lineage to ‚Äčthe Prophet Mohammad.
Critics say Mojtaba lacks the clerical credentials to be supreme leader - Hojjatoleslam is a notch below the rank of Ayatollah, the ‚Äčposition held by his father ‚Ā†and Ruhollah Khomeini, who founded the Islamic Republic.
But he has remained in the frame, particularly after another leading candidate for the role - the former President Ebrahim Raisi - died in a helicopter crash in 2024.
A U.S. diplomatic cable written in 2007 and published by WikiLeaks cited three Iranian sources describing Mojtaba as an avenue to reach Khamenei.
Mojtaba was widely believed to have been behind the sudden rise of hardliner Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, who was ‚Ā†elected president in ‚Äč2005.
Mojtaba backed Ahmadinejad in 2009 when he won a second term in a disputed election which resulted in anti-government protests ‚Äčthat were violently suppressed by the Basij and other security forces.
Mehdi Karroubi, a moderate cleric who ran in the election, wrote a letter to Khamenei at the time objecting to what he alleged was Mojtaba's role in supporting Ahmadinejad. Khamenei rejected the ‚Äčaccusation.
Mojtaba's wife, who was killed in Saturday's airstrikes, was the daughter of a prominent hardliner, the former parliament speaker Gholamali Haddadadel.

item.title: US says it is winning against Iran, can fight as long as needed
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense Secretary ‚ÄčPete Hegseth said ‚ÄĆon Wednesday the United States was ‚Äčwinning in ‚Äčthe war against Iran ‚Ā†and that ‚Äčthe U.S. military ‚Äčcould fight as long as needed.
"Our air ‚Äčdefenses and ‚Äčthat of our allies ‚ÄĆhave ‚Ā†plenty of runway. We can sustain this fight ‚Äčeasily ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†as long as we ‚Äčneed to," ‚ÄčHegseth ‚Ā†said.

item.title: Iran could disrupt the Strait of Hormuz with drones for months
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Iranian drone attacks could disrupt the Strait of Hormuz for months, but how long the Islamic Republic could sustain its missile barrage is less clear, according to ‚Äčintelligence sources and military analysts.
Since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on Saturday, Iran has launched hundreds of missiles and more than 1,000 drones at Gulf states allied with Washington. ‚ÄĆMost were intercepted by air defences, but some residential and commercial buildings, infrastructure and U.S. military bases have sustained damage.
Tehran is a major drone manufacturer and has the industrial capacity to produce around 10,000 per month, according to the Centre for Information Resilience, a non-profit research group funded by Britain's Foreign Office.
The size of its missile stockpile is unknown, with estimates ranging from 2,500 by Israel's military to around 6,000 according to other analysts. How much of Iran's arms ‚Äčcache remains could be a key factor in determining the course of the war.
Closing the Strait of Hormuz , a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which a fifth of the world‚Äôs ‚Äčcrude oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has been one of Iran's main objectives, and shipping through the crucial energy artery has ground to a near ‚Ā†halt after Iranian hits on six vessels.
Energy prices have jumped, with Brent crude rising 12% and a European natural gas benchmark up about 50% so far this week.
"Iran is not going to fold easily ‚Äčor quickly, they have the means to make it unsafe for commercial traffic to flow through Hormuz," said Bob McNally, president of Rapidan Energy Group.
"The U.S. is prioritising attacking Iran‚Äôs munitions, bases and facilities that ‚Äčthreaten the Strait. But all Iran has to do is show they can hit a few tankers and concern will take care of the rest, folks just won‚Äôt go through,‚ÄĚ McNally said.
Strategic missile supplies are a vulnerable point for Iran, according to a former director at Britain‚Äôs MI6 intelligence agency.
‚ÄúRussia is in no position to resupply and China will be quite cautious about this. If it became known that China was actually providing some sort of ‚Äčserious military hardware to Iran, that would play very badly with the GCC states,‚ÄĚ he said, referring to members of the Gulf Cooperation Council comprising Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman and the United ‚ÄčArab Emirates.
Missile stores may be lower because Tehran had been supplying Hezbollah in Lebanon and the Houthis in Yemen, a second Western intelligence source said.
Stocks were also reduced during a 12-day war with Israel in June, but they ‚ÄĆhave been partially ‚Ā†restored, according to Israeli military intelligence.
A key constraint may be launchers for the missiles. Supplies have at least halved over the last year due to strikes by Israel and the U.S. and further declined over the past five days, research by Britain‚Äôs CIR showed.
Despite this, Iran is likely to be able to sustain a fight with its drones , opens new tab . The country‚Äôs latest generation of Shahed-136 drones have a range of 700 to 1,000 kilometres (435 to 620 miles), sufficient to reach anywhere in the southern Gulf coast when launched from the Iranian mainland or vessels, according to Farzin Nadimi, a senior fellow at the Washington Institute.
Many are produced at ‚Äčdual-use plants and other facilities could be retooled to ‚Äčramp up production, a CIR analyst said.
These ‚Ā†drones were able to penetrate Gulf states‚Äô air defence systems, with 65 entering the UAE since the conflict broke out.
They struck Amazon‚Äôs data centres , Dubai International Airport and a Fairmont hotel . Bahrain has suffered material damage to infrastructure, a U.S. naval base and a tower containing a hotel and apartments from drones.
Oil traders are bracing for further price spikes as the length of the disruption to the Strait of Hormuz becomes clearer in the coming days.
‚ÄúI ‚Äčam very concerned, this risk ‚Ā†is currently underpriced in oil markets,‚ÄĚ said a senior executive at Vitol, a global commodity trading house. "The prevalent theory is that Iran is using old missiles and drones first to deplete air defences. If so, their response is yet to start properly."
And if missiles and drones start to run out, Iran could deploy sea mines. Tehran has an inventory of 5,000 to 6,000 such mines, according to Dryad Global, a maritime risk intelligence firm.
They ‚Ā†can be moored ‚Äčto the seabed, rocket-propelled or drift in waters, exploding when a vessel comes into contact. There is no indication that mines ‚Äčhave been laid in the Strait of Hormuz at this point, analysts said.
‚ÄúIf sea mines are laid, it will take a long time to deal with them," said Cormac McCarry, director at Control Risks with a focus on maritime intelligence and security services. "That's ‚Äčwhere we will be looking at months of destruction."

item.title: Mideast crisis highlights risk of dollar liquidity shock
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 4 (Reuters) - Investors have piled into dollars this week amid the turmoil in the Middle East , a reminder that the ongoing transition away from a dollar-centric financial universe toward a more fractured, multi-polar world could be very rocky.
The war now spreading across the region following the joint U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran on Saturday is lifting ‚Äčthe dollar's value, as investors seek the relative safety of the world's most liquid asset.
Equity indices that were major outperformers in the first two months of the year are ‚ÄĆtanking: South Korea's KOSPI (.KS11) , opens new tab ‚Äď which soared 50% through February ‚Äď has lost nearly 20% in two days. Redemptions from private credit funds are soaring, the dollar spiked as much as 2% in two days, and Treasury yields are shooting higher too.
Matt King, founder of Satori Insights, says this dollar surge isn't the result of a sudden rethink in the growth or inflation outlook. The issue is simply "money flow" - a rapid unwinding of the speculative froth that boosted many markets in recent ‚Äčmonths as investors now scramble for liquidity.
Despite all the fears about dollar debasement, investors in a foxhole still want - and need - dollars.
This raises the wider question of what ‚Äčwill happen in future crises if the long-term erosion of dollar dominance persists.
The dollar's (.DXY) , opens new tab decline as the undisputed leader in global trade, financing, and FX reserves has ‚Ā†been underway for the best part of a quarter century, since the advent of the euro in 1999 and China's accession to the World Trade Organization in 2001.
The U.S. currency's share of global FX ‚Äčreserves has fallen to 57% today from over 70% in the early 2000s, according to the International Monetary Fund.
But because the erosion has been smooth and gradual, dollar liquidity has continued to increase, and the ‚Äčglobal financial system has built buffers against liquidity squeezes after overcoming the historic shocks of 2008 and 2020.
However, the U.S. alliances, rules-based order, and forces of globalization that once ensured dollar liquidity greased the wheels of the world economy and markets, are crumbling. Major trade, political and military conflicts have erupted in the past year, turning the global investment landscape into extremely hazardous terrain.
This is the backdrop against which Barry Eichengreen, University of California, Berkeley professor and renowned expert on international capital flows ‚Äčand currencies, publishes his new book "Money Beyond Borders: Global Currencies from Croesus to Crypto" on March 17.
Eichengreen traces the 2,500-year history of money, explains why systemically important currencies rise to prominence and then fade ‚Äčaway, and offers his assessment of what the future role of the dollar and cryptocurrencies will be.
While he argues that there is still no rival to the dollar as the preeminent currency in FX reserves and international trade, financing, and ‚ÄĆinvoicing, he ‚Ā†fears that its decline on these fronts, which has so far been "glacial", could accelerate.
"I'm much more worried than I was in the past," Eichengreen says. "There isn't an obvious alternative to the greenback, and we have to continue to hope that the coming transition will be very gradual and smooth. But I think we're learning that we don't live in a world where things occur smoothly anymore."
The past few days have been anything but smooth, and they highlight how much the world still needs dollars.
The U.S. dollar is on one side of 89% of all FX transactions, according to the Bank for International ‚ÄčSettlements. That's the highest in 25 years. The next ‚Äčmost traded currency is the euro, which ‚Ā†is on one side of 29% of all FX transactions.
Additionally, the dollar's share of international payments is about 50%. If you include intra‚ÄĎeuro zone payments in the calculation, that share rises to around 60%, according to a Federal Reserve study. Some 55% of international and foreign currency bank claims, and 60% of liabilities are ‚Äčdenominated in dollars.
Looking at oil, it is estimated that as much as 20% of the world's crude trade is now priced in currencies other than ‚Äčthe dollar, such as the ‚Ā†euro or Chinese yuan. Of course, that means around 80% is still denominated in dollars.
Eichengreen says he has long believed that a multi-polar global monetary and financial system would be good for the world, just as a more diverse ecosystem is good for the planet.
"But we're not yet at a point where other sources of global liquidity could step up and substitute for the dollar. So we are at a very delicate point in ‚Ā†time," Eichengreen ‚Äčsays.
At a time when trade wars and real wars are flaring up, that seems something of an understatement.
(The opinions expressed here ‚Äčare those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ‚Äčbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Hormuz reopening is not solely in Trump‚Äôs gift
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Donald Trump is waking up to the fact that cutting off the world's largest oil and gas shipping route has dire consequences. That's clear from his scramble to restart flows through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčby offering insurance , opens new tab for tankers and dispatching U.S. Navy escorts. The idea evokes the late-1980s ‚ÄúTanker War‚ÄĚ, when Washington protected oil shipments during the Iran-Iraq ‚ÄĆWar. But this time a quick fix is unlikely.
Traders rely on the narrow channel at the end of the Arabian Gulf to transport a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas. Just days after an audacious attack on Iran that effectively halted trade flows, Trump rushed to reassure them of U.S. help. Firstly, he is offering to underwrite risks that private insurers no longer ‚Äčwant to bear via the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation (DFC). The entity is authorised to provide $1 billion in political risk insurance per entity, well ‚Äčabove the value of a typical new tanker costing up to $120 million, per Evercore analysts. It currently has a total ‚Ā†maximum liability of $205 billion.
In theory, such coverage could coax reluctant tanker owners back into the Gulf. In practice, the opposite looks likely. Freight rates for very large ‚Äčcrude carriers have nearly doubled since January, while many war-risk insurers are pulling back entirely. A government guarantee might cover financial losses, but it does little to counter ‚Äčthreats of drones, sea mines and Iranian-led forces. A subsidiary of China‚Äôs state-owned COSCO Shipping, hours after the DFC announcement, suspended , opens new tab all new booking services for relevant routes.
Operational snags loom as well. The DFC's insurance product may still require lengthy approvals, Evercore reckons. Tanker operators, meanwhile, must decide now whether it is worth the danger.
And even if financial cover materialised quickly, military protection ‚Äčmay be even trickier to pull off. During the 1980s Iran-Iraq war, the U.S. led escort operations but had a larger fleet and could present itself as ‚Äča semi-neutral guardian of shipping lanes. Today the U.S. Navy has fewer available assets, according to AXSMarine analysts, while Washington is a direct participant in the confrontation. That makes escorted ‚ÄĆtankers more ‚Ā†likely to become targets.
The strategic backdrop is even less forgiving. Attacks on export infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates have shown even backup routes are vulnerable. Saudi Aramco is exploring alternatives through the Red Sea, but that route itself faced threats and harassments from Iran-aligned Houthi militants and has been effectively closed since 2024. In fact, the risk is so high that container shipowners stayed away even after Trump brought the Houthis to a peace deal last May and they have only recently ‚Äčstated their intention to return.
If the chokepoint ‚Äčstays disrupted, oil prices will rocket ‚Ā†further. A full closure without any offsets could remove roughly 20 million barrels a day from global supply, Goldman Sachs analysts reckon. Apply Goldman‚Äôs rule of thumb that every million barrels per day of lost oil supply for a year adds $8 ‚Äčto oil prices, based on data since 1998. If the Hormuz disruption lasts three months, that formula suggests crude ‚Äčcould jump $40 per barrel. ‚Ā†In that scenario, $100 oil would become the new normal.
Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran crisis jumbles the Gulf state pecking order
item.description:
LONDON, March 2 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The six countries of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) have entered a new and disturbing world. Amid sustained Iranian rocket and drone attacks on most members of the group ‚Äď which includes Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman ‚Äčand the United Arab Emirates ‚Äď Western investors and tourists may now cool on a region that saw foreign direct investment more than quadruple to $83 ‚ÄĆbillion in the decade to 2024. At the very least, a reshuffling of the pecking order in Saudi's favour seems possible.
With UAE airspace closed at the weekend, Saudi probably looks immediately more appealing to expats and foreign financiers, who have flocked en masse to the Gulf for years. Some companies have been driving staff across the border , opens new tab to Riyadh, where flights out of the region have remained in ‚Äčoperation.
Global banks like JPMorgan, HSBC and Morgan Stanley arguably have financial reasons to move staff to the Saudi capital and away from the UAE, not least ‚Äčthe promise of more advisory mandates from state-linked corporate giants like the Public Investment Fund. But bankers still find Dubai more ‚Ā†livable, partly because alcohol is available. Even though local defences have downed most of the 689 drones thrown at the UAE, the shocking spectacle of the Fairmont Hotel in ‚ÄčDubai catching fire on Saturday may change attitudes.
The bigger strategic issue worrying Western corporations with a big presence in the Gulf, according to a person familiar with one firm's ‚Äčthinking, is what sort of Iran emerges next. A scenario referenced by U.S. President Donald Trump, in which Iranians rise up to overthrow the Islamic Republic and embrace democracy, is one possibility. But corporate leaders are well aware that an Iran that remains in the grip of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) could be an even greater threat than the status quo.
Thus far, GCC states have ‚Äčlargely restricted themselves to a joint statement , opens new tab on Sunday, which highlighted their right to defend themselves. But the news on Monday that QatarEnergy had been forced by drone attacks ‚Äčto halt production may now shift Gulf countries further towards a military response ‚Äď Qatar itself said later in the day it had shot down two Iranian aircraft. The risk of sustained conflict ‚ÄĆacross the ‚Ā†region might also further complicate the movement of fossil fuel shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries a fifth of the world's daily oil consumption.
It‚Äôs hard to disentangle where this would leave the relative economic health of Saudi and the UAE, who are comfortably the GCC‚Äôs biggest economies and who have of late been in a well-publicised spat . JPMorgan analysts on Monday cut estimates for 2026 UAE non-oil GDP growth by 0.5 percentage points, while only reducing Saudi‚Äôs by 0.3 percentage points. Though the Emirati economy is starting ‚Äčfrom a healthier fiscal position, Riyadh has more ‚Äčscope to move oil exports by ‚Ā†pipeline to avoid the disrupted Gulf. Equally, the energy sectors of both economies would benefit from higher prices .
The big picture, however, is that the UAE has outshone Saudi in attracting foreign money in the past few years, as reflected in an inflated property market. If ‚Äčit continues to suffer Iranian attacks, that trend may not continue.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Turkey has prepared plans for possible migrant flow from Iran, minister says
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 4 (Reuters) - Turkey has drawn up plans to deal with a potential inflow of people fleeing ‚Äčthe war in neighbouring Iran , Interior Minister Mustafa ‚ÄĆCiftci said on Wednesday, with preparations including possible buffer zones along the frontier and tent camps.
There was currently ‚Äčno unusual movement at the three border ‚Äčgates along the Iran-Turkey border, Ciftci added.
Speaking in ‚Ā†Ankara, Ciftci said three contingency plans had ‚Äčbeen drawn up by authorities: managing any potential migrant ‚Äčflow on the Iranian side of the border; creating buffer zones along the frontier if movement cannot be stopped; ‚Äčand letting people into Turkey under controlled conditions.
He ‚Äčadded that Turkey had prepared initial capacity to host up ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†90,000 people in the event of a sudden inflow, including tent camps and temporary accommodation sites.
On Monday, a Reuters witness saw hundreds of Iranians crossing ‚Äčthe border into ‚ÄčTurkey. Others ‚Ā†were reportedly waiting to cross. Ciftci said authorities had been informed that ‚ÄčIran was restricting its own citizens from ‚Äčleaving ‚Ā†the country, while allowing Turkish nationals and third-country citizens to exit.
According to data provided by the ‚Ā†minister, some ‚Äč5,010 people entered Turkey between ‚ÄčMarch 1 and 3, while 5,495 people exited.

item.title: New Zealand deploys two defence planes to the Middle East in preparation for evacuations
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - The New Zealand government said on Wednesday that it would send two Defence Force C-130J ‚ÄčHercules aircraft to the Middle East in case ‚ÄĆthey were needed for evacuating New Zealanders from the region.
New Zealanders are among the tens of thousands of travellers across ‚Äčthe Middle East, who have become stranded after escalating ‚Äčconflict between the U.S., Israel and Iran disrupted flights ‚Ā†worldwide and forced the closure of major airports including ‚ÄčDubai, the world's busiest international hub.
The government said there ‚Äčwere currently around 3000 New Zealanders registered as living in the Middle East.
‚ÄúThe events in the Middle East are fast-moving, dangerous ‚Äčand complex - and our primary focus is on ‚Äčassisting New Zealanders in the region to the extent possible in ‚ÄĆthis ‚Ā†very challenging security environment,‚ÄĚ Foreign Minister Winston Peters said in a statement.
He added that sending two planes along with consular staff was so "they can be ready when ‚Äčconditions allow to ‚Äčassist with ‚Ā†any civilian evacuation operations."
Peters added that with airspace closed and most commercial flights ‚Äčnot operating, the government continues to recommend ‚Äčthat New ‚Ā†Zealanders shelter in place - or take any safe and practical opportunities that are available to leave the region.
The ‚Ā†statement ‚Äčsaid that decisions on where the ‚Äčconsular response team and the two Hercules would be deployed were still ‚Äčbeing made.

item.title: Milei appoints chief Buenos Aires prosecutor as new justice minister
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, March 4 (Reuters) - Argentine President Javier Milei said on Wednesday he has appointed chief ‚ÄčBuenos Aires prosecutor Juan Bautista Mahiques ‚ÄĆas the country's new justice minister.
Mahiques, who has been the attorney general of the Argentine capital since ‚ÄčOctober 2019, will replace Mariano Cuneo ‚ÄčLibarona, who had been justice minister ‚Ā†since December 2023, Milei announced in a posting on X.
Local ‚Äčnewspaper La Nacion reported that Libarona had ‚Äčcited personal reasons for his departure.
"I assume this role with the conviction that without legal certainty there ‚Äčis no investment, without stable rules ‚Äčthere is no development, and without independent judges there ‚ÄĆis ‚Ā†no republic," Mahiques said in a separate post on X.
In a speech before Congress last Sunday, Milei announced that this year he will ‚Äčseek to ‚Äčpass more ‚Ā†reforms to the penal code to introduce harsher sentences for criminals.
The government ‚Äčwon several legislative victories last ‚Äčweek ‚Ā†when Congress approved a controversial labor reform and a change to the penal code that lowers the ‚Ā†age ‚Äčof criminal responsibility from ‚Äč16 to 14.

item.title: China to send envoy to Middle East for mediation, foreign minister says
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - China will send a special envoy to the ‚ÄčMiddle East for mediation, Foreign ‚ÄĆMinister Wang Yi told his Saudi Arabian and UAE counterparts on ‚ÄčWednesday according to statements ‚Äčfrom his ministry, as conflict ‚Ā†in the region continued to ‚Äčescalate.
China appreciates Saudi Arabia's restraint ‚Äčand insistence on resolving differences through peaceful means, Wang was quoted as ‚Äčsaying in a phone call ‚Äčwith the Saudi minister by the Chinese ‚ÄĆministry.
In ‚Ā†a separate phone call with the foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates, Wang ‚Äčsaid the "red ‚Äčline" ‚Ā†of protecting civilians in conflicts must not be ‚Äčcrossed, and that non-military ‚Äčtargets, ‚Ā†including those involving energy, should not be attacked. He also ‚Ā†called ‚Äčfor protecting the ‚Äčsafety of shipping routes.

item.title: Exclusive: US carried out strike on Iranian warship off Sri Lanka coast, officials say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. military carried out a strike ‚Äčthat sank an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka's coast and triggered ‚ÄĆa rescue for dozens of Iranian sailors by Sri Lanka's navy, three U.S. officials told Reuters on Wednesday.
One of the officials, ‚Äčwho spoke on condition of anonymity due to the ‚Äčsensitivity of the issue, said the strike was carried ‚Ā†out by a U.S. military submarine.
The U.S. strike ‚Äčtriggered a distress call and the Sri Lankan armed forces ‚Äčlaunched a search and rescue operation. Sri Lankan authorities said they had rescued 32 people so far and recovered several bodies from the ‚Äčsea.
Sources in Sri Lanka's navy and defence ministry said ‚Äčthe vessel had been attacked by a submarine and at least ‚ÄĆ101 ‚Ā†people were missing in the incident that took place off Sri Lanka's Indian Ocean coast.
The defence sources said it was unclear who attacked the ship.
A Sri Lankan navy ‚Äčspokesman said the ‚Äčreport of ‚Ā†101 people being missing was not true and rejected any reports on the cause ‚Äčof the ship sinking.
U.S. strikes on Iran's ‚ÄčNavy is ‚Ā†a central part of the war against Iran, launch by the United States on Saturday.
On Tuesday, the U.S. military's ‚Ā†Central ‚ÄčCommand said it had already sunk ‚Äč17 Iranian warships and was on track to destroy Iran's entire navy.

item.title: Malta-flagged container ship hit by projectile in Hormuz, vessel abandoned, sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - The Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen Prestige was damaged ‚Äčby a projectile as it ‚ÄĆsailed towards the top of the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, prompting ‚Äčits crew to abandon ‚Äčship, shipping sources said.
The vessel was ‚Ā†hit some two nautical miles ‚Äčnorth of Oman while transiting ‚Äčeastbound in the Strait of Hormuz at 1109 GMT, British maritime risk management ‚Äčcompany Vanguard said.
"The vessel was ‚Äčstruck by an unknown projectile just above ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†waterline, resulting in a fire in the engine room. No environmental impact has been reported ‚Äčat this ‚Äčtime," Vanguard ‚Ā†said.
British navy agency UKMTO reported it had received ‚Äča report involving a ‚Äčunnamed ‚Ā†container ship being hit by an unknown projectile and the crew ‚Ā†abandoned ‚Äčthe vessel with ‚Äčno reported injuries.

item.title: Top Vatican cardinal calls US-Israeli strikes 'truly alarming'
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 4 (Reuters) - The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the ongoing U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran undermine international law and ‚Äčsaid nations do not have a right to launch "preventive wars", ‚ÄĆin an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised as having a right to 'preventive war' ‚Ä¶ the entire world could risk going ‚Äčup in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's Secretary of ‚ÄčState, said in an interview with Vatican News.
Asked about ‚Ā†the U.S.-Israeli strikes, which have pressed on for the fifth day, ‚ÄčParolin said they had caused a "weakening of international law (that) is truly alarming".
"The ‚Äčrule of force has replaced the force of law, with the conviction that peace can arise only after the enemy has been annihilated," the cardinal told ‚Äčthe Vatican's media outlet.
It is unusual for Vatican diplomats to openly ‚Äčcritique specific military campaigns. Vatican officials usually prefer to avoid press coverage and operate ‚ÄĆbehind ‚Ā†the scenes, leaving open the possibility of the Church serving as mediator in conflicts.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said the strikes against Iran were needed to prevent the country from developing a nuclear weapon, ‚Äčwhich Tehran denies ‚Äčseeking, and to ‚Ā†thwart its long-range ballistic missile program.
Parolin, the Vatican's lead diplomat since 2013 and a front-runner in the ‚Äč2025 conclave of cardinals that eventually elected Pope Leo, ‚Äčis known ‚Ā†as someone who is usually very cautious with words.
Leo did not address the ongoing conflict in his weekly audience on Wednesday with pilgrims ‚Ā†in St. ‚ÄčPeter's Square.
The pope made an impassioned ‚Äčappeal on Sunday for an end to the conflict. He called for a stop what ‚Äčhe termed a "spiral of violence".

item.title: Chinese Communist Party elder Song Ping dies aged 109
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 - Chinese Communist Party elder and the longest-lived member of ‚Äčthe elite Politburo Standing Committee, ‚ÄĆSong Ping, died on Wednesday at the age of 109, the official Xinhua ‚Äčnews agency reported.
