Song died in ‚ÄčBeijing due to illness, according ‚Ā†to Xinhua.
Born in 1917, Song was ‚Äčat the core of the ‚Äčcommunist party's second generation of leadership, an era when reform-minded Deng Xiaoping remade China ‚Äčafter the 27-year rule of ‚ÄčMao Zedong.
Song was credited with bringing Hu Jintao, ‚ÄĆChina's ‚Ā†leader before current President Xi Jinping, to the attention of Deng.
Song was in the Politburo Standing Committee, ‚Äčthe ‚Äčapex of ‚Ā†power in China's political system, from 1989-1992.
Earlier, he ‚Äčhad worked in key party ‚Äčand ‚Ā†government posts.
He had worked as a personal secretary of Zhou Enlai, who ‚Ā†served ‚Äčas communist China's ‚Äčfirst premier from 1949 until his death in ‚Äč1976.

item.title: Hungary foreign minister hopes Russia will free two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war during talks
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 4 (Reuters) - Hungary's Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto said on Wednesday that he hopes two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war will be freed ‚Äčby Russia during his talks in Moscow, where he is scheduled to meet Russian ‚ÄĆPresident Vladimir Putin later.
Szijjarto travelled to Moscow a day after Hungary's Prime Minister Viktor Orban held a phone call with Putin where they discussed the situation in the ‚ÄčMiddle East, Ukraine and the availability of crude oil and natural ‚Äčgas for Hungary.
Orban's government has made Russia's war on ‚Ā†Ukraine a key topic in his campaign for the April 12 parliamentary election, ‚Äčadding to tensions between Budapest and Kyiv.
Szijjarto said in a broadcast ‚Äčon his Facebook page from Moscow that two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war have recently asked Hungary for help.
"I hope that after our talks more people will fly ‚Äčhome on the plane than who came in this direction," Szijjarto said.
‚ÄčUkraine is home to around 150,000 ethnic Hungarians, most of them in the Transcarpathia ‚ÄĆregion. ‚Ā†Orban's government and Kyiv have long clashed over the community's language rights.
Orban's government has also accused Kyiv of conscripting ethnic Hungarians, who Budapest said should not have been called up. Last Friday the foreign minister summoned Kyiv's ‚Äčambassador to Budapest ‚Äčto protest the ‚Ā†conscription of two men.
Hungary's Orban has maintained warm relations with Moscow even after the start of the war ‚Äčin Ukraine, and is not willing to give up ‚Äčits purchases ‚Ā†of Russian oil and gas, which has led to tensions with the European Union. Last month Hungary said that it will block the European Union's next ‚Ā†package of ‚Äčsanctions against Russia as well as a ‚Äč90 billion euro ($105 billion) EU loan for Ukraine to fund its defence against Russia until ‚Äčshipments on the Druzhba pipeline resume.

item.title: US extends flight restrictions to Haiti capital until early September
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration said on ‚ÄčThursday it was extending its prohibition on U.S. ‚ÄĆflights landing in Haitian capital Port-au-Prince through September 3, citing risks from armed groups to civil aviation.
In November 2024, the FAA halted ‚Äčall flights to Haiti after three U.S. ‚Äčcommercial jetliners were struck by gunfire. It later ‚Ā†allowed flights to resume to six other airports in ‚Äčnorthern Haiti, but kept the ban on Port-au-Prince.
That restriction ‚Äčhad been set to expire on Saturday, but the FAA said on Thursday it was extending the bar in response to ‚Äčsecurity forces' continued inability to prevent attacks against aircraft ‚Äčin Port-au-Prince and surrounding regions.
U.S. planes can transit over Port-au-Prince above ‚ÄĆ10,000 ‚Ā†feet (3,048 m).
Armed gangs control much of the Haitian capital amid a political limbo, with no president in place or elections scheduled and a U.N.-backed expanded security force pending .
Since ‚ÄčSeptember 2025, ‚ÄčHaitian groups have ‚Ā†used small-arms fire to attack at least three aircraft in the area where ‚ÄčU.S. civil aviation is prohibited, according to ‚Äčthe FAA.
The ‚Ā†FAA said an "expanded and shifted gang operating area raises the risk from small arms fire to civil aviation ‚Ā†operations ‚Äčat lower altitudes."
The agency continues to ‚Äčallow flights to six airports in Haiti: Port-de-Paix, Cap-Haitien, Pignon, Jeremie, ‚ÄčAntoine-Simon and Jacmel.

item.title: Russian LNG tanker sinks in Mediterranean, Moscow blames Ukrainian drones
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 4 (Reuters) - A Russian tanker carrying liquefied natural gas (LNG) sank in ‚ÄĆthe Mediterranean on Wednesday after what Moscow described as an attack by Ukrainian drones launched from Libya.
The Arctic Metagaz sank in waters between Libya and Malta after catching fire a ‚Äčday earlier, Libya's maritime rescue agency said in an advisory seen ‚Äčby Reuters.
"Information indicates that the tanker experienced sudden explosions followed ‚Ā†by a massive fire, which ultimately led to its complete sinking," the ‚Äčagency said.
Ukraine has frequently targeted Russian oil refineries and other energy infrastructure in ‚Äčan attempt to deprive Russia's war machine of funding, but if confirmed, this would be its first attack on an LNG tanker.
The Security Service of Ukraine did not respond ‚Äčto a Reuters request for comment.
Russia's transport ministry said the tanker, which ‚Äčhad been carrying LNG from the Arctic port of Murmansk, was attacked by Ukrainian naval drones ‚ÄĆlaunched ‚Ā†from the coast of Libya.
It said the 30 crew members, all Russian nationals, were safe, and thanked Maltese rescue services.
"We qualify what happened as an act of international terrorism and maritime piracy, a gross violation of the fundamental ‚Äčnorms of international maritime ‚Äčlaw," the ‚Ā†ministry said.
Libya's National Oil Corporation said it had no involvement with the tanker, adding that it was en route ‚Äčto Port Said, Egypt.
The ship's Russia-based manager, LLC SMP Techmanagement, ‚Äčand Russian ‚Ā†LNG producer Novatek (NVTK.MM) , opens new tab have not commented on the incident.
The Libyan agency warned vessels to avoid the site and asked them to report any pollution in the ‚Ā†area ‚Äčwhich one industry source said would likely be ‚Äčlimited to the fuel oil on board.

item.title: 'Everyone cheered': stranded Gulf travellers gamble on their route home
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Stranded passengers in the Gulf states are waiting for one thing: a phone call confirming their flight home will depart.
Even then, few celebrate until the plane has taken off and cleared Emirati airspace.
"There was just ‚Äčthis eerie feeling on the plane. Everyone was just dead quiet. No one really spoke," said Zoe Jackson, ‚ÄĆwho was on one of the first flights out of Dubai on Tuesday.
She said it was not until lunch that passengers finally began to relax, sensing the ordeal might be over.
Now safely home in Britain, Jackson said she received confirmation she could fly only hours before departure, when her hotel rang at 1 ‚Äča.m. to say she had to leave "now" if she wanted a seat.
Airports across the Gulf have begun operating at very ‚Äčreduced capacity as airlines and governments hurry to bring tens of thousands of stranded citizens and residents home amid the escalating U.S.-Israeli conflict ‚Ā†with Iran .
Dubai, which normally handles more than 1,000 flights a day , and nearby Doha and Abu Dhabi sit at the crossroads of east‚ÄĎwest ‚Äčair travel, funnelling long-haul traffic between Europe and Asia through tightly scheduled connecting flights.
Many of the stranded passengers had expected to spend no ‚Äčmore than a few hours on the ground and have been marooned - often without luggage - since Saturday.
Dozens of repatriation flights were due to leave Dubai on Wednesday.
Securing a seat remains uncertain.
In hotels, strangers swap updates on whether flights have been booked and the odds they will actually depart. In Facebook groups, travellers ‚Äčdebate whether to attempt overland journeys rather than risk further cancellations at major hubs.
"The biggest decision is whether we stay put or try ‚Äčto cross a land border," said Deirdre Amola, an American travel blogger stranded in Dubai. "Then it's: where should I even try to fly?"
James Gaskin was ‚ÄĆflying home ‚Ā†to Britain from India on Saturday when he got stranded in Dubai. By Wednesday, he had got as far as Istanbul, with one final leg still to Manchester.
His journey has been far from smooth.
At Mumbai, on little sleep, Gaskin found he did not have an onward flight and had to corral two travel agents and use his corporate credit card to get a seat home.
Then, after finally boarding a ‚Äčconnecting flight in Dubai, it was ‚Äčdelayed for more than two ‚Ā†hours. When the plane finally began taxiing, it was turned around and delayed again.
"When we got out of UAE airspace, everyone kind of cheered," Gaskin said, adding he felt guilty when so many people ‚Äčwere still unable to get out.
Those stranded include Grzegorz Markiewicz ‚Äčand his wife Malgorzata, ‚Ā†a Polish couple, and one of their three daughters who got stuck in Doha, Qatar, on their way back from a wedding in Australia.
They have received no update on when their flight might depart.
Now they are considering a more than six-hour drive across the desert to Riyadh, Saudi ‚Ā†Arabia, where ‚Äčsome fellow hotel guests have already headed in hopes of flying out.
"We are ‚Äčwaiting to hear what they're going to say about the road, about safety on the road," Malgorzata said. "And then we will decide."

item.title: Hardline cleric Arafi joins wartime leadership as Iran juggles conflict, succession
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Ayatollah Alireza Arafi has emerged as a central figure in Iran‚Äôs power structure at a moment of unprecedented ‚Äčturmoil , propelled into a leadership role from the ranks of top clerics after the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei .
Once known largely as a scholar‚ÄĎadministrator, ‚ÄĆthe hardline cleric is now one of three senior officials entrusted with steering the state through its most volatile period since the 1979 revolution.
Arafi was appointed to an interim three-member leadership council to manage state affairs alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian and Chief Justice Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei, following Khamenei's killing at the start of U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran on February 28.
The leadership's top priority, alongside responding to U.S.-Israeli attacks, is managing the leadership succession , a ‚Äčprocess subject to extraordinary pressure at a time of war : If a supreme leader is not named quickly, the clerical establishment risks being seen as weak and unable ‚Äčto guarantee continuity.
Arafi, in his late 60s, is lesser known abroad than other heavyweight clerics, but successive promotions by the fiercely anti-Western Khamenei ‚Ā†have shaped his career and given him moments in the spotlight. Some clerics have even viewed Arafi as a potential successor to Khamenei.
However, ‚Äčfor Arafi to ascend to the role, he would need to clear several procedural hurdles. A committee within the 88-member Assembly of Experts ‚ÄĒ the body tasked with selecting the next ‚Äčleader ‚ÄĒ would first have to put his name forward at a session attended by at least two-thirds of its members.
From there, he would still require the backing of two-thirds of those present ‚ÄĒ roughly 40 senior clerics. There are no guarantees at any stage of the process.
Although he lacks hands-on political experience, Arafi, born in 1959, is widely seen as firmly aligned with Khamenei and his ideological vision. He has ‚Äčconsistently argued that governance should be rooted in the comprehensive application of Shi'ite jurisprudence.
His clout is rooted chiefly in the powerful posts he has held: head of Al-Mustafa International ‚ÄčUniversity and dean of the seminary at the Iranian holy city of Qom. He is also a member of the Guardian Council, which approves all candidates for elected office, and a member of ‚ÄĆthe Assembly of ‚Ā†Experts ‚ÄĒ positions he secured with Khamenei‚Äôs backing and trust.
His credentials as a heavyweight within Iran's religious establishment date back to his early years as an 11-year-old student of Islam when he moved to the city of Qom, the epicenter of Shi'ite theological scholarship.
Over the years Arafi, the son of an Ayatollah, completed advanced studies in jurisprudence and philosophy.
Arafi's career kicked off after Khamenei became supreme leader in 1989. He began his work in his hometown of Meybod as a Friday prayer leader at the young age of 33, one of the ‚Äčfirst signs that he had won the ‚Äčconfidence of Khamenei, who strategically promoted loyalists.
He would later fill that same role in Iran's holy Shi'ite city of Qom, gaining religious authority and political clout in Iran's clerical system.
Arafi also became chairman of Al-Mustafa International University , which has affiliated religious seminaries and Islamic colleges in over 50 ‚Äčcountries and seeks to promote the Islamic Republic's ideology overseas.
With over 50,000 foreign students enrolled, according to Al-Mustafa‚Äôs Persian-language website, students ‚Äčon its Iran campuses receive ‚Ā†free housing, home loans and health care for themselves and their families.
He stepped down from the post in 2018. Two years later, the U.S. State Department formally labeled the Quds Force a foreign terrorist organization, highlighting its recruitment networks.
It said the force had enlisted Pakistani and Afghan students from Al-Mustafa International University into the Zaynabiyoun Brigade and the Fatemiyoun Division ‚ÄĒ militias deployed in ‚Ā†Syria under the ‚Äčcommand of the Revolutionary Guards' Quds Force and previously sanctioned under U.S. counterterrorism and human rights measures.
During ‚Äčhis tenure, Arafi claimed that approximately 50 million people converted to Shi'ite Islam within eight years through the institution‚Äôs outreach. Critics questioned this assertion.
In 2016, he was appointed head of Iran's national seminary system.
Arafi advanced his political ‚Äčstanding three years later when he was handpicked by Khamenei to join the Guardian Council, a powerful election watchdog body that reviews legislation and qualifies candidates for elections.

item.title: Iranian government making case for demise with indiscriminate attacks, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
WARSAW, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran's government is making a strong case for ‚Äčits demise by indiscriminately attacking its ‚ÄĆneighbours, EU's top diplomat Kaja Kallas told reporters on Wednesday.
"Iran's strategy is to sow chaos ‚Äčand set the region on ‚Äčfire," Kallas said.
Turkey said earlier on ‚Ā†Wednesday that NATO air defences destroyed an ‚ÄčIranian ballistic missile headed into Turkish airspace.
Asked ‚Äčabout the incident, Polish Foreign Minister RadosŇāaw Sikorski said: "Iran is broadening the war to countries ‚Äčthat did not attack it ... there ‚Äčis a well known saying it's worse than a ‚ÄĆcrime, ‚Ā†it's a mistake."
Kallas said the war in the Middle East is diverting attention from Ukraine, but it was important ‚Äčnot to ‚Äčlet ‚Ā†the war in Ukraine recede into the background. She added ‚Äčthat the conflict in Iran ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†cost Russia an ally, but cautioned that Russia could benefit from a spike ‚Ā†in ‚Äčoil prices stemming from ‚Äčthe crisis.

item.title: Northern European nations agree to draw up joint evacuation plans in event of crisis or military conflict
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 4 (Reuters) - Ten nations across northern Europe have agreed to prepare for possible cross-border evacuations of civilians in the event of a ‚Äčcrisis or military conflict in the region, in a bid ‚ÄĆto draw lessons from the war in Ukraine, Sweden said on Wednesday.
The 10 will jointly prepare plans covering transport, border controls, travel corridors and other matters.
Germany ‚Äčand Poland, along with fellow NATO members Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, ‚ÄčSweden, Norway, Finland, Iceland and Denmark, have ramped up their ‚Ā†planning in recent years for a possible future armed conflict ‚Äčwith Russia.
"Experience from Ukraine has shown that temporary movements of the population ‚Äčenable the continued defence of the country while protecting civilians," Sweden's ministry of defence said in a statement announcing the northern European agreement.
Millions of people have fled ‚ÄčUkraine in the four years since Russia's full-scale invasion in February ‚Äč2022, most of them seeking refuge in other European nations while the conflict ‚ÄĆat ‚Ā†home continues.
Sweden said that in addition to transport and travel corridors, the planning for cross-border evacuations will include the reception and registration of people and the protection of vulnerable groups.
"The purpose of the agreement is ‚Äčto improve the ‚Äčprotection of the ‚Ā†civilian population in the event of major crises or, in the worst case, war," it said.
The Kremlin ‚Äčhas repeatedly said that Russia does not want to invade ‚ÄčNATO ‚Ā†countries.
Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania last year made a similar agreement between themselves, drawing up contingency plans to deal with the possibility of hundreds of thousands ‚Ā†of ‚Äčpeople fleeing a Russian troop buildup or ‚Äčattack.
Finland, which shares a 1,340 kilometre-long border with Russia, in 2024 signed a similar deal ‚Äčwith Sweden.

item.title: Iran FM says strikes targeted US, not Qatar; Doha rejects claim
item.description:
DOHA, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi ‚Äčtold his Qatari ‚ÄĆcounterpart Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman al-Thani that Iranian ‚Äčmissile attacks were ‚Äčdirected at U.S. interests ‚Ā†and not at ‚ÄčQatar.
Qatar's top diplomat "categorically rejected" ‚Äčthat claim and called for an immediate halt to ‚ÄčIran‚Äôs attacks, the ‚ÄčQatari Foreign Ministry said on ‚ÄĆX.
Sheikh ‚Ā†Mohammed bin Abdulrahman said his country would confront any aggression ‚Äčwith ‚Äčits "right ‚Ā†to self-defence," stressing that Doha has ‚Äčalways been inclined ‚Äčtowards ‚Ā†dialogue and diplomacy conducted in good faith.

item.title: Russian gas tanker sinks near Libya after explosions, maritime authority says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Russian gas tanker Arctic Metagaz (IMO number 9243148) sank near ‚ÄčLibya's coast following explosions and ‚ÄĆa fire, Libya's maritime rescue agency said in an advisory on ‚ÄčWednesday seen by Reuters.
"A distress ‚Äčcall was received on Tuesday ‚Ā†evening, March 3, 2026, from ‚Äčthe commercial tanker named Arctic ‚ÄčMetagaz. Information indicates that the tanker experienced sudden explosions followed by a massive ‚Äčfire, which ultimately led ‚Äčto its complete sinking", the agency said ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the note.
"The tanker has been confirmed to have completely sunk at the site of the ‚Äčincident. The ‚Äčwreckage ‚Ā†lies in the maritime area between Libya and ‚ÄčMalta (within the Libyan search and ‚Äčrescue ‚Ā†zone) ...", the note said.
Russia's transport ministry said on Wednesday that Ukrainian ‚Ā†naval ‚Äčdrones were responsible ‚Äčfor on the Arctic Metagaz.

item.title: Mexico court orders army to hand in missing documents on students that disappeared
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 4 (Reuters) - A Mexican court ordered the army to hand over long-awaited documents that could advance the landmark investigation into the 2014 ‚Äčdisappearance of 43 students in southern Guerrero state at the alleged ‚ÄĆhands of organized crime and local security forces.
The ruling, seen by Reuters on Wednesday, rejected the army's prior assertion that 853 missing pages of information generated by the CFRI, an ‚Äčarmy intelligence agency, did not exist, and ordered that these must now ‚Äčbe handed over.
The government has for over a decade promised to ‚Ā†find those responsible for one of the country's worst human rights atrocities, ‚Äčbut no one has been convicted, though over 100 have been arrested and face ‚Äčongoing prosecutions.
The case of the disappearance of the students from Ayotzinapa Rural Teachers College has been marred by missteps and interference. International probes have ruled that they were killed by ‚Äčorganized crime members in cahoots with police.
The documents may not be considered classified ‚Äčdue to the "overriding interest on the part of the victims' families, as well as society ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†general, to know the truth," the ruling said.
The families' legal representative, the Agustin Pro Human Rights Centre, known as Centro Pro, hailed the order.
"This ruling recognizes the right to the truth of the families and of society as a whole, ‚Äčunderlining the obligation ‚Äčof SEDENA (defense ministry) ‚Ā†and other military bodies to provide key information for the search for the students," it said in a statement.
Only the ‚Äčremains of three of the 43 students have been positively ‚Äčidentified. The ‚Ā†locations of the others remain unknown.
Mexico's defense ministry did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the ruling, dated February 19.
Asked about the case in a ‚Ā†morning press ‚Äčconference on Wednesday, President Claudia Sheinbaum said she ‚Äčhad no knowledge of the ruling but would meet with the victims' parents at the end ‚Äčof this month.

item.title: Portuguese leader, in contrast with Spain, defends decision to let US use base in Iran campaign
item.description:
LISBON, March 4 (Reuters) - Portuguese Prime Minister Luis Montenegro defended his decision to let the United States use the Lajes ‚Äčairbase on Portugal's Azores Islands during the Iran bombing campaign, a ‚ÄĆposition at odds with that of neighbour Spain which refused such requests.
A longstanding agreement with the United States allows Washington to use the base without prior ‚Äčauthorisation during peacetime, but required Portugal to give approval once ‚Äčhostilities began.
Montenegro told parliament on Wednesday that U.S. use of ‚Ā†the base was in compliance both with Portuguese law and ‚Äčagreements with the United States. Authorisation was granted for defensive purposes, based on ‚Äčnecessity and against military targets, following international law, he said.
"Portugal objectively has a close relationship with our ally, the United States," Montenegro told lawmakers.
"Iran repeatedly violated international norms ‚Äčwith its nuclear ambitions and long-range missile capabilities," he said, describing ‚ÄčTehran as a state sponsor of international terrorism.
Portugal's stance starkly contrasted with that ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†Spain, which angered President Donald Trump by refusing permission to use its bases. Montenegro did not directly address the difference in policy between the neighbours but noted that Portugal was a founding member of the ‚ÄčNATO alliance in ‚Äč1949, whereas Spain ‚Ā†joined only in 1982.
He said the government believed diplomacy must be the priority, but since the start ‚Äčof the conflict, Iran had attacked other countries that ‚Äčhad ‚Ā†not previously engaged in any hostilities.
The government will also implement an "extraordinary and temporary" reduction of the tax on petroleum and energy products to offset ‚Ā†any ‚Äčfuel price increase above 0.10 euros ($0.12) of ‚Äčthis week's price due to the conflict, he added.
($1 = 0.8595 euros)

item.title: Iran conflict may divert US weapons from Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV/WASHINGTON March 4 (Reuters) - With the United States focused on its conflict with Iran , Ukraine could face a critical shortage of U.S. air defence missiles at a time when Russia shows no signs of easing its campaign of striking Ukrainian cities.
Since the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israel began strikes on Iran on Saturday, Iran has fired hundreds of ballistic missiles and drones at Gulf countries. Most have been intercepted, including with the PAC-3 Patriot missile interceptors that Ukraine relies ‚ÄĆon to defend its energy and military infrastructure from ballistic missiles.
The roughly 600 PAC-3s produced yearly by Lockheed Martin are already insufficient to cover U.S. needs and those of its allies in the Gulf, let alone Ukraine's, said Serhii Kuzan, head of the Kyiv-based Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Center think tank.
"It's the very simple mathematics of war," Kuzan said, adding the Franco-Italian SAMP/T air-defence system - which has similar capabilities - had not ramped up production fast enough to offer an alternative.
Fabian Hoffmann, a doctoral research fellow at Oslo University who specialises in missiles, said stockpiling of Patriots ‚Äčby Gulf states meant they were unlikely to run out completely, particularly as the intensity of Iranian missile bombardments appeared to be decreasing, but they might need to become more selective in their use as time ‚Äčgoes on.
A wider shortage could be avoided if the U.S. and Israel are successful at destroying Iran's missile stockpiles and launchers in the coming days, according to Mykola Bielieskov of ‚Ā†the state-run National Institute for Strategic Studies in Kyiv.
Russia, which has invested heavily in military production, has fired more than 700 missiles during this year's winter campaign against Ukraine's energy infrastructure, Kyiv says. Last month, it launched 32 ballistic missiles ‚Äčin a single night.
The vast majority of Patriots supplied to Ukraine are provided by European nations under the Prioritised Ukraine Requirements List (PURL), a NATO-led initiative launched last year to purchase U.S.-made weapons for Ukraine.
Ukraine's allies have committed to send 37 PAC-3 missiles ‚Äčsince their last meeting in mid-February, a source with knowledge of the matter told Reuters. Italy has ruled out taking away from Kyiv's air defences to support the Gulf states, according to a separate source with knowledge of the matter.
But there is concern that, if the Iran war drags on, delays to supplies under PURL could worsen as the U.S. depletes its own stockpiles, two European diplomats said.
A senior U.S. defense official confirmed production-related delays in supplies to PURL in the past, and said the logjams could get worse if the war against Iran ‚Äčbecomes protracted. "We can only crank out so much at a time," the official said.
The U.S. can assert its right to preempt deliveries to other countries at any time, for any reason.
Although Lockheed Martin is scaling up production of PAC-3s to 2,000 ‚Äčannually under a deal announced in January, that will come too late to address any shortages this year.
The Pentagon did not respond to a request for comment on weapons supplies to Ukraine.
Zelenskiy said on Monday that ‚ÄĆa prolonged and ‚Ā†intense war in Iran could reduce the air defence systems available to Ukraine and that Russia was preparing a new wave of attacks on infrastructure, logistics and water supplies.
He has been an outspoken supporter of the U.S. attack on Iran, striking a contrast with Moscow, which has condemned the attacks on its ally.
U.S. President Donald Trump, who has pressured Ukraine for a quick peace deal that could involve ceding land to Russia, said on Tuesday that ending the Ukraine war remained high on his priority list.
But a new round of U.S.-mediated peace talks will not be taking place in Abu Dhabi on Wednesday and Thursday as expected due to Iran's attacks on Gulf states in response to the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, and no new location has been ‚Äčannounced.
If talks do continue, Russia could try to convince a ‚Äčdistracted U.S. to further pressure Ukraine into accepting bad terms, ‚Ā†said Yevhen Mahda of the Institute of World Policy in Kyiv.
Ukraine has ruled out giving up land and has retaken territory in recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023 , opens new tab , according to Black Bird Group, a Finland-based security and intelligence analysis team.
Spring temperatures may also bring relief to Ukrainians whose power supplies have been depleted by ‚ÄčRussian drones and missiles, while mud may slow any advances on the battlefield.
But Emil Kastehelmi of Black Bird Group said that, if there was a severe decline in Ukraine's ‚Äčair defences, it would force the ‚Ā†military to make difficult decisions about what to defend.
"Ukraine has to be able to protect not just energy infrastructure, but its industry and its military bases," he said.
Hoffmann said Ukraine needed to develop its ability to strike at Moscow's missile production facilities deep inside Russian territory with its own missiles.
"Missile defense is supposed to be a temporary measure until you can degrade the adversary's offensive capabilities," he said. Ukraine and its allies need to invest in its missile capabilities because the payload on Ukraine's long-range drones was ‚Ā†too small to ‚Äčinflict significant damage, he said.
Trump's suggestion in October that the U.S. could supply long-range Tomahawk cruise missiles to Ukraine never materialized, following warnings from ‚ÄčMoscow that this would seriously harm relations.
Ukraine said last month it carried out a strike using its domestically produced FP-5 Flamingo cruise missile on a Russian missile factory - the Votkinsk Machine Building Plant in the remote Udmurtia region, around 1,400 km (870 miles) from the Ukrainian border.
"In the end, Ukraine is going ‚Äčto have to invest in offensive capabilities," Hoffmann said. "This is the only way."

item.title: White House: Spain has agreed to cooperate with US military
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Spain has agreed to ‚Äčcooperate with the U.S. military, White ‚ÄĆHouse Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Wednesday, a day after President Donald Trump threatened to cut ‚Äčtrade with Madrid over its stance ‚Äčagainst the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran.
"I ‚Ā†think they heard the president's message yesterday ‚Äčloud and clear. It is my understanding ‚Äčover the past several hours they've agreed to cooperate with the US military," Leavitt told a ‚Äčnews briefing.
Trump floated imposing a trade ‚Äčembargo on Madrid over its refusal to allow U.S. ‚ÄĆaircraft ‚Ā†to use jointly operated naval and air bases in southern Spain for the offensive against Tehran. Spain has denounced the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israeli ‚Äčbombings ‚Ā†of Iran as reckless and illegal.
Spain "will not be vassals" to another ‚Äčcountry, Deputy Prime Minister Maria ‚ÄčJesus ‚Ā†Montero said on Wednesday. In a televised address earlier, Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez reiterated ‚Ā†Spain's ‚Äčanti-war stance, warning that ‚Äčthe conflict risked triggering a major global disaster.

item.title: Ayatollah's whereabouts impacted timing of US operation, White House says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Information ‚Äčon Ayatollah ‚ÄĆAli Khamenei's whereabouts ‚Äčimpacted ‚Äčthe timing of ‚Ā†the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äčstrike ‚Äčon Iran that killed the ‚Äčsupreme ‚Äčleader, White House ‚ÄĆspokeswoman ‚Ā†Karoline Leavitt told reporters ‚Äčon ‚ÄčWednesday.

item.title: Blackout hits most of Cuba amid US oil chokehold
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A power outage struck most of Cuba, including the capital of Havana, the country's national electricity union ‚ÄčUNE said on Wednesday, as the island's communist-run ‚ÄĆgovernment faces increasing pressure from the Trump administration.
Cuba has experienced a series of major blackouts in recent years, even before ‚Äčthe U.S. cut off oil shipments to the ‚ÄčCaribbean's largest island.
Cuba's government has attributed its ‚Ā†economic crisis to decades of economic sanctions from the ‚ÄčU.S.
A more recent scarcity of oil from Venezuela and ‚ÄčMexico due to U.S. pressure has worsened existing shortages.
The UNE electricity union said it was working to restore services, and ‚Äčthat the blackout affected the island from the central ‚Äčprovince of Camaguey to Pinar del Rio in the far west.
The ‚ÄĆFelton ‚Ā†1 thermoelectric power plant, located in Holguin province in eastern Cuba, remains online and recovery protocols have been activated, Cuba's energy ministry said.
Venezuela, Cuba's top oil ‚Äčsupplier, has not sent ‚Äčshipments since ‚Ā†December. Its President Nicolas Maduro was captured in a U.S. attack on its ‚Äčcapital early January, after which the U.S. ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†controlled the country's oil exports.
Mexico said it would halt supplies after the U.S. threatened tariffs on countries supplying ‚Ā†Cuba ‚Äčwith oil.
The power cuts have ‚Äčcaused the government to ration key services.

item.title: Netherlands summons Iranian ambassador over attacks
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 4 (Reuters) - The Netherlands summoned the Iranian ‚Äčambassador on Wednesday ‚Äčover the Iranian attacks on Gulf ‚Ā†countries and the wider ‚Äčregion, Dutch Foreign Minister ‚ÄčTom Berendsen said on Wednesday.
"This afternoon, I summoned the Iranian ‚Äčambassador
to lodge a ‚Äčformal protest against the ongoing ‚ÄĆIranian
drone ‚Ā†and missile attacks on countries in the Gulf
and the broader region, ‚Äčincluding Cyprus ‚Äčand
Turkey," ‚Ā†Berendsen said on social media ‚Äčplatform X.
The Iranian embassy ‚Äčin ‚Ā†The Hague did not immediately reply to ‚Ā†an ‚Äčemailed request ‚Äčfor comment.

item.title: Trump weighing US role in Iran after conflict, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump is discussing with his advisors what role the U.S could have in Iran after ‚Äčthe military campaign while U.S. intelligence is monitoring reports that Mojtaba Khamenei, the ‚ÄĆson of Iran's slain supreme leader, has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, the White House said on Wednesday.
"We've seen those reports as well, of course, and this is something that our intelligence agencies ‚Äčin looking at. We The truth is, we'll have to wait and see," ‚Äčpress secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba has emerged as the frontrunner ‚Ā†to succeed his late father as Iran's supreme leader after years spent forging close ‚Äčties with the elite Revolutionary Guards and building influence in the clerical establishment.
Leavitt added that Trump ‚Äčwas actively considering and discussing with his national security team what role Washington may have in Iran's future once the operation is over but the primary focus at the moment was the success of the ‚Äčmilitary operation.
Leavitt also defended the U.S. goals of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran amid criticism ‚Äčthat Washington has failed to provide evidence for the imminent threat that Tehran posed directly for the ‚ÄĆUnited States, ‚Ā†while adding that Trump believed that the American people supported the war.
"This decision to launch this operation is based on a cumulative effect of various direct threats that Iran posed to the United States of America," Leavitt said.
"Again, this is a rogue terrorist regime that has ‚Äčbeen threatening the United ‚ÄčStates, our allies ‚Ā†and our people for 47 years and the American people are smart enough to know that," Leavitt said.
Trump has rejected suggestions that Israel ‚Äčpushed the U.S. into the conflict, as his administration gave varying accounts ‚Äčand faced ‚Ā†criticism from some supporters and Democrats who accused him of launching a "war of choice."
A Reuters/Ipsos poll released earlier this week showed that only one in four Americans approves of U.S. strikes on ‚Ā†Iran that ‚Äčhave plunged the Middle East into chaos, while about ‚Äčhalf ‚ÄĒ including one in four Republicans ‚ÄĒ believe Trump is too willing to use military force.

item.title: Landslide kills over 200 people at Congo's Rubaya¬†mine, mines ministry says
item.description:
KINSHASA, March 4 (Reuters) - More than 200 ‚Äčpeople including ‚ÄĆaround 70 children died on ‚ÄčTuesday in ‚Äča landslide triggered ‚Ā†by heavy ‚Äčrains at the ‚ÄčRubaya coltan mine in eastern Democratic ‚ÄčRepublic ‚Äčof Congo, the mines ‚ÄĆministry ‚Ā†said on Wednesday.
A senior M23 official told ‚ÄčReuters ‚Äčearlier ‚Ā†that only five ‚Äčor six ‚Äčdied ‚Ā†in the accident.

item.title: US Interior Secretary meets with Rodriguez, mining companies in Venezuela visit
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum met with ‚ÄčVenezuelan acting President Delcy Rodriguez on Wednesday afternoon after arriving in the South American country earlier in the ‚ÄĆday, in an encounter filmed briefly by media outlets.
The two met with the U.S. representative in Venezuela, Laura Dogu, in Caracas' Miraflores presidential palace. Burgum met earlier with executives from foreign mining companies, two sources said, as the interim Venezuelan government weighs changes to mineral regulations.
The visit is part of ‚ÄčU.S. efforts to open Venezuela to American investment, especially in oil, gas and minerals, as the Trump administration tries to exert ‚Äčmore control over the country following a January U.S. raid that captured President Nicolas Maduro. It is the ‚Ā†second visit by a U.S. cabinet secretary since the ouster of Maduro, who courted the likes of China and Russia as ‚Äčallies.
In public, Trump has heaped praise on Rodriguez for cooperating with the U.S. and hailed Venezuela as ‚Äúour new friend and partner‚ÄĚ in ‚Äčhis annual State of the Union address. Behind the scenes, the Trump administration has been applying pressure, building a legal case against Rodriguez that could include corruption and money laundering charges, Reuters reported Tuesday, citing four people familiar with the matter.
Burgum, who also heads the U.S. Energy Dominance Council, which focuses ‚Äčon boosting U.S. energy production, is in Venezuela to discuss supply chains for critical minerals, the Venezuela Affairs Unit, as the U.S. ‚Äčdiplomatic mission in Venezuela is known, said in a post on X.
Venezuela's national assembly is preparing a reform of the country's main ‚ÄĆmining law, ‚Ā†including provisions that would allow foreign companies to exploit gold, diamonds and rare earths, Rodriguez's brother Jorge Rodriguez, the assembly's head, said this week. Venezuela's current mining legislation dates to 1999.
The South American country owes billions of dollars to industrial conglomerates, oil and mining companies after deep waves of nationalizations two decades ago, including to Crystallex, Gold Reserve and Rusoro Mining (RML.V) , opens new tab .
Burgum is paying attention to the ‚Äčmining law proposal, the sources told ‚ÄčReuters, and met on Wednesday ‚Ā†morning with several mining companies. He is expected to meet with oil and gas companies on Thursday to discuss expansion and investment, the sources added. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright visited last ‚Äčmonth.
Exploration has not yet taken place in Venezuela to confirm reserves of rare earths, a grouping ‚Äčof 17 minor ‚Ā†metals used to make magnets that turn power into motion. Rare earths are a subset of critical minerals, much of which is produced globally by China.
A report from the Venezuelan government in 2018 on mineral deposits used key mining industry terms like reserve and resource interchangeably, making ‚Ā†it difficult ‚Äčto ascertain what the true measurements are. An official map published in 2021 ‚Äčshowed reserves of antimony, copper, nickel, coltan, molybdenum, magnesium, silver, zinc, titanium, tungsten and uranium, but did not list volumes.
As part of bilateral agreements, Iranian companies in past ‚Äčyears have explored for mining resources in the country, but the work did not lead to investments.

item.title: US Interior Secretary Burgum meeting with Venezuela acting President Rodriguez
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum ‚Äčwas meeting with ‚ÄĆVenezuelan acting President Delcy Rodriguez ‚Äčon Wednesday afternoon, ‚Äčafter arriving in ‚Ā†the South American ‚Äčcountry earlier in ‚Äčthe day, in an encounter filmed briefly ‚Äčby media ‚Äčoutlets.
The two met with the ‚ÄĆU.S. ‚Ā†representative in Venezuela, Laura Dogu, in Caracas' Miraflores ‚Äčpresidential ‚Äčpalace.
Burgum ‚Ā†met earlier with executives ‚Äčfrom foreign ‚Äčmining ‚Ā†companies, two sources said, as the ‚Ā†interim ‚ÄčVenezuelan ‚Äčgovernment weighs changes to mineral ‚Äčregulations.

item.title: Airstrike kills Iran-aligned militia commander south of Baghdad ‚Äď police sources
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 4 (Reuters) - A ‚Äčcommander from ‚ÄĆIslamic Resistance in Iraq, ‚Äčan ‚Äčumbrella group of ‚Ā†Iran-backed ‚Äčarmed factions, ‚Äčwas killed in an airstrike ‚Äčon ‚ÄčWednesday, police sources ‚ÄĆsaid.
The ‚Ā†sources said the commander ‚Äčand ‚Äčhis ‚Ā†driver were killed ‚Äčwhen ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†strike hit their ‚Ā†vehicle ‚Äčsouth ‚Äčof Baghdad.

item.title: Rubio spoke with Turkish foreign minister and pledged full US support, State Department says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Secretary of State ‚ÄĆMarco Rubio spoke with Turkish Foreign ‚ÄčMinister Hakan Fidan ‚Äčon Wednesday and pledged ‚Ā†Washington's full support ‚Äčfollowing missile attacks ‚Äčfrom Iran, the U.S. State Department said.
Turkey on Wednesday ‚Äčsaid that ‚ÄčNATO air defenses destroyed an ‚ÄĆIranian ‚Ā†ballistic missile headed into Turkish airspace.
Rubio told Fidan that "the ‚Äčattacks on ‚ÄčTurkey‚Äôs ‚Ā†sovereign territory were unacceptable and ‚Äčpledged full ‚Äčsupport ‚Ā†from the United States," the State ‚Ā†Department ‚Äčsaid in ‚Äča statement.

item.title: Zelenskiy spoke to leaders of Bahrain, Kuwait about Iran conflict
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he discussed ‚Äčthe Middle East conflict on Wednesday ‚ÄĆwith the king of Bahrain and the crown prince of Kuwait.
Zelenskiy also ‚Äčsaid that his administration was ‚Äčin daily contact with the U.S. and ‚Ā†once the security situation around Iran ‚Äčpermitted it, trilateral talks with Moscow ‚Äčand Washington on a settlement of the war with Moscow would resume.
In his nightly ‚Äčvideo address, Zelenskiy added:

item.title: Rubio speaks with Saudi foreign minister about regional stability, US State Department says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Secretary of ‚ÄĆState Marco Rubio spoke with Saudi Foreign Minister Prince ‚ÄčFaisal bin Farhan on ‚ÄčWednesday and discussed the ‚Ā†threats from Iran to ‚Äčregional stability and other ‚Äčdevelopments in the region, the U.S. State Department said.
"The Secretary expressed ‚Äčgratitude to the ‚ÄčForeign Minister for Saudi Arabia's response ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†the attack on U.S. Embassy Riyadh," State Department spokesperson Tommy Pigott said ‚Äčin a ‚Äčstatement.
"They ‚Ā†also discussed the continued threats the ‚ÄčIranian regime poses to ‚Äčregional ‚Ā†stability, as well as other developments in the ‚Ā†region," ‚ÄčPigott added.

item.title: Iran will target Israeli nuclear site if regime change is sought, Iranian official says
item.description: March 4 (Reuters) - Iran will ‚Äčtarget the ‚ÄĆIsraeli nuclear ‚Äčsite ‚Äčof Dimona if ‚Ā†Israel ‚Äčand the ‚ÄčU.S. seek regime change ‚Äčin ‚Äčthe Islamic Republic, ‚ÄĆsemi-official ‚Ā†ISNA news agency ‚Äčreported ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Wednesday, citing ‚Äčan ‚ÄčIranian ‚Ā†military official.

item.title: Majority of US Senate votes to block bid to rein in Trump Iran war powers, voting continues
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - A majority of ‚Äčthe U.S. Senate ‚ÄĆon Wednesday backed President Donald Trump's military ‚Äčcampaign against Iran , ‚Äčvoting to block a ‚Ā†bipartisan resolution ‚Äčaiming to stop ‚Äčthe air war and require that any ‚Äčhostilities against ‚ÄčIran be authorized by Congress.
As ‚ÄĆvoting ‚Ā†continued, the tally in the 100-member chamber ‚Äčwas ‚Äč52-47 ‚Ā†not to advance the war ‚Äčpowers resolution.

item.title: Ecuador declares Cuban ambassador 'persona non grata'
item.description:
QUITO, March 4 (Reuters) - Ecuador declared Cuban ‚ÄčAmbassador Basilio Gutierrez and ‚ÄĆhis diplomatic staff as "persona non grata," the foreign ministry said ‚Äčon Wednesday, giving ‚Äčthem 48 hours to leave ‚Ā†the country.
Authorities did not ‚Äčexplain why President Daniel Noboa's ‚Äčgovernment made the decision, but cited Article 9 of the Vienna ‚ÄčConvention on Diplomatic Relations.
The ‚Äčministry did not address whether the ‚ÄĆmove ‚Ā†implied a formal break in diplomatic relations with Havana.
Ecuadorean police and military personnel ‚Äčwere seen ‚Äčpatrolling ‚Ā†the area outside the Cuban embassy in ‚ÄčQuito, according to ‚ÄčReuters ‚Ā†witnesses.
A press officer at the Cuban embassy told Reuters ‚Ā†they ‚Äčcould not comment ‚Äčon the matter.

item.title: White House says Trump using preventative skin treatment for neck rash, offers no other details
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is using a preventative ‚ÄĆtreatment for a red rash on his neck, according to his physician, but the White House declined on Wednesday to share further details about the condition.
A red, ‚Äčblotchy rash was visible on the right side of Trump's neck ‚Äčjust above the collar line in photographs from his appearance ‚Ā†at a Medal of Honor ceremony on Monday.
In a statement after the ‚Äčevent, Dr. Sean Barbabella, the White House physician, said Trump was using ‚Äča common cream as "a preventative skin treatment."
"The President is using this treatment for one week, and the redness is expected to last for a few weeks," Barbabella said.
Asked on ‚ÄčWednesday about why the treatment was necessary, White House press secretary ‚ÄčKaroline Leavitt told reporters she had no additional information to share beyond the details provided ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the physician's statement.
Trump's health, at age 79, has been more of a focus in his second term, as bruises on his hands and swelling in his legs have been visible at times.
In January, Trump attributed the ‚Äčhand bruising to his ‚Äčfrequent use ‚Ā†of aspirin, and the White House said the president hit his hand on a table when traveling abroad.
Last July, after ‚Äčthe president's ankles appeared swollen, the White House physician said ‚Äčan ultrasound ‚Ā†on the president's legs "revealed chronic venous insufficiency, a benign and common condition, particularly in individuals over the age of 70."
As he took office for a ‚Ā†second ‚Äčterm last year, Trump was the oldest U.S. ‚Äčpresident ever inaugurated, and he frequently compares his health to former Democratic President Joe Biden.

item.title: More than 2,000 Canadians request assistance leaving Middle East, official says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - More than ‚Äč2,000 Canadians have ‚ÄĆrequested assistance from the Canadian government to leave the ‚ÄčMiddle East amid ‚Äčthe intensifying U.S. and ‚Ā†Israeli conflict with Iran, ‚Äča Canadian official said ‚Äčon Wednesday.
Half of those inquiries came from Canadians in ‚Äčthe United Arab Emirates, ‚Äč200 from Qatar and 160 ‚ÄĆfrom ‚Ā†Lebanon, the official said.
The Canadian government is working to secure seats on ‚Äčcommercial ‚Äčflights ‚Ā†out of some cities in the ‚Äčregion, the official ‚Äčsaid, ‚Ā†including 75 that were made available on Wednesday ‚Ā†and ‚Äčmore expected ‚Äčin the coming days.

item.title: Iran denies Axios report that messages were sent to US
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - An Iranian official said the country ‚Äčhad not sent any ‚ÄĆmessages to the United States, in response to an Axios ‚Äčreport, Iran's Tasnim news ‚Äčagency said early on Thursday.
"No ‚Ā†message has been sent ‚Äčfrom Iran to the ‚ÄčUS, nor will any response be given to US messages. Iran's ‚Äčarmed forces have prepared ‚Äčthemselves for a long war," the ‚ÄĆofficial, ‚Ā†who was not named, was quoted as saying by Tasnim.
On Wednesday, Axios reported the Iranians had ‚Äčsent messages ‚Äčto ‚Ā†the United States over the last few ‚Äčdays but the U.S. ‚Äčdid ‚Ā†not respond, citing an American official and a second ‚Ā†source.

item.title: Australia says more flights from Middle East expected, situation still volatile
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia said three more commercial flights were scheduled to depart the United Arab Emirates on Thursday as the first group of Australians stranded ‚Äčin the Middle East returned home overnight following the outbreak of ‚ÄĆthe Iran conflict.
Commercial air traffic remained largely absent across much of the region, with major Gulf hubs - including Dubai, the world's busiest airport for international passengers - largely shut for a fifth ‚Äčstraight day, in the biggest travel disruption since the COVID-19 pandemic.
Australian Foreign ‚ÄčMinister Penny Wong said she hoped the scheduled three flights ‚Ā†would proceed, but warned the situation remained volatile after NATO air defences destroyed an ‚ÄčIranian ballistic missile fired towards Turkey.
"This could move very quickly if there is ‚Äčfurther military activity that makes it unsafe for people to fly, obviously then these flights will not proceed," Wong told ABC News on Thursday.
"This is a conflict zone and we're operating ‚Äčin an environment which is highly unpredictable."
About 200 Australians arrived in an ‚ÄčEmirates flight from Dubai on Wednesday night, with emotional scenes at arrival gates as families embraced ‚ÄĆreturning ‚Ā†travellers, including children on a school trip, television footage showed.
Sam Brown, who had recently moved to Dubai with her husband and four-year-old daughter, told ABC News the family had been left "just totally shocked by the situation" but were relieved ‚Äčto make it home.
"We ‚Äčjust couldn't believe ‚Ā†we got on that flight. We're so grateful. We felt nervous, but in very safe hands."
According to airport information, ‚Äčan Emirates flight to Sydney from Dubai is scheduled to ‚Äčleave at ‚Ā†9 a.m. (2200 GMT Wednesday), with another flight to Melbourne set for 5 p.m. (0600 GMT). An Etihad Airways flight to Sydney from Abu Dhabi scheduled for Thursday has ‚Ā†been delayed.
About ‚Äč115,000 Australians remain in the Middle East, with ‚Äč24,000 in the United Arab Emirates. Australian officials view commercial flights as the most viable option ‚Äčto bring stranded travellers home.

item.title: Colorado Governor Polis signals he is open to clemency for Tina Peters
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Colorado Governor Jared Polis signaled on Tuesday he was willing to consider clemency for Tina Peters , a former state county clerk convicted of illegally tampering with voting machines as ‚Äčshe pursued claims that the 2020 presidential election had been stolen.
Polis, citing the case ‚ÄĆof another public official who was given a lighter penalty, said clemency may be appropriate to avoid sentencing disparities, although Colorado's secretary of state said Peters had committed a more grave offence.
Polis has drawn attention to the ‚Äčrelatively light sentence - probation and community service - handed down to State Senator Sonya Jaquez ‚ÄčLewis, who was convicted of attempting to influence a public official and other charges ‚Ā†after prosecutors said she forged letters from former aides.
"It is not lost on me that she ‚Äčwas convicted of the exact same felony charge as Tina Peters ‚ÄĒ attempting to influence a public ‚Äčofficial ‚ÄĒ and yet Tina Peters, as a non-violent first-time offender got a nine-year sentence," Polis, a Democrat, wrote on X. He said that justice needed to be applied evenly.
He extended the clemency application deadline until April 3, allowing ‚Äčhim to review the case.
Colorado Secretary of State Jena Griswold, the state's top election official, said ‚Äčon Wednesday that it was inaccurate, however, to suggest that the actions of Lewis and Peters had the ‚ÄĆsame impact.
"Peters ‚Ā†organized the breach of the election equipment, broke the public trust and attacked the very foundations of our democratic process," Griswold said in a statement.
Peters, a Republican, was an outspoken supporter of U.S. President Donald Trump 's unsubstantiated claims that he lost the 2020 U.S. presidential election because of widespread fraud.
She was indicted ‚Äčin 2022 following an ‚Äčelection security breach at ‚Ā†her office that led to voting equipment passwords getting posted on a right-wing blog. She denied wrongdoing.
Peters, 70, was convicted in 2024 on seven charges, ‚Äčincluding three counts of attempting to influence a public servant. She is ‚Äčnot eligible ‚Ā†for release until November 2028.
Peter Ticktin, a lawyer for Peters, said he hopes the governor commutes the former county clerk's sentence this week, saying her nine-year sentence was "too harsh."
Trump has repeatedly called for Peters to ‚Ā†be ‚Äčreleased from the La Vista Correctional Facility, a state prison ‚Äčin Colorado, and has criticized Polis for not granting her clemency.
Trump pardoned Peters in December in a move that was considered ‚Äčsymbolic since Peters is not in federal custody.

item.title: Trading Day: Respite, but for how long?
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. and European stock markets registered solid gains on Wednesday, on hopes that conflict in the Middle East might soon cool , while stabilization in oil and energy markets also helped lay the ground for a rebound in other beaten-down assets.
More on that below. In my column today , ‚ÄčI look at why geopolitical crises and the risk of a dollar liquidity shock are reminders that the transition away ‚ÄĆfrom a dollar-centric financial universe toward a more fractured, multi-polar world could be very rocky.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* And ... breathe?
Call it profit-taking, position-squaring, tactical trading, ‚Äča technical bounce, or hope that the war may end soon. Either way, some of the outsized market moves sparked by the Middle East ‚Äčturmoil stalled or reversed a bit on Wednesday, as investors took a figurative breather.
That meant oil, the dollar and volatility all fell, while Wall Street, European stocks, emerging ‚ÄĆFX and ‚Ā†silver rebounded strongly. Not only did Asia miss out, stocks there got slammed on Wednesday. Catch-up Thursday, perhaps?
* Underlying economic strength
Health warning - purchasing managers' index surveys for February pre-date the Middle East crisis. Still, figures on Wednesday showed that business activity around the world was humming along at a surprisingly solid clip last month.
Service sector PMIs show that U.S. activity surged to more than a 3-1/2-year high in February, Chinese activity was the strongest in three years, and European growth ‚Äčpicked up pace too. This follows ‚Äčfairly upbeat manufacturing PMIs too.
* ‚Ā†Warsh nomination goes to the Senate
The ball to make Kevin Warsh the new Chair of the U.S. Federal Reserve is officially rolling, after President Donald Trump on Wednesday submitted his nomination of the former Fed governor to the Senate.
The confirmation ‚Äčprocess won't be plain-sailing. Some Senators object to the administration's investigations into current chair Jerome Powell and Governor ‚ÄčLisa Cook, and ‚Ā†others say Warsh will be merely a puppet for Trump, further eroding the central bank's independence.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ‚Äčwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Nepal goes to the polls; voters seek change after youth-led protests
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 5 (Reuters) - Nearly six months after a wave of unprecedented youth-led protests and the deaths of 77 people forced Nepal's then ‚Äčprime minister to quit, people began voting on Thursday in a general election that will choose a new parliament in ‚ÄĆthe Himalayan nation.
Perched between China and India, the country of 30 million people has been plagued for decades by political instability , crippling a largely agrarian economy and worsening unemployment - structural issues compounded by rampant corruption.
The long-festering, deep-seated malaise erupted into street demonstrations last September, triggered by a social media ban, that brought thousands on the streets, leading ‚Äčto clashes and fatalities that forced the resignation of Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli.
Oli, who leads the moderate Communist Party of ‚ÄčNepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist, UML), is once again in the fray on Thursday, along with more than 3,400 other candidates ‚Ā†from 65 parties.
They include the country's oldest party, the Nepali Congress led by 49-year-old Gagan Thapa, and the Nepali Communist Party (NCP) comprising former ‚ÄčMaoist insurgents who joined mainstream politics.
Together with UML, these parties have dominated Nepali politics for the last three decades, although the country has ‚Äčseen 32 government changes in the past 35 years.
But the frontrunner for these polls is the three-year-old Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP), which has fielded the charismatic rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah as its prime ministerial candidate.
The 35-year-old former mayor of Nepal's capital Kathmandu is drawing large crowds, connecting with legions of young voters clamouring for change on ‚Äčthe ground and online, even as he takes on Oli , 74, on his home turf along the Indian border.
"The election is critical to address ‚Äčthe aspirations of the youths expressed during the Gen Z protests," said political analyst Puranjan Acharya.
"If the newly elected leaders are seen as unfit to do ‚ÄĆso, there ‚Ā†is a risk of further trouble."
Some 19 million voters are eligible to cast their ballot for 275 members of parliament through a mixed electoral system ‚Äď 165 seats in direct first-past-the-post elections and 110 through proportional representation.
Voting started at 7 a.m. local time (0115 GMT) and closes at 5 p.m., with counting scheduled to start soon after, according to the country's election commission.
Early trends are likely to emerge by ‚ÄčFriday but complete results could take ‚Äča week or more as ‚Ā†counting of proportional representation votes would take time, election commission officials said.
"Voting is not just about sending someone to victory," Interim Prime Minister Sushila Karki, who took over after Oli, said in a public broadcast ‚Äčthis week.
"It's a decision you make about your future and that of your children."
Promises of jobs, ‚Äčreining in corruption and ‚Ā†improving governance - all demands raised during the September protests - have dominated much of the election campaign, which officially ended on Monday ahead of a two-day cooling-off period.
In Kathmandu, many candidates, wearing marigold garlands and vermillion smeared on their forehead walked the streets, rode on trucks and appeared from the ‚Ā†sunroof of ‚Äčcars, to woo voters one last time before the polls.
But some voters are still ‚Äčon the fence.
"Old parties did nothing except corruption," said Ramkrishna Pandey, 50, discussing politics with half a dozen others at a newspaper kiosk on the outskirts of the ‚Äčcapital.
"New ones don't seem any better. I have not decided who to support."

item.title: China to build 'birth-friendly society', refine social security system
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China said on Thursday that it would build a "childbirth-friendly society" in the next five years as it pledged to ‚Äčaddress concerns over employment, education, medical care, health and income, according to ‚ÄĆan official government report.
Authorities will improve population services and respond proactively to population ageing, including "promoting high-quality, full employment, improving the income distribution system, and refining the social security system."
The announcement comes after China's ‚Äčpopulation fell for a fourth consecutive year in 2025, as the birth rate ‚Äčplunged to a record low, official data showed in January, with ‚Ā†experts warning of further decline.
China's population has been shrinking since 2022 and is ageing rapidly, ‚Äčcomplicating Beijing's plan to boost domestic consumption and rein in debt.
New policies will be ‚Äčintroduced to promote "high quality development of silver economy", targeted at those aged 60 and older, with elderly care services to be increased, particularly in rural areas, the report said.
Authorities will also draw ‚Äčup measures to hone supportive policies designed for seniors including pension finance, wellness and ‚Äčcare, it said.
By 2035, the number of people aged over 60 is set to hit 400 ‚ÄĆmillion - ‚Ā†roughly equal to the populations of the United States and Italy combined - meaning hundreds of millions of people are set to leave the workforce at a time when pension budgets are already stretched .
China has already increased retirement ages, with men now expected to ‚Äčwork until they are ‚Äč63 rather than ‚Ā†60, and women until they are 58 rather than 55.
China will foster "positive attitudes toward marriage and childbearing," the report said, adding ‚Äčthat it would boost housing support for families with children.
Services for ‚Äčwomen in ‚Ā†early stages of pregnancy as well as reproductive health would be improved while authorities aimed to better prevent and treat birth defects.
Authorities will also refine policies on free preschool ‚Ā†education ‚Äčand increase the supply of regular senior secondary school ‚Äčplaces, with government spending on education mandated to be higher than 4% of GDP, the report said.

item.title: Who is contesting Nepal's polls and what is at stake?
item.description:
March 5 - Nepal votes in a general election on Thursday, its first since deadly youth-led anti-graft protests toppled the government ‚Äčof then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli in September.
Here is a look at ‚ÄĆthe key contenders, and what is at stake.
Nearly 19 million of Nepal's 30 million people are eligible to vote in the for the 275-member assembly.
About one million of the voters - most ‚Äčof them youth - were added after last year's protests , which killed 77 people ‚Äčand injured more than 2,000.
While direct contests will decide 165 seats, ‚Ā†which means the person who gets the most votes will win, the rest ‚Äčwill be filled through proportional representation, where seats are allocated to parties in proportion ‚Äčto their vote share.
Election authorities say 65 political parties are in the fray.
Apart from corruption, job creation is among the main issues, analysts say, with about a fifth of the population ‚Äčliving in poverty, and high youth unemployment.
Ties with India and China, which border Nepal ‚Äčand are among its major trade partners, will also be a factor in the election as the ‚ÄĆlandlocked ‚Ā†nation negotiates a balance between the Asian powers.
While India accounts for two-thirds of Nepal's international trade, China accounts for 14% and has also lent the country - among the world's poorest - more than $130 million, according to the World Bank.
Rapper-turned-politician and former Kathmandu ‚Äčmayor Balendra Shah, ‚Äč35, of the centrist ‚Ā†Rastriya Swatantra Party is among the frontrunners for prime minister.
Facing him in the Jhapa 5 constituency is four-time prime minister Oli , 74, of ‚Äčthe Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist‚ÄďLeninist), vying for the top post again ‚Äčbut facing ‚Ā†an uphill battle to win back young voters who ousted him barely six months ago.
Other contenders include the centrist Nepali Congress party's 49-year-old Gagan Thapa and three-time prime ‚Ā†minister Pushpa ‚ÄčKamal Dahal, 71, who now leads the Nepali Communist ‚ÄčParty.
Oli has been a liberal communist since the 1990s while Dahal led a bloody Maoist insurgency for a ‚Äčdecade before joining mainstream politics in 2006.

item.title: Australia, Canada sign new deals on critical minerals
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia and Canada said on Thursday they had signed new agreements ‚ÄĆon critical minerals as Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney made a landmark address to the Australian parliament, a sign of the developing bond between the "middle powers".
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific region also taking in Japan and India. His ‚Äčstop in Australia included the first address to Australia's parliament by a Canadian leader since 2007.
"In ‚Äča world of great power rivalry, middle powers have a choice: compete for favour ‚Ā†or combine for strength," he told lawmakers.
Introducing Carney in parliament, Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said his address ‚Äčrepresented the closeness of the ties between the two nations.
"Australia and Canada are middle powers in a world ‚Äčthat is changing. We cannot change it back, but we can back ourselves, back our citizens, and back each other," he said.
Albanese told a press conference that Australia would join Canada's G7 critical minerals production alliance.
"We have agreed to deepen ‚Äčour relationship across several areas, building on our joint declaration of intent on critical minerals that we signed ‚Äčlast year," he told a press conference.
The G7 alliance is a Canada-led initiative to diversify and secure global critical minerals ‚ÄĆproduction ‚Ā†and supply.
Canada and Australia together produce about a third of global lithium and uranium, as well as more than 40% of global iron ore.
Western nations have been attempting to diversify their supply chains away from China, which still controls the majority of production and processing of critical minerals, essential for semiconductors and defence applications.
Canada believes ‚Äčthat the best way ‚Äčto address the issue ‚Ā†of concentrated supply of critical minerals is through a production alliance or a buyers' club rather than just a price floor, Energy and Mining Minister Tim Hodgson ‚Äčtold Reuters on Tuesday.
Australia has already allocated A$1.2 billion ($850 million) to build a ‚Äčcritical minerals stockpile, ‚Ā†beginning with antimony, gallium and rare earths.
That will now be more closely aligned with Canada's defence stockpiling regime that has a similar aim, Albanese said.
"There's a lot Canada and Australia can do together on critical minerals as producer ‚Ā†nations," ‚ÄčAustralian Resources Minister Madeleine King told Reuters ahead of Carney's visit.
Australia ‚Äčand Canada will also deepen cooperation in areas including defence and maritime security, trade and artificial intelligence, the two leaders said.
($1 = 1.4122 Australian ‚Äčdollars)

item.title: China's defence spending to rise 7% in 2026 vs 7.2% set for 2025
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China's 2026 defence spending will rise 7%, a copy of an official government ‚Äčreport reviewed by Reuters showed on Thursday, slightly ‚ÄĆlower than the 2025 increase of 7.2% but roughly in line with rises over the last decade.
Regional military attaches and security analysts ‚Äčare closely watching the budget as China's military ‚Äčmodernisation effort continues apace and its deployments increase ‚Ā†across East Asia amid regional tensions.
The on-going rise in ‚Äčspending also reflects President Xi Jinping's goal of completing ‚Äčthat modernisation by 2035, with China's military developing new advanced missiles, ships, submarines and surveillance technologies.
The budget announcement comes amid an on-going ‚Äčcorruption crackdown that was sparked by the highest-profile ‚Äčpurge of upper military ranks in decades.
China's two most senior generals ‚ÄĆhave ‚Ā†become ensnared in disciplinary investigations, with Zhang Youxia - a veteran military ally of Xi - placed under investigation in January and He Weidong expelled in October last year.
The International ‚ÄčInstitute of ‚ÄčStrategic Studies said ‚Ā†in a report last month that rises in Chinese military spending were consistently ‚Äčoutpacing the rest of Asia amid a global ‚Äčsurge ‚Ā†in defence budgets.
China's share of the total amount spent on the military in Asia grew to almost 44% in ‚Ā†2025, ‚Äčup from an average of ‚Äč37% between 2010 and 2020, the report said.

item.title: China to support Hong Kong's integration with country's overall development
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China will ensure ‚ÄčHong Kong ‚ÄĆis more effectively governed in ‚Äčaccordance with ‚Äčthe law, in ‚Ā†a copy ‚Äčof an official ‚Äčgovernment report reviewed by Reuters on ‚ÄčThursday.
The ‚Äčreport also said China ‚ÄĆwould ‚Ā†support Hong Kong and Macau in better ‚Äčintegrating ‚Äčand ‚Ā†contributing to the ‚Äčcountry's overall ‚Äčdevelopment.

item.title: Australia to join G7 critical minerals alliance, Canada's Carney says
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia and Canada on Thursday signed a series of new agreements on critical ‚Äčminerals, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said, including Australia ‚ÄĆjoining the G7 minerals alliance.
Western nations have been attempting to diversify their supply chains away from China, which still controls the majority of production and ‚Äčprocessing of critical minerals, essential for semiconductors and defence applications.
"Earlier ‚Äčtoday, we signed a series of new agreements on ‚Ā†critical minerals, including Australia joining the G7 minerals alliance ‚Äď the largest ‚Äčgrouping of trusted democratic mineral reserves in the world," Carney said ‚Äčin a speech to Australia's parliament, on his final day of a three-day visit to the country.
The two countries produce around a third of global ‚Äčlithium and uranium, as well as over 40% of global iron ‚Äčore.
Canada believes that the best way to address the issue of concentrated supply ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†critical minerals is through a production alliance or a buyers' club rather than just a price floor, Energy and Mining Minister Tim Hodgson told Reuters on Tuesday.
Australia has already allocated funds to build ‚Äča critical minerals ‚Äčstockpile, beginning ‚Ā†with antimony and gallium.
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific region also taking in Japan ‚Äčand India, with his stop in Australia aimed ‚Äčat bolstering ‚Ā†relations between the two so-called "middle powers".
As well as critical minerals, Australia and Canada are also expected to deepen cooperation in areas including defence ‚Ā†and ‚Äčmaritime security, trade and artificial intelligence, ‚ÄčCarney's office said ahead of the visit.
(Reporting by Alasdair Pal in Sydney, editing by Jamie ‚ÄčFreed)
(( Alasdair.Pal@thomsonreuters.com , opens new tab ; +61 291 717 228; Reuters Messaging: ))
Keywords: AUSTRALIA CANADA/

item.title: Iran launches wave of missiles at Israel; US Republicans block measure to halt US air campaign
item.description:
DUBAI/JERUSALEM/ANKARA, March 5 (Reuters) - Iran launched a wave of missiles at Israel early on Thursday, sending millions of residents into bomb shelters as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran entered ‚Äčits sixth day and just hours after moves to halt the U.S. air assault were blocked in Washington.
Republican senators in Washington voted against a motion aimed ‚ÄĆat stopping the air campaign and requiring that military action be authorised by Congress, leaving President Donald Trump's power to direct the war largely unbound, as the conflict continues to widen across the Middle East and beyond.
The U.S. Senate voted 53 to 47 not to advance the resolution, largely along party lines, with all but one Republican voting against the procedural motion and all but one Democrat supporting it.
The U.S.‚ÄďIran war has ‚Äčwidened sharply, with a U.S. submarine sinking an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka on Wednesday, killing at least 80 people, and NATO air defences destroying an Iranian ballistic ‚Äčmissile fired towards Turkey.
The escalation came as the powerful son of Iran's slain supreme leader emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, suggesting Tehran ‚Ā†was not about to buckle to pressure from the United States and Israel's military campaign that has killed hundreds and convulsed global markets .
The missile incident is the first time that Turkey ‚Äď ‚Äčwhich borders Iran and has NATO's second-largest military ‚Äď has been drawn into the conflict, but U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said there was no sense that it would trigger the Atlantic ‚Äčalliance's collective-defence clause.
The war continued to paralyse shipping through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, choking off vital Middle East oil and gas flows. Trump has pledged to provide insurance and naval escorts for ships to contain soaring costs, with oil prices rising on Thursday . At least 200 vessels remain anchored off the coast, according to Reuters estimates.
The U.S. Navy will escort oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz "as soon as it ‚Äčcan" but is focused on the conflict for now, U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Fox News on Wednesday.
"No, not yet ... We'll do that as soon as we can. ‚ÄčRight now, our Navy, and of course, our military, is focused on other things, which is disarming this Iranian regime," Wright said, when asked if any commercial vessels had requested U.S. Navy assistance in ‚ÄĆthe Gulf.
Asian ‚Ā†shares rallied on Thursday after days of sharp losses, while U.S. stocks closed up on Wednesday on hopes that the war might end soon. Some traders said the improved sentiment followed a New York Times report that Iranian intelligence had reached out to the CIA early in the war about a path towards ending it.
A source from the Iranian intelligence ministry rejected the article as "absolute lies and psychological warfare in the midst of war", Iran's semi-official news agency Tasnim reported.
Repatriation flights departed the Middle East on Wednesday as governments rushed to bring home tens of thousands ‚Äčof citizens stranded by the war.
Commercial air traffic ‚Äčremained largely absent across much of ‚Ā†the region, with major Gulf hubs including Dubai, the world's busiest airport for international passengers, affected.
Plans were in doubt for a funeral for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei , 86, killed by Israeli forces on Saturday in the first assassination of a nation's top ruler by an airstrike.
The ‚Äčbody had been expected to lie in state in a vast Tehran mosque from Wednesday evening, but Iran announced that three days ‚Äčof farewell ceremonies had ‚Ā†been indefinitely postponed and no funeral date had been announced.
Two Iranian sources, speaking on condition of anonymity, told Reuters that Mojtaba Khamenei , son of Iran's slain supreme leader, was not in Tehran when his father was killed.
Iran said the Assembly of Experts that will select the new leader would announce its decision soon, only the second time it has done so since the Islamic Republic's founding ‚Ā†in 1979.
Assembly ‚Äčmember Ayatollah Ahmad Khatami told state TV the candidates had already been identified but did not name them.
Israel ‚Äčsaid it would hunt down whoever was chosen.
Other candidates for supreme leader include Hassan Khomeini , grandson of the Islamic Republic's founder and a champion of the reformist faction sidelined in recent decades.

item.title: Pakistani man says Iran forced him into plot to kill Trump, media say
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man accused of planning to kill President Donald Trump told jurors on Wednesday that he did not willingly work with Iran's elite ‚ÄčIslamic Revolutionary Guards Corps to devise the plot, media said.
The Justice ‚ÄĆDepartment accused Asif Merchant of trying to recruit people in the United States in the plan targeting Trump and other U.S. politicians in retaliation for Washington's killing of the ‚ÄčCorps' top commander, Qassem Soleimani .
The Corps has a central role in Iran, with ‚Äčits combination of military and economic power and an intelligence ‚Ā†network.
"I was not wanting to do this so willingly," the New York ‚ÄčTimes quoted Merchant as telling a court during his trial for terrorism and ‚Äčmurder-for-hire charges, adding that he participated to protect his family in Tehran.
Prosecutors rejected Merchant's claim, citing a "lack of evidentiary support for a true duress or coercion," according to a ‚Äčletter sent on Tuesday to the judge in the case dating from ‚Äč2024.
According to the newspaper, Merchant said he had never been ordered to kill a specific ‚ÄĆperson ‚Ā†but that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
In addition to Trump, these were Joe Biden, the president at the time; Nikki Haley, who unsuccessfully sought the Republican nomination for ‚Äčthe 2024 presidential ‚Äčelection.
Lawyers for Merchant ‚Ā†did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The White House did not immediately comment.
The trial started last ‚Äčweek, days before Trump ordered strikes on Iran carried out ‚Äčwith Israel ‚Ā†that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and top officials in the Middle Eastern nation.
Trump cited an alleged Iranian plot when he spoke to ABC News on ‚Ā†Sunday about ‚Äča joint U.S.-Israeli operation that killed Khamenei, ‚Äčsaying, "I got him before he got me."
Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump and other U.S. ‚Äčofficials.

item.title: Iranian sailors recovering in Sri Lankan hospital after US submarine attack, authorities say
item.description:
GALLE, Sri Lanka, March 5 (Reuters) - Iranian sailors who survived a U.S. submarine strike in the Indian Ocean were recovering at ‚Äča hospital in the Sri Lankan port city of ‚ÄĆGalle, authorities said on Thursday, a day after at least 87 were killed in the attack.
Authorities at the National Hospital in Galle and ‚Äčnavy sources said 87 bodies were brought in by ‚Äčmilitary rescuers who responded to an early-morning distress call ‚Ā†from the IRIS Dena on Wednesday.
Search and rescue operations for ‚Äčan estimated 60 people on board who remain unaccounted for would ‚Äčcontinue on Thursday, authorities said.
The 32 rescued sailors were being treated for minor injuries and could be released from hospital on Thursday, authorities said. ‚ÄčTwo policemen guarded the entrance to ward No. 58 of ‚Äčthe hospital as nurses milled about and doctors conducted morning rounds.
The attack, which ‚ÄĆdramatically ‚Ā†widens the scope of the war, happened hundreds of miles across the Indian Ocean from the Gulf, where U.S. and Israeli forces are striking Iran and Tehran is retaliating with missile and ‚Äčdrone attacks.
"An American ‚Äčsubmarine sank an ‚Ā†Iranian warship that thought it was safe in international waters," U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äčat the Pentagon. "Instead, it was sunk by a ‚Äčtorpedo. ‚Ā†Quiet death."
A Pentagon video purporting to have captured the attack showed the warship being hit by a huge explosion, which blew apart ‚Ā†the ‚Äčrear of the vessel, lifting it from ‚Äčthe water, and causing it to begin sinking from the stern.

item.title: Singapore police announce arrest of 3 linked to alleged scam syndicate Prince Group
item.description: SINGAPORE, March 5 (Reuters) - Singapore police have announced arrests of three Singaporeans as part of investigations into alleged transnational scam ‚Äčsyndicate Prince Group and its founder and chairman, Chen ‚ÄĆZhi , who is in Chinese detention.

item.title: Pentagon identifies two soldiers killed in Iran war
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 4 (Reuters) - The Pentagon on Wednesday identified two more soldiers who were killed in the war ‚Äčagainst Iran.
The two Army Reserve soldiers died ‚ÄĆon Sunday in a drone attack on a U.S. military facility in Port Shuaiba, Kuwait that also killed four other reservists .
The ‚ÄčPentagon said Major Jeffrey O'Brien, 45, of Iowa, ‚Äčwas killed in the attack and announced ‚Ā†the "believed death" of Chief Warrant Officer 3 Robert M. ‚ÄčMarzan, 54, of Sacramento, California. Positive identification of Marzan ‚Äčwill be completed by the medical examiner, the Pentagon said.
Both O'Brien and Marzan served in the 103rd Sustainment Command from ‚ÄčDes Moines, Iowa, part of the Army's global ‚Äčlogistics and supply operation.
