* U.S. weekly jobless claims (8:30 AM EST), January import prices (8:30 AM EST), ‚Ā†Q4 productivity (8:30 ‚ÄčAM EST)
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every ‚Äčweekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect ‚Äčthe views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Iran reveals stretched Trump tariff trades
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Financial markets have swung from fear to hope, pricing in the inflationary impact of the war in the Middle East but also seeming oddly blas√© about how long ‚Äčthe disruption will last. Tellingly, in both bouts of optimism and pessimism this week, the winning trades of the past ‚ÄĆyear appear to have run their course.
At first glance, financial markets seem to be painting a coherent picture since Donald Trump began his Iran war. Brent hovered around $83 a barrel on Thursday ‚ÄĒ up 21% on the month but far from the $100 plus levels expected under a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The implication ‚Äčis that blocking this vital trade artery for more than a few weeks would be so devastating that the countries involved ‚Äčwon‚Äôt allow it.
Other markets reflect a similar view that the war will push up inflation a bit but ‚Ā†not be catastrophic. Equity indexes are slightly lower, energy stocks are higher, and the U.S. dollar has risen 1.3% since the attack on ‚ÄčIran thanks to America‚Äôs status as a net oil exporter. The S&P 500 has held up better than European and emerging market indexes, which ‚Äčrely more on imported energy. Government bonds have edged down on the assumption that central banks will turn a little more hawkish .
But many other current trends make little sense. Gold, a traditional inflation hedge, is down about 3% since the attacks. Consumer staples shares, think food and cleaning products, have been beaten by consumer discretionary ‚Äčones ‚ÄĒ apparel, travel and cars ‚ÄĒ despite being less sensitive to household spending. ‚ÄúGrowth‚ÄĚ stocks, normally hurt most by rising rates, have beaten cheaper ‚Äúvalue‚ÄĚ names, like ‚ÄčUniversal Health Services. This includes software firms such as Adobe (ADBE.O) , opens new tab , which had been battered by AI fears and had no obvious reason to do well. These movements ‚ÄĆare mostly ‚Ā†the opposite of what happened in 2022 after a similar energy shock: Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine.
What ties this together is simple: assets that had been outperforming are now lagging, and vice versa. The year‚Äôs winning trades before the war were extensions of strategies that surged after Donald Trump‚Äôs tariff package last year. Many had become overstretched. So-called dedollarisation wagers were so popular that net short bets on the greenback using derivatives hit ‚Äča five-year high of $22.8 billion in ‚ÄčFebruary, according to the Commodity ‚Ā†Futures Trading Commission. Gold seemed to have become an unbeatable all weather asset , which is unsustainable. Fund flows into U.S. staples were at record highs.
Some of those underlying trends could theoretically continue: central banks may keep buying ‚Äčgold, and global investors look for alternatives to the dollar. But it is telling that, even as ‚Äčmarkets rebounded Wednesday, ‚Ā†the old winning wagers kept underperforming ‚Äď staples, for instance, fell another 0.5%. Every investment playbook eventually tires, and this crisis has revealed how worn out some pages already were.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia charges ex-deputy defence minister with corruption
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Russia has charged a former deputy defence minister ‚Äčwith corruption, investigators said on ‚ÄĆThursday, in the latest of a series of prosecutions against top ‚Äčfigures in the military ‚Äčestablishment.
The state Investigative Committee said ‚Ā†Ruslan Tsalikov was accused of ‚Äčembezzlement, money laundering and bribe-taking.
Tsalikov ‚Äčserved as first deputy defence minister from 2015 to 2024, when a ‚Äčseries of corruption cases led ‚Äčto the arrest of a string of ‚ÄĆgenerals, ‚Ā†deputy ministers and civilian defence civilians.
Another former deputy minister, Timur Ivanov, was jailed for 13 ‚Äčyears in ‚ÄčJuly ‚Ā†2025.
Ivanov and Tsalikov served under Sergei Shoigu, a ‚Äčlongtime ally of Russian ‚ÄčPresident ‚Ā†Vladimir Putin. Shoigu was removed as defence minister in 2024, ‚Ā†and ‚Äčappointed secretary of ‚ÄčRussia's Security Council.

item.title: UK sending four extra Typhoon jets to Qatar, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Thursday that four additional ‚ÄčTyphoon fighter jets would be sent to ‚ÄĆQatar as the conflict in the Middle East escalates, insisting that the UK has the right plan for defence.
Britain's ‚Äčcautious response to the Iran crisis and ‚Äča drone attack on its key military base ‚Ā†in Cyprus have led to doubts among ‚Äčpartners about its military effectiveness.
U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčhas also criticised Starmer for failing to provide sufficient support for his strikes on Iran.
Starmer told a press conference Britain ‚Äčhad the right plan and had already ‚Äčbeen pre-deploying military equipment across the region before the start ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†the war.
"My focus is providing calm, level headed leadership in the national interest," Starmer said.
"That means deploying our military and diplomatic strength to protect our ‚Äčpeople, and ‚Äčit means ‚Ā†having the strength to stand firm by our values and our principles, ‚Äčno matter the pressure to do otherwise."
He ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†Britain was sending four additional typhoon jets to join the squadron in Qatar to strengthen our defensive operations there and ‚Ā†in ‚Äčthe region.
"We have the right ‚Äčplan for defence," he said.

item.title: Israel orders residents to leave southern Beirut
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel warned residents to leave Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs on Thursday, sparking panic as people fled a swathe of the Lebanese capital on the fourth day of full-scale ‚Äčhostilities between the Iran-backed group and Israel.
An Israeli military spokesperson, in ‚Äča post on X, ordered residents of the southern suburbs ‚Ā†to move east and north, posting a map showing four sprawling districts of ‚Äčthe capital he said they must leave, part of the area is adjacent ‚Äčto Beirut airport.
"Save your lives, evacuate your homes immediately," Israeli military spokesperson Avichay Adraee posted, saying that any movement southwards may endanger their lives.
Roads out of the suburbs were clogged ‚Äčas people fled by car and on foot, television footage showed. Gunfire ‚Äčwas heard in the southern suburbs, warning residents to leave.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in ‚ÄĆthe Middle ‚Ā†East on Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire, sparking intensified Israeli airstrikes largely focused on the southern suburbs, southern Lebanon and eastern Lebanon.
On Wednesday, the Israeli military ordered residents to leave an area of southern Lebanon amounting to about 8% of its ‚Äčterritory.
Israeli bombardment and warnings ‚Äčhave already forced ‚Ā†tens of thousands of Lebanese to flee homes in the southern suburbs and the south this week. The Lebanese ‚Äčhealth ministry has said 77 people have been killed.
There ‚Äčhave been ‚Ā†no reports of fatalities in Israel as a result of Hezbollah attacks.
Beirut's predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern suburbs are among the capital's most densely populated areas. The ‚Ā†area ‚Äčwas pounded by Israeli airstrikes during a war ‚Äčwith Hezbollah in 2024, and during a previous war with Israel in 2006.

item.title: Algerian military aircraft crashes after takeoff from Boufarik base, two crew killed
item.description: March 5 (Reuters) - A small ‚ÄčAlgerian military ‚ÄĆtransport aircraft ‚Äčcrashed ‚Äčshortly after taking ‚Ā†off ‚Äčfrom Boufarik ‚Äčair base on ‚ÄčThursday, ‚Äčkilling two crew ‚ÄĆmembers ‚Ā†and injuring four others, ‚ÄčEnnahar ‚ÄčTV ‚Ā†reported ‚Äčciting the ‚Äčdefence ‚Ā†ministry.

item.title: US opposes IAEA board resolution condemning attacks on Ukraine's power grid
item.description:
VIENNA, March 5 (Reuters) - The United States joined Russia, China, and ‚ÄĆNiger on Thursday in opposing a resolution adopted by the U.N. nuclear watchdog's board denouncing attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure as a threat to nuclear safety, diplomats said.
The resolution, passed by the ‚ÄčInternational Atomic Energy Agency's 35-nation Board of Governors, is the seventh on Ukraine ‚Äčsince Russia invaded its neighbour four years ago.
This is the first time ‚Ā†the United States has opposed one.
"While we continue supporting the IAEA's work in-country, ‚Äčwe do not support the Board's current consideration of an unnecessary resolution that does not ‚Äčhelp achieve peace between Ukraine and Russia," the United States said in its statement to the board before the vote.
U.S. President Donald Trump has in the past year pressured Ukraine for a quick peace ‚Äčdeal that could involve ceding land to Russia. Ukraine has ruled out giving up ‚Äčterritory.
The IAEA board passed the resolution with 20 votes in favour, including France, Britain, Australia, Canada, South ‚ÄĆAfrica ‚Ā†and Argentina, 10 abstentions and the four votes against, diplomats at the closed-door meeting said. Brazil, Egypt, Morocco and Saudi Arabia were among the abstentions.
The resolution's wording was not as strong as previous ones.
The text seen by Reuters said the board "reemphasizes that attacks ‚Äčtargeting Ukraine's energy infrastructure ‚Äčfor the off-site power ‚Ā†supply of nuclear power plants, including at the ZNPP (Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant), represent a direct threat to nuclear safety and security".
The ‚Äčmove follows the United States' abstention at the U.N. General Assembly on ‚Äčthe anniversary ‚Ā†of the invasion in February when it passed a resolution supporting Ukraine, backing its international borders and voicing concern over intensifying Russian attacks on civilians and critical energy infrastructure.
That resolution ‚Ā†passed ‚Äčwith 107 countries voting in favour, 12 against, and ‚Äč51 abstentions.
The United States said at the time that the General Assembly resolution included language likely to distract ‚Äčfrom ongoing negotiations aimed at ending the conflict.

item.title: Cartels can only be defeated with military power, White House's Miller says
item.description:
MIAMI, March 5 (Reuters) - Cartels operating in the ‚ÄčWestern Hemisphere can ‚ÄĆonly be defeated with military power, ‚ÄčWhite House ‚ÄčDeputy Chief of Staff ‚Ā†Stephen Miller ‚Äčsaid on Thursday.
"We ‚Äčhave learned after decades of effort is that ‚Äčthere is ‚Äčnot a criminal justice ‚ÄĆsolution ‚Ā†to the cartel problem," Miller told Latin American ‚Äčdefense leaders ‚Äčgathered ‚Ā†at the U.S. Southern ‚ÄčCommand headquarters ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†a conference.

item.title: Greek seafarers strike over crews stranded in the Gulf by Iran war
item.description:
PIRAEUS, Greece, March 5 (Reuters) - Greek seafarers began a 24-hour strike on Thursday, halting local ferry services, as they protested over vessel crews stranded in the Gulf amid the escalating ‚ÄčMiddle East war, and demanded the area is declared a war risk ‚ÄĆzone to enable their repatriation.
The Iran conflict threatens Gulf ports and has already disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery accounting for about 20% of global oil and gas supply. ‚ÄčAlthough the Strait is not shut, Iran has warned that it will fire ‚Äčon any ship trying to pass through.
Greece is a dominant force in ‚Ā†global shipping, controlling one of the world‚Äôs largest merchant fleets. More than 325 ships of ‚ÄčGreek interests, their crews including dozens of Greek seafarers, are in the wider Gulf ‚Äčarea.
"We demand that all of our colleagues, currently in the dangerous Gulf area, the Gulf of Oman and the Red Sea, are evacuated and safely repatriated," said Angelos Galanopoulos, head of Greece's lower engine ‚Äčcrews' union Stephenson.
Dozens of protesters rallied outside the shipowners union near the port of ‚ÄčPiraeus and spray-painted: "No sacrifice for profits and wars" on the ground. A motorcycle convoy drove to ‚ÄĆthe shipping ‚Ā†ministry.
The International Maritime Organization says it is concerned for about roughly 20,000 seafarers in the region. At least nine ships have been damaged in strikes since the conflict began on Saturday.
Seafarers usually have the contractual right to refuse to sail in designated war zones ‚Äčand demand repatriation at ‚Äčthe shipowner's expense.
Dozens ‚Ā†of ships remained at anchor in open waters off the coast of major Gulf producers and dozens of oil tankers were inside the ‚ÄčStrait, ship tracking data showed.
Risks include not only attacks but also ‚Äčpotential food ‚Ā†and supply shortages said Apostolis Kypraios, head of the marine engineers' union PEMEN.
"The government and shipownwers are responsible for the people trapped in war areas," Kypraios said.
"We demand that they ‚Ā†find ‚Äča solution for our colleagues to return home. Their ‚Äčfamilies are worried and seafarers don't know if they will return alive, if they will be injured."

item.title: As Iran conflict rages, Pope Leo asks God to help leaders renounce war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Pope Leo released a video on Thursday praying that God would help world leaders renounce war ‚Äčas a means of resolving conflicts in an unusual ‚ÄĆappeal as the U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran pressed on for the sixth day.
"Lord, enlighten the leaders of the nations, so they may have the courage ‚Äčto abandon projects of death," the pontiff said in the ‚Äčvideo message.
"Today we lift up our prayer for peace ‚Ā†in the world, asking that nations renounce weapons and choose the ‚Äčpath of dialogue and diplomacy," he said.
Leo releases a video message ‚Äčeach month to announce his prayer intentions for that month. The pope's intention for March is "for disarmament and peace".
It was unclear if Thursday's video was ‚Äčcreated specifically to respond to the U.S.-Israeli military campaign, which has ‚Äčset off a regional war with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and ‚ÄĆIraq, ‚Ā†and Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
The Vatican did not immediately respond to a question about when the video was recorded.
The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the U.S.-Israeli strikes undermined international law and said ‚Äčnations did not ‚Äčhave a ‚Ā†right to launch "preventive wars", an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised ‚Äčas having a right to 'preventive war' ... the entire ‚Äčworld could ‚Ā†risk going up in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's secretary of state, said in an interview with Vatican News.
In his video, the ‚Ā†pope ‚Äčasked God to help the world ‚Äčunderstand "that true security does not come from control fuelled by fear but from trust, justice ‚Äčand solidarity among peoples."

item.title: Diplomats in Riyadh ordered to shelter in place due to potential threat, sources say
item.description:
RIYADH, March 5 (Reuters) - Diplomats and staff at ‚ÄĆembassies in Riyadh's Diplomatic Quarter were told to shelter ‚Äčin place on ‚ÄčThursday due to a potential ‚Ā†but unspecified threat, ‚Äčfour people with direct ‚Äčknowledge of the matter said.
They did not have further details, ‚Äčand the Saudi government ‚Äčmedia office did not immediately ‚ÄĆrespond ‚Ā†to a request for comment.
The gates to the Diplomatic Quarter, a neighborhood on ‚Äčthe western ‚Äčedge ‚Ā†of the Saudi capital that houses ‚Äčmost foreign missions ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the country, were closed as of Thursday afternoon, ‚Ā†two ‚Äčof the people ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO's Rutte 'not deaf' to criticism of his Trump praise, offers more
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said on Thursday he was "not ‚Äčdeaf" to criticism that he goes overboard in praising Donald Trump but argued the U.S. president ‚ÄĆdeserved it.
However, in an interview with Reuters , Rutte also offered praise for Spain - a target of Trump's ire for refusing to allow U.S. military bases on its soil to be used in the American-Israeli assault on Iran .
Rutte, a Dutch former prime minister, was selected to lead NATO partly ‚Äčdue to his strong bond with Trump , which diplomats say has helped keep the military alliance together despite the ‚ÄčU.S. president's criticism of the organisation and its members.
But some officials, analysts and commentators have ‚Ā†accused Rutte of going over the top in lavishing praise on Trump, arguing it is demeaning to Europe and ‚Äčdownplays the continent's ability to defend itself.
"I hear the criticism, obviously. I'm not deaf," Rutte said in the interview at ‚ÄčNATO headquarters in Brussels.
Famously, at a NATO summit, Rutte likened Trump to a "daddy" intervening in last year's conflict between Israel and Iran.
Most recently, he has offered strong backing for the new U.S.-Israeli military action against Iran, even though leaders of ‚Äčsome NATO nations such as Spain and Turkey have been critical of the intervention and some others have not endorsed ‚Äčit.
In the interview, Rutte said Trump had played a crucial role in getting NATO allies to agree a new spending target of ‚ÄĆ5% ‚Ā†of GDP last year, composed of 3.5% on core defence and 1.5% on broader security-related spending.
The previous NATO target was to spend 2% of GDP on core defence.
Rutte also called the U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran "decisive action to take out this capability of Iran to pose a threat as an exporter of terrorism and chaos to the Middle East, to Europe, ‚Äčto the world".
"I ‚Ā†think if a president of a country is ... providing that type of leadership, some praise is warranted," he said.
On Tuesday, Trump threatened to impose a full U.S. trade embargo on ‚ÄčSpain due to the row over the military bases and also renewed criticism of ‚ÄčMadrid for not ‚Ā†signing up to the 5% defence spending target.
Asked about the spat between Trump and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez , Rutte offered praise for Spain's contribution to NATO.
"Their troops are part of many foreign land forces, many initiatives, many NATO missions. I really want ‚Ā†to commend ‚Äčthem for that," he said.
In response to a question about whether ‚Äčhe had spoken to Trump and Sanchez to lower the tension between them, Rutte said: "When there are debates between allies, I always try to stay ‚Äča bit muted, and therefore being able, if necessary, to help a bit."

item.title: Zelenskiy taunts Hungary's Orban for blocking aid to Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy taunted Hungarian leader Viktor Orban ‚ÄĆon Thursday for blocking a 90 billion euro ($104 billion) aid package from the European Union critical to Kyiv's survival as it fends off Russia.
Ukraine relies on financial aid from partners to cover its budgetary needs while it funnels most state funds ‚Äčtoward defence. EU member Hungary, which has maintained cordial ties with Moscow, vetoed the package this ‚Äčmonth amid a dispute over oil supplies.
"We hope a certain person in the EU will ‚Ā†not keep blocking the 90 billion... and Ukrainian soldiers will have weapons," Zelenskiy told reporters in Kyiv.
"Otherwise, we ‚Äčwill give the address of this person to our armed forces, our guys. Let them call him, speak ‚Äčwith him in their own language."
The remark will likely heighten the tension between the two leaders during Russia's four-year war on Ukraine, which Orban - who faces elections on April 12 - has been careful not to condemn.
Hungary's veto of the aid package, ‚Äčas well as new EU sanctions on Russia, came in response to what it says was Ukraine deliberately ‚Äčcutting supplies from the Druzhba pipeline carrying Russian crude to Europe.
Kyiv says the oil flows stopped after a Russian attack ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†pipeline infrastructure in January and it is fixing the damage as fast as it can. Zelenskiy said on Thursday that the Soviet-era pipeline could be operational within a month and a half.
"They (the Russians) are killing us, and we're supposed to give poor little Orban oil, because without it he won't win elections?" he said.
Orban has ‚Äčmade the Ukraine war ‚Äča focal point in his ‚Ā†campaign for re-election.
Ukraine's troops are fending off grinding Russian assaults along several parts of the 1,200-kilometer front line, as Kyiv faces U.S. pressure to secure peace while ‚Äčfending off Russian demands to cede land.
Despite being outgunned and outmanned, Kyiv's army ‚Äčhas retaken territory ‚Ā†in recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023, according to the Finland-based Black Bird Group analysis team.
European foreign ministers had tried unsuccessfully this month to persuade Budapest not to punish ‚Ā†Ukraine ‚Äčfor delays restarting the pipeline, which also supplies Slovakia, another EU ‚Äčneighbour of Ukraine.
Slovakia, whose Prime Minister Robert Fico is also sympathetic to the Kremlin, said it would refuse requests from Kyiv for emergency ‚Äčelectricity supplies until oil flows resume via the Druzhba pipeline.
($1 = 0.8625 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump tells Reuters US should have a role in choosing Iran's next leader, encourages Kurdish opposition
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he wants the United States to play a role in ‚Äčchoosing Iran's next leader and encouraged Kurdish opposition groups to go on the ‚ÄĆoffensive.
Trump said in a telephone interview it was very early in the process of picking a new leader but that the late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba was an unlikely choice, without elaborating.
"We want ‚Äčto be involved in the process of choosing the person who is going ‚Äčto lead Iran into the future," Trump said. "We don't have to go ‚Ā†back every five years and do this again and again."
Trump also encouraged Iranian Kurdish forces to go ‚Äčon the offensive.
"I think it's wonderful that they want to do that, I'd be all ‚Äčfor it," the president said.
When asked if the U.S. would provide or had offered air cover, he responded, "I can't tell you that," but added that the objective for the Kurds would be "to win."
"If they're ‚Äčgoing to do that, that's good," Trump added.
Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United ‚ÄčStates in recent days about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the western part ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†the country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based on the Iran-Iraq border in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, as the ‚ÄčUnited States and Israel ‚Äčpound Iranian targets ‚Ā†with bombs and missiles.
Trump also signaled confidence that the major shipping route near Iran, the Strait of Hormuz, will remain open.
Closing the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz , a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which ‚Äča fifth of ‚Ā†the world‚Äôs crude oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has been one of Iran's main objectives, and shipping through the crucial energy artery has ground to a near halt after Iranian ‚Ā†hits ‚Äčon six vessels.
"They have no navy, you know the ‚Äčnavy is now at the bottom of the sea," Trump said. "I'm watching Hormuz very closely."

item.title: Isolated and under fire: Iran strikes out as Russia and China stand aside
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - With its supreme leader killed and its war machine under relentless U.S. pressure , Iran now stands largely alone - its longtime partners Russia and China offering nothing more than diplomatic condemnations and expressions of concern.
Tehran has responded to the U.S. ‚Äčand Israeli attacks by widening the conflict beyond the Middle East, firing missiles and drones with an impact that is reverberating through global energy markets , rattling capitals from Washington to Beijing, and ‚ÄĆparalyzing the shipping that carries 20% of the world's oil supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iranian missiles reached as far as Cyprus, Azerbaijan , Turkey and Gulf states, taking the war to their doorstep by targeting critical businesses, energy infrastructure and U.S. bases. Oil facilities, refineries and key supply routes were hit, causing severe disruption to crude and natural gas supplies.
With the Strait of Hormuz already shut, the attacks have sent energy prices soaring, destabilising global markets and forcing major economies to scramble , underscoring the world‚Äôs exposure to the ‚Äčfallout from Tehran‚Äôs response to the war.
Russia and China‚Äôs restraint reflects a cold calculation, analysts say: intervening as Iran faces Israel and the United States would bring high costs, limited gains and unpredictable ‚Äčrisks - burdens neither power appears willing to shoulder.
‚ÄúPutin has other priorities, and chief among them is Ukraine,‚ÄĚ said Anna Borshchevskaya, a Russia expert at the Washington Institute. ‚ÄúIt ‚Ā†would be foolish for Russia to go into a direct military confrontation with the United States.‚ÄĚ
A senior Russian source said ‚Äúthe escalation in and around Iran and the Gulf is already diverting attention from the war in Ukraine. That‚Äôs ‚Äčjust a fact. Everything else is just emotion about a ‚Äėfallen ally‚Äô,‚ÄĚ the source said.
Beijing and Moscow have both helped Iran build military capacity to counter U.S. and Israeli pressure, supplying missiles, air‚ÄĎdefence systems and technology intended to ‚Äčbolster deterrence, complicate U.S. operations and raise the costs of attack. That support, however, now appears capped.
China has spent years embedding itself in Middle Eastern diplomacy, while Russia has cast Iran as a pillar of its anti-Western alignment.
Yet as the conflict flared, both powers were constrained -China by its dependence on Gulf energy and trade and by security priorities in Asia, and Russia by a grinding war in Ukraine that has sapped its capacity to shield partners and sharpened its need to preserve ties with ‚Äčoil-rich Gulf states.
The result is a stark paradox: Iran remains strategically useful to both, but not useful enough to fight for.
With Russia‚Äôs military, diplomatic bandwidth and economic resources still absorbed by the war in Ukraine, President Vladimir ‚ÄčPutin‚Äôs priority is to avoid escalation with Washington and safeguard Russia‚Äôs interests in the Middle East, rather than gamble on Iran‚Äôs battlefield fortunes.
‚ÄúIf Russia had supported Iran directly, it would have alienated the Gulf states and Israel,‚ÄĚ Borshchevskaya said. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs not ‚ÄĆwhat Putin wants."
Beijing‚Äôs ‚Ā†restrained response reflects a longstanding strategy: avoiding binding security commitments far from its core interests.
Unlike the United States, whose alliances rest on mutual defence obligations, China prefers partnerships built on trade, investment and arms sales, ties that stop short of dragging it into costly conflicts beyond East Asia, said Evan A. Feigenbaum from the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
Beijing, one of the world‚Äôs largest trading powers and energy buyers, maintains ties with Iran and Gulf Sunni rivals, and in Latin America, never placed all its bets on Venezuela alone.
"If Beijing wanted to do more, it won‚Äôt redirect strategic attention or military assets from core security," argues Henry Tugendhat ‚Äčat the Washington Institute. "It only cares about its name ‚Äčabroad. It cares about Taiwan, the South China ‚Ā†Sea, and perceived threats from the U.S. and Japan.‚ÄĚ
The conflict may even carry advantages for Beijing. From the sidelines, China can watch as U.S. forces are tied down far from East Asia and military stockpiles are depleted, while gaining a real-time view of American capabilities and operations, insights that could inform its thinking on a future ‚ÄčTaiwan scenario.
China‚Äôs key vulnerability remains energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries some 45% of its oil imports. But Beijing has built strategic reserves ‚Äčand substantial volumes of Iranian oil ‚Ā†already sitting in tankers or storage, experts say.
They say the crisis has allowed Moscow and Beijing to recast themselves as mediators. China said Foreign Minister Wang Yi has spoken with European and Arab ministers to press for dialogue, while Putin has held similar calls with Gulf leaders and Iranian officials.
Russia also sees concrete benefits: rising oil prices strengthen its war economy, and a U.S. administration tied up in the Middle East has less bandwidth for ‚Ā†Ukraine.
Russia does not ‚Äčbenefit from the collapse of the Iranian regime, but it is also not tying its fate to Tehran‚Äôs survival, said Borshchevskaya. Moscow ‚Äčis hedging, preserving flexibility, regardless of the conflict‚Äôs outcome, and would build ties with any new government, even one aligned with Washington.
The Russian source pointed to Syria as a precedent. Despite backing ousted president Bashar al‚ÄĎAssad for years, Moscow retained its Mediterranean bases and quickly ‚Äčbuilt ties with Syria‚Äôs new leader, Ahmed al‚ÄĎSharaa, underscoring its willingness to trade loyalty for long-term leverage.

item.title: Trump says he needs to be involved in selecting Iran's next leader, Axios reports
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios ‚Äčon Thursday that he needs to ‚ÄĆbe personally involved in selecting Iran's next leader.
"Khamenei's son is unacceptable to me. ‚ÄčWe want someone that will ‚Äčbring harmony and peace to Iran," ‚Ā†Axios quoted Trump as saying ‚Äčin an interview.
"I have to be ‚Äčinvolved in the appointment, like with Delcy (Rodriguez) in Venezuela," Trump said.
Mojtaba Khamenei, son of ‚ÄčIran's late Supreme Leader, has survived ‚Äčthe U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†which his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed, Iranian sources told Reuters on Wednesday.
A mid-ranking cleric with close ties ‚Äčto Iran's ‚Äčelite ‚Ā†Revolutionary Guards, hardliner Mojtaba is one of the most ‚Äčinfluential figures in the Iranian ‚Äčclerical ‚Ā†establishment and is seen as a possible successor to his father.
Iran has ‚Ā†not ‚Äčyet announced a new ‚Äčleader.

item.title: Trump renews Netanyahu pardon call, saying only Iran should be troubling him
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday called again on Israel's president to grant Prime Minister Benjamin ‚ÄčNetanyahu a pardon for corruption charges, saying the premier should ‚ÄĆhave nothing on his mind but the war with Iran.
KEY CONTEXT
WHAT'S NEXT

item.title: Swiss and EU vow to deepen cooperation on security and defence
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - Switzerland and the European Union on Thursday said they planned to deepen ‚Äčcooperation on security and defence, including exchange ‚ÄĆof information in areas ranging from Ukraine to the Middle East.
The two sides issued a joint ‚Äčdeclaration stating that aim following talks in ‚ÄčZurich between Swiss Foreign Minister Ignazio Cassis ‚Ā†and EU foreign policy chief Kaja ‚ÄčKallas.
A draft of the declaration said the two ‚Äčsides' top diplomats aimed to hold annual dialogue and step up exchanges on issues and places of ‚Äčjoint interest including Ukraine, the Middle East, ‚ÄčAfrica, Asia, Latin America and the United States.
The two sides ‚ÄĆpledged ‚Ā†to strengthen cooperation on foreign policy, security and defence, and Swiss representatives would be invited, as appropriate, to meetings of the ‚ÄčEU Foreign Affairs ‚ÄčCouncil and ‚Ā†relevant preparatory bodies, it said.
Issues of common interest also encompassed human ‚Äčrights, peace mediation, multilateral affairs, restrictive ‚Äčmeasures, ‚Ā†consular affairs, and crisis management abroad.
The declaration said the two planned to boost cooperation between ‚Ā†EU ‚Äčdelegations and offices and ‚ÄčSwiss embassies and missions in third countries and to international ‚Äčorganisations.

item.title: France ramps up military aid to Lebanon as Macron calls on Hezbollah, Israel to halt attacks
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - France will strengthen its cooperation with the Lebanese Armed ‚ÄčForces and provide armored transport vehicles as well ‚ÄĆas operational and logistical support, French President Emmanuel Macron said on Thursday, as Lebanon was pulled deeper into the war in ‚Äčthe Middle East earlier this week.
"Everything must be ‚Äčdone to prevent this country, so close to ‚Ā†France, from once again being drawn into war," Macron ‚Äčsaid in a post on X.
"At this moment of ‚Äčgreat danger, I call on the Israeli prime minister not to expand the war to Lebanon. I call on Iranian leaders not ‚Äčto further draw Lebanon into a war that ‚Äčis not its own," the French leader added.
After meeting with the ‚ÄĆchief ‚Ā†of staff of the French military in Beirut, Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun asked Macron to intervene to "prevent the targeting of the southern suburbs following threats by the Israeli ‚Äčarmy against ‚Äčits residents," the ‚Ā†Lebanese presidency said in a separate statement.
France has said that it aims to ‚Äčprevent escalation across the region and has taken steps ‚Äčto ‚Ā†protect its own positions amid the wider conflict involving the United States, Israel and Iran .
"Hezbollah must immediately cease its fire ‚Ā†toward ‚ÄčIsrael. Israel must refrain from ‚Äčany ground intervention or large-scale operation on Lebanese territory," Macron said.

item.title: Flights for US citizens stuck in Middle East ramping up, State Dept says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department said on Thursday it was ramping up the provision of charter flights from the Middle East ‚Äčafter criticism over the Trump administration's planning and initial assistance to ‚ÄĆU.S. citizens trying to leave the region since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began.
The strikes that began on Saturday and Iran's retaliatory attacks on its neighbors have led to widespread ‚Äčairspace closures, clogging busy aviation routes throughout the region and leaving ‚Äčthousands stranded.
The department, headed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, announced ‚Ā†on Wednesday the first U.S. government charter flight departed the region bound ‚Äčfor the U.S., giving few details.
"At the direction of @SecRubio, Department of State charter ‚Äčflight and ground transportation operations are underway and will continue to ramp up with additional flights and ground transports taking place today," Assistant Secretary of State for Global Public ‚ÄčAffairs Dylan Johnson said in a post on X on Thursday.
It was ‚Äčunclear from which countries charter flights would be departing.
American citizens in Kuwait, Bahrain, the United ‚ÄĆArab ‚Ā†Emirates, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Israel should fill out a crisis intake form to receive information about upcoming flights and ground transportation options, Johnson said, adding that a new task force has helped more than 10,000 Americans with ‚Äčguidance since the crisis ‚Äčbegan.
The State Department ‚Ā†had only issued warnings to U.S. citizens in Israel and Lebanon as tensions rose, but Iran's response has targeted ‚ÄčU.S. missions and civilian infrastructure in many more places, ‚Äčincluding in ‚Ā†Gulf Arab nations that are major transport hubs.
U.S. security alerts for Americans in Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq, Qatar and Bahrain went out after the conflict ‚Ā†began. ‚ÄčThe U.S. on Monday urged Americans across 14 ‚Äčcountries in the Middle East to immediately depart the region using "available commercial transportation" without offering any U.S.-government-vouched ‚Äčmeans, drawing the ire of U.S. lawmakers .

item.title: Ukraine, Russia exchange 200 POWs each in latest swap
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Ukraine and Russia exchanged 200 prisoners of war each on Thursday in their latest swap, ‚Äčofficials on both sides said, despite a ‚ÄĆdeadlock in U.S.-backed negotiations to end the four-year conflict .
Kyiv and Moscow have carried out regular POW exchanges throughout the ‚Äčwar, committing during recent talks in Geneva to ‚Äča new round of such swaps even ‚Ā†as negotiations remain stuck over the question of ‚Äčterritory.
"Every time our people come home, it proves ‚Äčthat Ukraine is working to bring everyone back. No one is forgotten," President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on X.
"We involve mediators. ‚ÄčI am grateful to everyone who helps Ukraine. ‚ÄčI thank the United States for its support in making ‚ÄĆthis ‚Ā†exchange possible."
Some of the POWs freed on Thursday were captured as far back as Moscow's siege of Mariupol in early 2022, Zelenskiy added.
Tearful ‚Äčrelatives greeted loved ‚Äčones - many ‚Ā†of them draped in Ukrainian flags - as they emerged from a ‚Äčbus that had arrived at an undisclosed ‚Äčlocation ‚Ā†to chants of "Welcome!"
The two sides are expected to trade a further 300 POWs each in a ‚Ā†second ‚Äčday of exchanges on Friday, ‚Äčofficials said.

item.title: Finland to lift full ban on hosting nuclear arms, government says
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 5 (Reuters) - Finland plans to lift a long-standing ban on having nuclear arms on its territory, the government said on Thursday, aligning with Nordic neighbours ‚Äčin a move that could open the door to deploying atomic ‚ÄĆbombs on Finnish soil during times of war.
Finland's Nuclear Energy Act, passed in 1987, prohibits the import, manufacture, possession and detonation of nuclear explosives on its soil, seen by some ‚ÄčFinns as a clause that would benefit only Russia if there ‚Äčever was a war.
While Finland maintained neutrality during the Cold War ‚Ā†era, the country in 2023 joined the NATO military alliance in response ‚Äčto nuclear-armed Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine the preceding year.
"The amendment is necessary ‚Äčto enable Finland's military defense as part of the alliance and to take full advantage of NATO's deterrence and collective defence," Finnish Defence Minister Antti Hakkanen told a press conference.
The proposed ‚Äčchange will next go to parliament where the right-wing coalition government holds ‚Äča majority.
Neighbours Sweden, Denmark and Norway have long-standing policies against nuclear weapons on their territories in ‚ÄĆpeacetime ‚Ā†but do not have legislative bans during war.
NATO allies France and Germany announced plans on Monday to deepen cooperation with European partners on nuclear deterrence, marking a policy shift as the continent faces rising threats from Russia and instability linked ‚Äčto the Iran conflict .
Sweden's ‚Äčdoctrine is to ‚Ā†station no permanent foreign troops or nuclear weapons on its soil in peacetime, Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson told reporters last ‚Äčweek, when asked about the possibility of his country hosting French ‚Äčnuclear arms .
"If ‚Ā†we were to find ourselves in a completely different situation, that particular formulation would not apply," Kristersson said.
Finland shares a 1,340-kilometre border with Russia and in 2024 ‚Ā†signed ‚Äča defence pact with the United States, allowing ‚Äčit to use 15 of Finland's military facilities and zones.

item.title: Middle East chaos stems from erosion in international law, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - The current turmoil in the Middle East is a direct ‚Äčresult of the erosion in international law, EU foreign ‚ÄĆpolicy chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
In a speech at the University of Zurich, Kallas said the international order has been undermined by major powers ‚Äčacting unilaterally, pointing the finger particularly at Russia, but also ‚Äčdirecting criticism at China and the United States.
"Today, the ‚Ā†chaos we see around us in the Middle East is ‚Äča direct consequence of the erosion of international law," she said, ‚Äčarguing that Russia's invasion of Ukraine had encouraged others to act with impunity.
China, she said, was also taking advantage of the erosion of international rules to ‚Äčextend its influence in the Asia-Pacific region and put pressure ‚Äčon Europe's economies.
"Without restoring international law, together with accountability, we are doomed to ‚ÄĆsee ‚Ā†repeated violations of the law, disruption and chaos," Kallas argued.
Turning to the United States, she said Washington's foreign policy shift had "rocked the transatlantic relationship to its foundation, with aftershocks in other parts of ‚Äčthe world," and ‚Äčcalling its impact ‚Ā†on the international order "seismic."
"The current direction is a new world order characterised by competition and coercive ‚Äčpower politics, a world order dominated by a handful ‚Äčof ‚Ā†military powers who aim to establish and secure spheres of influence," Kallas said.
Kallas was delivering the Churchill Special Lecture some 80 years after ‚Ā†Britain's ‚Äčwartime leader Winston Churchill spoke at the ‚ÄčUniversity and called for the building of a "United States of Europe" following the devastation ‚Äčof World War Two.

item.title: Seafarers can refuse to sail through Mideast Gulf region, main union says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - Seafarers have the right to refuse to sail on ships passing ‚Äčthrough the Middle East Gulf, including the Strait ‚ÄĆof Hormuz, after the threat level for the region was raised to its highest level, the leading labour union and shipping industry groups ‚Äčsaid on Thursday.
Around 300 ships are anchored on ‚Äčboth sides of the Strait while the U.S.-led war against ‚Ā†Iran escalates . Since February 28, nine ships have been ‚Äčdamaged and at least one seafarer has been killed.
As ‚Äčpart of negotiated arrangements reached on Thursday between mariners and commercial shipping companies, known as the International Bargaining Forum (IBF), seafarers can refuse to ‚Äčsail into the area, with repatriation at the company‚Äôs cost ‚Äčand compensation equal to two months‚Äô basic wage.
In addition, they will ‚ÄĆreceive ‚Ā†higher pay and compensation will be doubled in the event of death or disability, the International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF) said in a statement.
"Today‚Äôs designation ensures that seafarers on vessels ‚Äčcovered by IBF ‚Äčagreements have ‚Ā†critical protections if they operate in this dangerous region," ITF General Secretary Stephen Cotton said.
"The ‚Äčfact we have to take these measures at ‚Äčall ‚Ā†is a damning indictment of the situation facing seafarers today. No worker should have to risk being killed or maimed simply ‚Ā†for ‚Äčdoing their job ‚Äď particularly when that ‚Äčjob is transporting the oil and goods that keep the world‚Äôs economies running."

item.title: Israel decided to kill Khamenei in November, defence minister says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel took the decision to kill Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in November and was planning to carry ‚Äčout the operation around six months later, Defence ‚ÄĆMinister Israel Katz said on Thursday.
Khamenei was killed in the first hours of the U.S.-Israeli air campaign that began on Saturday ‚Äčin the first assassination of a country's top ‚Äčruler by an airstrike.
The joint air assault is nearing ‚Ā†the end of its first week after opening ‚Äčsalvos killed the country's leaders and set off a ‚Äčregional war, with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and Iraq, and Israeli attacks against Iran's ally Hezbollah in Lebanon .
"Already in November ‚Äčwe were convened with the prime minister in ‚Äča very tight forum and the prime minister (Benjamin Netanyahu) set the ‚ÄĆgoal ‚Ā†of eliminating Khamenei," Katz told Israel's N12 TV news. The timing was set for mid-2026, he said.
The plan was eventually shared with the Washington and brought forward around ‚ÄčJanuary after ‚Äčprotests broke ‚Ā†out Iran, when Israel was concerned its pressured clerical rulers might launch an attack ‚Äčagainst Israel and U.S. assets in the ‚ÄčMiddle ‚Ā†East, Katz said.
Israel has said its aim is to eliminate the existential threat it sees in Iran's nuclear ‚Ā†programme and ballistic ‚Äčmissile project, and to bring ‚Äčabout regime change. Iran's rulers have so far shown no sign of ‚Äčrelinquishing power.

item.title: Europol warns Iran crisis raises threat of terror, extremism and cyberattacks
item.description:
MADRID, March 5 (Reuters) - The Middle East conflict will have "immediate repercussions" for European Union security with an increased threat of terrorism, serious and ‚Äčorganised crime as well as violent extremism and cyberattacks, ‚ÄĆEuropean police body Europol told Spanish news agency EFE on Thursday.
Europol spokesman Jan Op Gen Oorth said he expected to see more cyberattacks against ‚ÄčEuropean infrastructure and an increase in online fraud using increasingly ‚Äčsophisticated Artificial Intelligence and exploiting the flurry of information ‚Ā†swirling about the conflict online, EFE reported.
Groups linked to Iran ‚Äčcould seek to carry out "destabilising activities" within the EU, he added, ‚Äčreferring to groups linked to the so-called Axis of Resistance, the network of anti-American and Israeli Shi'ite militias in countries including Iraq, Lebanon and Yemen. ‚ÄčThese could include terrorist attacks, intimidation campaigns, terrorist financing and ‚Äčcybercrime.
"The level of terrorist threat and violent extremism in EU territory is considered ‚ÄĆhigh," ‚Ā†he told the news agency.
The terror threat could be heightened by individuals acting alone or small cells acting on their own initiative, he said.
"The rapid spread of polarising content on the internet ‚Äčcan accelerate short-term ‚Äčradicalisation processes among ‚Ā†diaspora communities within the EU and other individuals," he said.
Europol did not immediately return a Reuters ‚Äčrequest for comment on the reported statements.
Iran and ‚ÄčIsrael on ‚Ā†Thursday were exchanging fire on a sixth day of war after Israel and the United States launched joint air strikes on Iran ‚Ā†on ‚Äčthe weekend. So far the attacks have ‚Äčkilled more than 1,000 people including Iran's Supreme Leader, prompted Iran to attack neighbours ‚Äčincluding Qatar and UAE along with energy shipments.

item.title: US, Qatar discuss acquiring Ukrainian drones to down Iran's Shaheds, source says
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Qatar are in discussions with Kyiv about acquiring Ukrainian interceptor drones as a cheap alternative to down Iranian Shahed UAVs amid the war in the Gulf, a source familiar with the ‚Äčmatter told Reuters.
The early-stage talks are taking place between government officials, not companies, and the technology being discussed includes systems to listen out for ‚ÄĆincoming enemy drones and disrupt their communication signals, the source said.
Qatar's International Media Office did not respond to a request for comment. The Pentagon declined to comment.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday evening the United States had asked Kyiv for help in downing Shaheds.
"I have instructed that the necessary resources be provided and that Ukrainian specialists be present to ensure ‚Äčthe necessary security," he said, without giving further details or mentioning Ukrainian interceptor drones.
Zelenskiy said earlier that Ukraine had also received similar requests from Middle ‚ÄčEastern countries and that he would only go ahead with deals if they did not weaken Ukraine's own defences against Russia's invasion.
He has ‚Ā†also said he would be open to exchanging drones for air defence missiles.
A second source - a Western diplomat in the Gulf - said a Ukrainian delegation had travelled to Doha ‚Äčthis week to meet with Qatari officials to share Ukraine's experience in drone defence. A delegation also travelled to Abu Dhabi, the diplomat said.
Tehran has fired hundreds of missiles ‚Äčand drones at Gulf countries after the U.S. and Israel launched a massive campaign of air strikes at Iran on Saturday and killed much of the Islamic Republic's senior leadership.
Gulf countries have managed to intercept most of those strikes, making use of the U.S.-made PAC-3 Patriot systems that Ukraine relies on to defend its own energy and military infrastructure from Russian missiles.
Ukraine, however, has developed ‚Äčfar cheaper methods of downing the Shahed kamikaze drone during its four-year conflict with Russia, which has been using the Iranian-designed UAVs for much of the war.
Russia has ‚Äčlaunched 19,000 long-range drones at Ukraine this winter, most of which it has downed, Kyiv said.
After the Iran war erupted, Ukraine's SBU security service warned Ukrainian companies not to sell weapons to ‚ÄĆMiddle Eastern ‚Ā†countries without permission from Kyiv, the first source said.
The SBU and Ukraine's defence ministry did not respond to requests for comment.
A third source said Britain was supporting Ukraine in early-stage talks with Gulf states about the use of Ukrainian drones to intercept Shaheds.
That source said some drones could potentially be supplied by Project Octopus, an existing interceptor drone joint venture between London and Kyiv.
Britain's Ministry of Defence did not respond to questions. Ukrspecsystems, the Ukrainian company behind the Octopus drone factory in the UK , declined to comment.
Zelenskiy said on ‚ÄčWednesday he had spoken with leaders of ‚Ā†the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Jordan and Kuwait, without providing details.
Ukraine's discussions with Washington were first reported by the Financial Times, but Doha's interest and Britain's involvement have not previously been reported.
The U.S. and its Gulf allies have expended hundreds of air defence missiles , costing ‚Äčmillions of dollars apiece, since the Iran conflict began.
Lockheed Martin produces around 600 PAC-3 missiles a year, although it plans ‚Äčto increase that to ‚Ā†2,000 under a seven-year deal with the Pentagon.
Kyiv has been running low on those missiles for months, raising concerns that it would be unable to down Russian ballistic missiles, against which the Patriot is the only effective weapon in its arsenal.
Zelenskiy has proposed swapping Ukrainian interceptor drones for missiles for Patriot systems.
Taras Tymochko from Come Back Alive, a Ukrainian foundation that ‚Ā†has bought ‚Äčtens of thousands of interceptor drones with donations, said it was unclear who, other than Ukrainian crews, ‚Äčcould operate them.
"It's rather difficult to remove our pilots from their operations and send them to the Middle East," Tymochko said. "There's a significant need to scale up existing training capacities in Ukraine to share experience with ‚Äčour partners."

item.title: Brazil rocked by probe of central bankers aiding failed Banco Master
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 5 (Reuters) - Evidence that two senior regulators at Brazil's central bank secretly advised embattled banker Daniel Vorcaro has sent shockwaves through the capital Brasilia, threatening to drag the institution deeper into a snowballing scandal.
The revelations add to a widening blast radius surrounding Vorcaro, owner of the liquidated Banco Master, whose downfall has exposed ‚Äča network of influence and conflicts of interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful institutions.
Vorcaro was arrested on Wednesday in the latest phase of an ongoing criminal investigation, accused of bribing ‚ÄĆthe two central bank officials - in addition to plotting to attack and intimidate several people he perceived as acting against his interests with an associate he called "Sicario," named after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Among his targets were former employees, domestic workers and journalists.
For months, Brazilians have watched as the probe - first focused on fraud in Master's loan portfolio - grew to implicate public pension funds, a state-owned bank and high-ranking officials with ties to Vorcaro.
Unusual interventions by a federal audit court and the Supreme Court questioning the lender's shutdown - despite neither body having banking supervisory authority - failed ‚Äčto derail the investigation but added to questions about the banker's outsized influence.
Amid all that, the central bank's decision in November to close Banco Master had reinforced views of the regulator as a bastion of hard-nosed public servants ‚Äčsteeled against Brazil's politics.
Federal police shattered those impressions on Wednesday, alleging that Vorcaro likely bribed former central bank director Paulo Sergio Neves de Souza and Belline Santana, ex-head of ‚Ā†the banking supervision department, for tips and advice, according to cellphone messages obtained through court-authorized access to communications records.
Reuters could not immediately reach Souza or Santana or identify their lawyers.
The central bank declined to comment on the implications for its ‚Äčreputation or regulatory decisions involving the officials.
In a public statement, the central bank said the federal police probe was key to clarifying the facts and any violations would receive the appropriate sanctions under the law.
A person with direct knowledge of the recent ‚Äčinvestigations said the conduct uncovered was "absolutely unacceptable, absurd and horrifying," even if the central bank's decision-making structure ultimately led the regulator to the right outcome.
Despite any dissent from Souza and Santana, the source said, the central bank blocked regional lender BRB's bid to acquire Banco Master before liquidating the troubled lender.
Still, the revelations have amplified criticism that the central bank may have taken too long to act, allowing Master's problems to deepen while technical assessments that passed through Souza and Santana may have downplayed the severity of the situation, undermining decisions that could have ‚Äčcome sooner.
"The central bank was slow to rein in Master and it was slow to liquidate it," said another source with knowledge of the regulator's decision-making.
Souza, who was directly responsible for overseeing banks as director of supervision, sat for nearly ‚Äčsix years on the rate-setting Copom committee, between 2017 and 2023 - a period when Banco Master was expanding aggressively.
A court decision authorizing Wednesday's police operation cited messages showing he and Santana helped Vorcaro with regulatory matters, including prior review of filings to the central ‚ÄĆbank.
The ruling cited ‚Ā†evidence they may have received bribes while in their senior roles, including efforts by Vorcaro to formalize sham service contracts through consulting firms, which were then used to transfer funds to the two officials.
Both officials remained at the central bank in different banking supervisory roles until January, when they stepped down from their senior posts amid an internal probe. They remained career staff, now suspended by Wednesday's court order. A formal dismissal would require separate administrative proceedings.
"I was surprised and, above all, saddened. There is deep dismay," said one former director who worked with Souza. Three other sources who served alongside him voiced similar shock.
"It is very sad. But any wrongdoing by two officials must be separated from the institution and its staff," one source ‚Äčsaid, adding that the central bank's own internal reviews contributed ‚Äčto the investigation by federal police and prosecutors.
A federal ‚Ā†police source, also speaking on condition of anonymity, agreed that findings so far pointed to corruption of individual civil servants rather than institutional failure.
A central bank public employee since 1998, Souza joined the bank's board under former President Michel Temer and remained through the administration of former President Jair Bolsonaro into the early months of President Luiz Inacio Lula ‚Äčda Silva's current term, leaving in July 2023.
His tenure overlapped with the rapid rise of Banco Master, built largely on the sale of high-yield debt marketed to retail investors ‚Äčas covered by the Credit ‚Ā†Guarantee Fund (FGC).
When Banco Master was previously known as Banco Maxima before Vorcaro bought it in 2017, it was already on the central bank's confidential watchlist of troubled institutions, flagged for lending that ignored principles of "selectivity, liquidity and guarantees."
Approval for the young banker to take over the rebranded bank came in 2019, under then-governor Roberto Campos Neto. Two sources familiar with Vorcaro's influence said he long benefited from strong political ties in Brasilia that helped him cast a risky growth model as a boon for competition.
Although the lender held ‚Ā†less than 1% ‚Äčof Brazil's banking assets, its collapse last November - amid a cash crunch and mismanagement cited by the central bank - has cost the FGC fund ‚Äčsome 40 billion reais ($7.7 billion), roughly a third of its available resources, a bill that later rose with the liquidation of other institutions under its umbrella.
The FGC is financed with mandatory contributions from banks, especially larger lenders, which will bear the brunt of new funding.
That process gained breathing room this week after ‚Äčthe central bank allowed 30 billion reais in reserve requirements that would otherwise have been deposited at the regulator to be redirected to the FGC this year.
($1 = 5.2312 reais)

item.title: US suspending operations at embassy in Kuwait
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United ‚ÄčStates has ‚ÄĆsuspended operations at ‚Äčits ‚Äčembassy in Kuwait ‚Ā†City, the ‚ÄčState ‚ÄčDepartment said in a ‚Äčstatement ‚Äčon Thursday, amid ‚ÄĆU.S. ‚Ā†and Israeli strikes ‚Äčagainst ‚ÄčIran.

item.title: Czech parliament votes to shield PM Babis from trial on EU subsidy fraud charges
item.description:
PRAGUE, March 5 (Reuters) - The lower house of the Czech parliament voted on Thursday to deny a court request for billionaire businessman and Prime Minister Andrej Babis ‚Äčto face trial in long-running prosecution over an alleged fraud in drawing ‚ÄĆa European Union subsidy.
Babis, head of the populist ANO party, returned to power after winning an election in October last year, despite charges in the case involving a 2 million ‚Äčeuro subsidy granted in 2008, before he entered politics, for building ‚Äča hotel and conference centre outside Prague called Stork Nest.
Deputies for ‚Ā†ANO and ruling coalition partners, the far right, pro-Russian SPD party and the ‚Äčanti-Green Deal Motorists, voted on Thursday not to lift parliamentary immunity from Babis, ‚Äčvoting records showed. The vote means Babis is protected from prosecution in the case until the end of the parliament's four-year term in 2029.

item.title: Iceland to propose holding EU referendum during autumn, broadcaster RUV reports
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 5 (Reuters) - Iceland's government will propose to its parliament to ‚Äčhold a referendum during the autumn ‚ÄĆon whether to resume European Union accession talks abandoned more than a ‚Äčdecade ago, public broadcaster RUV ‚Äčreported on Thursday, citing sources.
RUV reported ‚Ā†on its website that the government ‚Äčwould submit the bill to parliament ‚Äčnext week and that the vote, according to sources, looked most likely to take ‚Äčplace late September.
KEY CONTEXT

item.title: EU's Kallas says Iran is seeking to escalate conflict
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - Iran is seeking to escalate ‚Äčthe conflict in the Middle East by ‚ÄĆattacking other countries in the region indiscriminately, EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
"Iran is ‚Äčan exporter of war," Kallas told ‚Äčreporters at a press conference with Swiss Foreign ‚Ā†Minister Ignazio Cassis during a visit to ‚ÄčZurich. "Right now, the regime tries to drag as ‚Äčmany countries into this war as possible."
When asked whether she thought Iran was trying to suck NATO into ‚Äčthe conflict and whether the EU had ‚Äča strategy to avoid getting involved, Kallas accused Tehran ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†trying to "sow chaos" in the region and attacking other countries "indiscriminately".
Both NATO and the EU had mechanisms enabling members to decide on whether they ‚Äčneeded to ‚Äčcall for ‚Ā†help, she said. This had not been the case so far, ‚Äčshe added.
Kallas said Iran had been ‚Äčconsiderably ‚Ā†weakened, and that she saw a chance for the Iranian people to "determine their own future."
She ‚Ā†also ‚Äčsaid the EU was continuing ‚Äčto work through diplomatic channels to pursue de-escalation in the ‚Äčregion.

item.title: Germany says it has no plans to send extra forces to Middle East
item.description:
BERLIN, March 5 (Reuters) - Germany said on Thursday it had no plans to send additional military support ‚Äčto the Middle East, after European allies announced plans ‚ÄĆto move forces to the eastern Mediterranean following a drone strike at a British air base in Cyprus.
Italian Defence ‚ÄčMinister Guido Crosetto told parliament on Thursday ‚Äčthat Italy, Spain, France and the Netherlands ‚Ā†would send naval assets to protect Cyprus ‚Äčin the coming days.
Britain is also deploying a destroyer and ‚Äčadditional helicopters with anti-drone capabilities to the region and is joining France and Greece in boosting air defences in Cyprus.
Germany's ‚Äčnavy participates in the long-running United Nations ‚ÄčUNIFIL monitoring mission in Lebanon, but the defence ministry said ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†military's focus remained on NATO defence operations in eastern Europe.
"Germany is currently not planning any additional military capabilities beyond its existing contributions to international ‚Äčmissions in the ‚ÄčMiddle East," ‚Ā†the ministry said in a statement.
The statement said the anti-aircraft frigate "Sachsen" was ‚Äčcurrently taking part in NATO's Cold ‚ÄčResponse ‚Ā†exercise in the Arctic.
The statement from the ministry reiterated comments from Defence Minister Boris Pistorius, who declared ‚Ā†in ‚Äčparliament: "Germany is not a party ‚Äčin this war. The Bundeswehr is not taking part in ‚Äčthis war."

item.title: US, others express concern over violations of ceasefires in eastern Congo
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United ‚ÄčStates, European ‚ÄĆUnion and others said ‚Äčin ‚Äča joint statement on ‚Ā†Thursday ‚Äčthey were ‚Äčprofoundly concerned about "continued and recent" ‚Äčviolations of ‚Äčceasefires in eastern ‚ÄĆDemocratic ‚Ā†Republic of Congo and called ‚Äčon ‚Äčall ‚Ā†parties to recommit ‚Äčto end ‚Äčfighting ‚Ā†and return to ‚Ā†negotiations.

item.title: Flight to bring French nationals from UAE forced back, minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - An Air France flight chartered ‚Äčby the French government ‚ÄĆto bring French nationals back from the United ‚ÄčArab Emirates was ‚Äčforced to turn back ‚Ā†on Thursday due to ‚Äčmissile fire in ‚Äčthe area, French Transport Minister Philippe Tabarot said on X.
"This ‚Äčsituation reflects the ‚Äčinstability in the region and ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†complexity of repatriation operations," Tabarot added.
France started r epatriation flights from the ‚ÄčMiddle ‚ÄčEast ‚Ā†on Wednesday as governments rush to ‚Äčbring home tens ‚Äčof ‚Ā†thousands of citizens stranded by the intensifying ‚Ā†U.S. ‚Äčand Israeli ‚Äčconflict with Iran.

item.title: Trump ousts Kristi Noem, names Oklahoma senator as homeland security nominee
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - Kristi Noem, who oversaw U.S. President Donald Trump's aggressive immigration crackdown and faced bipartisan criticism in hearings this week, will leave her role as homeland security chief at the end ‚Äčof the month.
Trump will tap Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin to replace her, he said on his Truth Social platform, a move that would require U.S. Senate ‚ÄĆconfirmation.
Noem, a former governor of South Dakota, became one of Trump's most high-profile Cabinet secretaries with social media posts that portrayed immigrants in harsh terms, highlighting cases of alleged criminal offenders and using vitriolic language.
She faced criticism in January when she quickly labeled two U.S. citizens fatally shot by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis as committing "domestic terrorism." Videos that emerged after the deaths undercut the assertion by Noem and other Trump officials that ‚Äčthe two deceased - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - were violent aggressors.
The public backlash for deaths led the Trump administration to move to a more targeted approach to immigration ‚Äčenforcement in Minnesota after months of sweeps through U.S. cities that led to violent clashes with residents opposing the crackdown.
Democrats in the U.S. House ‚Ā†of Representatives moved to impeach Noem and at least two Republicans in Congress called for her to lose her job after the incidents. During congressional hearings in March, Democrats and some Republicans ‚Äčcriticized Noem for her approach to the immigration crackdown and management of DHS, including concern over a $220 million ad campaign that heavily featured Noem.
Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he did not ‚Äčsign off on the ad campaign.
"I never knew anything about it," he said in a phone interview.
The ads prominently featured Noem, including a scene of her on horseback at Mount Rushmore in her home state of South Dakota.
In one of the congressional hearings this week, U.S. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican from Louisiana, asked Noem if Trump had approved the commercials.
"The president approved ahead of time you spending $220 million running TV ‚Äčads across the country in which you are featured prominently?" Kennedy asked Noem.
"Yes, sir. We went through the legal processes, did it correctly," she replied.
The staffing change raises questions about whether the Trump administration could seek to intensify its mass deportation push or retreat to a more targeted approach. Under Noem's leadership, masked immigration agents surged into Los Angeles, ‚ÄĆChicago and Washington, ‚Ā†D.C. , scouring neighborhoods and Home Depot parking lots in search of possible immigration offenders.
Mullin, who spent a decade in the House of Representatives before becoming a senator in 2023, also supports Trump's hardline immigration agenda. To become DHS secretary, Mullin would require Senate confirmation.
The popularity of Trump's immigration approach fell as agents detained U.S. citizens and tear-gassed streets in an attempt to drive up deportations, which last year fell short of the administration's goal of 1 million per year.
While Noem, 54, served as a prominent proponent of Trump's agenda, White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen ‚ÄčMiller, a long-time Trump aide, controls Trump's immigration policy.
Noem ‚Äčwas quickly confirmed to lead the 260,000-employee ‚Ā†Department of Homeland Security in January 2025 after Trump took office. On social media, she referred to immigrants convicted of crimes as "scumbags" even as the number of non-criminals arrested by immigration authorities rose under Trump.
She joined immigration enforcement operations on the ground in New York City and visited a ‚Äčmaximum-security prison in El Salvador where Venezuelan immigrants deported by the Trump administration were being held without charges or access to lawyers.
The ‚Äčnumber of migrants caught trying ‚Ā†to illegally cross the U.S.-Mexico border plummeted under Trump's restrictive policies, a steep drop after high levels of illegal immigration under former President Joe Biden, a Democrat.
Noem, reflecting Trump's agenda, also took steps to cut legal immigration programs and increase vetting. She ended several Temporary Protected Status programs that provided work permits to hundreds of thousands of immigrants from Venezuela, Haiti and other nations, drawing ‚Ā†legal challenges.
After an ‚ÄčAfghan immigrant was accused of attacking National Guard members in Washington, D.C., Noem said she recommended that Trump ‚Äčplace "a full travel ban on every damn country that‚Äôs been flooding our nation with killers, leeches, and entitlement junkies."
Critics said Noem demonized immigrants and promoted an immigration enforcement strategy that targeted non-criminal, working immigrants and families.
During Noem's tenure, ‚Äčthe number of deaths in immigration detention rose to a two-decade high while staff in DHS oversight offices were slashed sharply.

item.title: US military says it has sunk over 30 Iranian ships so far
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on ‚ÄčThursday it has ‚ÄĆsunk over 30 Iranian ships so far during ‚Äčthe U.S. ‚Äčand Israel's war against Iran, ‚Ā†including an Iranian ‚Äčdrone ship that ‚Äčis on fire.
Ballistic missile attacks by Iran have decreased ‚Äčby 90% since ‚Äčthe first day of the ‚ÄĆwar, ‚Ā†U.S. Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. forces in the ‚ÄčMiddle ‚ÄčEast ‚Ā†as the head of Central ‚ÄčCommand, told reporters.

item.title: Syria opens Mediterranean-Aleppo air corridor that could help ease regional snarl
item.description:
DAMASCUS, March 5 (Reuters) - Syria has opened a newly reactivated air corridor from the northern city of Aleppo toward the Mediterranean Sea for use by ‚Äčforeign airlines as well as its national carrier, the head of ‚ÄĆthe country‚Äôs Civil Aviation Authority told Reuters on Thursday, as air traffic gradually resumes through Aleppo International Airport.
Civil Aviation Authority director Omar Hosari said the route is intended to provide a safe flight path for ‚Äčaircraft arriving at and departing from Aleppo and will be available to ‚Äčany airline as long as it ‚Äúmeets international safety standards.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúThe corridors are ‚Ā†not dedicated exclusively to Syrian Air,‚ÄĚ said Hosari. ‚ÄúOther airlines can use them to transit ‚Äčor operate through Syrian airspace according to the usual regulatory procedures.‚ÄĚ
In a Thursday ‚Äčstatement, the authority said it had also reopened air routes in the northern part of Syrian airspace toward Turkey after conducting operational and technical assessments and reviewing regional developments affecting ‚Äčaviation.
The authority said the first Syrian Air flight departed Aleppo for Istanbul ‚Äčon Thursday and arrived safely, marking the gradual resumption of operations at Aleppo International Airport. ‚ÄĆA flight ‚Ā†from Aleppo to the Saudi city of Jeddah is scheduled for Friday, with authorities studying the addition of other destinations including Riyadh.
Hosari said Royal Jordanian is expected to use the Mediterranean corridor on Friday for a flight from ‚ÄčAmman to Aleppo as ‚Äčpart of the ‚Ā†phased return of air traffic.
Commercial air traffic remained largely absent across much of the Middle East, with major Gulf ‚Äčhubs - including Doha and Dubai, the world's busiest airport for ‚Äčinternational passengers - ‚Ā†largely shut for a sixth straight day after the U.S.-Israeli launched air strikes on Iran , which responded with retaliatory missile and drone strikes on the entire region.
A week ‚Ā†into ‚Äčthe regional conflict, Syria is emerging relatively sidelined, ‚Äčwith Iranian presence ceased after the fall of President Bashar al-Assad in late 2024, and U.S. military bases ‚Äčin the country evacuated last February.

item.title: Trump: want to finish Iran then Cuba 'question of time'
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump said ‚ÄĆon Thursday he wants to finish the war ‚Äčin Iran first, ‚Äčbut then it "will be ‚Ā†just a question ‚Äčof time before you ‚Äčand a lot of unbelievable people are going to ‚Äčbe going back ‚Äčto Cuba."
Trump, speaking at an event ‚ÄĆwith ‚Ā†the Inter Miami soccer team at the White House, said ‚ÄčCuba wants "to ‚Äčmake ‚Ā†a deal so badly."
"We want ‚Äčto finish this ‚Äčone ‚Ā†first," he said, referring to the conflict ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčIran.

item.title: Oilfield in Iraqi Kurdistan halts production after drone strike, sources say
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - A drone attack struck an oilfield operated by U.S. firm HKN Energy in Iraq‚Äôs ‚ÄčKurdistan region on Thursday, causing a fire ‚ÄĆand halting production, security sources and an oilfield engineer said.
The Sarsang field produces about 30,000 barrels of oil per ‚Äčday and is operated by HKN Energy, ‚Äčwhich has a 62% stake. HKN is a ‚Ā†privately held U.S. oil and gas company owned ‚Äčby Hillwood Energy, part of the Hillwood group ‚Äčfounded by Ross Perot Jr.
No group claimed responsibility, but Kurdish officials accused Iraqi militias allied with Iran of carrying out ‚Äčthe attack.
If so, the attack would mean Iran‚ÄĎaligned ‚ÄčIraqi militias, who have vowed to retaliate for the killing ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†Iran‚Äôs supreme leader, have expanded targets from U.S. military bases in Iraqi Kurdistan to U.S. energy interests.
Production at the field was halted as a precaution ‚Äčafter an ‚Äčexplosion at ‚Ā†its power unit, the engineer told Reuters.
Kurdistan regional authorities confirmed the attack and ‚Äčsaid it was carried out by two ‚Äčdrones.
Some ‚Ā†energy companies operating in Iraqi Kurdistan shut oil and gas production at their fields as a precaution ‚Ā†after ‚Äčthe United States and Israel ‚Äčlaunched strikes on neighbouring Iran.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine will help US to counter Iranian drones
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday that Ukraine would provide assistance to the U.S. in response to its request for help in dealing ‚Äčwith Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Tehran has fired hundreds of drones at ‚ÄĆU.S. targets in neighboring countries after the U.S. and Israel began a massive campaign of air strikes against Iran on Saturday.
During its war with Russia, Ukraine has developed highly ‚Äčeffective means of downing the Iranian-designed Shahed kamikaze drone, which Moscow has ‚Äčbeen using for much of the four-year war.
"We received a ‚Ā†request from the United States for specific support in protection against 'Shaheds' in ‚Äčthe Middle East region," Zelenskiy wrote in English on the X social media ‚Äčplatform.
"I gave instructions to provide the necessary means and ensure the presence of Ukrainian specialists who can guarantee the required security."
The U.S. had requested assistance in protecting its bases and ‚Äčsoldiers in certain countries in the region, according to a source familiar ‚Äčwith the matter, who said Zelenskiy had instructed his military to do so.
Ukrainians would start ‚ÄĆwork "in ‚Ā†the next few days", the source said.
In a telephone interview with Reuters on Thursday, U.S. President Donald Trump said that he would take assistance from any country, when asked about Zelenskiy's offer of support to help defend against Iranian drones.
Zelenskiy ‚Äčexpressed concern earlier ‚Äčthis week that ‚Ā†the war in Iran - if it proves long and intense - could diminish weapons supplies to Ukraine from its Western ‚Äčallies, in particular the air defence systems it needs to ‚Äčprotect against ‚Ā†Russian missiles.
In recent months, Ukraine has already experienced shortages of the U.S.-made Patriot air defence systems - the only effective weapon in its arsenal for downing Russia's ‚Ā†ballistic missiles.
On ‚ÄčTuesday, Zelenskiy said Ukraine would be open to ‚Äčexchanging interceptor drones for air defence missiles from the Middle East allies, which have their own ‚Äčstocks of Patriots.

item.title: Canada, EU sign deal to modernize economic and trade agreement
item.description:
TORONTO, March 5 (Reuters) - Canada and the European Union ‚Äčagreed on Thursday ‚ÄĆto expand trade ties and ease trade ‚Äčrules under their ‚Äčexisting pact as countries ‚Ā†across the world ‚Äčstrive to increase trade ‚Äčand diversify away from the United States.
Canadian Trade ‚ÄčMinister Maninder Sidhu ‚Äčand European Union Commissioner for ‚ÄĆTrade ‚Ā†and Economic Security MaroŇ° Ň†efńćovińć signed an agreement in Toronto ‚Äčon ‚Äčsettling ‚Ā†investment disputes and launched talks on ‚Äča digital ‚Äčtrade ‚Ā†deal that would support data sharing, digital ‚Ā†services, ‚Äčcybersecurity cooperation ‚Äčand emerging technologies.

item.title: Iran calling US about a deal, says Trump
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump on Thursday said Tehran was reaching out to ‚Äčthe United States about making a deal amid ‚ÄĆU.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, adding that further action to reduce pressure on oil was imminent.
"They're calling, they're saying 'how do we ‚Äčmake a deal?' I said you're being ‚Äča little bit late," said Trump, speaking at ‚Ā†an event with the Inter Miami soccer team ‚Äčat the White House.
Trump touted the U.S. ‚Äčmilitary actions in Iran, saying they were destroying Tehran's missile and drone capability and that "their navy is gone - 24 ships ‚Äčin three days," as he called on Iranian ‚Äčdiplomats to request asylum and help shape a better country.
"We also ‚ÄĆurge ‚Ā†Iranian diplomats around the world to request asylum and to help us shape a new and better Iran," he said.
Iran's mission to the United Nations ‚Äčin New ‚ÄčYork declined to ‚Ā†comment.
While Trump said oil had "pretty much stabilized," he said further action ‚Äčto reduce pressure on it was imminent.
The U.S. ‚ÄčTreasury ‚Ā†Department is expected to announce measures as soon as Thursday aimed at combating rising energy prices, including potential action involving the ‚Ā†oil ‚Äčfutures market, a senior White ‚ÄčHouse official said earlier on Thursday.

item.title: US not expanding military objectives in Iran, Hegseth says
item.description:
TAMPA, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äčon Thursday the United States was ‚ÄĆnot expanding its military objectives in Iran, after President Donald Trump told Reuters that the United States ‚Äčmust be involved in choosing the ‚Äčnext leader of Iran .
The Pentagon earlier this week ‚Ā†said the military campaign, known as ‚ÄčOperation Epic Fury, is focused on destroying ‚ÄčIran's offensive missiles, missile production and Navy, while not allowing Tehran to have a nuclear weapon.
"There's no ‚Äčexpansion in our objectives. We know exactly ‚Äčwhat we're trying to achieve," Hegseth said.
He added that ‚ÄĆTrump ‚Ā†was "having a heck of a say in who runs Iran given the ongoing operation."
In a telephone interview with Reuters on Thursday, ‚ÄčTrump said ‚Äčthe United ‚Ā†States would have to help pick the next person to ‚Äčlead the country.
The United States has ‚Äčhit ‚Ā†more than 2,000 targets in Iran over the past six days, including Iranian warships.

item.title: Ukraine says Ukrainian bank employees detained with cash shipment in Hungary
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha accused Hungary early on ‚ÄčFriday of detaining seven employees ‚ÄĆof Ukraine's state savings bank while they were transporting cash from Austria ‚Äčback to Ukraine.
Andrii Sybiha ‚Äčwas writing on the X social ‚Ā†media platform after Hungarian ‚ÄčPrime Minister Viktor Orban said Budapest ‚Äčwould force Ukraine with "political and financial tools" to reopen the Druzhba pipeline carrying ‚ÄčRussian oil to Hungarian ‚Äčrefineries.
Sybiha said the employees were travelling in ‚ÄĆtwo ‚Ā†cars through Hungary when they were detained. He said their whereabouts were unknown at the moment.
"In ‚Äčfact, we ‚Äčare ‚Ā†talking about Hungary taking hostages and stealing money," ‚ÄčSybiha wrote. "If this is ‚Äčthe 'force' ‚Ā†announced earlier today by Mr Orban, then this is a force ‚Ā†of ‚Äča criminal gang. ‚ÄčThis is state terrorism and racketeering."
