Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: 'Sustainable' logging operations are clear-cutting Canada‚Äôs climate-fighting forests
item.description:
Nonprofit environment watchdogs put their stamps of approval on countless wood products that get touted as responsibly produced. But Reuters found that the timber firms these groups certify are harvesting large swaths of Canada‚Äôs older forests, which are critical to containing global warming.
By CHRIS KIRKHAM , GRANT SMITH and JESSICA DINAPOLI Photographs by CHRIS HELGREN and DAVID JACKSON
Filed Sept. 7, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
With its vast expanses of forest, Canada has the most ‚Äúcertified‚ÄĚ sustainable timber operations of any nation, according to the nonprofit organizations that attest to the environmental soundness of logging practices.
Such forestry-standards groups were born in the 1990s out of rage¬†over tropical rainforest destruction. Today, they put their leafy seals of approval on toilet paper, two-by-fours and other wood and paper goods to assure eco-conscious consumers and investors they were responsibly produced.
Yet research shows Canadian forests have seen some of the world‚Äôs largest declines in ecologically critical primary and old-growth woodlands over the last two decades, even as sustainability-certification programs grew to include nearly all of Canada‚Äôs logging.
To track destruction of older woodlands in these certified zones, Reuters analyzed forestry data in Ontario, a major logging province. The analysis found that about 30% of the certified boreal forests harvested from 2016 to 2020 were at least 100 years old. That resulted in the loss of 377 square miles of these older forests, an area the size¬†of New York City and Washington D.C. combined, the analysis found.
Canada‚Äôs forests ‚Äď accounting for 9% of the world‚Äôs total ‚Äď are considered critical to containing global warming. Environmental advocates have long pushed to end logging in primary or old-growth forests, which soak up far more climate-damaging carbon than logged-and-replanted areas. Primary forests are those that show no sign of previous harvesting. They can include old-growth areas ‚Äď some with trees hundreds or thousands of years old ‚Äď but also relatively newer woodlands that, for instance, might have regrown after wildfires.
Forest-certification nonprofits have chosen to allow logging of older forests through a host of concessions to industry. The harvesting of such areas in Ontario came despite the fact that 94% of the province‚Äôs managed forests are certified by one of the two dominant environmental-certification¬†organizations in Canada, the analysis found. Reuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data, government forest-age estimates and forest-certification maps to estimate the harvest of forests at least 100 years old in Ontario‚Äôs certified zones.
‚ÄúWhy the heck are they allowing logging ‚Äď certified logging ‚Äď in primary forests that are over 100 years old?‚ÄĚ asked Dominick DellaSala, a conservation biologist with environmental group Wild Heritage who studies Canadian logging impacts.¬†‚ÄúFor Canada to claim that it‚Äôs doing sustainable management, it‚Äôs laughable. To put a certification seal of approval on it is more alarming.‚ÄĚ
The rapid loss of older Canadian forests¬†highlights the flaws of certification programs that have come under heavy influence of the logging and forest-products¬†industries, a Reuters investigation has found. The damage has come under the watch of the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), the world‚Äôs first such certification organization, founded in 1993 with environmentalist support; and the Sustainable Forestry Initiative (SFI), a rival founded by a timber¬†and forest-products¬†trade group the following year.
This account is based on the Reuters analysis of Ontario forests, a review of hundreds of pages of FSC and SFI audits, along with policy and strategy documents, and interviews with 20 current or former FSC employees or members and more than a half-dozen¬†researchers who study the environmental effects of Canadian logging.
In a statement, FSC said it has not wavered from its original commitment to responsible forest¬†management and¬†that its certification standards are ‚Äúrobust and credible.‚ÄĚ SFI said its standards are ‚Äústrong and continuously improving‚ÄĚ and that its certification has become a ‚Äúhighly trusted solution‚ÄĚ to the growing demand for products from sustainably managed forests.
Neither organization commented on the Reuters analysis or on whether they considered harvesting large sections of century-old¬†forests to be sustainable.
The FSC and SFI certify logging companies‚Äô practices in specific forests and examine consumer-product supply chains. Their seals of approval ‚Äď a leaf insignia¬†for SFI, and a tree with a checkmark for the¬†FSC ‚Äď have become essential to timber and forest-products¬†firms amid rising pressure for environmental stewardship.
But these companies hold immense leverage over the big forest-certification nonprofits, which depend heavily on the industry for funding through certification fees, Reuters found. And since its inception, the FSC has watered down its forestry standards in response to the competitive threat posed by SFI and other industry-friendly certifiers, according to environmentalists and more than a dozen current and former FSC staffers and members, who advise the organization on policy and strategy.
Companies are free to choose which certifier to use, allowing them to avoid those with stricter standards and giving them clout to lobby all certifiers for permissive policies, said the FSC staffers and members.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs easy to pull the wool over people‚Äôs eyes about what is good forestry.‚ÄĚ
Widespread certification of British Columbia timber operations over the past two decades hasn‚Äôt stopped the disappearance of more than half of the province‚Äôs old-growth woodlands over that period. Logging caused the vast majority of the declines in the biggest old-growth trees storing the most carbon, according to one 2021 study in the Canadian Journal of Forest Research and another last year in the journal Frontiers in Forests and Global Change.¬†Studies in 2009 and 2017 examined areas of Quebec woodlands and found¬†sections¬†of forests dominated by trees more than a century old had dwindled to between 13% and 28% of the forest amid heavy logging. Without logging, these older sections would account for between 40% and 68%¬†of these woodlands, the researchers estimated.
Herb Hammond, a veteran forest ecologist,¬†ran a British Columbia nonprofit¬†organization that conducted some of Canada‚Äôs first FSC audits in the late 1990s.¬†He later left the organization, frustrated with what he described as¬†too many compromises with industry.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs easy to pull the wool over people‚Äôs eyes about what is good forestry,‚ÄĚ he said. ‚ÄúCertification has turned out to be a bit of a dog‚Äôs breakfast. It doesn‚Äôt really mean anything.‚ÄĚ
A ‚Äėchess move‚Äô
Forestry certification has become ubiquitous in the global forest-products¬†trade, helping companies such as Procter & Gamble, Starbucks and Penguin Random House appeal to eco-conscious consumers and investors. Those three companies declined to comment.
The certifying trend started in the 1990s when environmental organizations including Greenpeace, Friends of the Earth and the World Wildlife Fund helped launch the FSC after failing to secure forest-conservation pledges from governments worldwide. They hoped to incentivize companies instead with a market-driven system that branded goods as sustainable, stimulating demand from discerning buyers.¬†The FSC was founded in 1993 with a membership of business, environmental and community representatives.
Still, many companies were wary of aligning with environmentalists. The following year, the American Forest &¬†Paper Association, a trade-group, started the SFI as an industry-friendly alternative. The trade association said its discussions about sustainable forestry began earlier, in 1990, and included input from academics and conservation groups.
Competition from the industry-backed SFI forced the FSC to reckon with how to maintain rigorous forestry standards while recruiting companies to certify, 10¬†current and former FSC members said. A 2002 FSC management report highlighted the need to ‚Äúrapidly increase the supply of certified timber‚ÄĚ or risk losing out to ‚Äúan ever-increasing number of competing certification schemes.‚ÄĚ
The FSC launched an internal push to boost its market share that led to compromises with industry and weaker harvesting restrictions, according to FSC documents and the FSC members.
Weakening FSC standards didn‚Äôt stop the SFI‚Äôs growth, however. The FSC certified about 46 million hectares of Canadian forests¬†at the end of 2023, less than half the SFI‚Äôs 119 million hectares, according to the Forest Products Association of Canada, an industry group. Globally, the FSC certifies 160 million hectares compared to 295 million hectares by the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification¬†(PEFC). The PEFC is a global organization that oversees the SFI, which covers North America, and affiliated certifiers in other regions.
Both the FSC and the SFI largely survive on industry-paid fees. FSC International reported in 2022 that such fees accounted for 86% of its $58 million in annual revenue. The SFI derived 77% of its $12 million in revenue from such fees, according to its 2022 tax return.
Some environmental groups and advocates, while acknowledging the FSC‚Äôs shortcomings, continue to view the organization as the best option among imperfect alternatives. Jen Skene, a policy director at the Natural Resources Defense Council, said FSC certification represents a minimum standard.
‚ÄúFSC is the most credible certification system out there,‚ÄĚ she said, while adding that it should be viewed as ‚Äúa floor, not a ceiling‚ÄĚ for sustainability standards.
FSC told Reuters it had not weakened standards in response to SFI competition. Instead, FSC said, the rivalry has prompted it to ‚Äúimprove and refine‚ÄĚ its certification process to ensure it remains ‚Äúthe gold standard for responsible forestry.‚ÄĚ
SFI said competition among certifiers doesn‚Äôt ‚Äúexert a downward pressure on standards‚ÄĚ but rather promotes continuous improvement. The PEFC said it allows regional groups including the SFI to develop their own standards, which the PEFC said¬†‚Äúcontributes to long-term commitment to sustainable forest management¬†practices.‚ÄĚ
Though some corporations favor FSC-certified wood, few customers know the difference among certifying groups and their labels.
Peter Wood, a forestry lecturer at the University of British Columbia who has served on FSC-rulemaking committees, called the SFI‚Äôs creation a ‚Äúchess move.‚ÄĚ
The industry ‚Äúwanted to take the power away from FSC, and it worked,‚ÄĚ he said. ‚ÄúNow, everything is certified.‚ÄĚ
Race to the bottom
FSC‚Äôs early standards emphasized the need to protect primary and old-growth forests. One pivotal provision read: ‚ÄúPrimary forests ‚Ä¶ shall be conserved. Such areas shall not be replaced by tree plantations or other land uses.‚ÄĚ
But companies complained the policy was too restrictive and difficult to enforce, said Grant Rosoman, a Greenpeace forests advisor and former FSC International board member.
FSC members spent years debating policy changes and in 1999 removed requirements to conserve primary forests. Instead, the FSC adopted a more subjective requirement to protect forests with ‚Äúhigh conservation value,‚ÄĚ based on a complex matrix of environmental, economic¬†and cultural characteristics.
That vague language, still in effect, gives companies broad influence over which forests qualify for protection. It has also spawned an industry of consultants ‚Äď hired and paid¬†by forest-products firms¬†‚Äď to perform studies determining which woodlands¬†have high conservation value, according to FSC audits and six current and former FSC members.
Rosoman of Greenpeace was among the FSC‚Äôs¬†members¬†who approved the language at the time. He now regrets it, believing its subjectivity enabled destruction of critical forests. ‚ÄúThe continued logging of primary forests and old-growth forests was never resolved,‚ÄĚ he said.
FSC acknowledged that its rules allow certified logging in such areas but said the high conservation value designation aims to ensure such harvesting is ‚Äúconducted with the highest level of scrutiny and responsibility.‚ÄĚ
In another major concession, FSC in 2004 introduced the ‚ÄúFSC Mix‚ÄĚ system, which created a new label for products containing up to 30% wood¬†from non-certified sources.
The move came after pressure from pulp-and-paper companies including Klabin of Brazil, SCA of Sweden and Mondi of South Africa, as well as book publishers and furniture manufacturers, said Rosoman, who participated in the negotiations. Mondi did not comment. SCA said it could not address its role at the time because the company has since been split into two firms. Klabin did not answer questions on whether the company influenced the FSC Mix rules. But¬†it said the label eased the logistical burden of separating wood from certified and non-certified sources, a view echoed by SCA.
FSC Mix has since become the certification group‚Äôs dominant label, accounting for more than three-fourths of the FSC-product trade, according to a 2017 FSC paper. The paper added that FSC Mix was¬†‚Äúthe main source of income for the operating costs of FSC.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúI just wanted to turn away from the whole project, and warn people: ‚ÄėDon‚Äôt trust it.‚Äô‚ÄĚ
The FSC told Reuters it doesn‚Äôt know what percentage of FSC-certified products use the Mix label today. The label, it said, helps companies ‚Äútransition to more sustainable practices.‚ÄĚ
FSC Mix rules give companies wide latitude to use the label.¬†Some consumer-products firms¬†are allowed to put the Mix label on products that contain no FSC-certified material at all because the FSC gives them credit for certified content in other products they sell.
The SFI also offers a label ‚Äď ‚ÄúSFI Certified Sourcing‚ÄĚ ‚Äď that makes no guarantees that products contain any wood from certified forests, so¬†long as companies meet certain other conditions.
Phil Guillery, a former FSC United States board member and supply chain integrity director, said allowing uncertified wood into the FSC system brought many more timber and forest-products¬†companies into the organization and gave them more clout.
‚ÄúThey understood and learned about the politics of FSC, and they became very powerful,‚ÄĚ he said.
Wood, the University of British Columbia lecturer, served on two FSC groups that starting in 2011 tried to overhaul what internal critics had called a weak system of company self-assessments to ensure their FSC Mix products did not contain wood from ‚Äúunacceptable sources,‚ÄĚ such as illegally harvested forests. The rules changes took eight years in a process that was heavily influenced by industry, he said.
The FSC told Reuters the process resulted in a ‚Äúsubstantial strengthening‚ÄĚ of rules governing non-certified wood.¬†Wood had a different take, saying the endless deliberations did little to screen out problematic sources of timber. He called his involvement ‚Äúan awful experience.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúI just wanted to turn away from the whole project,‚ÄĚ he said, ‚Äúand warn people: ‚ÄėDon‚Äôt trust it.‚Äô‚ÄĚ
Certified forest devastation
Environmentalists criticize the FSC but generally take a harsher view of the SFI, citing its founding by an industry group and weaker forestry standards.
The SFI disputes that it serves only industry interests, telling Reuters its standards reflect input from a diverse group of collaborators including environmentalists on its board.
Environmental groups including the Sierra Club, Stand.earth and the Natural Resources Defense Council say the impact of the SFI‚Äôs industry-friendly approach is clear in British Columbia, where the organization has dominated certification.
The province, a showcase of Canada‚Äôs raw beauty and diverse ecosystems, has seen old-growth forests decline¬†by more than 50% over the last two decades, according to the 2021 and 2023 studies. A subset of ‚Äúhighly productive‚ÄĚ old-growth woodlands ‚Äď forests with the largest¬†trees storing the most carbon, and also the most attractive to logging companies ‚Äď has declined by an estimated 85%.
The SFI became the certifier of choice in British Columbia largely because industry perceived the FSC‚Äôs early rules as too onerous, said Karen Tam Wu, an FSC consultant during the 2000s.
The timber industry and Canada‚Äôs government share in the logging wealth. Canada‚Äôs forests are generally on public land, which means provincial governments get a cut of the revenue from every felled tree. In British Columbia, that totaled more than $7.3 billion¬†over the decade ending in March of this year, according to the province‚Äôs forest ministry.
British Columbia in 2020 announced a plan to protect its dwindling old-growth forests after years of public pressure. A year later, officials released maps showing at-risk areas where it called for a deferral of logging. But the government never barred logging in those zones, instead leaving it to industry discretion.
Some major firms chose instead to continue harvesting, including Vancouver-based Canfor Corp, a global timber-and-pulp producer.
Canfor in 2022 whacked about 3,700¬†acres of old-growth forest the government had recommended¬†for deferral of logging, according to satellite imagery analysis from Stand.earth. The provincial government said earlier this year that more than 50,000 acres of old-growth forest had been harvested in areas it sought to protect.
BC‚Äôs Ministry of Forests said it is not seeking to end all old-growth logging and that harvesting in some areas is ‚Äúpossible and necessary‚ÄĚ to support ‚Äúlocal, sustainable jobs‚ÄĚ while protecting forests.
SFI certified Canfor‚Äôs vast western Canada operations in 2019, 2021, 2022¬†and again last year. None of the publicly released audit summaries ever mentioned the cutting of old-growth forests. Major auditing firm KPMG, which conducted the reviews, had no comment.
Nothing in SFI‚Äôs standards would have prevented¬†logging of old-growth forests.
SFI said old-growth-forest harvesting in British Columbia is ‚Äúcontentious,‚ÄĚ involving negotiations among governments, industry and indigenous communities. It said its standards require compliance with ‚Äúall applicable laws.‚ÄĚ
Canfor said it is working with indigenous groups, communities and government to ‚Äúreview old-growth management and seek input into our proposed harvesting.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄėLike printing money‚Äô
Logging companies‚Äô ability to choose their own watchdogs poses the biggest obstacle to promoting high sustainability standards, environmental advocates said.
The auditing structure all but guarantees logging companies can get certified, said Simon Counsell, who was an FSC founding member while with the nonprofit group Friends of the Earth. He‚Äôs now an FSC critic.
‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a clear, vested financial interest‚ÄĚ for the auditor, because granting FSC certifications leads to more auditing opportunities, Counsell said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs like printing money.‚ÄĚ
The FSC said it avoids conflicts of interest by outsourcing reviews and certification to independent auditors who examine companies‚Äô forestry practices and are paid by the firms being certified. The companies, it said, pay a separate ‚Äúannual administration¬†fee‚ÄĚ based on their forest-products revenue that goes to the FSC after being collected by the auditor.
In one example of industry influence over sustainability audits, a major Canadian timber company, Resolute Forest Products, defeated an effort in 2014 to strip its FSC certification in a western Ontario forest by suing and ultimately firing its auditor.
Resolute for years faced charges from scientists and environmentalists that its clear-cuts in the FSC-certified Black Spruce Forest had decimated habitat for threatened woodland caribou. As early as 2012, auditors at the Rainforest Alliance, a nonprofit hired by Resolute, found the timber company failed to meet FSC habitat-protection requirements. Another 2013 Rainforest Alliance audit examined complaints from environmental groups that Resolute‚Äôs logging ‚Äúwill lead to the extirpation of caribou from the Black Spruce Forest.‚ÄĚ
Auditors suspended Resolute‚Äôs certification in January 2014, citing a failure to meet FSC forest-protection requirements.¬†In May 2014, Resolute sued the Rainforest Alliance and its auditors, personally, calling their reviews flawed and biased. The company sought $400,000 in damages.¬†It also asked for an injunction blocking the audit‚Äôs public release, which an Ontario court granted.¬†The lawsuit noted that certification was ‚Äúcritical to Resolute‚Äôs business model.‚ÄĚ
The lawsuit was settled in 2015, with the alliance agreeing to assign new auditors to redo Resolute‚Äôs negative review. The follow-up audit found Resolute met FSC standards and had resolved the issues from the earlier audit.
Chris Wedeles, one of the original auditors Resolute sued, said he was ‚Äúdisappointed‚ÄĚ that the new auditors ‚Äúreviewed the same evidence and came to a different conclusion.‚ÄĚ
The Rainforest Alliance reinstated Resolute‚Äôs certification.¬†Resolute dumped the alliance anyway, moving its auditing business in 2016 to SAI Global, which has re-certified the company every year since.
After the settlement, Resolute‚Äôs then-CEO Richard Garneau told FSC‚Äôs international director¬†general in a 2015 letter that the firm would pull out of FSC unless the certifier addressed the company‚Äôs complaints about burdensome FSC requirements. A top Resolute executive was elected to FSC Canada‚Äôs board in 2021 and continues to serve today.
Resolute did not answer questions about its forestry practices or its lawsuit but said it upholds ‚Äúthe highest standards‚ÄĚ in forestry management.
SAI Global, Garneau and the Rainforest Alliance, which no longer conducts FSC forestry audits, declined to comment.
The FSC said it was not involved in the dispute between Resolute and its auditor and that it wasn‚Äôt influenced to change its standards by Garneau‚Äôs 2015 letter. FSC pointed to recent suspensions of certifications in Quebec as evidence of its commitment to protect caribou.
Meanwhile, problems with caribou in the Black Spruce Forest persist.
In 2020 and 2021, SAI Global auditors found that Resolute could not substantiate the effectiveness of its caribou-conservation plan. The auditors resolved the matter, however, after a Resolute consultant argued that logging would decline to a level that could sustain caribou populations ‚Äď though not until 2039.
Bulldozing forests for oil
One of¬†the world‚Äôs largest stretches of certified forests is in northern Alberta, where the FSC has signed off on the logging practices of Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries Inc.
Over the last two decades, about 878,000 acres of these woodlands, an area more than twice the size of¬†Los Angeles, have been set aside to make way for oil companies to operate open-pit mines, drilling sites and pipelines in Canada‚Äôs oil¬†sands. The oil exploration involves clear-cutting and bulldozing the forest. Some environmentalists consider it one of the world's most destructive industrial projects.
Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries holds logging rights to the forest, which includes old-growth woodlands, according to company disclosures and environmental studies. A clause in the company‚Äôs agreement with Alberta allows regulators to allocate chunks of the woods for oil-and-gas development.
When that happens, the FSC allows Alberta-Pacific to do a carve-out: removing the FSC certification from the land marked for oil development, while retaining certification for the surrounding forest. The arrangement has allowed Alberta-Pacific to retain certification in the area since 2005 despite the oil-related destruction.
FSC said it encourages certified firms to ‚Äúengage in dialogue‚ÄĚ and use their influence to affect land-use decisions such as oil-and-gas development. But the organization said such decisions are ‚Äúoutside of FSC‚Äôs direct certification scope‚ÄĚ and are governed by provincial and national laws.
Alberta-Pacific said it is proud to have been FSC-certified since 2005 and that it has a goal of ‚Äúmaintaining biodiversity and other forest values.‚ÄĚ It said FSC‚Äôs policies allow carve-outs for oil development because the resulting environmental impacts are ‚Äúbeyond the full control‚ÄĚ of Alberta-Pacific.
Alberta-Pacific makes money from the oil¬†development: Under an agreement with Alberta, it receives compensation from oil-and-gas firms for the destroyed forests. It can also sell wood from forests cleared for oil mining under the ‚ÄúFSC Mix‚ÄĚ label, FSC audits show.
Some of the oil is extracted through surface mining, a process that requires the forest to be bulldozed and stripped of vegetation and soil to make way for pits that can be hundreds of feet deep.
‚ÄúThe mining is totally unsustainable,‚ÄĚ said Barry Robinson, an Alberta environmental attorney who has specialized in oil-and-gas issues. ‚ÄúIt will be generations before it ever grows trees again.‚ÄĚ
To estimate the amount of Ontario primary and old-growth forest harvested in areas with sustainable certification,¬†Reuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data from the National Terrestrial Ecosystem Monitoring System for Canada , forest-stand attribute data from Ontario‚Äôs Forest Resources Inventory and forest-certification maps from the Forest Products Association of Canada .
The news organization focused on the province‚Äôs productive boreal forest stands within Ontario‚Äôs forest management units, as defined in the Forest Resources Inventory, and calculated ages in 2016 through 2020 based on the last-known age of the stand.
Reuters used forest certification maps from 2016 through 2020 to determine how much of the annual harvest was in these areas. Reuters then combined the certified forests harvest data with boreal forest stands to estimate the harvests by age in those areas.
Reuters consulted with nine experts in either forestry or geographic information systems in developing and reviewing its methodology.

Edited by Vanessa O‚ÄôConnell and Brian Thevenot
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äď but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Azerbaijan says it is evacuating its diplomats from Iran for their own safety
item.description:
BAKU, March 6 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan is evacuating its diplomats from Iran for their own safety, Foreign Minister ‚ÄčJeyhun Bayramov said on Friday, a day ‚ÄĆafter Baku said four Iranian drones had crossed its border and injured four people in the Nakhchivan exclave.
Speaking at ‚Äča news conference in Baku, Bayramov ‚Äčsaid that Azerbaijan was evacuating employees from its ‚Ā†embassy in Tehran and its consulate general in ‚ÄčTabriz, the largest city in Iran's northwest, where many ‚Äčethnic Azerbaijanis live.
"On the orders of President Ilham Aliyev, instructions have been given to evacuate our embassy in ‚ÄčTehran and the consulate general in Tabriz ‚Äčfrom Iran, and these instructions are already being implemented. ‚ÄĆWe ‚Ā†cannot put the lives of our people at risk," he said.
Azerbaijan reacted angrily on Thursday to what it said was an incursion ‚Äčby Iranian ‚Äčdrones into ‚Ā†its Nakhchivan exclave, with President Aliyev threatening retaliation. Iran said it had ‚Äčnot launched the drones.
The two ‚Äčcountries ‚Ā†have long had tense relations, with Azerbaijan's close relations with Israel drawing Tehran's ire.
Azerbaijan shares ‚Ā†a ‚Äčmajority Shi'ite Islamic faith with ‚ÄčIran, but its government is firmly secular.

item.title: UN human rights chief to travel to Washington amid concerns over Middle East crisis
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - The United Nations human rights chief will travel to Washington ‚Äčthis month, he said on Friday, ‚ÄĆbut expressed doubt he could influence the development of the Middle East crisis amid mounting ‚Äčconcerns.
"I hope to go to ‚ÄčWashington D.C. later this month, and we ‚Ā†will see what this will ‚Äčbring," UN human rights chief Volker Turk ‚Äčtold reporters in Geneva.
"We have obviously been in touch, but to be honest, I don't ‚Äčthink it's in any way meaningful ‚Äčin terms of influencing the current trajectory," he ‚ÄĆadded.
Turk ‚Ā†urged international actors to rapidly de-escalate the regional conflict, now into its seventh day after U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran.
Israel said ‚Äčon Friday ‚Äčit ‚Ā†launched "broad-scale" air strikes on Iran's infrastructure. It also continued strikes on Beirut's ‚Äčsouthern suburbs, which are under ‚Äčan evacuation ‚Ā†order .
Large booms rang out across Tel Aviv and other parts of Israel as ‚Ā†Israeli ‚Äčdefence systems attempted to ‚Äčintercept an Iranian rocket volley on Friday morning.

item.title: Russia says it will respond if Finland hosts nuclear weapons, accuses Helsinki of stoking tension
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 6 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Friday that Finland's plan to lift a ban on hosting nuclear arms raised tensions in ‚ÄčEurope and posed a potential threat to Russia, to which Moscow ‚Äčwould respond if such a deployment took place.
Finland, ‚Ā†which shares a long border with Russia, maintained neutrality ‚Äčduring the Cold War but joined NATO in 2023 ‚Äčin response to Russia's war in Ukraine.
On Thursday it said it was planning to lift a longstanding ban on having nuclear weapons ‚Äčon its territory in a move that could open ‚Äčthe door to placing them there during times of war.
"This is a ‚ÄĆstatement ‚Ā†that leads to an escalation of tensions on the European continent," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"This statement adds to Finland's vulnerability, a vulnerability provoked by the actions ‚Äčof the Finnish ‚Äčauthorities. The ‚Ā†fact is that by deploying nuclear weapons on its territory, Finland is beginning to ‚Äčthreaten us. And if Finland threatens us, ‚Äčwe take ‚Ā†appropriate measures."
Russia's actions in Ukraine and the unpredictable actions of U.S. President Donald Trump, especially his stated aim ‚Ā†of ‚Äčtaking over Greenland, have caused European ‚Äčgovernments to rethink their security, including the role of nuclear arms.

item.title: Blanket evacuation orders in Lebanon raise international law concerns, says UN human rights chief
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - Large-scale evacuation orders issued by the Israeli army for southern Lebanon and Beirut's southern suburbs raise serious concerns ‚Äčunder international law, the U.N. human rights chief said on Friday.
"These blanket, ‚ÄĆmassive displacement orders we are talking here about hundreds and thousands of people," said United Nations human rights chief, Volker Turk.
"This raises serious concern under international humanitarian law, ‚Äčand in particular when it comes to issues around forced transfer," ‚Äčhe added.
Israel carried out heavy airstrikes on the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of ‚Ā†Beirut overnight after ordering its residents to leave, while the Iran-backed ‚Äčgroup warned Israelis to leave towns and villages at the frontier.
On Thursday, an ‚ÄčIsraeli military spokesperson told residents of the southern suburbs to move east and north, posting a map showing four large districts of the capital he said they must leave, ‚Äčincluding areas adjacent to Beirut's airport.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in ‚Äčthe Middle East on Monday when Hezbollah opened fire, triggering a new Israeli offensive, ‚ÄĆwith ‚Ā†airstrikes focused on Beirut's southern suburbs and on southern and eastern Lebanon.
"Lebanon is becoming a key flashpoint. I'm extremely concerned and worried about the latest developments," Turk said in Geneva following the firing of rockets into Israel by ‚ÄčHezbollah and the ‚ÄčIsraeli army's strong ‚Ā†response in recent days.
Hezbollah, in a message published in Hebrew on its Telegram channel early on Friday, warned Israelis ‚Äčto leave towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the ‚Äčborder.
During fighting ‚Ā†between Hezbollah and Israel in 2024, tens of thousands of Israelis were evacuated from towns in the border area, but many have since returned. Israeli officials ‚Ā†have ‚Äčpreviously said there are no plans to ‚Äčremove them for now.
"The world urgently needs to see steps to contain the Middle East crisis," ‚ÄčTurk added.

item.title: Iran war exposes fragility of Western aluminium market
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - The Iran war is exposing a growing vulnerability in the West's supply of aluminium , a metal classified as a critical manufacturing input by both the United States and the European Union.
The London Metal Exchange (LME) aluminium price hit a four-year high of $3,418 per metric ton on Wednesday after one Gulf producer, the Qatalum joint venture between Norsk ‚ÄčHydro (NHY.OL) , opens new tab and Qatar Aluminum Manufacturing (QAMC.QA) , opens new tab , started powering down its smelter and another, Aluminium Bahrain (ALBH.BH) , opens new tab , declared force majeure .
The continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz risks further disruption to a regional production hub that ‚ÄĆaccounts for 23% of non-Chinese supply.
Aluminium has historically been cushioned against such unforeseen supply hits by high inventory and excess smelter capacity in China, where producers would lift run-rates at the first sign of rising prices.
Inventory cover is now much reduced and China no longer has much spare capacity to flex, rendering the market much more sensitive to the sort of disruption currently unfolding in the Middle East.
The LME's daily stocks reports show that aluminium has been leaving LME warehouses ‚Äčin Malaysia's Port Klang at a daily rate of 2,000 tons since the start of January.
No one has paid much attention.
LME aluminium stocks lost much of their signalling power over the ‚Äčlast 10 years as traders and banks tussled for metal to lock into lucrative warehouse deals.
The resulting churn, shifting metal in and out of the LME's warrant ‚Ā†system, served to obscure any read-through to what was happening in the physical supply chain.
But the daily stocks noise has been masking a steady depletion of what was a 3-million-ton aluminium mountain at the start ‚Äčof the decade.
Combined registered and off-warrant stocks ended February at 583,000 tons, the lowest level since the LME started publishing off-warrant inventory figures in 2020.
Moreover, a significant amount of what remains is Russian aluminium, which ‚Äčaccounted for 58% of warranted stocks at the end of January.
That's not a lot of use to many Western buyers. The U.S. and Britain banned the import of Russian metal in 2024 to prevent Moscow from funding its war in Ukraine, and the EU will follow suit this year.
The pool of usable metal in the LME system is therefore far smaller than even the falling headline figure suggests.
The shift in stock dynamics is a reflection of profound structural changes in aluminium's supply ‚Äčlandscape.
Chinese producers are now running up against the government's mandated annual capacity cap of just over 45 million tons.
Chinese production growth slowed from 4% in 2024 to 2% last year, with smelters running at ‚Äčan annualised rate of 44.5 million tons in December, according to the International Aluminium Institute (IAI).
The production slowdown is causing China's trade with the rest of the world to change.
Chinese manufacturers have been importing more primary metal, particularly from ‚ÄĆRussia. The ‚Ā†world's largest producer imported a record 2.5 million tons last year together with just over 1 million tons of unwrought alloy, according to the World Bureau of Metal Statistics, which sources data from official customs figures.
And China has been exporting less semi-manufactured products such as tube, sheet and foil. Outbound shipments fell by almost 10% year-on-year in 2025, equivalent to a Western market loss of almost 600,000 tons.
In other words, China is importing more aluminium metal and exporting fewer finished products, tightening Western supply at both ends of the chain.
Western smelters have even less capacity flex than their Chinese peers.
Production outside of China did no more ‚Äčthan flat-line last year, according to the IAI.
Here the ‚Äčcore problem is the price of energy, a ‚Ā†crucial cost component in the electrolytic smelting process.
There is significant idle smelter capacity in both the U.S. and Europe but restarts need to compete with other sectors, particularly data centres, for scarce long-term power supplies.
Indeed, high power prices continue to take their toll on existing plants. South32 (S32.AX) , opens new tab is currently in the process of placing its ‚ÄčMozambique smelter on care and maintenance after failing to negotiate an economically viable power contract.
While Gulf producers are currently in the firing line, the ‚Äčevolving energy shock from the ‚Ā†Iran war risks further undermining the West's ability to build longer-term supply resilience.
Aluminium is a ubiquitous feature of modern life, used in everything from homes to cars to food packaging.
It is also key to the energy transition.
Back in 2020 the World Bank identified aluminium as a "high-impact" and "cross-cutting" metal in all existing and potential green energy technologies.
However, it is a metal facing ever more price volatility as the global market emerges from a prolonged period ‚Ā†of surplus to ‚Äčone where supply looks a lot more problematic and stocks are much lower.
The Iran war is a wake-up call for a ‚Äčvery important metal.
( Andy Home is a Reuters columnist. The opinions expressed are his own)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) for thought-provoking, data-driven commentary on markets and finance. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe ‚Äčto hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Asia has limited options to diversify from Mideast energy reliance
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Asian energy buyers are scrambling to find alternatives as the Iran war creates unprecedented supply disruption, but the region has limited longer-term options to reduce its heavy reliance on Middle Eastern oil.
The world's top crude importing ‚Äčregion buys 60% of its oil and petrochemical feedstock from the Middle East, where the war that started with Israeli and U.S. attacks on Iran nearly a week ‚ÄĆago has pushed up global energy prices and threatens to drive inflation and hurt economic growth.
Unable to receive Middle Eastern crude, refiners from China to Southeast Asia are looking for expensive alternatives that will take weeks or months to arrive, while some are cutting output. This week, China and Thailand , opens new tab suspended exports of oil products while Vietnam halted crude exports, which typically go to Australia.
However, alternative sources have drawbacks including distance, refinery configurations, long-term contracts and cost.
For example, oil shipped from ‚ÄčWest Africa and the Americas takes 1-1/2 to 2 months to reach China, meaning orders need to be placed three months in advance.
By comparison, it takes roughly 25 ‚Äčdays for oil to reach China via the Strait of Hormuz.
Also, switching crude grades changes product yields at refineries, which must adjust their operations.
"If ‚Ā†you put a new crude into the refinery, you have to change the cutoff points (boundaries separating crude into different products). You have to change gasoline blending. There's a lot of things ‚Äčyou need to change. It's hard work," said Adi Imsirovic, director of consultancy Surrey Clean Energy.
"This is why diversification has been so poor in a lot of countries," he said.
Energy Aspects analyst Richard ‚ÄčJones said some governments may seek diversification at the margins, but many Asian refiners are tied to Middle East term contracts.
"Simply put, even replacing a modest share of the roughly 16 million barrels a day of Middle Eastern crude that arrives to Asia with Atlantic basin supply is not feasible," he said.
In Japan, which has sourced 95% of its oil from the Middle East since halting nearly all Russian oil imports after Moscow's invasion ‚Äčof Ukraine, refiners run ageing plants optimised for Middle Eastern crude.
With gasoline demand declining, refiners have been wary of investing in upgrades needed to take on new sources such as Canada's ‚Äčheavy TMX.
Muyu Xu, senior analyst at Kpler, said Japanese refiners could seek to blend lighter WTI or West African crude with heavier grades from the Americas to approximate the characteristics of Middle Eastern medium-sour.
"The caveat, ‚ÄĆhowever, is the ‚Ā†logistical complexity and refinery operational risks," she said.
For the nearer term, Japan can tap a stockpile of roughly 250 days.
Top importer China has smaller reserves - roughly 78 days' worth - but a more diverse supplier profile, sourcing roughly half of its oil from the Middle East including Iran, where it has been the top buyer. China also buys from Russia despite western sanctions, as well as from mainstream producers.
India, with just 25 days of reserves and reliance on the nearby Middle East for 55% of its oil, is scrambling to find alternatives, with Washington this week giving it a ‚Äčone-month reprieve to buy Russian oil after U.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump pressured it with punitive tariffs to curb ‚Ā†its purchases from Moscow.
The market for liquefied natural gas is much smaller and tighter. No.2 producer Qatar's move to halt production due to the war has had a swift impact, with India rationing gas to industrial customers.
Michal Meidan, head of China energy research at the Oxford Institute for ‚ÄčEnergy Studies, said the situation could lead to fuel switching and demand destruction.
"Long term, South Asian countries could look to limit the share of gas ‚Äčin their energy mix and ‚Ā†follow China's model of reliance on coal and renewables," she said.
Tim Zhang, founder of Singapore-based Edge Research, said Asia could increase the share of non-fossil fuel such as renewables and nuclear in its energy mix or diversify its conventional fuel supply.
Surrey's Imsirovic said a prolonged disruption could prompt governments to reconsider their reliance on Middle East energy entirely.
"It's going to be like the Asian Currency Crisis or something. Definitely, ‚Ā†people will ‚Äčseriously have to rethink," he said.
"In sunny Asia, get some solar panels and buy an EV. End of story."

item.title: Exclusive: Slovakia seeks to raise Russian gas imports before EU ban kicks in
item.description:
PRAGUE/WARSAW, March 6 (Reuters) - Slovakia's national gas company SPP is in talks with ‚ÄĆGazprom to increase Russian gas imports this year and next, compared with 2025 levels, ahead of a European Union ban on Russian gas, two sources familiar with the matter said.
EU gas prices have jumped 50% this week after ‚ÄčQatar halted liquefied natural gas exports following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and ‚ÄčTehran's retaliation. SPP began the talks before the conflict erupted.
SPP said in November it ‚Ā†had received only one third of its gas from Russia in 2025 to date, after transit ‚Äčthrough Ukraine ended in December 2024. Russian supplies had previously made up most of its roughly 3 ‚Äčbillion cubic metres of annual imports.
The company may lift Russian purchases to as much as 100% of its needs until 2027 if discussions succeed, the sources said.
Slovakia last year switched to importing Russian gas via Turkey, but capacity ‚Äčconstraints limited the flows.
SPP, which covers about two-thirds of Slovak gas demand, has a long-term supply ‚Äčcontract with Gazprom running until 2034.
SPP and Gazprom declined to comment.
EU rules bar countries from increasing contract volumes ‚ÄĆfor Russian ‚Ā†gas as part of the bloc's drive to punish Moscow for its invasion of Ukraine , but they allow some necessary amendments to existing deals.
One source said the talks included unspecified changes to the contract, which covers "large volumes". Any amendment would require an EU exemption to be assessed by the Slovak ‚ÄčEconomy Ministry, the source added.
The ‚Äčministry did not ‚Ā†respond to a request for comment.
EU states must end Russian LNG imports by the end of 2026, though pipeline flows can continue until November 1, ‚Äč2027, if a country is struggling to fill storage with non-Russian gas.
After ‚Äčthen, SPP has ‚Ā†been exploring options to potentially import LNG mainly via Poland and Germany, but also Italy, the sources said. One source added it had received around 20 offers for non-Russian gas after 2027.
Slovakia, along with ‚Ā†Hungary, is ‚Äčalso exempt from the EU's ban on buying Russian ‚Äčoil due to difficulties in accessing alternative supplies, though those imports have been disrupted recently by damage to the Druzhba pipeline.

item.title: Kremlin says the war in Iran has fuelled significant demand for Russian energy
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 6 (Reuters) - The Kremlin ‚ÄĆsaid on Friday that the war in Iran had fuelled demand for Russian energy products, a day after the U.S. Treasury issued ‚Äča 30-day waiver allowing India to buy ‚ÄčRussian oil currently stuck at sea.
The conflict, ‚Ā†which entered its seventh day on Friday, has left ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping passage all-but ‚Äčshut, with countries around the world cut off from a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry ‚ÄčPeskov told reporters that Russia has been and remained ‚Äča reliable supplier of oil and gas, both via pipelines and ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†liquefied form.
"We are seeing a significant increase in demand for Russian energy resources in connection with the war in Iran. Russia has been and remains a reliable ‚Äčsupplier of ‚Äčboth oil and ‚Ā†gas ‚ÄĒ including pipeline gas and liquefied natural gas," Peskov told reporters.
"It also remains ‚Äčcapable of guaranteeing the continuity of all ‚Äčdeliveries for ‚Ā†which contracts have been concluded."
Peskov declined to disclose possible volumes of Russian oil supplies to India following Washington's ‚Ā†waiver, ‚Äčwhich followed months of U.S. pressure on ‚ÄčNew Delhi not to buy Russian oil.

item.title: What's in China's new five-year plan for commodity markets
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China unveiled its 15th five-year plan on Thursday at its annual parliamentary meeting, outlining Beijing's priorities for the economy and sectors slated for policy support and funding.
Here's a summary of what matters for commodity markets:
METALS ‚ÄčAND CRITICAL MINERALS
OVERCAPACITY
CLIMATE, POWER AND COAL
OIL AND GAS
AGRICULTURE

item.title: Snam CEO sees no short-term gas supply issues for Italy
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - Italy's gas supplies should not face short-term disruption from the crisis in the Middle East, the CEO of state-controlled gas grid operator Snam (SRG.MI) , opens new tab said on Thursday.
"All the vessels (due ‚Äčin March) that take gas from Qatar left before the explosion of ‚Äčthe crisis, they passed the Strait of Hormuz and are already moving ‚Ā†towards the final destination so we do not expect any disruptions during March," ‚ÄčAgostino Scornajenchi said while presenting Snam's strategy to 2030.
"We will see what happens in April," ‚Äčhe added, noting there were limited volumes due to come to Italy from Qatar.
Qatar accounts for around 7 billion cubic metres (bcm) of Italy's annual gas imports, covering roughly 11% of the Mediterranean ‚Äčcountry's consumption.
Italian gas storage facilities are currently 45% full, compared with an average of ‚Äčaround 30% in Europe.
Under its new strategy, Snam plans to invest 14 billion euros ($16 billion) over ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†next five years, focusing on gas infrastructure including storage assets and liquefied natural gas terminals.
The gas grid operator expects to divest non-core assets worth 1.6 billion euros and sees potential selective acquisitions worth 1.2 billion euros.
Scornajenchi said Snam was happy with its 11.4% ‚Äčstake in gas ‚Äčdistributor Italgas (IG.MI) , opens new tab , but its ‚Ā†holding in De Nora (DNR.MI) , opens new tab was not a core investment, though there was no urgency to sell it.
Snam also said it was ‚Äčdoubling down on developing a carbon capture and storage project ‚Äčlaunched with ‚Ā†Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab and would inject 400 million euros into the storage business.
The group reported a 10.3% rise in adjusted full-year net profit to 1.42 billion euros, in line with its guidance. It ‚Ā†forecast ‚Äča profit of around 1.45 billion euros for 2026. ‚ÄčNet income is expected to grow an average 4.5% per year until 2030 under its new plan.
($1 = ‚Äč0.8621 euros)

item.title: Ukraine returns 300 soldiers in second day of POW swaps with Russia, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Ukraine brought home ‚Äč300 servicemen and ‚ÄĆtwo civilians from Russia on the ‚Äčsecond day ‚Äčof a prisoner swap, ‚Ā†President Volodymyr ‚ÄčZelenskiy said on ‚ÄčFriday.
Zelenskiy said that privates, sergeants, and officers ‚Äčwere defending ‚ÄčUkraine at different front ‚ÄĆareas ‚Ā†from Donetsk and Luhansk in the east to ‚ÄčKherson ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the south. Zelenskiy posted ‚Äčpictures of ‚Äčmen ‚Ā†exiting buses, hugging and sharing ‚Ā†cigarettes.

item.title: Sri Lanka moving 208 rescued Iranian ship crew to naval camp, sources say
item.description:
GALLE, Sri Lanka, March 6 (Reuters) - Sri Lankan authorities said on Friday they were escorting a second Iranian naval vessel to ‚ÄĆharbour and moving 208 of its crew to a camp, two days after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship in the same area.
President Anura Kumara Dissanayake said his island nation had a "humanitarian responsibility" to take in the crew, as the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israeli war with Iran raged , wreaking havoc on global markets and disrupting trade and travel.
The ‚ÄčSri Lankan Navy identified the second Iranian ship as naval auxiliary vessel IRIS ‚Ā†Booshehr.
Iranian sailors were seen dragging suitcases and carrying bags as they disembarked ‚Äčin Sri Lanka, in pictures shared by the Presidential Media Division.
Other images showed Sri Lankan navy tug boats ‚Äčand naval vessels approaching the Booshehr, which Dissanayake earlier said would be moved to the Trincomalee harbour on the eastern coast.
"About 15 crewmen are still aboard the Iranian ship to help with navigation," a Sri Lankan government source said.
The ship ‚Äčwas experiencing engine problems, that source and another government official said. Both asked not to be named ‚Äčgiven the sensitivity of the issue.
The crew was brought to the port in Sri Lanka's commercial capital Colombo where they ‚ÄĆhad ‚Ā†medical check-ups and were then moved in groups to a navy camp in Welisara, about 18 km (10 miles) away, the officials added.
The defence ministry declined to comment and the navy spokesman's office could not be reached.
The ship Booshehr, which had found itself stranded in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone outside ‚Äčits maritime boundary, reached the ‚Äčarea a day after ‚Ā†the Iranian warship IRIS Dena was sunk while returning from India after a naval exercise.
Dena was hit by a torpedo from a U.S. submarine in the ‚ÄčIndian Ocean, about 19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's coast, killing 87 people ‚Äčon board ‚Ā†and dramatically widening the scope of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
Iran's foreign minister thanked Sri Lanka for helping rescue survivors from the warship.
"That vessel ... was ceremonial, unloaded, unarmed," Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Saeed Khatibzadeh told reporters in ‚Ā†New Delhi, ‚Äčwhere he is attending a conference.
Both Washington and Tehran are ‚Äčkey trade partners for Sri Lanka. The United States accounts for about 40% of its apparel exports and Iran is one of ‚Äčits main tea buyers.

item.title: Middle East situation is 'major humanitarian emergency', UN refugee agency says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.N. refugee agency said on Friday that nearly 100,000 people ‚Äčhave been displaced within Lebanon and tens of ‚ÄĆthousands of Syrian refugees there have fled back over the border, calling the situation in the region a "major humanitarian ‚Äčemergency".
Israel has issued large-scale evacuation orders for southern Lebanon ‚Äčand parts of Beirut amid hostilities with ‚Ā†the Iran-backed Lebanese group Hezbollah since a U.S.-Israeli ‚Äčair campaign against Iran began on February 28.
"UNHCR has ‚Äčdeclared the escalating crisis in the Middle East as a major humanitarian emergency requiring an immediate response across the region ‚Äčand into Southeast Asia," Ayaki Ito, the U.N. ‚Äčrefugee agency's Director of Emergency and Programme Support, told a Geneva ‚ÄĆpress ‚Ā†briefing.
Ito added that the figures given for the scale of displacement so far are likely an underestimate.
He said that some 100,000 people have been displaced within Iran in ‚Äčthe first days ‚Äčof the ‚Ā†conflict and that UNHCR staff there are receiving hundreds of calls daily from ‚ÄčIranians seeking assistance.
The World Health Organization is ‚Äčstepping ‚Ā†up disease surveillance in Lebanon due to the mass displacement, said regional director Hanan Balkhy.
"It worries us very ‚Ā†much, the ‚Äčnumbers of the displaced populations ‚Äčand the lack of adequate water and sanitation," she said.

item.title: Lithuania prosecutes five people over 2024 parcel blasts
item.description:
VILNIUS, March 6 (Reuters) - Lithuania's deputy ‚ÄĆgeneral prosecutor said on Friday he has initiated court proceedings against five ‚Äčpersons over detonations of ‚Äčparcels carried by DHL and DPD in ‚Ā†Europe in 2024 .
The suspects were ‚Äčcitizens of Russia, Ukraine and ‚ÄčLithuania and were being accused of terrorism, Deputy General Prosecutor Arturas Urbelis told ‚Äča press conference.
Lithuania's prosecution service ‚Äčand criminal police last year said the ‚ÄĆdetonations ‚Ā†of the parcels in Germany, Britain and Poland were organised and supervised by Russian citizens with ‚Äčties to ‚ÄčRussia's military ‚Ā†intelligence.
Security officials in Lithuania said in 2024 the ‚Äčfour parcels were part ‚Äčof ‚Ā†a test run for a Russian plot to trigger explosions on ‚Ā†cargo ‚Äčflights to the ‚ÄčUnited States.

item.title: Hungary to expel seven Ukrainian nationals, government spokesperson says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 6 (Reuters) - Hungary is going to ‚Äčexpel all seven ‚ÄĆUkrainian nationals who were detained while ‚Äčthey were ‚Äčtransporting cash and gold ‚Ā†from Austria ‚Äčto Ukraine, government ‚Äčspokesman Zoltan Kovacs wrote on X on Friday.
Kovacs ‚Äčalso said ‚Äčthat the team transporting ‚ÄĆcash ‚Ā†and gold was "supervised by a former general of ‚Äčthe Ukrainian ‚ÄčSecurity ‚Ā†Service, with a former ‚Äčmajor of ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†Ukrainian Air Force acting as deputy ‚Ā†and ‚Äčassisted ‚Äčby individuals with military experience."

item.title: Israel's Hezbollah attacks are likely to continue beyond Iran war, source says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 6 (Reuters) - Israeli attacks against Lebanon's Iranian-backed ‚ÄčHezbollah group will likely continue after its joint air war with the U.S. against Iran ends, ‚ÄĆa source briefed on Israel's military strategy told Reuters, describing the two fronts as unconnected.
Israel warned Lebanon before the war that it would strike the country hard if Hezbollah, the most powerful of Iran's regional proxies, gets involved. On Monday, Hezbollah ‚Äčfired rockets at Israel, sparking Israeli strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs and Lebanon's east and south.
The military ‚Äčon Thursday warned Lebanese to leave Beirut's southern suburbs after ordering them to clear ‚Ā†a broad swathe of the south as it carries out air strikes that a military source said ‚Äčare aimed at "removing the threat" posed by Hezbollah.
Early on Friday, Hezbollah warned Israelis to leave towns near the border.
The ‚Äčsource, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to describe Israel's assessment of fighting with Hezbollah, said Israel would not tolerate residents of northern Israel, who have not evacuated their towns and villages, to be under fire. The source did not ‚Äčrefer to the Hezbollah warning.
That meant that Israeli operations in Lebanon would likely continue even when the ‚ÄčIran strikes draw to a close, the source said. Israel's military did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Two ‚ÄĆsenior Lebanese ‚Ā†security officials and a foreign security official based in Lebanon said they also expected Israel to pursue military operations in Lebanon even once the broader conflict with Iran came to a close.
"This is about ending Hezbollah once and for all," one of the Lebanese security officials said, of the group, which held major sway over ‚Äčthe Lebanese state before Israeli ‚Äčattacks in 2024 killed ‚Ā†its leader and many of its fighters.
All three officials said a long-term Israeli military occupation of the entire border strip of southern Lebanon was likely.
Israel has said ‚Äčit will not evacuate its border towns and has sent more soldiers into ‚ÄčLebanon, saying ‚Ā†this was a defensive measure meant to protect its citizens who live nearby.
The Lebanese health ministry has reported that 123 people have been killed and another 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks this week. Its figures ‚Ā†do not ‚Äčdistinguish between civilians and combatants.
Trump said on Tuesday the U.S. ‚Äčand Israeli air attacks that began on Saturday had been projected to last four to five weeks but could go on longer.

item.title: EU halts visa-free travel for Georgian officials
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 6 (Reuters) - The European Commission said on Friday it ‚Äčwas suspending visa-free travel for ‚ÄĆholders of Georgian diplomatic, service and official passports in response to ‚Äčwhat it called a ‚Äčdeliberate and persisting violation of ‚Ā†the commitments taken under ‚Äčits visa-free regime.
"Representatives of Georgian authorities ‚Äčhave to use their diplomatic or service passport when travelling to ‚Äčthe EU for official and ‚Äčdiplomatic purposes. Not doing so may ‚ÄĆresult ‚Ā†in the issuance of an entry ban," the Commission said in a statement.
Once among the ‚Äčmost ‚Äčdemocratic and ‚Ā†pro-Western of the successor states to emerge ‚Äčfrom the Soviet Union, ‚ÄčGeorgia ‚Ā†has turned increasingly authoritarian since the outbreak of the war in ‚Ā†Ukraine ‚Äčand has deepened economic ‚Äčties with neighbouring Russia.

item.title: UN official: 100,000 Lebanese in shelters after 'unprecedented' Israeli warnings
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 6 (Reuters) - About 100,000 people have fled to shelters in Lebanon and the number of displaced is expected to rapidly increase ‚Äčfollowing "unprecedented" Israeli warnings ordering people out of large parts ‚ÄĆof the country, a senior U.N. official said on Friday.
With war raging between Israel and Lebanon's Hezbollah, the Israeli military on Thursday ordered residents ‚Äčout of Beirut's southern suburbs, including areas controlled by ‚Äčthe Iran-backed group, as well as parts of the eastern ‚Ā†Bekaa Valley, after ordering people out of a swathe of ‚Äčsouth Lebanon on Wednesday.
"What we saw in the last couple of ‚Äčdays is, I would say ... unprecedented in terms of the scale here in Lebanon of the warnings, the displacement orders, and the reaction, the panic ‚Äčalso, that this has all created," Imran Riza, U.N. Humanitarian ‚ÄčCoordinator in Lebanon, told Reuters.
"At the moment, there are about 100,000 people that ‚ÄĆare, ‚Ā†as of this morning, in some 477 collective shelters. There are some 57 shelters that still have some space, but basically the capacity is being reached very, very quickly," Riza said.
Noting the ‚Äčpanic and gridlock ‚Äčcaused by ‚Ā†the Israeli displacement orders, Riza said: "We had people moving all over the place and not knowing ‚Äčwhere to go to. So yes, I think we're ‚Äčgoing ‚Ā†to have an increased number quite quickly," he said.
He noted that more than a million people were uprooted in Lebanon during a ‚Ā†war between ‚ÄčHezbollah and Israel in 2024, 75-80% ‚Äčof whom were not in shelters. "This time again, the majority will not be in ‚Äčshelters probably," he said.

item.title: Indonesian court acquits four activists accused of inciting deadly 2025 student protests
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 6 (Reuters) - An Indonesian court on Friday acquitted an activist accused of inciting the country's worst riots in over two decades in August last year, a ‚Äčruling described by rights groups as a small victory for civil liberties in the Southeast ‚ÄĆAsian country.
Thousands of students, human rights activists and civilians joined nationwide protests against the government's spending priorities, including a decision to increase financial incentives for lawmakers.
The protests turned violent after the killing by police of a motorcycle taxi driver in ‚Äčthe capital Jakarta, with the riots lasting into September and ending with 10 ‚Äčpeople killed.
Nearly 7,000 people have been arrested as of February 2026, with 500 ‚Ā†sentenced and 125 were still in detention, according to a report released by an ‚Äčindependent fact-checking organisation set up by human rights groups.
A court in Central Jakarta on Friday acquitted ‚ÄčDelpedro Marhaen, who works for the Lokataru Foundation, a non-government organisation providing legal assistance for protesters. Delpedro was widely considered to be the face of the protests after his arrest in September.
Three other people, including Delpedro's colleague ‚Äčand two other activists, were also acquitted.
All the defendants were declared not guilty of inciting people ‚Äčto join the August protests, and were also acquitted on the charges of triggering riots by spreading fake ‚ÄĆnews ‚Ā†and hate speech through social media, the judge said.
The judge also ruled that they had not incited minors to join the protests, thus absolving them of the charge of child exploitation.
"The ruling not only belongs to us but also belongs to all political prisoners.. Imagine we ‚Äčhave been arrested for ‚Äčsix months but ‚Ā†now we are proven not guilty," said Delpedro after the trial, as he wrapped himself in an Iranian flag.
A rights group known ‚Äčas the Commission for the Disappeared and Victims of Violence called ‚Äčthe ruling ‚Ā†an "oasis in the rollback of Indonesia's democracy".
Another group, Indonesian Legal Aid Foundation, described the verdicts as a small victory for civil liberties but said the government must act to protect freedom of expression.
The protests ‚Ā†were President ‚ÄčPrabowo Subianto's first test since his landslide election victory in ‚Äč2024 , analysts have said.
The unrest has been described as the worst since 1998, when student protests led to the toppling ‚Äčof former President Suharto and three decades of authoritarian rule.

item.title: Saudi Aramco boosts Red Sea oil shipments as Hormuz disruption curbs exports
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - Top oil exporter Saudi Arabia is increasing shipments from the Red ‚ÄĆSea, but the volumes are far from enough to offset the drop from the crisis-hit Strait of Hormuz, shipping data showed.
Saudi Arabia and other Gulf producers - the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Iraq - have halted Hormuz shipments since Saturday after the United States ‚Äčand Israel attacked Iran and Tehran retaliated with drone and missile strikes.
With hundreds of vessels now stuck ‚Äčnear the strait, Aramco has told some buyers to load cargoes at the Red Sea ‚Ā†port of Yanbu, sources said.
The terminal loaded 9.4 million barrels, or 1.9 million barrels per day (bpd), in the ‚Äčfirst five days of March, up about 60% from 1.1 million bpd in February and 1.3 million bpd ‚Äčin January, according to LSEG data.
Saudi Arabia exports over 7 million bpd, of which around 6 million bpd pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The kingdom can, in theory, reroute up to 5 million bpd to the Red Sea via the East-West pipeline.
The ‚ÄčRed Sea port could handle in excess of 4.5 million bpd, but rarely loaded more than 2.5 million ‚Äčbpd, traders said, and the pipeline is mainly designed to transport Arab Light, one of several crude grades the kingdom ‚ÄĆproduces.
The Red ‚Ā†Sea route also carries risks from Houthi forces, whose attacks disrupted shipping during the Israel-Gaza war.
Some tanker fixtures for Yanbu failed due to high freight rates and security risks, traders and ship brokers said. One such was tanker Pantanassa, which had been due to load at Yanbu on March 28 and 29 to deliver oil to South Korea.
Around 10 ‚Äčtankers are expected to call ‚Äčat Yanbu, according to ‚Ā†Kpler data. Deals for at least four more tankers were being fixed on Wednesday and Thursday, shipping sources said.
India's Reliance provisionally agreed to lift one supertanker with up ‚Äčto 2 million barrels of crude and a smaller Suezmax ship that can carry ‚Äčup to 1 ‚Ā†million barrels, the sources, speaking on condition of anonymity because the deals are confidential, said.
Reliance was not immediately available for comment.
Shippers are struggling to find tankers in proximity to Yanbu and it will take several days for new ships to ‚Ā†arrive, ‚ÄčJaniv Shah from Rystad Energy said on a conference call on Thursday.
Nearly ‚Äč10% of mainstream oil tankers are trapped near the Strait of Hormuz, according to Rystad.

item.title: BP trims board as part of chairman Manifold's reset plan
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab Chairman Albert Manifold on Friday announced plans for a slimmer board as ‚ÄĆpart of the British oil company's reset strategy, with former Shell chief financial officer Simon Henry among those departing.
The leaner board is one of Manifold's changes since arriving as chairman in October and urging a ‚Äčfaster shift at BP back to oil and gas investments.
In December, Murray Auchincloss abruptly departed ‚Äčas CEO, with Woodside Energy's Meg O'Neill set to take over the ‚Ā†job in April.
The other directors stepping down from the board are Melody Meyer and Karen ‚ÄčRichardson, Manifold said. Henry was appointed to the board in September, while Richardson and Meyer ‚Äčjoined in 2021 and 2017, respectively.
After the changes, the board will consist of 10 directors, down from 13. Manifold said the majority will have been appointed in the last five years.
"We believe that a leaner ‚Äčboard is a more agile board, and that this is essential at this point in ‚Äčthe execution of BP‚Äôs reset strategy," Manifold said. "It will allow for faster decision-making and sharper oversight, both ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†which are critical to driving long-term shareholder value."
BP's profits for last year were 16% lower than the previous year's at $7.5 billion. The British oil company announced in February it had suspended its share buybacks to focus on shrinking its debt.
The company embarked on a major strategy shift last ‚Äčyear, cutting billions in ‚Äčplanned renewable energy initiatives ‚Ā†and shifting its focus back to traditional oil and gas.
This followed a foray into renewables by Auchincloss' predecessor, Bernard Looney.
Woodside's O'Neill is ‚ÄčBP's first external hire for the post in more than a century. ‚ÄčShe will ‚Ā†receive a base salary of 1.6 million pounds, according to the company's annual report on Friday.
Outgoing CEO Auchincloss earned 5.3 million pounds ($7.07 million) last year, down from 5.4 million pounds in 2024, ‚Ā†the ‚Äčannual report showed.
BP's Annual General Meeting will take place ‚ÄčApril 23.
Shares in BP rose 10% last year, while shares in rivals Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab and Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab increased by 12% ‚Äčand 11%, respectively.
($1 = 0.7499 pounds)

item.title: BREAKINGVIEWS
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - What endgame for Iran are investors pricing in? Judging by moves in financial markets since U.S. President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched the bombing campaign that killed ‚ÄčIranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Saturday, they seem to expect a swift end to the conflict. Yet that ‚ÄĆbegs the question of what happens afterwards. There's a risk that investors are confusing base-case and best-case scenarios.
There are many possible outcomes in Iran. One scenario is that the current government falls apart following the death of scores of senior leaders. The country could then split along factional or ethnic lines, as Libya did after Muammar Gaddafi ‚Äčwas overthrown. A second option is that the Islamic Republic limps on in roughly its current form. Or Iran could embrace a ‚Äčmore open and democratic form of government, reintegrating its economy with the world and encouraging Washington to phase out ‚Ā†sanctions.
The first two scenarios could see oil prices spiking above $100 a barrel, from around $87 today, Capital Economics reckons. That‚Äôs because internal turmoil would disrupt ‚ÄčIran‚Äôs daily output exceeding 3 million barrels, while an outcome where the hardline Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) keeps its grip on power would probably involve ‚ÄčTehran continuing to threaten tankers that sail through the Strait of Hormuz. Only the last scenario would lead to increased Iranian oil production, an economic recovery, and prices of the black stuff falling to $50 a barrel over time.
The relative calm in financial and commodities markets suggests investors expect a swift end to the conflict and to ‚Äčdisruption, plus a high probability of a benign scenario thereafter. Despite the recent jump, oil prices remain far below the $130 a barrel level they ‚Äčapproached following Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine four years ago. European gas prices have jumped 60% to 52 euros per megawatt-hour, way below the 100 euros per MWh at ‚ÄĆwhich they ‚Ā†consistently traded in 2022. Meanwhile, gas prices for delivery a few years hence have hardly budged, implying a short interruption.
Washington claimed on Thursday it had destroyed over 60% of Tehran‚Äôs missiles and launchers, plus 30 of its ships. Trump also asserted that Iran wanted talks, that the U.S. would have to be involved in choosing the next leader of Iran, and IRGC ally Mojtaba Khamenei, the late leader's hardline son, was an unlikely choice as a ‚Äčsuccessor.
Still, a fifth of the world‚Äôs ‚Äčoil supply is currently trapped ‚Ā†behind the Strait of Hormuz. On Goldman Sachs figures, oil prices will rise even if the status quo persists for only a few months. The IRGC has been taking a stronger governing role since the latest war ‚Äčcommenced, Reuters reported on Wednesday citing six Iranian and regional sources. As such, the market may be attaching too ‚Äčmuch weight to ‚Ā†a swift end to the conflict ‚ÄĒ and choosing the wrong base case.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Nepal's rapper-mayor Balendra Shah poised to become prime minister
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - After Nepal's historic youth-led uprising last September killed 77 people and forced then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli to resign, a 35-year-old rapper-turned-politician posted a typically terse ‚Äčmessage to millions of followers on social media.
"Dear Gen Z, the resignation of your killer has come," Balendra Shah - popularly known only as Balen - wrote. "Now your generation ‚ÄĆwill have to lead the country. Be prepared."
Five months on, the musician who cut his political teeth in 2022 when he became the mayor of the capital Kathmandu, is poised to become Nepal's next prime minister following the country's first election since the September uprising.
Shah's Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) was leading in around 100 seats, far ahead of its main rivals, early counting trends from the election commission showed on Friday.
Final results, covering 165 seats decided by direct vote and 110 through proportional ‚Äčrepresentation, are expected within days.
The Nepali Congress, currently in second place, has already conceded defeat, and analysts said the RSP's dominant showing means it will likely form the next government.
"Balen ‚ÄčShah is so popular that now buses coming to Kathmandu have stickers on them saying, 'Headed to Balen's city'", said Bipin Adhikari, a constitutional law ‚Ā†expert who teaches at Kathmandu University.
If Shah is able to take power, it would cap a dramatic rise for a man who entered the public spotlight with rap music critical of the establishment ‚Äčand parleyed his popularity to ascend to high political office.
It would also potentially reshape the politics of Nepal , a small Himalayan nation wedged between China and India, that has long been dominated by a handful ‚Äčof established parties .
Some of Shah's nationwide appeal is driven by the work he has done as the mayor of Kathmandu, where he focused on improving the urban infrastructure, such as waste management, and ensuring the delivery of services like healthcare.
He has also faced criticism, including from Human Rights Watch, for allegedly using police to seize the properties of street vendors and landless people.
Shah - who resigned as mayor in January to contest the general election - ‚Äčdid not respond to requests for an interview and questions from Reuters sent via email.
Unlike much of Nepal's political elite comprising veterans from older generations, Shah has made it a habit to ‚Äčlargely shun the mainstream press.
Instead, it is his prolific social media presence, with over 3.5 million followers on platforms like Facebook, that enables him to connect directly with young Nepalis.
"What makes Balen special is that he stays ‚ÄĆconnected with ‚Ā†the youth through his short messages on social media, but it would not be a cakewalk for him after becoming prime minister," said independent political analyst Puranjan Acharya.
Born to a father who practiced traditional Ayurvedic medicine and a homemaker mother, Shah showed an early inclination towards poetry that evolved into a love of rap music, influenced by American artists including Tupac Shakur and Curtis "50 Cent" Jackson, according to an aide.
After securing an undergraduate degree in civil engineering in Nepal, Shah went on to study for a master's degree in structural engineering in southern India - by which time he had ‚Äčalready emerged as a rap star in his ‚Äčhome country.
His songs, often taking on Nepal's ‚Ā†ruling class, struck a chord with many in a country where about 20% of the 30 million population live in grinding poverty.
Released in 2019, one of Shah's best-known songs, "Balidan" - or sacrifice in the Nepali language - has over 12 million views on YouTube.
Its lyrics read:
"Let me speak, sir, it is not a ‚Äčcrime,
Let me open the mind, I am not a curse to the palace,
My mind is not bad, it is not afraid to speak ‚Äčthe truth."
Last ‚Ā†December, Shah joined the RSP, led by former TV host-turned-politician Rabi Lamichhane, as its prime ministerial candidate.
In its manifesto, Shah's RSP has vowed to create 1.2 million jobs and reduce forced migration, in an effort to tap into frustration over unemployment and low wages that have pushed millions of Nepalis to search for work overseas.
The party has also pledged to raise Nepal's per capita income from $1,447 to $3,000, more than double the ‚Ā†nation's economy to $100 ‚Äčbillion GDP and provide safety nets such as healthcare insurance for the entire population - all within five years.
At the national ‚Äčlevel, analysts foresee that if he is elected, much of Shah's success will depend on the talent he surrounds himself with to overhaul a moribund administrative system, riven by corruption.
"It needs a team, experts and support," Acharya said, "Under the existing ‚Äčstate apparatus, he can't perform and he will be finished like wood attacked by termites."

item.title: Exclusive: Iran spent years fostering proxies in Iraq. Now, many aren‚Äôt eager to join the war
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 6 (Reuters) - Iran has spent decades and billions of dollars preparing foreign proxy fighters like A.J., a commander in a pro-Iranian paramilitary group in Iraq, for a moment just like this. Since the U.S. and Israel went to war on the Islamic Republic a week ago, A.J. has been awaiting marching orders from Tehran.
But they have yet to come. And so as the leadership in Tehran faces a potentially existential threat, many of the fighters and militia groups the Iranians cultivated in Iraq have so far not entered the fight for them. There has been no mass mobilization of Iran's proxies inside Iraq, one of the last redoubts of the Islamic Republic's once-formidable system of alliances stretching from Gaza, Lebanon and Syria to Yemen and Iraq.
Some pro-Iranian groups in Iraq have claimed attacks in recent days, to be sure. One group said it had fired drones at "enemy bases in Iraq and the region," and several explosions rocked the northern city of Erbil, a Kurdish stronghold that hosts a U.S. base. But most missile and drone attacks have come directly from Iran, Kurdish officials say. The more than two-dozen ‚Äčattacks claimed online in the name of the Islamic Resistance of Iraq ‚Äď a label used by various militants ‚Äď have caused no significant damage, and in some cases there is no evidence of the attacks.
Even if direct orders do come from Tehran, A.J. believes that they'll only be issued to two or three of the dozens of Iraqi Shi'ite Muslim paramilitaries nurtured by Iran. "I just don't think most of them are reliable anymore," he told Reuters. "Some will act. Others would have front groups that could launch attacks with deniability. But many are just ‚ÄĆlooking out for their own interests these days."
The trajectory of A.J.'s personal journey as a member of an Iranian-backed force in Iraq tracks the rise and fall of Iran's strategy of spreading proxy militias through the region, under the leadership of the elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps and its expeditionary Quds Force, to fight America and Israel. His is the story of how the Israelis and Americans wore down and diminished most of these proxies, leaving the Islamic Republic facing its most perilous moment largely alone.
